- [x] **[UI]** Sidebar and TOC never finished the PR #169 token migration - cool grays, pure white, and a stale pre-redesign teal glow — PR #169 moved the shell onto warm glass tokens (hue-88 surfaces, --bd/--track/--field, olive accent 118), but the sidebar and TOC still mix in pre-redesign values: cool Tailwind grays for hovers/chips/track/rail text, a pure-white light-theme drawer over the warm paper base, and - most visibly - the overall-progress bar's glow hardcodes oklch hue 192 (the OLD teal accent, now only section 0's identity hue) under a bar that is an olive 118 gradient, producing a cyan halo around an olive fill in both themes. _Fix:_ Sweep both components onto the existing tokens: `var(--bar-glow)` for the progress glow (kills the teal remnant and makes it theme-aware), `bg-(--track)` for the progress track, `bg-(--surface-1)`-based mix (mirroring the dark side's color-mix) for the light drawer, `bg-(--field)`/`hover:bg-(--field)` for chip and hover fills, and `border-(--bd)` + `text-(--mut)` on the TOC rail. Zero new CSS - every replacement token already exists and is used by the header ten lines away. _Subsumes the narrower sidebar progress-glow reports from the F2 and F5 audits._ Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:190-191`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:184`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:160`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:43,52`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** Drawer focus trap excludes the visible X close button - Tab can never reach it while the drawer is open — When the mobile drawer opens, the trap collects focusables only from inside the aside, but the toggle button (which now renders as the X close affordance, floating above the overlay at z-50) is a sibling outside the aside. Keyboard users see an X they can never Tab to; the only keyboard exits are Escape (undiscoverable) since overlay click is pointer-only. The APG modal pattern expects the dialog's visible close control inside the Tab cycle. _Fix:_ Include the toggle in the cycle: build the focusables list as `[toggleRef.current, ...aside.querySelectorAll(...)]` (or treat the toggle as the wrap point) so Tab order runs X button -&gt; drawer links -&gt; back to X. No markup changes; the existing sidebar.test.tsx Escape/overlay tests stay green. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:103-105`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:110-116`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:131-142`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** On lesson pages the skip link still lands keyboard users in front of the repeated 7-link sidebar — The global 'Skip to content' link targets #main, but on lesson pages #main's first focusables are the Sidebar's toggle + 7 section links (the same block repeated on every lesson), so after skipping, a keyboard user still tabs ~8 stops before the first link inside the lesson body - on every page turn. Because the aside is position:fixed, its DOM position is layout-free: it can sit after the content with zero visual change. _Fix:_ In page.tsx, render &lt;Sidebar /&gt; after the #lesson-content div (and after the TOC aside). Tab order after 'Skip to content' then goes straight into the lesson, with the learning-path nav reachable at the end - visually identical, and the mobile FAB (part of Sidebar) ends up at the end of the tab order matching its bottom-corner position. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/layout.tsx:69-76`, `web/src/app/learn/[section]/page.tsx:71-72`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:160`, `web/__tests__/components/sidebar.test.tsx:44-48`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[UI]** Drawer height uses 100vh, clipping the last section links behind iOS Safari's expanded toolbar — The drawer is sized h-[calc(100vh-4rem)]. On iOS Safari (and Android Chrome with the URL bar shown), 100vh equals the LARGEST viewport, so the drawer's bottom edge - and the end of its internal scroll range - sits behind the browser toolbar. Because opening the drawer also locks body scroll, the toolbar can never collapse while it is open, so the final learning-path entries (05/06) stay partially hidden and the internal overflow-y-auto cannot scroll them fully into view. _Fix:_ Size against the dynamic viewport: `h-[calc(100vh-4rem)] supports-[height:100dvh]:h-[calc(100dvh-4rem)]` (or simply swap to 100dvh - the 100vh declaration remains as the natural CSS fallback if written as two declarations). One class change, desktop (lg) rendering unaffected. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:160`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:94-95`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:197-206`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[UX]** Prev/next cards at 375px truncate titles into indistinguishable 'Quantum...' stubs — PrevNext keeps both cards side-by-side at every width. At 375px each card gets ~170px; after p-4 padding, the chevron, and the gap, roughly 90-100px remains for the title, which `truncate` cuts to ~11 characters. Four of the seven section titles begin with 'Quantum' ('Quantum Computing Foundations', 'Quantum Hardware on Amazon Braket', 'Quantum Algorithms', 'Quantum Machine Learning'), so on phones the destination labels of the primary end-of-lesson controls collapse into identical 'Quantum Co...' stubs - the reader cannot tell where either card goes. _Fix:_ Stack the cards below sm: change the wrapper to `flex flex-col sm:flex-row ...` (and drop the empty `flex-1` spacer divs below sm or leave them - they collapse in column flow). Full-width cards restore readable titles and enlarge the touch targets; &gt;=640px keeps the current side-by-side layout untouched. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/prev-next.tsx:15`, `web/src/components/prev-next.tsx:26,39`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** Drawer's closed modal-contract work (inert, scroll lock, focus return, Tab trap) has zero regression coverage — The 2026-06-17 closed finding #3 (focus trap + inert background + body scroll lock) is implemented but entirely untested: sidebar.test.tsx asserts only aria-expanded, Escape closing, overlay clicks, and translate classes. The inert marking depends on two hardcoded getElementById ids owned by other files ('site-header' in nav.tsx, 'lesson-content' in the lesson page); getElementById returning null is silently swallowed by optional chaining, so a rename during the next restyle (the surface was already rewritten by PR #169 and the AuthWall commit touches layout.tsx) would void the aria-modal claim with every test still green — an undetected regression of the closed finding. _Fix:_ Extend sidebar.test.tsx: render stub landmarks (`<header id="site-header">`, `<div id="lesson-content">`) alongside &lt;Sidebar /&gt;, open the drawer, and assert both stubs gain the inert attribute and document.body.style.overflow becomes 'hidden'; close via Escape and assert inert removed, overflow restored, and focus returned to the toggle button; drive a Tab keydown at the last focusable and assert wrap to the first. This pins both the modal behaviors and the getElementById id contract so a rename fails a test instead of silently shipping. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:89-92`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:94-95,110-116,120-125`, `web/__tests__/components/sidebar.test.tsx:83-146`, `web/src/components/nav.tsx:16`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** PrevNext duplicates its entire card markup for the prev and next branches — The previous-link and next-link JSX blocks are near-identical 15-line twins: same border/hover/focus-ring classes, same label/title typography, same chevron SVG shell. They differ only in direction (icon side, justify-end/text-right, chevron path, 'Previous'/'Next' label). The PR #169 restyle had to touch both copies in lockstep, and any future hover/token change must again be made twice, with silent drift if one side is missed. _Fix:_ Factor a small internal component (e.g. PrevNextCard({ section, direction })) inside the same file - the pattern the feature already uses for SidebarItem/CheckBadge in sidebar.tsx - so the shared class string, label row, and chevron shell exist once and direction picks the ordering and path. Behavior and rendered output unchanged; existing prev-next.test.tsx passes as-is. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/prev-next.tsx:16-31`, `web/src/components/prev-next.tsx:32-47`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Consistency]** Sidebar test's 'no overlay initially' guard is vacuous - it still queries the pre-#169 bg-black/50 class — sidebar.test.tsx asserts the overlay is absent before opening by querying '.bg-black\/50', but the component's overlay has been 'bg-black/60' (PR #169 glass restyle). The selector can never match anything, so the not-in-document assertion passes vacuously - if a regression rendered the overlay permanently, this guard would still be green. The very next assertions in the same file already use the robust "[class*='bg-black']" idiom, so the file is internally inconsistent. _Fix:_ Use the same "[class*='bg-black']" selector (or better, a stable hook such as a data-testid/aria attribute on the overlay) for the initial-absence assertion so the guard actually fails when the overlay renders while closed. One-line test fix; also worth a quick sweep of the suite for other pre-#169 class-string selectors. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/__tests__/components/sidebar.test.tsx:119`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:147`, `web/__tests__/components/sidebar.test.tsx:126`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** TransitionLink advertises LinkProps['href'] (string | UrlObject) but navigates via String(href) — The component's prop type accepts Next's full Url type, yet the click handler calls router.push(String(href)). Passing a UrlObject - which the declared type invites - would stringify to '[object Object]' and navigate to garbage, but only on primary clicks with the View Transitions path active (modified clicks and reduced-motion fall through to &lt;Link&gt;, which handles objects fine), making it an inconsistent, hard-to-diagnose latent bug. Every current call site (sidebar, prev-next, section-card, section-gate-modal, glossary components) passes a template string, so the wide type buys nothing. _Fix:_ Make the contract honest: narrow the prop to href: string (Omit 'href' from LinkProps and redeclare), so a future UrlObject caller gets a compile error instead of a broken navigation, and the String() coercion becomes a plain pass-through. No behavior change for any existing call site. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/transition-link.tsx:7-10`, `web/src/components/transition-link.tsx:43`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:37`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Consistency]** TableOfContents styles its rail and inactive links with raw gray classes instead of the post-#169 glass tokens its sibling chrome uses — Within the same navigation feature, the same visual roles are expressed two ways: the sidebar and PrevNext draw hairline borders with the border-(--bd) token and muted text with text-(--mut)/text-caption, while the TOC's rail uses border-gray-200/70 dark:border-gray-800 and its inactive links use text-gray-500 dark:text-gray-400 with per-mode hover grays. A future retune of the shared --bd/--mut tokens (the point of the PR #169 token system) would update the sidebar and prev/next cards but silently skip the TOC, letting the lesson chrome drift apart. The sidebar itself still carries a few raw-gray leftovers for the same roles. _Fix:_ Swap the TOC rail and inactive-link colors (and the three sidebar leftovers) onto the existing tokens: border-(--bd) for hairlines and text-(--mut)/text-caption for muted text, matching what sidebar.tsx and prev-next.tsx already do. Visual output stays effectively identical today; the win is that the feature then retunes as one unit through the token layer. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/table-of-contents.tsx:64`, `web/src/components/table-of-contents.tsx:77`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:160`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:43`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** PrevNext with an unrecognized slug renders a misleading 'Next' link to the first section, and the test enshrines it — When currentSlug matches no section, findIndex returns -1, so prev = sections[-2] = undefined but next = sections[0] — the component renders a 'Next: Prerequisites' card that is navigationally wrong (the reader is not before section 00). Today's only caller validates the slug via notFound() first, but the unit test explicitly locks in the accidental behavior instead of guarding an intended one, so any future caller (review dashboard, playground handoff) passing a stale slug inherits a confidently wrong nav card that a green suite endorses. _Fix:_ Add an early guard in PrevNext — `if (currentIndex === -1) return null;` — and flip the prev-next.test.tsx case to assert that an unrecognized slug renders no links. Pure defensive clamp on the existing component; no behavior change for any valid slug. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/prev-next.tsx:10-12`, `web/__tests__/components/prev-next.test.tsx:71-78`, `web/src/app/learn/[section]/page.tsx:47,106`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[UX]** Cmd/ctrl-click on a drawer link (open lesson in new tab) still slides the drawer shut with no focus return — TransitionLink invokes the consumer's onClick before its own modified-click guard, and SidebarItem's onNavigate is an unconditional `setOpen(false)` - not `close()`. So a cmd/ctrl/shift-click that opens the lesson in a background tab (a flow TransitionLink's guard explicitly preserves) also closes the drawer in the current tab even though no navigation happened there, and because `close()` is bypassed, focus is never returned to the toggle - it stays stranded on the link that just translated off-screen. The auditors reviewed TransitionLink's guard and the drawer's trap separately; the seam between them is where this lives. _Fix:_ Have the drawer ignore modified/aux clicks when deciding to close: pass the event through (`onNavigate={(e) => { if (e.defaultPrevented || e.metaKey || e.ctrlKey || e.shiftKey || e.altKey || e.button !== 0) return; setOpen(false); }}`). Export that predicate (e.g. `isModifiedClick(e)`) from transition-link.tsx and reuse it in both files so the two guards cannot drift - which also feeds the auditors' extract-shared-logic theme without adding any capability. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/transition-link.tsx:26-37`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:203`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:75-78`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** Vestigial next/link mocks in sidebar and prev-next test suites never engage - dead scaffolding from before TransitionLink — Both suites mock next/link AND @/components/transition-link, but Sidebar and PrevNext render exclusively through TransitionLink (which the second mock replaces with a plain anchor), so the next/link mock is unreachable dead code left behind when these components migrated to TransitionLink. It misleads the next maintainer into believing the components still consume next/link directly, and it quietly masks any future regression where someone reintroduces a raw Link (the test would keep passing identically instead of surfacing the inconsistency). _Fix:_ Delete the `jest.mock("next/link", ...)` blocks from sidebar.test.tsx and prev-next.test.tsx, keeping only the TransitionLink mock each suite actually exercises. Pure removal - if either suite then fails, that failure is itself the signal that a raw next/link crept back into these components. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/__tests__/components/sidebar.test.tsx:8-15`, `web/__tests__/components/prev-next.test.tsx:8-15`, `web/__tests__/components/transition-link.test.tsx:13-28`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)

### 4. Dark / light theme
- [x] **[Accessibility]** Theme toggle never announces state and keys off `theme` not `resolvedTheme` — `aria-label` is a static "Toggle theme" (icon is `aria-hidden`), and with `enableSystem` set a `"system"` value would mismatch the shown icon and first-click target. Derive icon + dynamic label ("Switch to light/dark theme") from `resolvedTheme`. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/theme-toggle.tsx:9,16,18-28`, `web/src/app/layout.tsx:28`) — added 2026-06-17


> **2026-07-19 re-audit** — 18 verified findings on the fresh #169 Instrument token system. Cross-ref: the FogField
> per-frame render loop is recorded under ### 1.

- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** All WCAG claims of the fresh #169 token system are comment-only; both contrast tests are class-string greps blind to token values - and one claim is already false — PR #169 moved contrast-critical colors into runtime oklch custom properties (--mut, --btn-fill/--btn-ink, --accent per theme, chip-selected fill), but the two guard suites only grep Tailwind class strings: contrast-guard checks for the `bg-accent`+`text-white` pairing and caption-contrast checks only that `.text-caption` contains `var(--mut)`. Nothing computes contrast from the actual token values, so retuning any oklch value in globals.css can silently break AA in one theme. This is not hypothetical: the light-theme `--accent` is commented "legible on light bg" but computes to 2.79:1 against --surface-base (fails 4.5:1 AA and even 3:1 large-text), and it is used as resting text via `text-accent` on eyebrow micro-labels; meanwhile the --mut comment claims ~4.9:1 on light where the real value is 6.54:1 - documented numbers drift with nothing failing. _Fix:_ Add one unit suite (e.g. web/__tests__/token-contrast.test.ts) with ~30 lines of dependency-free oklch-&gt;linear-sRGB-&gt;WCAG-luminance math that parses the light and dark token blocks out of globals.css and asserts minimum ratios for each pairing the comments promise: --ink and --mut on --surface-base/-1/-2, --btn-ink on --btn-fill, gray-950 on --accent (.chip-selected), and --accent/--accent-dark wherever used as resting text - in BOTH themes. This pins every future token retune to a computed number instead of a comment, and immediately surfaces the existing light-theme text-accent failure for a deliberate decision (e.g. mapping light-theme micro-label text to --accent-dark, which computes 4.93:1). Impact: High. Effort: Medium. (`web/__tests__/contrast-guard.test.ts:20-21`, `web/__tests__/caption-contrast.test.ts:72`, `web/src/app/globals.css:156`, `web/src/app/page.tsx:240`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** Focus indicator fails 3:1 non-text contrast in the light theme (1.60:1) — The site-wide .focus-ring utility draws a 2px ring in accent at 50% alpha. The light theme's --accent is oklch(0.66 0.1 118); composited at 50% over the light --surface-base it renders #c1c8a1, which measures 1.60:1 against the surface — far below the 3:1 WCAG 1.4.11 requirement for focus indicators (even at full alpha the light accent only reaches 2.79:1). The dark theme passes (3.81:1 blended). Keyboard users in light mode get a barely-visible focus ring on every interactive element: nav links, the theme toggle, the skip link, all CTAs. _Fix:_ Make the ring color a per-theme token like every other Instrument color: add e.g. --focus: var(--accent-dark) (oklch(0.52 0.1 118), ~5:1 solid on the light surface) under :root and --focus: var(--accent) under .dark in the existing @layer base blocks, then change .focus-ring to `focus-visible:ring-(--focus)` and drop the /50 alpha (keep it only on dark if the softer look is wanted there, where it still clears 3:1). One utility edit fixes focus visibility everywhere at once. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/globals.css:454-456`, `web/src/app/globals.css:156`, `web/src/components/theme-toggle.tsx:21`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Architecture]** bg-atmosphere/-light and bg-grid-dots/-light are the only theme-paired class duos in a var-driven token system — Every other theme-dependent value in the Instrument system flips through one runtime var set under :root/.dark (--glass, --bd, --ink, --bar-fill, --shadow-*, --accent...). The atmosphere and grid-dot backgrounds instead exist as light/dark class pairs, forcing every consumer to write the double-class incantation 'bg-atmosphere-light dark:bg-atmosphere' - currently repeated at 8+ page shells - and making a silent wrong-theme background possible if a callsite drops the dark: prefix or pairs them backwards. The reduced-motion drift gate must also enumerate both classes (:552-557) instead of one. _Fix:_ Hoist the two radial-gradient stacks into an --atmosphere var (and the dot color into --grid-dot) set in the existing :root and .dark blocks, make .bg-atmosphere and .bg-grid-dots read the vars, delete the -light variants, and collapse each callsite to the single class. One utility per surface, theme resolution in the same place as every other token, no possible mispairing, and the motion gate names one class. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/app/globals.css:538`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/app/page.tsx:222`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/app/globals.css:552`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Consistency]** Two parallel ink systems ship: 23 headings still wear text-gray-900 dark:text-white beside 112 text-(--ink) — cool pure-white vs the warm Instrument ink — The #169 token system routes body/heading ink through the warm runtime vars --ink and --mut, but a fifth of the app's heading tier and 33 muted-text sites still use the legacy cool-gray Tailwind pairs. These are not the same color: gray-900 is Tailwind's cool blue-gray and dark:text-white is pure #fff, versus the warm hue-82 --ink values — so identical heading tiers visibly shift in temperature between pages (e.g. /pricing warm vs /glossary and /learn cool), and some single elements mix both systems. The auditors flagged only the theme toggle's hover fill; the same drift runs through the text-color tier app-wide. _Fix:_ Mechanically converge the remaining legacy pairs onto the tokens they duplicate: text-gray-900 dark:text-white → text-(--ink); text-gray-600/700 dark pairs → text-(--mut) (or .text-caption where it is caption-tier); border-gray-200 dark:border-white/10 → border-(--bd). Roughly 56 call sites, all one-class swaps; afterwards the ink tier has a single source of truth and heading temperature stops shifting between routes. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/app/learn/[section]/page.tsx:82`, `web/src/components/pricing/cost-estimator.tsx:119`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** Dead pre-#169 decoration layer (OrbitalDecoration + 3 keyframes + 2 utilities + --cap token) still ships in every page's CSS — A whole slice of the pre-Instrument decoration system is orphaned but still compiled and shipped. OrbitalDecoration is imported by nothing except its own unit test (no page or component renders it). Its two keyframes (orbit-spin, dot-pulse) plus the never-referenced glow-pulse, and the handwritten .text-gradient and .card-glow utilities, therefore have zero consumers - and because they are handwritten in globals.css (not Tailwind-generated on demand) they ship in the compiled CSS on all 110+ static pages. Both dead utilities also hardcode stale palette literals: .text-gradient inlines oklch(0.78 0.12 75) (the exact value of --color-warm) and .card-glow inlines oklch(0.82 0.1 118 ...) accent values that bypass the runtime --accent var the #169 system was built around - so anyone reviving them would reintroduce non-theme-aware accent color. Separately, the --cap token is defined in both theme blocks and name-dropped in the .text-caption comment as 'reserved for large/decorative labels' but has zero consumers anywhere (no var(--cap), no text-(--cap)). And .focus-ring carries a literally redundant dark:focus-visible:ring-offset-(--surface-base) that duplicates the identical unprefixed declaration (the var is already theme-aware). _Fix:_ Delete orbital-decoration.tsx and its test, the orbit-spin/dot-pulse/glow-pulse keyframes, and the .text-gradient/.card-glow utilities; drop the redundant dark:focus-visible:ring-offset duplicate from .focus-ring. For --cap, either remove it or make the .text-caption comment honest ('currently unconsumed'). Also update the project CLAUDE.md line that still documents OrbitalDecoration as a live pattern. Pure removal - no behavior change (nothing renders any of it), slimmer shipped CSS, and the fresh token file stops carrying two utilities whose hardcoded accents contradict the runtime --accent architecture. _The Performance auditor filed the same dead layer (plus 114KB of unreferenced hero images - see also the F1 orphaned-assets item); merged here. PR #172 note: the image-deletion sub-clause was stale by implementation time - WS-A1 had already deleted the true orphans and the remaining welcome images are all referenced; everything else in this item shipped._ Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/orbital-decoration.tsx:12`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/app/globals.css:102`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/app/globals.css:107`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/app/globals.css:514`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[UX]** ::selection hard-codes the dark-theme olive - text selection is nearly invisible in light mode (1.11:1) — The global selection highlight uses the literal dark-theme accent oklch(0.85 0.11 118 / 0.25) in BOTH themes instead of the theme-aware var(--accent). Over the light theme's white surfaces that blends to #f1f5e0 - 1.11:1 against white, i.e. effectively no visible highlight. Learners constantly select formulas and code to copy; in light mode they get no feedback about what is selected. _Fix:_ Theme the selection like every other accent consumer: set the light value from the light accent at a higher alpha (e.g. `::selection { background: oklch(0.66 0.1 118 / 0.35); }`) and keep the current bright-olive value under a `.dark ::selection` override (or route both through a --sel var set in the existing @layer base theme blocks). No new tokens needed beyond one var. _The Consistency auditor filed the same root cause; merged here._ Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/globals.css:608-610`, `web/src/app/globals.css:156,196`, `web/src/app/globals.css:612-615`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[UI]** Theme toggle still wears pre-Instrument gray hover chrome inside the fully tokenized nav — The theme toggle's hover state is hover:bg-gray-200 dark:hover:bg-gray-800 with rounded-lg, while every sibling control in the same header uses the Instrument tokens (hover:bg-(--field), --bd/--glass, rounded-chip/rounded-control). Tailwind gray-800 (#1f2937) is a cool blue-gray; on the warm hue-80 smoke glass header it reads as an off-palette blue blob on every hover in dark mode. The adjacent account-menu items carry the same legacy grays (hover:bg-gray-100, text-gray-700). _Fix:_ Swap the toggle (and the account-menu items while in there) to the system tokens: `hover:bg-(--field)` and `rounded-control`, matching nav.tsx's pill links, so all header controls share one hover/geometry language and the cool blue-gray never appears on the warm smoke. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/theme-toggle.tsx:21`, `web/src/components/nav.tsx:17,49`, `web/src/components/auth/account-menu.tsx:151,166`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** Reduced-motion fallback clamps animation-duration but not iteration-count, so escaped infinite animations flicker for exactly the users who opted out of motion — The global reduce block sets `animation-duration: 0.01ms !important` but omits the canonical companion `animation-iteration-count: 1 !important`. Any infinite animation not individually opted out therefore keeps cycling: a 0.01ms infinite animation samples an essentially arbitrary progress on every frame, turning a spinner into frame-random rotation jitter and a skeleton pulse into opacity flicker, while keeping an active animation ticking style recalc forever. Two live consumers escape the per-utility opt-outs today: the AuthWall verifying spinner and the QPU panel skeleton both use Tailwind's infinite `animate-spin`/`animate-pulse` with no `motion-reduce:animate-none`. _Fix:_ Add `animation-iteration-count: 1 !important;` to the existing reduce block (one line), making the global fallback actually freeze any infinite animation that forgets the per-element `motion-reduce:animate-none`; also add the missing `motion-reduce:animate-none` to the two escapees to match the repo idiom used everywhere else. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/globals.css:674-677`, `web/src/app/globals.css:644-661`, `web/src/components/auth/auth-wall.tsx:27`, `web/src/components/quantum/qpu-submit-panel.tsx:182`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Architecture]** CLAUDE.md still documents the pre-#169 design system: Plus Jakarta Sans + Instrument Serif fonts and a live OrbitalDecoration pattern — The project's own instruction file — which every agent and contributor is required to follow exactly — describes the design system PR #169 replaced. It names the old font stack and presents the now-unmounted OrbitalDecoration as a key pattern, so any future UI work guided by it will reintroduce the pre-Instrument type system or wire the dead decoration back in. The auditors caught the dead code but not the doc that actively re-legitimizes it. _Fix:_ Update the Web App section of CLAUDE.md to match the shipped #169 system: fonts Sora (display) + Geist (body) + Geist Mono via next/font, and replace the OrbitalDecoration bullet with the FogField canvas + bg-atmosphere pattern (or delete it together with the dead component the auditors flagged). Two-line doc edit; prevents every future frontend task from being steered toward the retired design. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`CLAUDE.md:65`, `CLAUDE.md:74`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Consistency]** AccountMenu — the AuthWall's nav chrome — hand-rolls token values: dark:border-white/[0.08] literally re-implements --bd on a line that already uses --surface-1 — The unmerged AuthWall commit added AccountMenu into the tokenized #169 header, and it half-adopts the system: its dropdown panel consumes bg-(--surface-1) and shadow-(--shadow-resting) but hand-writes its border, ink, and hover fills in the legacy gray palette — including a dark border literal that is byte-for-byte the value of --bd. The auditors' toggle finding even asserts the toggle's 'nav siblings use the --field token', which is false for AccountMenu; fixing only the toggle leaves the control right next to it inconsistent, and a future retune of --bd/--field silently strands the menu. _Fix:_ Swap AccountMenu's hand-rolled values for the tokens its own panel already uses: border-(--bd) for the panel, text-(--mut)/text-(--ink) for item and trigger ink, hover:bg-(--field) for item hover — the exact idiom of its nav siblings at nav.tsx:49. Pure class substitution, no behavior change; do it together with the auditors' toggle fix so the whole header cluster reads from one palette. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/auth/account-menu.tsx:141`, `account-menu.tsx:151`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[UI]** No theme-color meta anywhere: mobile browser chrome stays default white around the near-black dark theme — The theme system styles every in-page surface but never declares theme-color, so on Android Chrome and iOS Safari the browser toolbar/status chrome keeps its default light color wrapped around the oklch(0.145) near-black smoke — the one surface of the dark theme left unthemed, and a visible clash on every mobile visit. This is completion of the existing theme, not new capability: Next.js's viewport export emits the meta statically, which fits the output:'export' build. _Fix:_ Add a viewport export in layout.tsx with a prefers-color-scheme themeColor pair mirroring --surface-base in each theme (light oklch(0.97 0.004 88) / dark oklch(0.145 0.004 80), expressed as hex). This tracks the enableSystem default statically; if the manual-override edge matters (user forces the non-OS theme), a two-line meta update alongside next-themes' class flip closes it — still within the existing stack. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/layout.tsx:47-60`, `globals.css:200`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** .glass opaque fallback is dead code - the documented --surface-1 fallback can never engage — The .glass recipe stacks two background-color declarations intending a fallback: 'Falls back to the opaque --surface-1 where backdrop-filter is unsupported' (comment at globals.css:300-303). But the double-declaration fallback trick only works when the second value fails to parse; here both values are always-valid var() references defined in :root/.dark, so the second (var(--glass), a 4.5%-alpha white in dark / 60%-alpha white in light) unconditionally wins in every browser. In a browser without backdrop-filter (the @supports block only adds the blur, it does not gate the translucent fill), .glass renders as a nearly transparent fill with no blur - not the documented opaque surface. The first declaration is dead in all browsers and the comment misdocuments the actual behavior on the flagship surface recipe of the fresh #169 system. _Fix:_ Move the background-color: var(--glass) declaration (and the glass-2 variant if desired) inside the existing @supports (backdrop-filter...) block, leaving background-color: var(--surface-1) as the base declaration. The documented fallback becomes real, the dead declaration disappears, and the comment becomes true - no visual change in any backdrop-filter-capable browser. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/app/globals.css:305`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/app/globals.css:310`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Consistency]** Theme toggle's hover fill uses the legacy gray palette instead of the Instrument --field token its nav siblings use — Inside the redesigned glass nav, the pill links hover with the theme-aware token pattern (hover:bg-(--field) hover:text-(--ink)), but the ThemeToggle button - a named surface of this feature - still hovers with the pre-#169 pair hover:bg-gray-200 dark:hover:bg-gray-800. gray-200/gray-800 are cool neutrals that sit off-palette against the warm hue-88 oklch surfaces of the daylight theme, and the two-class dark: dance is exactly what the runtime-var tokens were introduced to eliminate. The adjacent ReviewNavBadge and the nav pills already follow the token idiom, so the toggle is the odd one out in its own row. _Fix:_ Replace the pair with the single theme-aware hover:bg-(--field) (matching nav.tsx), dropping the dark: variant entirely. One class, on-palette in both themes, consistent with the header it lives in. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/theme-toggle.tsx:21`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/nav.tsx:49`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[UI]** Dead pre-#169 surface left inside the fresh Instrument token file — Six pieces of the old palette survive in globals.css/components with zero consumers, diluting a token system that is otherwise cleanly single-sourced: .text-gradient, .card-glow, the glow-pulse keyframe, the .glass-2 class, the --cap token (defined in both themes, consumed nowhere), and the never-mounted OrbitalDecoration component - the sole referent of the orbit-spin/dot-pulse keyframes via inline styles. .text-gradient also hard-codes the dark-era warm stop (oklch 0.78 0.12 75) that would fail light-theme text contrast if anyone resurrects it, and OrbitalDecoration's inline infinite animations are the only motion in the codebase relying solely on the 0.01ms reduced-motion blanket, which lacks animation-iteration-count:1 and would flicker if the component were ever mounted. _Fix:_ Delete the six dead pieces (or, for --cap, wire it to the large decorative labels its comment reserves it for). Removing OrbitalDecoration also removes the only animations that would flicker under reduced motion; if any future inline infinite animation is anticipated, add `animation-iteration-count: 1 !important` to the reduced-motion blanket as belt-and-suspenders. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/globals.css:514-517`, `web/src/app/globals.css:174,213`, `web/src/components/orbital-decoration.tsx:12-39`, `web/src/app/globals.css:674-677`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[UI]** contrast-guard.test.ts header misdocuments .surface-accent after the #169 rebuild — The guard test that enforces the design system's central contrast rule still describes .surface-accent as "(accent-dark base, ~5.1:1)" - the pre-#169 olive-filled recipe. PR #169 rebuilt it as the NEUTRAL --btn-fill/--btn-ink button (near-white on dark, near-black on light, explicitly never an olive fill). A maintainer reading the guard to understand the sanctioned CTA treatment gets the wrong token model. _Fix:_ Update the test's doc comment to describe the current neutral --btn-fill/--btn-ink recipe (and its AAA-on-own-fill rationale) so the enforcement test doubles as accurate design-system documentation. No assertion changes needed - the regex guard itself is still correct. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/__tests__/contrast-guard.test.ts:7-9`, `web/src/app/globals.css:320-336`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Performance]** body gradient uses background-attachment: fixed, which de-optimizes scrolling in engines that repaint fixed-attachment backgrounds — The base smoke gradient is attached to body with `background-attachment: fixed`. Desktop Safari and some Chromium paths cannot composite fixed-attachment backgrounds and fall back to repainting the background on scroll, and iOS Safari has historically mis-handled fixed attachment on body outright. On long lesson pages this adds scroll-time paint work underneath the already-costly .glass backdrop-filter surfaces - while the same viewport-pinned look is available for free from a composited fixed layer, which the design already uses for the fog canvas one z-level up. _Fix:_ Move the gradient off background-attachment: fixed onto a guaranteed-composited fixed layer: either a `body::before { position: fixed; inset: 0; z-index: -20; background: linear-gradient(...); }` or the existing FogField wrapper. Identical visual (gradient pinned to viewport), but scrolling never repaints it in any engine. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/globals.css:229-233`, `web/src/components/fog-field.tsx:152`, `web/src/app/globals.css:310-315`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** FogField samples prefers-reduced-motion once at mount and never listens for changes — the only animation in the system that ignores a live OS toggle — Every CSS animation in the design system responds instantly when the user flips the OS reduce-motion setting, because media queries re-evaluate live. FogField instead freezes the preference into a const at mount: a user who enables reduce-motion mid-session keeps the full-viewport infinite rAF loop running until a reload, and one who disables it is stuck with a frozen frame. This is orthogonal to the auditors' 60fps-cost finding — their pause-when-hidden fix would not make the field honor a live preference change. _Fix:_ Hold the MediaQueryList from the existing matchMedia call, subscribe to its 'change' event in the same effect, and on change update the flag, cancel or restart the rAF loop, and repaint one static frame — exactly mirroring the reseed path the component already runs for theme flips at lines 130-138. Cleanup alongside the existing listeners. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/fog-field.tsx:26`, `fog-field.tsx:119-138`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** focus-ring's dark: ring-offset variant re-applies the identical --surface-base var — a no-op duplicate in the app's most-used utility — The .focus-ring recipe sets the ring offset color to the runtime var --surface-base and then sets the exact same var again under the dark: variant. Because --surface-base already flips with the theme in @layer base, the dark: clause is dead weight that generates a redundant compiled rule and misleads readers into thinking the offset differs per theme — the same pre-runtime-var reflex the auditors caught elsewhere, surviving inside the token file itself. _Fix:_ Delete the 'dark:focus-visible:ring-offset-(--surface-base)' clause from .focus-ring. Identical rendered output in both themes, one fewer compiled dark-variant rule, and the utility reads as the runtime-var pattern it actually is. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/globals.css:455`, `globals.css:160`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)

### 5. Progress tracking
- [x] **[Performance]** Unscoped cross-tab `storage` listener recomputes on every unrelated localStorage write — `subscribe` passes the callback straight to `addEventListener("storage", ...)`, so theme/review/pyodide-cache writes in another tab all re-run `completedCount`/`isSectionComplete`. Filter on `e.key` (`null` or starts with `qc:`). Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/progress-store.ts:53-61`) — added 2026-06-17


> **2026-07-19 re-audit** — 11 verified findings. Cross-ref: the sidebar progress-bar teal glow is inside ### 3's
> token-migration entry.

- [x] **[Consistency]** The "qc-progress" event name is hardcoded at four dispatch/listen sites despite PROGRESS_EVENT_NAME existing to single-source it — The event channel IS the progress feature's cross-module contract (store writes, Rep widgets, sync debounce, and every useSyncExternalStore subscriber all meet on this one string), and progress-store.ts exports PROGRESS_EVENT_NAME precisely to single-source it. Four modules follow that pattern, but four other sites re-type the raw literal "qc-progress". A future typo at any raw-literal dispatch site fails silently: the write lands in localStorage but the sidebar/catalog never refresh and the sync debounce never arms — no error, just stale UI and unsynced progress. The literal inside progress-merge.ts also reveals a boundary wrinkle: it cannot import the constant because progress-store.ts:10 already imports registerLocalDeletion/clearLocalDeletion FROM progress-merge (a cycle), so the contract's name currently cannot live where its writers need it. _Fix:_ Hoist the constant to a leaf so every writer and listener can import it: declare `export const PROGRESS_EVENT_NAME = "qc-progress"` in a dependency-free spot (a two-line progress-event.ts, or the top of progress-merge.ts which imports only review-schedule), re-export it from progress-store.ts so all eight existing importers keep their import path unchanged, and replace the raw literals at progress-merge.ts:256, sync-client.ts:231, delete-account.tsx:58, and progress-sync.tsx:99/105 with the constant. This also collapses the PROGRESS_EVENT / PROGRESS_EVENT_NAME dual naming inside progress-store.ts. No behavior change; the existing dispatch-count tests (circuit-store.test.ts, progress-merge.test.ts) keep guarding the contract. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/progress-store.ts:13`, `web/src/lib/progress-store.ts:10`, `web/src/lib/progress-merge.ts:256`, `web/src/lib/sync-client.ts:231`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** Second same-device tab defeats the un-complete tombstone: sync re-adopts the server copy under the learner's click — The documented invariant that un-completing a section 'does not silently re-adopt the server's copy under the learner's click' only holds in the tab where the click happened. The tombstone registry is per-tab module state, but localStorage is shared across tabs. Un-complete in tab A removes the shared qc:section key and tombstones it only in tab A's Set; tab B (signed-in, sync configured) runs its own arrival/debounced sync, exports a snapshot without the key, merges the server's '1' back in one-sided, and applySnapshot in tab B - which has no tombstone - writes '1' straight back into the SHARED localStorage. Tab A's own storage listener then re-renders the section as Completed, silently reverting the learner's explicit undo. _Fix:_ Extend the existing session-tombstone semantics to all tabs of the device (still session-scoped, still never propagating deletion cross-device): install one 'storage' listener - e.g. where the sync driver mounts, or exported from progress-merge.ts - that on e.key?.startsWith('qc:') calls registerLocalDeletion(e.key) when e.newValue === null and clearLocalDeletion(e.key) when it is non-null. This mirrors the exact removeItem/setItem calls writeFlag already makes, so tab B refuses to re-adopt precisely the keys tab A deleted, and re-completing anywhere clears the tombstone everywhere. Add a jsdom test that dispatches StorageEvent({key:'qc:section:x', newValue:null}) and asserts applySnapshot({'qc:section:x':'1'}) leaves localStorage empty. No new capability - it closes the gap in an already-documented guarantee. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/progress-store.ts:35`, `web/src/lib/progress-merge.ts:55`, `web/src/lib/progress-merge.ts:246`, `web/src/components/progress-sync.tsx:98-99`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Consistency]** usePersistentSolved hand-rolls a private subscriber that drops the cross-tab storage leg progress-store.subscribe already provides — progress-store's header sells 'one channel' where a solved challenge and a completed section notify any subscriber through one listener, and its exported subscribe() implements that channel as TWO legs: the in-tab qc-progress event plus the qc:*-filtered cross-tab 'storage' event. But use-persistent-solved.ts — the shared solved-flag hook for all six Rep widgets — bypasses subscribe() and inlines its own subscriber with only the in-tab leg. Cross-tab, the channel silently forks: sidebar counts, runbook, and workspace bench (all on subscribe()) update when a Rep is solved in another tab, while the Rep widget itself does not. _Fix:_ Replace the inline addEventListener pair in usePersistentSolved with the already-exported subscribe() from progress-store (same module it already imports PROGRESS_EVENT_NAME from). Pure reuse: deletes the duplicated subscription logic and gains the qc:*-filtered cross-tab leg, so a Rep solved in one tab renders solved in a second open tab exactly like the sidebar it sits next to. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/use-persistent-solved.ts:38-41`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/lib/progress-store.ts:64-78`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:66`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** credentials-wall.tsx re-implements progress-store's completedCount inline — The snapshot fingerprint in credentials-wall.tsx hand-rolls the exact reduce that progress-store.ts already exports as completedCount, while its two sibling snapshot builders (use-workspace.ts and runbook-dashboard.tsx) call the shared helper for the identical computation. The duplicate forces a reader to verify by inspection that the wall counts sections the same way every other surface does, and any future change to how a completed section is read (e.g. a value other than "1") would have to find this stray copy. _Fix:_ Replace the inline reduce at credentials-wall.tsx:80 with `completedCount(getSections().map((s) => s.slug))`. The file keeps its isSectionComplete import for the per-section `done` flags built at line 104, so this only swaps the aggregate count onto the shared helper. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/credentials-wall.tsx:80`, `web/src/lib/progress-store.ts:55-57`, `web/src/hooks/use-workspace.ts:32`, `web/src/components/runbook-dashboard.tsx:90`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Architecture]** Two near-identical scan-and-wipe loops over the qc:* progress contract can drift independently — The "remove every qc:* key, then notify progress subscribers" dance is implemented twice: delete-account.tsx's clearLocal and sync-client.ts's resetLocalProgress. Both iterate localStorage by index, collect keys with the qc: prefix, remove them, and dispatch the progress event — but they already differ in two quiet ways (clearLocal additionally sweeps the qc-sync:meta key and dispatches unconditionally; resetLocalProgress guards the dispatch on keys.length &gt; 0). One of those differences is intentional (meta handling), the other is accidental drift. Because the wipe defines what "all progress" means to the sync contract, a future key-family addition or dispatch fix applied to one loop and not the other produces surfaces that disagree about what a wipe covers. _Fix:_ Extract one helper next to the contract's other storage-scan code (progress-merge.ts, or the same leaf module as the event constant from the other finding): e.g. `wipeLocalProgress(alsoKeys?: string[]): number` that scans, removes qc:* plus any explicitly passed keys, and fires the progress event once when anything was removed. resetLocalProgress becomes a one-line call; clearLocal calls it with ["qc-sync:meta"]. The intentional meta difference becomes an explicit argument instead of a divergence a reader must diff two files to discover, and the dispatch-guard inconsistency disappears. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/auth/delete-account.tsx:50-62`, `web/src/lib/sync-client.ts:223-233`, `web/src/lib/progress-merge.ts:71-84`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** SectionProgress toggle changes its accessible name and aria-pressed at once, against APG and the repo's own toggle idiom — The "Mark as complete" button encodes completion state in two channels simultaneously: aria-pressed flips true AND the accessible name flips to "Completed". The ARIA APG button pattern explicitly warns not to change a toggle's label while aria-pressed is used. In practice, on activation AT announces only the pressed-state change while the name silently flips, and on later encounters the button reads double-encoded ("Completed, pressed, toggle button"). The repo elsewhere follows the conforming pattern — code-block's wrap toggle keeps a constant aria-label with aria-pressed carrying state — so this button is also internally inconsistent with house idiom. _Fix:_ Pick one state channel. Since the visible label flip ("Completed") is a deliberate design and WCAG 2.5.3 Label-in-Name forbids masking it with a divergent constant aria-label, the cleanest fix is to drop aria-pressed and let the flipping name be the single source of state (re-announced on focus/browse), mirroring the sidebar's plain sr-only "completed" wording at sidebar.tsx:58. Alternatively keep aria-pressed and hold the name constant — but do not ship both changing together. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/section-progress.tsx:34`, `web/src/components/section-progress.tsx:50`, `web/src/components/code-block.tsx:64-66`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[UX]** With storage blocked, "Mark as complete" is a dead control with zero feedback — When localStorage access throws (Chrome/Safari with "block all cookies" / site data blocked), writeFlag's catch swallows the write AND the qc-progress dispatch, and readFlag returns false — so clicking "Mark as complete" changes nothing: no button flip, no sidebar checkmark, no error, and clicking again does the same. The module's own header promises graceful degradation ("progress just isn't remembered this session"), but the actual behavior is a fully inert control, which reads as the site being broken rather than storage being off. _Fix:_ Make the code match its documented degradation: add a module-level in-memory Map&lt;string, boolean&gt; fallback in progress-store — writeFlag records into it when setItem throws and still dispatches PROGRESS_EVENT; readFlag consults it when getItem throws. The toggle, sidebar checkmarks, and completion count then work for the session (merely not persisted), with no new UI and every consumer (isSectionComplete, completedCount, workspace.ts) picking it up through the existing read path. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/progress-store.ts:25-41`, `web/src/lib/progress-store.ts:17-23`, `web/src/components/section-progress.tsx:31-50`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** Delete-account's clearLocal leaves stale un-complete tombstones armed while also removing the account fence that would reset them — Session tombstones (registered when a learner un-completes a section) live in module state and are only ever reset inside runSync's boundSub-mismatch branch. DeleteAccount.clearLocal wipes every qc:* key AND qc-sync:meta but never calls resetLocalDeletions() — and wiping the meta erases boundSub, so the mismatch branch (the one place the reset lives) can no longer trigger. Un-complete section X, delete account A, then sign in as existing account B via email/password in the same tab (no reload, so module state survives): B's first sync adopts as a first-ever sync, and applySnapshot silently skips B's qc:section:X='1' under A's stale tombstone — B's completed section is missing on this device until a reload. _Fix:_ Call resetLocalDeletions() inside clearLocal (exported from progress-merge, already in the dependency graph): once this device's local copy is wiped, records of what this device deleted are wiped with it, so the next account's first sync applies its full server snapshot. One line plus a unit test in delete-account.test.tsx asserting a registered tombstone no longer suppresses a post-deletion applySnapshot. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/auth/delete-account.tsx:50-62`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/lib/sync-client.ts:276-283`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/lib/progress-merge.ts:55`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[UX]** The caption beside the completion toggle still commands 'Mark this lesson done' after the lesson is marked done — The lesson page renders a static server caption next to &lt;SectionProgress/&gt;: 'Mark this lesson done to track your progress through the path.' The button is a client island that flips to 'Completed', but the adjacent instruction never changes — a completed lesson shows a Completed button sitting beside copy telling the learner to mark it done, a small self-contradiction on the feature's only direct control surface. _Fix:_ Reword the caption to state-neutral copy that is true in both states (e.g. 'Completion is saved on this device and counts toward your path progress.'), keeping it server-rendered — a copy refinement, no structural change and no new client boundary. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/app/learn/[section]/page.tsx:98-103`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/section-progress.tsx:50`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[UI]** Completion medals are still enameled in hue 192 under a comment that falsely calls teal 'the platform accent' — credentials-wall.tsx pins the Completion medal group — the medal rendering of this feature's qc:section flags — to GROUP_HUE 192 with the justification comment 'teal — the platform accent'. Since the #169/#170 Instrument redesign the platform accent is olive (hue 118, both themes), so the one medal group whose color was chosen specifically to BE the platform accent no longer matches it, and the comment now documents a falsehood. Same design-sweep-miss class as the flagged sidebar glow, at a second site. _Fix:_ Either move the completion group's hue to the olive accent family (118) to restore the documented intent, or — if teal is now meant purely as a distinct medal enamel — rewrite the comment to say so. Either way the code stops asserting a retired design fact; verify the earned chip/seal still reads correctly in both themes. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/credentials-wall.tsx:54-55`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/app/globals.css:156`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/lib/sections.ts:29`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR #172)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** progress-store's header credits the qc-progress channel to the Challenge component, but ownership inverted when the solved-flag hook was unified — The store's opening comment says progress is 'broadcast over the same "qc-progress" event the Challenge component already dispatches' — pre-unification history. Today challenge.tsx contains no dispatch at all: it solves through usePersistentSolved, which imports PROGRESS_EVENT_NAME FROM progress-store. The comment points a maintainer hunting the channel's source of truth at the wrong file, and the unit test 'exposes the same event channel the Challenge component uses' repeats the inverted claim. _Fix:_ Rewrite the header to state present-tense ownership (this module defines the qc-progress channel; Challenge and the other Rep widgets consume it via PROGRESS_EVENT_NAME through usePersistentSolved) and rename the test accordingly.
