- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** The scrubber's play/pause/replay timer state machine has zero test coverage — WavefunctionScrubber's only timer-driven logic — the auto-advance effect (advance one step after STEP_MS while playing, stop scheduling at the last step), the togglePlay replay-from-end branch (reset to step 0 and resume), and the scrub-slider onChange that cancels play — is completely untested. The suite only asserts the Play button's presence when motion is allowed and absence under reduced motion; no quantum suite except vqe-explorer.test.tsx uses fake timers at all. A regression in the effect's dependency list or the safeStep clamp interplay (both subtle: the effect deliberately never setStates synchronously, and safeStep is a render-time clamp) would ship silently with a fully green suite. _Fix:_ Add a fake-timer block to wavefunction-scrubber.test.tsx mirroring vqe-explorer.test.tsx's existing pattern: click Play, advance STEP_MS, assert the step readout and aria-current chip moved; advance to the end and assert scheduling stops (button flips to Play); click Play again and assert the step-0 reset; move the scrub slider mid-play and assert play cancels. Pure test addition, no product code. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/wavefunction-scrubber.tsx:61-65`, `web/src/components/quantum/wavefunction-scrubber.tsx:68-75`, `web/src/components/quantum/wavefunction-scrubber.tsx:127-130`, `web/__tests__/components/quantum/wavefunction-scrubber.test.tsx:68-79`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Correctness]** ShotsSampler and CorrelationDemo silently pin a slider-bound theta to 0 while labeling results 'exact' and chips 'RY(θ)' — Both widgets evaluate their circuits with `opsFor(program, 0)` and never check `program.hasTheta`. The qsim DSL accepts `RY 0 theta` in any fence, so a qshots or qcorr block using the slider-bound token parses cleanly, renders a gate chip that reads 'RY(θ) q0' (advertising a θ control that doesn't exist in these widgets), and presents theta=0 probabilities under the explicit label 'Exact probability'. For `RY 0 theta` that is P(0)=100% — a plausible-looking, definitively-worded miseducation. No live GUIDE content currently uses theta in these fences (verified: 01-foundations/GUIDE.md's qshots block is `qubits 1 / H 0`, its qcorr circuits are H/CNOT only), so this is a latent authoring trap rather than a live defect — but the two sibling widgets (CircuitLab, WavefunctionScrubber) both handle hasTheta, making the gap easy for an author to fall into. _Fix:_ Reject slider-bound theta at parse time in these two widgets using the existing error idiom: in ShotsSampler, if `program.hasTheta` render the shared ErrorCard ('qshots does not support a slider-bound theta; use a literal angle'); in parseCorrelation, add `if (p.hasTheta) throw ...` beside the existing n!==2 check. Pure guard rail — turns a silent wrong-number state into the same fail-loud path malformed sources already take. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/shots-sampler.tsx:24`, `web/src/components/quantum/correlation-demo.tsx:90`, `web/src/components/quantum/qsim-dsl.ts:80-82`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:22-23`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Performance]** StateVector's per-frame lerp(0.18) makes the teaching sweep refresh-rate dependent, against the repo's delta-time idiom — The 3D state vector chases its target with a fixed per-frame factor: `current.lerp(target, 0.18)` inside useFrame. Convergence speed therefore scales with display refresh rate — on a 120/144Hz screen the sweep completes in roughly half the wall time of a 60Hz screen, and slower still on 30Hz low-power mode. The sweep IS the pedagogy here (the file's own header says 'scrubbing through a circuit shows the vector sweeping across the sphere'); on high-refresh devices it degrades toward an instant jump, quietly losing the gate-by-gate motion the scrubber and scrolly exist to show. PR #172 (WS-A1) already established delta-time as the repo idiom by converting FogField for exactly this reason. _Fix:_ Take the delta from useFrame's second argument and use an exponential-decay form: `const k = 1 - Math.exp(-delta / TAU)` (TAU ≈ 0.08s reproduces today's 60Hz feel), then `current.lerp(target, k)`. Same visual on 60Hz, identical sweep duration on every refresh rate; the existing convergence check and invalidate logic are untouched. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/bloch-sphere-3d.tsx:74-85`, `web/src/components/fog-field.tsx:19`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Consistency]** CorrelationDemo hand-rolls the LiveStatus recipe verbatim instead of the shared component it already imports beside — correlation-demo.tsx:140-144 renders a hand-rolled &lt;p className="sr-only" role="status" aria-live="polite"&gt; for the per-Measure announcement — byte-identical markup to the shared LiveStatus primitive (live-status.tsx:14-19), whose doc comment declares it the single source for exactly this pattern and which widget-ui.tsx:269 re-exports for the explorables. ShotsSampler in the same family already consumes it (shots-sampler.tsx:7,54). The clone predates nothing structural — CorrelationDemo already imports Bar/GateChips/WidgetCard from widget-ui on line 7 — so this is the one remaining live-region copy in F6, and the next tuning of LiveStatus (politeness, element, mounting rule) will silently miss it. _Fix:_ Add LiveStatus to CorrelationDemo's existing widget-ui import and replace the hand-rolled paragraph with &lt;LiveStatus&gt;{...same conditional string...}&lt;/LiveStatus&gt;. The existing test (correlation-demo.test.tsx:29-35 asserts getByRole("status")) passes unchanged. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/correlation-demo.tsx:140-144`, `web/src/components/live-status.tsx:14-19`, `web/src/components/quantum/shots-sampler.tsx:7,54`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:269`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** parseScrolly finiteness-checks theta but not phi, so a non-finite phi yields a silent NaN state on the sticky sphere — scrolly.ts:47-49 rejects a beat theta unless Number.isFinite(beat.theta), but the phi check at scrolly.ts:50-52 only tests typeof beat.phi === "number". JSON.parse maps an overflowing literal like "phi": 1e999 to Infinity, which passes the typeof check; interpolateState then lerps it (scrolly.ts:87) and stateForAngles feeds Math.cos/sin(Infinity) = NaN into the state (math.ts singleQubitState). Downstream nothing throws: BlochSphere3D lerps toward a NaN vector, the 2D StaticBeats dial computes NaN SVG coordinates (bloch-dial.tsx:61-62) so the state vector simply vanishes, and the sr-only readout announces "Bloch vector x NaN, y NaN, z NaN" (format.ts:22-23) — a broken teaching visual with no error card, in the exact widget whose parser otherwise fails loudly for every other malformed field. _Fix:_ Tighten the phi guard to mirror theta — if (beat.phi !== undefined && !Number.isFinite(beat.phi)) throw the beat-numbered error — and add the one missing parser test for a non-finite/invalid phi. Pure validation symmetry in the existing error path; no new behavior. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/scrolly.ts:47-49`, `web/src/components/quantum/scrolly.ts:86-88`, `web/src/components/quantum/bloch-dial.tsx:57-62`, `web/__tests__/components/quantum/scrolly.test.ts:29-32`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)

### 7. Hardware explorables

- [x] **[Correctness]** ✓verified — **Noise widget mislabels a classical distribution overlap as "fidelity"**: `fidelityDist` is `(Σ√(pᵢqᵢ))²` over diagonal populations only, so a pure-dephasing channel reports 100% "fidelity" while true state fidelity drops. Rename to "classical fidelity / agreement" or compute true `F(ρ,σ)` from the `rho` already built. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/noise.ts:121-128`, `noise-visualizer.tsx:78-95`) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Accessibility]** DONE — **Interactive readouts (SWAP count, fidelity %, total cost) now announced** via the cross-cutting live-region sweep. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`topology-explorer.tsx:333-341`, `noise-visualizer.tsx:94-96`, `cost-calculator.tsx:176-178`) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Correctness]** DONE (PR pending) — Topology explorer claimed "depth +N" for N routing SWAPs, understating true added depth (each SWAP = 3 CNOTs). FIXED: now reads "+3N two-qubit gates" (exactly defined, matches GUIDE prose; depth is layout-dependent so not claimed). Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`topology-explorer.tsx`, `topology.ts`) — added 2026-06-17

> **Clean verification pass — 2026-06-20.** The adversarial verify stage that was server-throttled on the 2026-06-17 run completed cleanly here (22 findings survived independent skeptic review). _Current state:_ The noise math (Kraus channels, density-matrix conjugation, fidelity) and topology/cost logic are correct — no functional bugs surfaced this pass — and the issues are labeling clarity, accessibility (contrast, focus, touch targets), and DRY against cost.ts/math.ts as single sources of truth. The most consequential refinements are a WCAG-AA contrast bump on informational text, the device-table sort-button focus indicator (the largest reuse win), and deriving the device cost column from cost.ts so rates stop being maintained twice.

- [x] **[Accessibility — WCAG-AA color contrast]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-3) — ✓verified — NEW — **Informational text uses text-gray-400 on white, below WCAG AA contrast**. Several pieces of genuine informational text render in text-gray-400 (#9ca3af) on the white card background, giving roughly 2.5:1 contrast against the 4.5:1 AA requirement for normal text — and these strings are not decorative. The noise widget's 'ideal'/'noisy' row labels are the only thing distinguishing the two probability bars in text, the topology 'path:' line conveys the routed qubit sequence, and the cost nudge carries the cost-safety guidance. _Fix:_ Bump these informational spans from text-gray-400/text-gray-500 to at least text-gray-500/text-gray-400 (light) and text-gray-400 (dark) — matching the readout text already used elsewhere (e.g. noise readout uses text-gray-500 dark:text-gray-400) — to clear 4.5:1. Touch only noise-visualizer.tsx:118,131, topology-explorer.tsx:343, cost-calculator.tsx:189. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (noise-visualizer.tsx:118 - &lt;span className="w-8 shrink-0 text-[10px] text-gray-400 dark:text-gray-500"&gt;ideal&lt;/span&gt; (10px label distinguishing the bars) · noise-visualizer.tsx:131 - &lt;span className="w-8 shrink-0 text-[10px] text-gray-400 dark:text-gray-500"&gt;noisy&lt;/span&gt; · topology-explorer.tsx:343 - &lt;p className="mt-0.5 text-xs text-gray-400 dark:text-gray-500 font-mono"&gt;path: ...&lt;/p&gt; · cost-calculator.tsx:189 - &lt;p className="text-xs text-gray-400 dark:text-gray-500 ..."&gt;Develop on LocalSimulator (free) first...&lt;/p&gt;) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Accessibility — focus indicators & keyboard reachability]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-3) — ✓verified — confirms prior — **Device-table sort buttons have no keyboard focus indicator and no AT sort affordance**. The three sortable column-header buttons in device-table.tsx carry only hover and color-transition classes; they have no focus or focus-visible ring, so a keyboard or switch user tabbing through the table sees no indication of which header is focused — inconsistent with every other interactive control in the feature, where the sibling technology &lt;select&gt; (line 49) and the project `.focus-ring` utility (globals.css:262, used by topology-explorer and noise-visualizer) establish the focus-visible pattern. Additionally the buttons' accessible name is just the bare column word ('Model'/'Technology'/'Qubits') with the sort glyph aria-hidden, so they do not announce that they are sort controls. (The parent &lt;th&gt; aria-sort DOES convey current sort state to AT, refining the prior reviewer's 'no affordance to AT' claim to focus + control-role only.) _Fix:_ In device-table.tsx add the shared `.focus-ring` utility to each of the three sort &lt;button&gt; elements so focused sort controls show the same accent ring already used by the selects, and give each button an aria-label like `Sort by ${column}` so AT names the control as a sort action while aria-sort continues to report state. Refine only — reuse the existing utility, leave aria-sort and the aria-hidden glyphs as-is. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (device-table.tsx:69-72 - Model sort button className is only `flex items-center gap-1 hover:text-gray-900 dark:hover:text-gray-100 transition-colors` (no focus-ring / focus-visible / outline) · device-table.tsx:81-83 - Technology sort button: same class set, no focus style · device-table.tsx:96-98 - Qubits sort button: same class set, no focus style · device-table.tsx:49 - the sibling technology &lt;select&gt; DOES have `focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-accent/50`, showing the buttons are the inconsistent case · device-table.tsx:162-170 - inactive/active SortIndicator glyphs (↕ ↑ ↓) are wrapped in aria-hidden="true" spans, so the button name is the bare column label · device-table.tsx:66/78/93 - each sortable &lt;th&gt; sets aria-sort, so sort STATE is conveyed to AT (resolves to ascending/descending/none via device-table.tsx:25-28) · globals.css:262-264 - `.focus-ring` = `outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent/50 focus-visible:ring-offset-2 ...` is the canonical focus token used by topology/noise controls) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Consistency & reuse]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-2 tail) — ✓verified — confirms prior — **Device cost strings in devices.ts are a hand-maintained second copy of cost.ts rates**. cost.ts is declared 'single source of truth for rates' and holds the per-task/per-shot/per-minute numbers, yet every Cost cell in the device table comes from a hand-typed string literal in devices.ts (e.g. '$0.30/task + $0.01/shot', '$0.075/min'). The numbers currently agree only by manual coordination; the device table neither imports nor derives from cost.ts, so a rate edit in cost.ts (and its lib/utils/cost.py mirror) leaves the table stale and silently divergent. The vendor labels in DEVICES ('IonQ','IQM','QuEra') already line up with the cost.ts Provider keys, so the duplication is avoidable. _Fix:_ Add a small formatter to cost.ts, e.g. `costLabel(provider: Provider): string` (or `formatRate(provider)`) that renders the canonical PRICING entry, and have devices.ts / device-table.tsx compute each row's cost from a Provider key instead of a literal string. The DM1 '(noise)' and Local 'Free' cases stay as small per-row decorations on top of the shared label. This makes cost.ts the only place rates live for both the calculator and the table. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (devices.ts:12-19 - each Device.cost is a free-text literal, e.g. line 14 IQM Garnet 'cost: "$0.30/task + $0.00145/shot"' and line 18 TN1 'cost: "$0.275/min"', duplicating the cost.ts numbers · cost.ts:1-11 - PRICING object holds the same numbers (IonQ perTask 0.3/perShot 0.01, IQM 0.00145, SV1 0.075, TN1 0.275) and is labeled 'single source of truth for rates' · device-table.tsx:147 - renders {device.cost} verbatim, with no reference to cost.ts or estimateCost) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Correctness]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-5b) — ✓verified — NEW — **Qubit selector labels q1/q2 collide with 0-indexed qubit values shown beside them**. The two gate-qubit dropdowns are labeled 'q1' and 'q2', but their option values are the actual 0-indexed qubit numbers (0..n-1), and the readout and graph label qubits as q0, q1, q2... So a learner can see the literal pairing 'q1' (the label) next to a selected value of '0', and the readout simultaneously says 'q0 and q1'. The label means 'first/second selector' while 'q0/q1' mean qubit indices — the overloaded 'q1' is ambiguous about 0- vs 1-indexing of physical qubits. _Fix:_ In topology-explorer.tsx rename the two selector labels from 'q1'/'q2' to index-neutral wording such as 'Qubit A'/'Qubit B' (or 'first'/'second'), matching the qA/qB framing already used in code comments, so the labels no longer read as 1-indexed qubit names that clash with the 0-indexed values and the q0/q1 readout. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (topology-explorer.tsx:352 - label text is `q1` immediately before a &lt;select&gt; whose options are indices `0..n-1` · topology-explorer.tsx:368 - second label is `q2` with the same 0-indexed option set · topology-explorer.tsx:337 - readout uses the real indices: `to make q{safeA} and q{safeB} adjacent`, and graph nodes are labeled with index `i` (line 251)) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Correctness]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-5b) — ✓verified — NEW — **All three noise channels share the label 'Error rate' though the parameter means different physical quantities**. The slider is labeled 'Error rate' and its value is announced as a single percentage for every channel, but the parameter p is a different physical quantity per channel: depolarizing uses the Pauli-error probability (capped at 0.75 where the qubit becomes maximally mixed), amplitude-damping uses gamma (the relaxation/decay probability, max 1.0), and bit-flip uses the flip probability (max 1.0). A learner switching channels sees the same 'Error rate %' wording even though, e.g., depolarizing 'maxes out' at 75% while the others reach 100%, which can imply the depolarizing slider is broken or that 75% is a generic error ceiling. _Fix:_ In noise-visualizer.tsx make the slider label channel-aware (e.g. 'Depolarizing p', 'Damping γ', 'Flip probability') or add a one-line caption noting that depolarizing reaches the maximally-mixed state at p=0.75 while the others run to 1.0. No math change — noise.ts is correct; only the visible labeling needs to stop conflating the three quantities. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (noise-visualizer.tsx:178 - the label is the generic string `Error rate` for all channels · noise-visualizer.tsx:31 - pMax is 0.75 for depolarizing but 1 for the others, so the same 'Error rate' tops out at different values without explanation · noise.ts:79-90 - krausFor treats p as Pauli-error prob (depolarizing), gamma (amplitude-damping), and flip prob (bit-flip) — three distinct quantities under one UI label) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Performance / UX — busy feedback]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-5b) — ✓verified — confirms prior — **Noise slider re-runs the full Kraus density-matrix simulation synchronously on every drag tick with no busy feedback**. The error-rate slider's onChange calls setP directly on every input event, and pClamped is a dependency of the rho useMemo, which calls noisyRho() — a full density-matrix Kraus-channel simulation (two 2^n x 2^n matrix multiplies per gate plus one per Kraus operator per qubit). With step=0.01 and a max of 0.75-1.0, dragging the slider fires 75-100 discrete React state updates, each forcing a synchronous main-thread recompute and re-render before paint, with no debounce, useDeferredValue, startTransition, or rAF throttle anywhere in the file — and no pending/busy indication, so on a multi-gate circuit the bars and fidelity readout can lag the thumb. (Because n is hard-capped at 3, d≤8, so per-tick cost is small in absolute terms; user-visible lag is realistically Low except on unusually long circuits.) _Fix:_ In noise-visualizer.tsx, decouple the slider's visual value from the simulated value: keep `p` immediate for the thumb/label but feed a throttled/deferred value (useDeferredValue, a startTransition in onChange, or a requestAnimationFrame-coalesced value) into the rho/noisy/fidelity memos so at most one simulation runs per frame and the thumb stays responsive. Optionally add a transient busy affordance (lower bar opacity or aria-busy on the bars container) while a recompute is pending. Keep idealState/ideal memos as-is (they correctly exclude p). No scope change — same simulation, same math. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (noise-visualizer.tsx:187 - slider onChange does `setP(parseFloat(e.target.value))` with no deferral/debounce/rAF throttle · noise-visualizer.tsx:42-45 - `rho` useMemo lists `pClamped` in its deps, so it re-runs noisyRho on every p change, synchronously in render · noise-visualizer.tsx:185 - `step={0.01}` means a full-track drag emits ~75-100 onChange events · noise.ts:109-123 - noisyRho loops over ops doing conjugate(U, rho) (two matMul, each O(d^3)) plus applyChannel1 per qubit; grep confirms no requestAnimationFrame/useDeferredValue/startTransition/debounce in the component · noise.ts:30-40 - matMul is a triple-nested O(d^3) complex multiply with no memo cache across slider ticks) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Performance]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-4 perf) — ✓verified — NEW — **krausFor() recomputed once per gate inside noisyRho even though it is loop-invariant for a fixed (channel, p)**. Inside noisyRho the per-op loop recomputes `const kraus = krausFor(channel, p)` on every gate, but the Kraus operator set depends only on (channel, p) and is identical for every op in a single call. For the live 2-gate qnoise block this is 2x redundant; the redundancy scales with circuit length. Each krausFor call allocates fresh Gate2 arrays (scaleGate builds new nested arrays), so this is needless allocation churn on the same drag-tick hot path as the simulation above. _Fix:_ In noise.ts hoist `const kraus = p &gt; 0 ? krausFor(channel, p) : null;` to before the op loop in noisyRho and reuse it inside the loop. This is a pure loop-invariant code motion within the existing function — no behavior change. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (noise.ts:114-121 - the `for (const op of ops)` loop computes `const kraus = krausFor(channel, p)` at line 118 inside the loop body, only used when p &gt; 0 · noise.ts:79-91 - krausFor allocates new Gate2 arrays per call (scaleGate at line 72-77 builds fresh nested arrays each invocation)) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Performance]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-4 perf) — ✓verified — NEW — **expandSingle rebuilds identity(2) matrices for every Kraus operator on every gate during each noisy recompute**. applyChannel1 calls conjugate(expandSingle(k, qubit, n), rho) for each of up to 4 Kraus operators per affected qubit per gate. expandSingle (noise.ts:53-57) rebuilds the full 2^n x 2^n expansion from scratch via repeated kron, allocating a fresh identity(2) for each non-target slot every call. For depolarizing on a 2-qubit CNOT this is 2 qubits x 4 Kraus x n kron-builds-with-fresh-identities, all redone on every slider tick, and identity(2) is a constant that never changes. _Fix:_ In noise.ts hoist a module-level `const I2_MAT = identity(2)` and reference it in expandSingle's kron instead of calling identity(2) inline; this removes one allocation per non-target slot per Kraus operator per gate without changing results. (Optional further refinement: the per-qubit single-channel superoperator is constant for a fixed (channel, p, qubit, n) and could be cached, but the identity hoist is the low-risk, in-scope win.) Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (noise.ts:93-96 - applyChannel1 loops kraus calling expandSingle then conjugate (two matMul) for each operator · noise.ts:53-57 - expandSingle calls `kron(res, q === qubit ? gate2ToMatrix(g) : identity(2))` building a new identity(2) per slot, per call · noise.ts:15-19 - identity() allocates a fresh d x d array (here always identity(2)) on every invocation, with no memoization) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Performance]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-4 perf) — ✓verified — NEW — **TopologyGraph rebuilds the full edge list outside useMemo while adjacent derivations are memoized**. Inside TopologyGraph, positions, adjacency, and the path-edge set are each wrapped in useMemo, but the `edges` array — an O(E) reconstruction that for all-to-all with the 16-qubit max is 16*15/2 = 120 edge objects — is built as plain code in the render body on every render, then filtered twice (.filter !highlighted and .filter highlighted) to map. It only re-renders on discrete select changes so this is not a per-frame hot loop, but it is an unmemoized allocation inconsistent with the surrounding memoized values and trivially hoistable. _Fix:_ In topology-explorer.tsx wrap the edges construction in a useMemo keyed on [positions, adj, pathEdgeSet, n] so it only recomputes when its inputs change, matching the existing memoization pattern in the same component. Optionally partition into faintEdges/pathEdges once inside that memo to drop the two per-render .filter passes. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (topology-explorer.tsx:167-177 - `const edges: {...}[] = []` is built in the render body (nested loop over n and adj[u]), not in a useMemo, unlike positions (line 149), adj (line 154), and pathEdgeSet (line 157) · topology-explorer.tsx:192-221 - edges is then iterated twice via .filter().map() each render, allocating two intermediate arrays) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Accessibility — ARIA roles/labels]** CLEARED 2026-06-20 (prior claim refuted on verification) — **Prior claim that sort buttons expose 'no action affordance to AT' is partly inaccurate — sort state IS announced**. The prior finding stated the sort buttons 'expose no action affordance to assistive tech (Unicode arrow glyphs only).' In fact the arrow glyphs are aria-hidden and the button's accessible name is the visible column label ('Model'/'Technology'/'Qubits'), and the parent &lt;th&gt; carries aria-sort that announces current sort direction to screen readers. What is genuinely missing is that nothing tells AT the button is a sort toggle (no 'sort by' wording); the glyphs are correctly hidden, not the problem. (The 'sort by' aria-label fix is folded into the merged focus-indicator finding above.) (device-table.tsx:162-164 - inactive SortIndicator glyph is aria-hidden="true", so the arrow is not the accessible name · device-table.tsx:167-170 - active SortIndicator glyph is also aria-hidden="true" · device-table.tsx:66 / 78 / 93 - each sortable &lt;th&gt; sets aria-sort={ariaSort(key)} which resolves to 'ascending'/'descending'/'none' (device-table.tsx:25-28), so sort state IS conveyed to AT · device-table.tsx:73 - button accessible name is the text 'Model' (plus the hidden glyph), not 'arrow glyphs only')
- [x] **[Accessibility — live regions for dynamic numeric readouts]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-5b) — ✓verified — NEW — **Per-basis ideal/noisy probabilities are not in a live region — slider drag announces only aggregate fidelity**. In noise-visualizer.tsx the only element wrapped in role=status/aria-live is the single 'fidelity X%' span; the per-basis ideal and noisy probability percentages, which are the core of the visualization, update silently when the slider moves. A screen-reader user dragging 'Error rate' hears the fidelity figure change but never learns how individual basis-state probabilities shift, which is the point of the side-by-side bars. _Fix:_ Keep the fidelity readout as the primary announcement but give the probability-bars container (noise-visualizer.tsx:107) a debounced/summarizing aria-live (e.g. an off-screen role=status summarizing the largest delta) so SR users get the per-state change without 8 chatty announcements per drag tick. Scope stays within the existing noise widget. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (noise-visualizer.tsx:96-103 - only the 'fidelity {fidelityPct}%' span carries role="status" aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="true" · noise-visualizer.tsx:125-127 and 138-140 - the ideal and noisy percentage spans ((idealP*100).toFixed(1)%, (noisyP*100).toFixed(1)%) are plain spans with no live region, yet they recompute on every slider change (noise-visualizer.tsx:187 setP) · noise-visualizer.tsx:46 - noisy = rho.map(...) re-derives these numbers each render, so the on-screen values change without any announcement) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Accessibility — adequate touch targets]** DONE 2026-06-21 (already covered by WS-3 #41: sort buttons carry -mx-2 -my-1 px-2 py-1 + focus-ring) — ✓verified — NEW — **Sort-button click/touch target falls below the 24px minimum**. The device-table sort buttons have no padding of their own (className is only 'flex items-center gap-1 hover:... transition-colors'); the px-4 py-2 padding lives on the parent &lt;th&gt;, not the button. The actual interactive box is just the text-sm line plus the 10px glyph, well under the WCAG 2.5.8 (AA) 24x24 CSS-px minimum target size, making them hard to hit on touch and for users with motor impairments. _Fix:_ Add vertical/horizontal padding to the sort &lt;button&gt; elements (e.g. -mx-2 -my-1 px-2 py-1 so the hit area grows without disturbing the cell layout) in device-table.tsx so each sort control meets the 24px minimum. Refines the existing buttons only. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (device-table.tsx:71 - button className is "flex items-center gap-1 hover:text-gray-900 dark:hover:text-gray-100 transition-colors" with zero padding · device-table.tsx:67 - the px-4 py-2 padding is on the &lt;th&gt;, outside the &lt;button&gt;, so the click target is text-height only · device-table.tsx:159-171 - SortIndicator renders a text-[10px] glyph, so the button content is roughly a ~20px tall line) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[UX — busy feedback / perceived speed]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-5b) — ✓verified — NEW — **Sort buttons give no pressed/active feedback and the up/down glyph is the only state cue**. The device-table sort buttons toggle direction on repeat clicks (device-table.tsx:13-20) but the button itself has no active/pressed visual (no `active:` scale, no background change); the only feedback that a click registered is the tiny `text-[10px]` Unicode arrow swapping between the inactive column's faint up-down glyph and the active column's up/down arrow (device-table.tsx:159-172). On a fast click the column reorders but the control gives no tactile/pressed confirmation, and the 10px arrow is easy to miss, hurting perceived responsiveness of the sort action. _Fix:_ In device-table.tsx give the sort buttons a lightweight pressed cue reusing existing patterns (e.g. the `.interactive` utility's `active:scale-[0.97]` from globals.css:266, or a subtle `aria-pressed`-driven background), so clicks read as responsive. Keep scope to feedback styling on the existing buttons. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (device-table.tsx:69-75 - sort button has only hover color + a SortIndicator child, no `active:`/pressed state · device-table.tsx:159-172 - SortIndicator renders a 10px `↕` (inactive) or `↑`/`↓` (active) as the sole sort-state signal · device-table.tsx:13-20 - handleSort flips direction silently; nothing else in the row visually confirms the click landed) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[UX — animation timing]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-5b) — ✓verified — NEW — **Noisy probability bars and fidelity readout animate at different speeds, so the bars trail the live-region announcement during a drag**. The ideal/noisy probability bars use `transition-[width] duration-200` so they ease over 200ms, but the `fidelity NN%` readout in the header (role=status aria-live=polite) and the percent label update instantly. During a continuous slider drag the announced/visible fidelity value can be ahead of where the bars have animated to, producing a brief perceptual mismatch between the headline number and the bars it summarizes. _Fix:_ In noise-visualizer.tsx, either drop the `duration-200` width transition on the bars while actively dragging (so bars track the number 1:1) or accept a tiny matching delay; simplest is to remove the transition on the bars since the slider already provides continuous motion. Refine the existing animation only. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (noise-visualizer.tsx:121 - ideal bar: `bg-accent transition-[width] duration-200` · noise-visualizer.tsx:134 - noisy bar: `bg-amber-500 transition-[width] duration-200` · noise-visualizer.tsx:96-104 - fidelity readout updates with no transition, instantly reflecting the new pClamped · noise-visualizer.tsx:190-193 - slider value label `(pClamped*100).toFixed(0)%` also updates instantly) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[UX — input editing friction]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-5b) — ✓verified — NEW — **Cost-calculator number inputs coerce empty/partial entry to 1 on each keystroke, fighting the user mid-edit**. The Tasks, Shots, and Minutes inputs all run `Math.max(1, Math.round(Number(e.target.value)))` directly in onChange. Clearing the field to retype (empty string -&gt; Number('')===0 -&gt; max(1,0)===1) snaps the value back to 1 instantly, so a user cannot blank the field to type a new number and a backspace-to-edit flow is interrupted on every keystroke, which reads as the control fighting the user. _Fix:_ In cost-calculator.tsx, allow a transient empty/partial value while typing and clamp to the &gt;=1 floor on blur (or only clamp when the parsed value is a finite number), so editing the field is not interrupted. The clamp still guarantees a valid value for `estimateCost`. Refine the existing inputs only. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (cost-calculator.tsx:109 - Tasks: `onChange={(e) =&gt; setTasks(Math.max(1, Math.round(Number(e.target.value))))}` · cost-calculator.tsx:129 - Shots: `onChange={(e) =&gt; setShots(Math.max(1, Math.round(Number(e.target.value))))}` · cost-calculator.tsx:148 - Minutes: `onChange={(e) =&gt; setMinutes(Math.max(1, Math.round(Number(e.target.value))))}`) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[UI & cleaner code]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-5b) — ✓verified — NEW — **Focus-style inconsistency across the four hardware widgets**. The feature uses three different focus treatments for the same kind of control. topology-explorer.tsx and noise-visualizer.tsx use the shared `.focus-ring` utility (focus-visible-based with an offset ring), but cost-calculator.tsx hardcodes `focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-accent/40` (note /40, no focus-visible, no offset) on every input/select, and device-table.tsx hardcodes `focus:ring-accent/50` on its technology select. The ring opacity, the focus-vs-focus-visible behavior, and the offset all differ between widgets that sit on the same page. _Fix:_ Replace the inline focus classes in cost-calculator.tsx (4 controls) and device-table.tsx (1 select) with the shared `focus-ring` class so all four widgets share one focus-visible treatment (offset ring, /50, mouse-click does not trigger the ring). Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (globals.css:262-264 - `.focus-ring` = `outline-none focus-visible:ring-2 focus-visible:ring-accent/50 focus-visible:ring-offset-2 ...` (the canonical token) · cost-calculator.tsx:85,110,130,149 - four inputs/selects use `focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-accent/40` (focus not focus-visible, /40 opacity, no offset) · device-table.tsx:49 - technology select uses `focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-accent/50` (also not focus-visible, no offset) · topology-explorer.tsx:356,372 and noise-visualizer.tsx:162,188 - selects/slider use the `focus-ring` utility) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[UI & cleaner code]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-5b) — ✓verified — NEW — **Control border-radius drifts from the --radius-control token across widgets**. The design system defines --radius-control (0.75rem, exposed as `rounded-control`) and topology-explorer.tsx uses it on its qubit selects. But device-table.tsx uses bare `rounded` (0.25rem) on its technology select, cost-calculator.tsx uses `rounded-md` (0.375rem) on all four of its controls, and noise-visualizer.tsx uses bare `rounded` on its channel select. So four widgets in the same feature render selects/inputs with four-way-inconsistent corner radii rather than the single control token. _Fix:_ Standardize the form controls in device-table.tsx, noise-visualizer.tsx, and cost-calculator.tsx on `rounded-control` to match topology-explorer.tsx and the --radius-control token; keep `rounded-card` for the outer cards and `rounded-chip` for the badge chips, which are already consistent. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (globals.css:18 - `--radius-control: 0.75rem;` (the control radius token, surfaced as `rounded-control`) · topology-explorer.tsx:356,372 - selects use `rounded-control` (correct) · device-table.tsx:49 - technology select uses bare `rounded`; noise-visualizer.tsx:162 - channel select uses bare `rounded` · cost-calculator.tsx:85,110,130,149 - device/tasks/shots/minutes controls use `rounded-md`) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[UI & cleaner code]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-5b) — ✓verified — NEW — **Device-table amber 'analog' rows have no legend or accessible explanation**. Rows whose device has no gate model (QuEra Aquila) are tinted amber via `bg-amber-50/60 dark:bg-amber-900/10` to flag analog hardware, but nothing in the table tells the reader what the amber tint means. There is no caption, no legend chip, and the color is the sole signal — which is also a color-only cue with no text equivalent for color-blind or screen-reader users (the Gate-model column already shows 'No', but the row color is never explained). _Fix:_ In device-table.tsx add a short legend (e.g. an amber swatch + 'Analog (non-gate-model) device' note) in the header bar beside the Technology filter, and/or a visually-hidden `&lt;caption&gt;` describing the table and the amber convention, so the color is no longer the only carrier of meaning. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (device-table.tsx:117 - `const isAnalog = !device.gateModel;` · device-table.tsx:122-126 - analog rows get `bg-amber-50/60 dark:bg-amber-900/10` while gate-model rows get a hover bg; no legend element exists anywhere in the component · device-table.tsx:62 - `&lt;table&gt;` has no `&lt;caption&gt;` element) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[UI & cleaner code]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-5b) — ✓verified — NEW — **Topology SVG carries dead width/height attributes that the className overrides**. The TopologyGraph &lt;svg&gt; sets both explicit `width={SVG_W} height={SVG_H}` (300x200) and `className="w-full max-w-xs mx-auto block"`. Tailwind's `w-full` always wins over the width attribute, so the rendered width is governed by CSS and the explicit width/height attributes are inert — the viewBox alone preserves the aspect ratio. They read as load-bearing sizing but do nothing. _Fix:_ In topology-explorer.tsx drop the `width`/`height` attributes from the &lt;svg&gt; (keep them only in the viewBox string and geometry math) so the sizing is unambiguously CSS-driven; optionally keep `height` if a fixed pixel height is wanted, but not a width that w-full negates. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (topology-explorer.tsx:184-189 - `&lt;svg viewBox={...} width={SVG_W} height={SVG_H} ... className="w-full max-w-xs mx-auto block"&gt;` (w-full overrides the width attr) · topology-explorer.tsx:136-137 - SVG_W=300, SVG_H=200 are needed for viewBox and geometry but not as render dimensions) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Consistency & reuse]** DONE 2026-06-21 (already resolved in #19: widget uses stateFidelity + noisyRho; fidelityDist kept as a tested teaching utility) — ✓verified — NEW — **noise-visualizer.tsx re-derives noisy probabilities inline instead of the existing noisyProbs, and exports noisyProbs/fidelityDist are UI-dead**. noise.ts exports noisyProbs(ops,n,channel,p) — which returns exactly the density-matrix diagonal as a probability vector and is covered by noise.test.ts — but the widget ignores it: it calls noisyRho itself and then re-extracts the diagonal by hand with `rho.map((row, i) =&gt; row[i][0])` (the verbatim body of noisyProbs). It also exports fidelityDist (squared Bhattacharyya), but the UI uses true stateFidelity, not the classical overlap. So both exports are dead from the feature's UI perspective (referenced only by tests) and the widget duplicates noisyProbs' one-liner rather than reusing it. (Note: the widget legitimately still needs the full rho for stateFidelity, so the cleanest fix may be a shared diagOf(rho) helper rather than calling noisyProbs.) _Fix:_ Either route the widget through noisyProbs / a shared diagOf(rho) helper to remove the hand-rolled diagonal extraction at noise-visualizer.tsx:46, or keep these as deliberately-tested public utilities and add a one-line comment in noise.ts noting they are exercised by tests / external callers, not the widget; if fidelityDist is meant only for the widget, drop it (the UI uses true stateFidelity, not the classical overlap). Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (noise.ts:126-128 - `export function noisyProbs(...) { return noisyRho(...).map((row, i) =&gt; row[i][0]); }` · noise.ts:150-155 - `export function fidelityDist(...)` (squared Bhattacharyya), never imported by the widget · noise-visualizer.tsx:6 - imports only `noisyRho, stateFidelity`; noise-visualizer.tsx:46 - `const noisy = useMemo(() =&gt; rho.map((row, i) =&gt; row[i][0]), [rho])` inlines noisyProbs' logic · __tests__/components/quantum/noise.test.ts:13-37 - noisyProbs is exercised by tests but never imported by the production widget (no usages of noisyProbs in src/ outside noise.ts); noise-visualizer.tsx:80 uses stateFidelity rather than fidelityDist) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Consistency & reuse]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-2 tail) — ✓verified — confirms prior — **noise.ts opMatrix duplicates the gate-name→matrix dispatch already in math.ts (NAMED_GATES is private)**. noise.ts opMatrix hand-writes a string-to-Gate2 chain — `g === "RX" ? rx(...) : g === "RY" ? ry(...) : ... : g === "H" ? H : g === "S" ? S : g === "T" ? T : I2` — that resolves the same gate set math.ts applyOp resolves via its NAMED_GATES record plus the rx/ry/rz branch. The application targets differ (opMatrix builds a full operator for density-matrix conjugation; applyOp acts on a state vector), but the gate-lookup table itself is identical and maintained twice. math.ts keeps NAMED_GATES private, so noise.ts cannot reuse the canonical table even though it wants the exact same mapping. _Fix:_ Export from math.ts a single gate-resolution primitive — either NAMED_GATES, or a `gateMatrixFor(op: Op): Gate2` that handles named gates + rx/ry/rz (throwing for CNOT) — and have noise.ts opMatrix call it for the single-qubit case, keeping only its CNOT/expandCNOT logic local. One table, one place to add a future gate. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (noise.ts:99-106 - opMatrix does `g === "RX" ? rx(op.theta ?? 0) : ... : g === "H" ? H : g === "S" ? S : g === "T" ? T : I2` plus a CNOT branch · math.ts:60 - `const NAMED_GATES: Record&lt;string, Gate2&gt; = { I, X, Y, Z, H, S, T }` is module-private · math.ts:125-140 - applyOp resolves the same names via NAMED_GATES and the same rx/ry/rz dispatch) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Consistency & reuse]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-5b) — ✓verified — NEW — **SWAP-count formula is duplicated between swapCost and topology-explorer's graph aria-label**. topology.ts swapCost already computes `swaps: Math.max(0, path.length - 2)` and returns it, and the main component consumes that returned value. But TopologyGraph independently recomputes the identical expression `const swaps = Math.max(0, path.length - 2)` solely to build its aria-label, instead of being passed the swaps value the parent already has. The same magic formula now lives in two files; if the SWAP-cost definition ever changes (e.g. a different gate-decomposition assumption) the aria-label would silently disagree with the visible readout. _Fix:_ Pass the already-computed `swaps` into TopologyGraph as a prop (it already receives topo, n, gateA, gateB, path) and use it for the aria-label, deleting the recomputation at topology-explorer.tsx:179. The formula then lives only in swapCost. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (topology.ts:59 - swapCost returns `swaps: Math.max(0, path.length - 2)` · topology-explorer.tsx:294 - parent already destructures `const { path, swaps } = swapCost(...)` · topology-explorer.tsx:179 - TopologyGraph recomputes `const swaps = Math.max(0, path.length - 2)` for its aria-label) — added 2026-06-20
