> **2026-07-19 Category B re-audit** — 11 adversarially-verified findings (independent skeptic per claim, default-refute; correctness claims re-derived from first principles, one by porting the shipped code to a measurement harness).

- [x] **[Accessibility]** Device-table horizontal scroller is not a keyboard-reachable, labelled scroll region — the standard PR #172 just set for every other overflowing table — The qdevices table wraps 7 whitespace-nowrap columns in a bare `<div className="overflow-x-auto">`. At 375px (and most tablet widths) the table overflows, hiding the Connectivity/Gate-model/Cost columns off-screen. The wrapper has no tabIndex, role, label, or focus ring, so there is no discoverable keyboard stop to scroll them into view — and because the scroller CONTAINS focusable children (the three sort buttons), Chromium's implicit focusable-scroller heuristic is suppressed. This is now the only lesson-page table without the house pattern: PR #172 (merged today) gave prose tables the measure-then-expose scroll-region treatment in MarkdownTable, whose own comment cites WCAG 1.4.10, and CodeBlock has carried the same idiom since 2026-06-17. The F7 ledger's closed keyboard items covered the sort buttons only, never the scroll container. _Fix:_ Apply MarkdownTable's measure-then-expose idiom to device-table.tsx:71: track `scrollable` with the same ResizeObserver measure (scrollWidth &gt; clientWidth) and, when true, render the wrapper with tabIndex={0}, role="region", aria-label="Scrollable device table", and the shared focus-ring utility. Extracting the small measure hook from markdown-table.tsx for reuse keeps one source; no behavior change when the table fits. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/device-table.tsx:71`, `web/src/components/quantum/device-table.tsx:82-132`, `web/src/components/markdown-table.tsx:30-39`, `docs/feature-optimization.md:321`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Correctness]** Retired IonQ Aria still listed as a live Braket device, priced at Forte's rate it never had — The device table's data still carries an Aria row presented identically to live hardware, directly contradicting the lesson prose that mounts the widget: 02-hardware/GUIDE.md:133-134 teaches 'Forte (36 qubits) is on Braket — its predecessor Aria (25 qubits) is now retired', then at line 152-156 says 'The interactive table makes the trade-offs concrete' and renders the qdevices fence — where Aria appears as a plain row with no retired marker. Worse, the row's cost cell is derived via costLabel(device.provider) with provider 'IonQ', so Aria displays '$0.30/task + $0.08/shot' — the Forte rate; cost.ts's own header states Aria's actual historical rate was $0.01/shot ('its old $0.01 under-quoted ~8x'). The learner is told a device is retired, then shown it live, at a price it never charged. The staleness is also fossilized by the test suite, which pins Aria's presence rather than guarding against it. _Fix:_ Remove the Aria row from DEVICES (the prose already covers the retirement and Forte remains as the trapped-ion exemplar; the GUIDE's Aria architecture-paper link at line 412 stays valid as history), and update the two suites to assert on Forte instead of Aria (devices.test.ts:6; device-table.test.tsx:11,18). Alternative if the row is wanted as historical context: reuse the existing Device.note cost-cell decoration (devices.ts:11, the DM1 'noise' pattern) to render 'retired' and suppress the misleading Forte rate for that row — but deletion is the cleaner lockstep with cost.ts/cost.py, whose comments both already say the catalog truth. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/devices.ts:15`, `web/src/components/quantum/device-table.tsx:167`, `web/src/components/quantum/cost.ts:3-5`, `02-hardware/GUIDE.md:133-134`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Correctness]** The lesson tells readers to "Drag the endpoints" of the topology graph, but TopologyExplorer has no drag affordance at all — only two select dropdowns — `02-hardware/GUIDE.md:96` introduces the qtopo widget with "Drag the endpoints below on a 3×3 grid (IQM-style nearest-neighbor lattice) and watch the SWAP chain the router has to insert." The widget directly below (the ```qtopo fence at GUIDE.md:104-106) renders an SVG that is explicitly non-interactive — `topology-explorer.tsx:186-191` sets `role="img"` with an aria-label and there is not a single pointer/mouse/drag handler anywhere in the file — plus two `&lt;select&gt;` dropdowns labelled "Qubit A" / "Qubit B" (topology-explorer.tsx:341-374). A learner reads the instruction, tries to drag a node in the graph, nothing happens, and may conclude the widget is broken and skip the SWAP-tax lesson entirely. The prose is stale copy written against an earlier widget design; it survived both deep audits because every auditor read the component and the component's tests, never the markdown that mounts it. _Fix:_ Rewrite the sentence to name the controls the widget actually ships, e.g. "Pick the two endpoints below on a 3×3 grid (IQM-style nearest-neighbor lattice) and watch the SWAP chain the router has to insert." Copy-only change; do not add drag (that would be new functionality). Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`02-hardware/GUIDE.md:96`, `topology-explorer.tsx:186-191`, `topology-explorer.tsx:341-374`, `web/src/lib/content.ts:25`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Consistency]** topology-explorer is the only F7 widget still on raw gray palette classes instead of the --ink/--mut/text-caption tokens — The smoke-and-glass token system routes body ink through --ink and the muted caption tier through --mut/.text-caption (AA-verified by token-contrast.test.ts). device-table, noise-visualizer, and cost-calculator are fully on tokens (text-caption used 2/6/9 times respectively; text-(--mut)/text-(--ink) throughout), but topology-explorer has zero token usage — grep for --mut/--ink/text-caption in the file returns nothing — and instead hardcodes text-gray-800 dark:text-gray-200 and four copies of text-gray-500 dark:text-gray-400. Any future palette retune (like this week's PR #172 heading-ink sweep) will move its three siblings and leave this widget's readout and labels rendering off-token. _Fix:_ Swap line 324 to text-(--ink) (or the ink token idiom used by device-table's cells) and the four gray-500/gray-400 pairs to text-caption / text-(--mut), matching the other three hardware widgets. (Raw-gray residue also survives in qaoa/vqe/pes/param-compile/vqc/checkpoint outside F7 — flagged here only for scope-awareness; this finding covers the F7 file.) Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/topology-explorer.tsx:324`, `web/src/components/quantum/topology-explorer.tsx:329,334,342,358`, `web/src/app/globals.css:544-548`, `web/src/components/quantum/device-table.tsx:50`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** device-table triplicates the sortable-header block across three &lt;th&gt; cells — The Model, Technology, and Qubits headers are three near-identical ~14-line blocks: same th classes, same aria-sort wiring, same button class string ('flex items-center gap-1 -mx-2 -my-1 px-2 py-1 rounded hover:text-(--ink) transition-colors interactive focus-ring'), same SortIndicator invocation — differing only in the SortKey and visible label. The long focus/touch-target class recipe (itself the resolution of a closed WS-3 a11y finding) is maintained in three places; a tweak to it must be applied thrice or the headers drift. _Fix:_ Extract a local SortableTh({ key, label }) sub-component (alongside the existing SortIndicator at line 191) that closes over handleSort/ariaSort/sortKey/sortDir, collapsing ~45 duplicated lines to three one-line usages and single-sourcing the a11y-critical button class recipe. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/device-table.tsx:79-92`, `web/src/components/quantum/device-table.tsx:93-106`, `web/__tests__/components/quantum/device-table.test.tsx:9-27`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** noise-visualizer scatters per-channel metadata across three parallel channel-keyed mappings, with the slider-max ternary duplicated twice — Facts about a noise channel live in three places that must stay in sync by hand: the CHANNELS array (select label), the parameterLabel() function (slider label), and the inline `channel === "depolarizing" ? 0.75 : 1` max, which is written out twice — once for the render-time clamp and once inside handleChannelChange. Adding a channel (e.g. phase-flip) or changing depolarizing's max requires touching four spots; missing the second ternary would silently desync the clamp from the switch-time clamp. _Fix:_ Fold the metadata into one table — CHANNELS entries of {value, label, param, max} — and derive both the slider label and both clamps by lookup (e.g. a channelInfo(channel) helper), eliminating parameterLabel() and both ternaries. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/noise-visualizer.tsx:17-21`, `web/src/components/quantum/noise-visualizer.tsx:26-30`, `web/src/components/quantum/noise-visualizer.tsx:41`, `web/src/components/quantum/noise-visualizer.tsx:92`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Architecture]** device-table's technology filter options are a hardcoded list maintained parallel to the DEVICES catalog, whose allowed values live only in a comment — TECHNOLOGIES in device-table.tsx hardcodes ['All', 'Trapped ion', 'Superconducting', 'Neutral atom', 'Simulator'] while Device.technology is typed as bare `string` with the legal values documented only in a trailing comment in devices.ts. The catalog is expected to churn (cost.ts already notes Aria retired and Rigetti reference-only, so device rows will be edited): adding a device with a new technology silently never appears under the filter, and retiring the last device of a technology leaves a filter option that yields an empty table — neither is caught by types or tests. _Fix:_ Single-source the list: either derive the options from the data (`["All", ...new Set(DEVICES.map(d => d.technology))]` — insertion order keeps today's rendering) or promote the comment to a `Technology` union type used by both Device.technology and TECHNOLOGIES so a mismatch fails typecheck. No behavior change today; it makes the next catalog edit safe. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/device-table.tsx:8`, `web/src/components/quantum/devices.ts:4`, `web/src/components/quantum/cost.ts:1-5`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[UI]** TopologyExplorer is the only F7 widget still on raw neutral grays instead of the --ink/--mut warm ink tokens — The qtopo readout and control labels use raw Tailwind grays (text-gray-800/200, text-gray-500/400) while the three sibling hardware widgets on the same GUIDE page are fully tokenized (text-(--ink), text-(--mut), .text-caption). The tokens are warm-hued (oklch hue 82/88) and darker than gray-500 in light theme, so identical-role text — e.g. topology's 'Qubit A' label vs noise-visualizer's 'Channel' label, sitting one scroll apart on 02-hardware — renders in visibly different cooler/lighter grays. A grep confirms topology-explorer is the sole F7 file with these classes (6 occurrences); device-table, noise-visualizer, and cost-calculator have zero. _Fix:_ Swap the five text classes in topology-explorer.tsx to the tokens: line 324 to text-(--ink), lines 329/334/342/358 to text-caption (or text-(--mut)), matching noise-visualizer's identical controls. Leave the SVG stroke/fill grays (lines 204, 234-249) alone — those are drawing colors, not the text tier. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/topology-explorer.tsx:324`, `web/src/components/quantum/topology-explorer.tsx:329,334`, `web/src/components/quantum/topology-explorer.tsx:342,358`, `web/src/components/quantum/noise-visualizer.tsx:166-169`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[UI]** DeviceTable hand-rolls its card header, so the 'Devices' eyebrow renders heavier and brighter than its three WidgetCard siblings on the same page — device-table.tsx builds its own shell and eyebrow span instead of the shared WidgetCard/EyebrowLabel that qtopo, qnoise, and qcost use. The hand-rolled eyebrow is `font-semibold ... dark:text-accent-light` while EyebrowLabel is `font-medium ... dark:text-accent` — so on the 02-hardware GUIDE (which mounts all four widgets: qnoise line 59, qtopo 104, qdevices 155, qcost 229) the 'Devices' header is one weight bolder and one accent step brighter in dark mode than 'Connectivity', 'Noise', and 'Cost calculator'. WidgetCard's eyebrow+headerRight variant is an exact structural match for this header (eyebrow left, Technology select right; only gap-4 vs gap-2 differs), so the drift buys nothing. _Fix:_ Render DeviceTable through the shared shell: WidgetCard with eyebrow="Devices" and headerRight={the Technology label+select}, letting LiveStatus and the table body be children — or, minimally, replace the line-44 span with &lt;EyebrowLabel&gt;Devices&lt;/EyebrowLabel&gt;. Either way the eyebrow weight/color single-sources to widget-ui.tsx and the dark-mode mismatch on the hardware page disappears. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/device-table.tsx:44-46`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:189-195`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:228-232`, `web/src/components/quantum/device-table.tsx:35`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** Device table's sort toggle and aria-sort state machine have zero component-level coverage — The sort interaction is the device table's only stateful state machine besides the tech filter: handleSort (device-table.tsx:16-23) toggles asc/desc on a repeated click and resets to asc on a new column, and ariaSort (device-table.tsx:29-32) drives the th[aria-sort] attribute that screen readers rely on. The component suite covers only rendering, the technology filter, and the LiveStatus count announcement (device-table.test.tsx:8-27) — no test clicks a sort header. The pure sortDevices function is tested in both directions (devices.test.ts:11-22), but nothing exercises the click -&gt; state -&gt; re-sort -&gt; aria-sort path, so a regression in the toggle (e.g. failing to flip direction, or aria-sort stuck at 'none') would ship green. _Fix:_ Add one component test to device-table.test.tsx: click the 'Sort by qubits' button (getByRole('button', { name: /sort by qubits/i })), assert the first data row is the lowest-qubit device and its th has aria-sort='ascending'; click again and assert the order flips and aria-sort becomes 'descending'; optionally click 'Sort by model' and assert the qubits th returns to aria-sort='none'. Matches the existing fireEvent idiom already used for the filter test. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/device-table.tsx:16-23`, `web/src/components/quantum/device-table.tsx:29-32`, `web/__tests__/components/quantum/device-table.test.tsx:8-27`, `web/__tests__/components/quantum/devices.test.ts:11-22`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Correctness]** Line-topology nodes overlap at the widget's own advertised maximum qubit count — `linePositions` (topology-explorer.tsx:14-19) spaces n nodes evenly across the full SVG width as `w * (i + 1) / (n + 1)`, with `SVG_W = 300` and `NODE_R = 10` (topology-explorer.tsx:137-139). That gives a center-to-center spacing of 300/(n+1) against a node diameter of 20px, so neighbors collide once 300/(n+1) &lt; 20, i.e. n &gt;= 15. The widget's own parser advertises up to 16 (`MAX_QUBITS = 16` at :8, enforced at :97-102 with the message `"qubits" must be <= 16`, a bound that topology-explorer.test.tsx:45-48 pins as contract), so a lesson author writing `{"topology": "line", "qubits": 16, "gate": [0, 15]}` — fully valid per the widget's stated limit — gets a chain of overlapping circles where the highlighted SWAP path is unreadable. The ring/all-to-all circle layout (r = 0.38 * min(w,h) = 76, chord 29.7px at n=16) and the grid layout (colStep 76, rowStep 50.7 at n=16) both stay clear; only the line layout scales its spacing linearly with n while the radius stays fixed. No current lesson triggers it — 02-hardware/GUIDE.md:104-106 is the sole qtopo fence and uses grid/9 — so this is latent, not live. _Fix:_ Make the line layout's node radius responsive to its own spacing — derive the drawn radius as `Math.min(NODE_R, spacing * 0.45)` (or inset the line's horizontal padding and shrink the radius) so the documented 16-qubit ceiling renders legibly instead of as overlapping disks. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`topology-explorer.tsx:14-19`, `topology-explorer.tsx:137-139`, `topology-explorer.tsx:8`, `topology-explorer.tsx:21-29`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)

### 8. Algorithms explorables

- [x] **[Correctness]** ✓verified — **QFT visualizer asserts a false "spikes every N/r" claim for periods that don't divide N**: `spacing=N/value` is accepted without a divisibility check, so period 3 / N 8 prints "spikes every N/r = 2.6666666666666665" and `idx % spacing` (fractional) highlights only bin 0. Reject non-dividing periods or gate the note/highlight on integer `spacing` and format it. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/qft-visualizer.tsx:64-66,150-156`, `qft.ts:29-37`) — added 2026-06-17

> **Clean verification pass — 2026-06-20.** The adversarial verify stage that was server-throttled on the 2026-06-17 run completed cleanly here (4 findings survived independent skeptic review). _Current state:_ The QAOA and Deutsch-Jozsa simulations are numerically correct (the cut math is endian-agnostic and validated), so this feature has no functional bugs — the one correctness item is a pedagogical labeling mirror-image, not a math error. The headline opportunity is performance: QAOA simulates the full state vector twice per slider tick and recomputes graph-only quantities (maxCut, the cut-value table) far more than necessary, all of which are clean refinements of the existing kernel.

- [x] **[Performance]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-4) — ✓verified — confirms prior — **QAOA simulates the full state vector twice and recomputes the graph-only maxCut on every gamma/beta slider tick**. The `live` memo recomputes on every gamma/beta change and calls both `qaoaExpectedCut(n, edges, gamma, beta)` and `qaoaDistribution(n, edges, gamma, beta)`. `qaoaExpectedCut` internally calls `qaoaDistribution`, which calls `qaoaState` (allocates a 2^n complex array, computes the cost phase via cutValue over all 2^n states, then applies n single-qubit gates each re-cloning the state). So the entire state-vector simulation runs twice per tick to produce two values (expected cut and distribution) that come from one identical state. The same memo also recomputes `maxCut` by looping all 2^n basis states and calling cutValue on each, even though maxCut depends only on the graph and never changes when gamma/beta move. _Fix:_ In qaoa-explorer.tsx, simulate once per tick: compute `const dist = qaoaDistribution(n, edges, gamma, beta)` and derive `expected` from `dist` (sum dist[x]*cut[x]) instead of calling qaoaExpectedCut, which removes the second qaoaState run. Move the `maxCut` loop out of the gamma/beta `live` memo into its own memo keyed on `[parsed]` (it is graph-only, like `landscape`/`gridMax`). Optionally add a `qaoaExpectedFromDistribution(dist, cuts)` helper in qaoa.ts so expected and distribution share one state vector. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (qaoa-explorer.tsx:229 - `const expected = qaoaExpectedCut(n, edges, gamma, beta);` · qaoa-explorer.tsx:230 - `const distribution = qaoaDistribution(n, edges, gamma, beta);` (second independent full simulation of the same state) · qaoa.ts:35-39 - `qaoaExpectedCut` calls `qaoaDistribution(...)` then loops cutValue over all 2^n states; `qaoaDistribution` (qaoa.ts:31-33) calls `qaoaState`, so each call is a full sim · qaoa.ts:19-29 - `qaoaState` allocates `new Array(N)`, runs cutValue per basis state, then applyGate1 n times (each cloning the whole state vector) · qaoa-explorer.tsx:232 - `for (let x = 0; x &lt; 1 &lt;&lt; n; x++) maxCut = Math.max(maxCut, cutValue(x, edges));` runs inside the gamma/beta-dependent `live` memo despite being graph-only · qaoa-explorer.tsx:234 - `}, [parsed, gamma, beta]);` so all of the above re-runs every slider tick) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Correctness]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-4) — ✓verified — confirms prior — **QAOA distribution bitstrings are reversed relative to the graph's vertex numbering**. qaoa.ts maps graph vertex i to bit position i via (x&gt;&gt;i)&amp;1 (LSB-indexed), so vertex 0 is the least-significant bit of the basis index. The distribution bars and the SVG graph are both labeled, but basisLabel renders the index MSB-first (qubit 0 leftmost), so the leftmost character of a bar label is vertex n-1 and the rightmost is vertex 0. A learner reading a bar label like |0001&gt; left-to-right as vertices 0,1,2,3 concludes 'vertex 3 is in the cut set' when the math actually places vertex 0 there — the partition shown by the bars is mirror-imaged versus the numbered nodes in GraphSvg. _Fix:_ Make the bar labels read in vertex order so they match the numbered nodes in GraphSvg. In qaoa-explorer.tsx, render the distribution label by reversing the bit order relative to basisLabel (e.g. build the per-vertex string as bits v0 v1 ... v(n-1) from (idx&gt;&gt;v)&amp;1), and/or add a short caption stating the read direction (left = vertex 0). Alternatively, flip cutValue/the vertex-to-bit mapping in qaoa.ts to use bit (n-1-i) so vertex i lines up with basisLabel's i-th character — but the explorer-side fix is lower-risk since it leaves the validated cut math untouched. Keep GraphSvg vertex numbering as the single source of truth. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (qaoa.ts:7 - cutValue uses ((x &gt;&gt; i) &amp; 1) so vertex i is bit position i (LSB-indexed, vertex 0 = least-significant bit) · math.ts:191-193 - basisLabel(i,n) returns i.toString(2).padStart(n,'0'), i.e. MSB-first with the most-significant bit (qubit 0 per the file's stated big-endian convention) as the leftmost character · qaoa-explorer.tsx:371 - each distribution bar is labeled |{basisLabel(idx, n)}&gt; with no reversal of the per-vertex reading order · qaoa-explorer.tsx:165 - GraphSvg prints the literal vertex index {i} on each node, so the displayed graph numbers vertices 0..n-1 left/around while the bar string runs vertex (n-1)..0 · /tmp/qaoa_check.mjs run - star graph edges [[0,1],[0,2],[0,3]], the cut=3 optimum 'vertex0 isolated' is index 1 which basisLabel renders '0001' (vertex 0 is the rightmost char, not leftmost)) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Performance]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-4) — ✓verified — confirms prior — **QAOA landscape recomputes the immutable cut-value table for all 2^n states on every grid cell instead of once per graph**. `qaoaLandscape` builds a RES x RES = 576-cell grid, calling `qaoaExpectedCut` for each cell. Every one of those 576 calls runs `qaoaState` (which recomputes cutValue for all 2^n basis states to build the cost phase) and then loops cutValue again over all 2^n states for the expectation. The per-basis-state cut values depend only on the graph, not on gamma/beta, so the same cut[x] table is reconstructed from scratch ~1152 times for a single graph. The landscape memo only runs once per graph so this is not a per-tick cost, but it is O(cells * 2^n * edges) redundant work that dominates first paint / graph-change time. _Fix:_ In qaoa.ts, precompute `cuts[x] = cutValue(x, edges)` once for x in 0..2^n-1 and thread that array through `qaoaState`, `qaoaExpectedCut`, and `qaoaLandscape` (e.g. build `cuts` once at the top of `qaoaLandscape` and pass it down). This collapses ~1152 cutValue table builds into one per graph, with no behavioral change. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (qaoa.ts:43-55 - `qaoaLandscape` nests `res` x `res` loops, each iteration calling `qaoaExpectedCut(n, edges, gamma, beta)` · qaoa.ts:6-10 - `cutValue` loops over all edges for one basis state; it is called for every x in qaoaState (qaoa.ts:24) and again in qaoaExpectedCut (qaoa.ts:38) · qaoa-explorer.tsx:23 - `const RES = 24;` so the landscape is 24*24 = 576 cells, each triggering two full cutValue passes over 2^n states · qaoa-explorer.tsx:200-203 - landscape memo (`[parsed]`) builds the whole grid; the cut table is identical across all 576 cells but never cached) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Performance]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-4 perf) — ✓verified — NEW — **applyGate1 clones the entire state vector on every gate application in the QAOA/DJ kernels**. `applyGate1` starts by `state.map((c) =&gt; [...])` to allocate a fresh output array of N complex pairs, then overwrites every entry. QAOA's mixer applies it n times per state (qaoa.ts:27) and the DJ circuit applies it 2n times (deutsch-jozsa.ts:24,26), so each simulation allocates n (or 2n) throwaway 2^n-length arrays plus 2^n nested two-element arrays. For QAOA this is multiplied by the 576-cell landscape build and by the twice-per-tick live sim, turning gate application into the dominant allocation source for this feature. _Fix:_ In math.ts, give applyGate1 an in-place butterfly variant (or reuse a single scratch buffer) so each gate mutates the existing pair of indices rather than cloning the whole vector; only the (i, j) pair per stride is touched, so a full clone is unnecessary. This is shared infrastructure used by every algorithm widget here, so the win compounds across QAOA landscape, DJ, and the live sims. Keep the existing non-mutating signature for external callers if needed. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (math.ts:72-84 - `applyGate1` does `const out = state.map((c) =&gt; [c[0], c[1]] as Complex);` allocating a full N-entry array of new pairs before the butterfly loop overwrites them · qaoa.ts:27 - `for (let q = 0; q &lt; n; q++) state = applyGate1(state, rx(2 * beta), q, n);` allocates a new array per qubit · deutsch-jozsa.ts:24 - `for (let q = 0; q &lt; n; q++) state = applyGate1(state, H, q, n);` (and again at deutsch-jozsa.ts:26) doubles the allocations per DJ run) — added 2026-06-20

_New (2026-06-25 deep audit):_
- [x] **[Accessibility]** DONE 2026-06-25 (WS-6b, #62 merged) — **Slider/select-driven outcome readouts in dj-demo, grover, and qaoa are not announced** — operating the controls recomputes the core teaching outcome (DJ Constant/Balanced verdict, Grover success probability, QAOA expected cut) but each readout is a plain `<p>` with no `aria-live`, and the sliders' `aria-valuetext` voices only the raw angle/iteration, never the result. Wrap each outcome in the house `role="status" aria-live="polite"` pattern. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`dj-demo.tsx:77-85,111-128`, `grover-visualizer.tsx:137-142`, `qaoa-explorer.tsx:330-334`) — added 2026-06-25 _(see cross-cutting aria-live item)_
- [x] **[Performance]** DONE 2026-06-26 (WS-6g, #64 — heatmap rects + grid-max marker precomputed in a `useMemo` keyed [landscape, gridMax] (qaoa-explorer.tsx:232-246), `GraphSvg` memoized (:114); only the current-point circle updates per tick; stale checkbox caught 2026-07-03) — **QAOA heatmap (576 `<rect>`s) + the graph SVG are reconciled on every gamma/beta slider tick.** The cost-landscape grid is interaction-invariant for the graph topology; precompute the rects and only move the current-point marker on slider input. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (`qaoa-explorer.tsx`) — added 2026-06-25
- [x] **[Resilience]** DONE 2026-06-26 (WS-6f, #63 merged) — **`periodicState` hangs on a degenerate period (`<= 0`)** — the exported QFT primitive loops without guarding a non-positive period, so a malformed config can spin. Validate `period > 0` and surface an ErrorCard. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`qft.ts`) — added 2026-06-25
- [x] **[Resilience]** DONE 2026-06-26 (WS-6f, #63 — parse failures now return error strings routed through the shared ErrorCard (qft-visualizer.tsx:33-40,140-142) with a malformed-JSON unit test; stale checkbox caught 2026-07-03) — **QFT visualizer silently swallows malformed/non-object source** instead of rendering the shared `ErrorCard` its siblings use, so a broken fence renders blank. Route the parse failure through `ErrorCard`. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`qft-visualizer.tsx`) — added 2026-06-25 _(see cross-cutting ErrorCard item)_
- [x] **[UI/UX]** PARTIAL 2026-06-26 (WS-6d, #65 merged) — dj-demo probability bars animate width with no `prefers-reduced-motion` guard; and dj/qft error cards use `my-8` while their valid cards use `my-6` (inconsistent vertical rhythm). Add the motion-reduce guard and unify the margin. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`dj-demo.tsx`, `qft-visualizer.tsx`) — added 2026-06-25. Motion-reduce guard fixed (dj-demo now uses shared ProbBars with `motion-reduce:transition-none`); error-card margin delta CLOSED 2026-07-04 (WS-B7, PR #96 — qft/dj error cards dropped `my-8` for the shared `my-6` default).



> **2026-07-19 Category B re-audit** — 9 adversarially-verified findings (independent skeptic per claim, default-refute; correctness claims re-derived from first principles, one by porting the shipped code to a measurement harness).

- [x] **[UI]** QAOA landscape axis caption is inverted — it names γ horizontal, but the heatmap renders β horizontal — The QAOA cost-landscape heatmap is the widget's flagship teaching visual: the learner drags γ and β and watches the current-point marker travel toward the grid maximum. The caption under it states "γ horizontal · β vertical", but the rendering does the exact opposite — beta is the x axis and gamma is the y axis. A learner who drags the γ slider sees the marker move VERTICALLY while the caption insists γ is the horizontal axis, so every conclusion drawn from the plot about which angle steers which direction is mirror-imaged. This is the same class of defect as the VQE landscape inversion and the (now closed) QAOA bitstring-order mirror-image; the caption has been wrong since the widget shipped in PR #8 and no test pins it. The `role="img"` label names both parameter ranges but never states which axis is which, so the axis mapping is unavailable to screen-reader users and wrong for everyone else. _Fix:_ Correct the caption at qaoa-explorer.tsx:298-300 to `β horizontal · γ vertical` (matching the render, which is internally consistent across cells, grid-max marker and current-point marker — changing the render instead would be the higher-risk edit). While there, extend the heatmap aria-label at :283 to carry the same mapping, e.g. "…beta on the horizontal axis in [0, pi/2], gamma on the vertical axis in [0, pi]…", so the axis orientation reaches AT as well. Add a unit assertion in qaoa-explorer.test.tsx pinning the caption text against the rect indexing so the pair cannot drift again. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/qaoa.ts:58-73`, `web/src/components/quantum/qaoa-explorer.tsx:235-240`, `web/src/components/quantum/qaoa-explorer.tsx:298-300`, `web/src/components/quantum/qaoa-explorer.tsx:289-296`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** The Deutsch-Jozsa oracle select bypasses `.focus-ring`, reinstating the exact 1.36:1 alpha focus ring PR #172 removed site-wide — PR #172 fixed a High-impact finding: the site focus ring drew accent at 50% alpha and measured 1.60:1 in light theme, far under the 3:1 WCAG 1.4.11 floor. The fix added a solid per-theme `--focus` token and routed every control through `.focus-ring`. The DJ oracle select was missed — it carries a hand-rolled `focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-accent/40`, which is the same alpha-accent anti-pattern, and measures 1.36:1 against the glass card in light theme versus 5.20:1 for the shared token. It is also the only interactive control in the entire quantum widget family still doing this (a repo-wide grep for bespoke `focus:ring-*` returns this one line plus four `focus:outline-none` on non-focusable `role="status"` divs). Two secondary defects ride along: it uses `focus:` rather than `focus-visible:`, so the ring also fires on mouse click unlike every sibling control, and it omits `ring-offset-2`, so the ring sits directly on the border with no separation. This is the DJ widget's only control, so a keyboard user tabbing to it in light mode effectively loses the focus indicator. _Fix:_ Replace the bespoke classes at dj-demo.tsx:100 with the sibling idiom: `rounded-control border border-(--bd) bg-(--field) px-2 py-1.5 text-sm text-(--ink) focus-ring`, matching grover-visualizer.tsx:167. That single edit picks up the solid per-theme `--focus`, the focus-visible semantics, and the ring offset. Since dj-demo also carries a redundant `aria-label="Oracle"` duplicating its `<label htmlFor>` at :89-94, drop the aria-label in the same pass. Consider extending contrast-guard.test.ts with a rule that flags any `focus:ring-` / `focus-visible:ring-` outside globals.css, so the next hand-rolled ring fails CI. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/dj-demo.tsx:100`, `web/src/app/globals.css:504-510`, `web/src/app/globals.css:20`, `web/src/components/quantum/grover-visualizer.tsx:167`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Correctness]** QAOA landscape axis caption is inverted — the heatmap plots beta horizontally and gamma vertically, but the caption says the opposite — The expected-cut heatmap renders each cell at `x = bi` (the BETA index) and `y = RES-1-gi` (the GAMMA index), and the live current-point circle likewise takes `cx` from `curBi` (beta) and `cy` from `curGi` (gamma). The caption underneath the SVG states the reverse: "gamma horizontal, beta vertical". This is the same family of defect as the already-fixed VQE landscape inversion — a mirror-image labeling bug sitting on top of numerically correct math. Three concrete failures follow. (1) The widget contradicts itself in plain view: dragging the gamma slider moves the dot VERTICALLY while the caption asserts gamma is the horizontal axis. (2) The two axes have DIFFERENT ranges (gamma spans [0,pi], beta spans [0,pi/2]), so reading a position under the swapped caption also mis-scales both angles by 2x in opposite directions — it is not a harmless relabeling. (3) A learner who reads the amber grid-max marker off the plot and types those angles into the sliders does not reproduce the marked maximum. For the star graph {"edges":[[0,1],[0,2],[0,3],[0,4]]} the true optimum is gamma=1.503, beta=0.410 giving expected cut 2.996; reading the same marker under the shipped caption yields gamma=0.820, beta=0.751 giving 2.131 — barely above the 2.000 you get by doing nothing (gamma=beta=0). The widget's central teaching claim, that the landscape shows you where the good angles are, inverts into a wrong answer. No test asserts axis semantics, which is why this survived two prior deep audits. _Fix:_ Swap the two glyphs in the caption at qaoa-explorer.tsx:299 so it reads "beta horizontal, gamma vertical" (matching the render), and extend it to carry the ranges the axes actually span, e.g. "beta 0..pi/2 horizontal, gamma 0..pi vertical" — the unequal ranges are what turn a swap into a mis-scaling. Mirror the same axis assignment into the SVG aria-label at :283 so the accessible name names the axes rather than just listing the two domains. Add one regression test to qaoa-explorer.test.tsx that pins the mapping from the render side rather than the prose side: move the gamma slider and assert the current-point circle's `cy` changes while its `cx` is unchanged (and the converse for beta). That test is what stops the caption drifting back out of sync. Leave the kernel and the render mapping untouched — the math and the plot are correct; only the label lies. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/qaoa.ts:58-72`, `web/src/components/quantum/qaoa-explorer.tsx:235-240`, `web/src/components/quantum/qaoa-explorer.tsx:289-296`, `web/src/components/quantum/qaoa-explorer.tsx:298-300`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Correctness]** The QFT visualizer's default and lesson configuration is an exact fixed point of the transform — input and output panels render byte-identical bar charts — The QFT explorable exists to show, in the GUIDE's own words, "a periodic comb become a frequency comb". Its shipped default is qubits=4, period=4, and the lesson mounts it with exactly that. But at N=16 the comb spacing r=4 and the spectral spacing N/r=4 coincide, so the QFT maps this state to itself: input magnitudes are 0.50 at j = 0,4,8,12 and the output magnitudes are 0.50 at k = 0,4,8,12. The two side-by-side panels — headed "Input |x&gt;" and "QFT output (magnitude)" — are pixel-for-pixel identical. The learner is invited to compare them and can draw only one conclusion from the visual: the QFT did nothing. The accompanying note ("period r = 4 -&gt; spikes every N/r = 4") is true but reads as a tautology, and the highlight rule `idx % spacing === 0` lights up the same four bars on both sides. The transformation the widget is the centerpiece for is invisible in its own default view. Every other period that divides 16 demonstrates it vividly — this is the single degenerate choice. Note this is not a math bug: qft.ts is correct and norm-preserving, and the existing kernel test at qft.test.ts:15-22 happens to pin this very r=4/N=16 case, so it passes while the demo teaches nothing. _Fix:_ Change the default period from 4 to 2 at qft-visualizer.tsx:60 (and its comment on :59), and update the lesson fence at 03-algorithms/GUIDE.md:249 to `{"qubits": 4, "input": "period:2"}`. At N=16 that turns the demo into eight evenly spaced input bars collapsing to two tall spikes at k=0 and k=8, and the existing note renders as the now-visibly-true "period r = 2 -&gt; spikes every N/r = 8 (N = 16)". It passes the existing divisibility guard unchanged (16 % 2 === 0) and needs no code beyond the two literals. Add one regression test asserting that for the default configuration the input and output magnitude vectors are NOT equal — that is the assertion that encodes "this widget must actually show a transformation" and prevents any future default from silently landing back on r = N/r. Keep the r=4 case in qft.test.ts as-is; it is a valid kernel test, just not a valid demo. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/qft-visualizer.tsx:59-60`, `03-algorithms/GUIDE.md:248-250`, `web/src/components/quantum/qft-visualizer.tsx:146-152`, `web/src/components/quantum/qft-visualizer.tsx:100`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Consistency]** dj-demo's oracle `<select>` is the last hand-rolled focus ring in web/src — it bypasses the shared `.focus-ring` / `--focus` token — Every other control in the repo routes its focus indicator through the `.focus-ring` utility (which resolves the per-theme, solid `--focus` token added in the Category A refinement). The DJ oracle select instead hard-codes `focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-accent/40`. Two divergences fall out of that: (a) it is an ALPHA ring, which is exactly the shape the `--focus` token was introduced to eliminate — globals.css states verbatim that an alpha ring "composites below the 3:1 WCAG 1.4.11 floor on the light surfaces", and token-contrast.test.ts asserts &gt;= 3:1 for `--focus` against every surface, a guard this select escapes because it never uses the token; (b) it uses `focus:` rather than `focus-visible:`, so unlike every sibling control it flashes a ring on mouse click. It also uses `rounded-md` where every other quantum select uses `rounded-control`. The tell that this is drift and not intent: dj-demo copied cost-calculator's label recipe byte-for-byte but not its select recipe. _Fix:_ Replace the dj-demo select's className with the existing cost-calculator recipe: `className={`${fieldClass} px-2 py-1.5 text-sm`}` (import `fieldClass` from ./widget-ui). That restores the solid `--focus` ring, the `focus-visible:` trigger, and `rounded-control`, with no visual change to the resting state. Optionally follow up with a repo-wide guard test (sibling to caption-contrast.test.ts) that fails on any `focus:ring-*` / `focus-visible:ring-*` literal outside globals.css, so the token can't be bypassed again. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/dj-demo.tsx:100`, `web/src/app/globals.css:504-510`, `web/__tests__/token-contrast.test.ts:136-137`, `web/src/components/quantum/dj-demo.tsx:91`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Architecture]** The QAOA landscape's (gamma, beta) domain is restated four independent times across two files, with nothing pinning the marker to the grid — `qaoaLandscape` owns the mapping from grid index to angle: gamma spans [0, pi] and beta spans [0, pi/2] over `res` cells. That same domain is re-derived by hand in three more places in the explorer — the inverse mapping that positions the current-point marker, the SVG's aria-label prose, and the two slider `max` props. All four agree today (I checked the endpoints: gamma=pi maps to curGi=23=RES-1, which qaoaLandscape maps back to exactly pi), so this is not a live bug. But it is the feature's central teaching visual — "where am I on the cost landscape" — and a future edit to either range in qaoa.ts silently lands the marker on the wrong cell and makes the aria-label lie, with no test to catch it: qaoa.test.ts only asserts the landscape is a res x res grid of finite numbers. _Fix:_ Export the domain from qaoa.ts as the single source — e.g. `export const QAOA_DOMAIN = { gammaMax: Math.PI, betaMax: Math.PI / 2 }` plus `landscapeAngles(gi, bi, res)` and its inverse `landscapeCell(gamma, beta, res)` — and have `qaoaLandscape` use the former internally. The explorer then reads `QAOA_DOMAIN.gammaMax` / `.betaMax` for the two slider maxes and the aria-label string, and calls `landscapeCell(...)` for curGi/curBi instead of re-deriving the inverse. Add one unit test asserting `landscapeCell(...landscapeAngles(gi, bi, res), res)` round-trips, which is the invariant that currently holds only by coincidence. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/qaoa.ts:62`, `web/src/components/quantum/qaoa-explorer.tsx:262-263`, `web/src/components/quantum/qaoa-explorer.tsx:283`, `web/src/components/quantum/qaoa-explorer.tsx:317`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Consistency]** Grover and DJ still hand-roll percentage formatting after the #79 single-sourcing sweep, and Grover repeats the identical expression three times — PR #79 added `formatPercent`/`percentSR` to format.ts and routed 32 readouts across 14 widgets through them; its ledger entry names shots/correlation/kernel/vqc as the percent conversions it single-sourced. The F8 widgets were only partially swept — qaoa's gamma/beta got `formatRadians`, but the Grover success probability and the DJ all-zeros probability were left on raw `.toFixed(1)`. Grover is the worse case: the same `(success * 100).toFixed(1)` expression appears three times in one file (live region, visible readout, slider aria-valuetext), so the widget's headline number has three independent formatting sites that can drift in precision. _Fix:_ In grover-visualizer, hoist `const successPct = formatPercent(success * 100);` once and use it at lines 100, 131 and 151 (with `percentSR` for the aria-valuetext if the SR wording should read "percent"); route the per-bar value through `formatPercent(p * 100)`. In dj-demo, wrap the all-zeros readout in `formatPercent(result.probs[0] * 100)`. Finish the sweep by routing `ProbBars`'s own `valueText` (widget-ui.tsx:148) through `formatPercent` so future widgets inherit it instead of copying `.toFixed`. Output is byte-identical today (both probabilities are magnitudes-squared, so no -0 case exists), so this is pure single-sourcing. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/format.ts:13-14`, `web/src/components/quantum/grover-visualizer.tsx:100`, `web/src/components/quantum/grover-visualizer.tsx:116`, `web/src/components/quantum/dj-demo.tsx:82-84`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** DJ oracle display names live in the component while the oracles live in the kernel, joined by two silent fallbacks — `ORACLES` (the four oracle functions, keyed by name) is owned by deutsch-jozsa.ts; the human-readable label for each of those same four keys is a parallel `Record<string, string>` in the component. The two maps are joined only by string keys and guarded by two silent `??` fallbacks, so the failure mode of adding a fifth oracle to the kernel is a dropdown option labelled with the raw key (e.g. "lowtwobits") rather than a compile error, and a typo'd `oracleKey` silently evaluates constant0 while the select still shows the typo'd option. Nothing in dj-demo.test.tsx pins the two maps in sync. _Fix:_ Co-locate the label with the oracle in deutsch-jozsa.ts: change `ORACLES` to `Record<OracleKey, { f: Oracle; label: string }>` and export `type OracleKey = keyof typeof ORACLES`. The component then maps `Object.entries(ORACLES)` straight into `<option>`s with no second map and no `??` fallback, and typing `oracleKey` as `OracleKey` makes the `ORACLES[oracleKey] ?? ORACLES.constant0` guard unnecessary — a missing label becomes a type error instead of a raw key on screen. This mirrors how the kernel already owns everything else the widget displays. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/deutsch-jozsa.ts:11-16`, `web/src/components/quantum/dj-demo.tsx:16-21`, `web/src/components/quantum/dj-demo.tsx:23`, `web/src/components/quantum/dj-demo.tsx:104`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** The QFT visualizer mounts a live region whose text can never change, duplicating its visible footer verbatim — `LiveStatus` is documented as the announcement channel for a recomputed result when a control changes it, with an explicit instruction to pass an empty string when there is nothing to announce. The QFT visualizer has no controls at all — it is configured entirely by the fence source, so `note` is constant for the component's lifetime — yet it wraps `note` in a `role="status" aria-live="polite"` region AND renders the identical string in a visible footer paragraph. The live region can never fire, and its only lasting effect is that the same sentence appears twice in the accessibility tree, once as an sr-only paragraph near the top of the card and once as visible text at the bottom. This is a discipline gap rather than a barrier, but it is the one place in the F8 family where the carefully-tuned live-region pattern is applied to something that is not live. _Fix:_ Drop the `<LiveStatus>` at qft-visualizer.tsx:159 and keep the visible footer at :176-178 as the single source of the note, removing the duplicate accessibility-tree entry for a string that can never update. The existing test asserting `getByRole("status")` has the spectrum note should be retargeted to assert the visible note text instead (the other seven assertions in that suite are unaffected). Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/qft-visualizer.tsx:159`, `web/src/components/quantum/qft-visualizer.tsx:176-178`, `web/src/components/quantum/qft-visualizer.tsx:144-150`, `web/src/components/live-status.tsx:3-13`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)

### 9. Quantum ML explorables

- [x] **[Correctness]** ✓verified — **Encoding explorer shows physically wrong Bloch dials for the IQP feature map**: only `amplitude` is special-cased, so `iqp` reuses product-state `singleQubitRy(x0)/(x1)` dials — but `iqpState` is *entangled*, so the true single-qubit reduced states are mixed (shorter vectors), not those pure RY qubits. Render reduced states via partial trace (or drop the dials for IQP). Impact: High. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/quantum/encoding-explorer.tsx:76-81,127-143`, `encoding.ts:26-38`) — added 2026-06-17

> **Inline verification pass — 2026-06-20.** The F9–F12 verify stage was again server-throttled (two workflow attempts rate-limited), so these were verified by direct source review (the fallback the Category A pass used). _Current state:_ the QML math is sound (the IQP partial-trace dial fix shipped); remaining items are perf (the kernel grid), one stale a11y claim now fixed, and DRY across the RNG/dataset/type helpers.

- [x] **[Performance]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-4 perf) — ✓verified (re-confirmed 2026-06-20) — confirms prior — **Kernel decision boundary rebuilds every training feature state inside each grid cell**. The `result` useMemo loops `GRID`×`GRID` = 36×36 = 1296 cells, each calling `kernelScore([cx,cy], train, ...)`, and `kernelScore` rebuilds `featureState(p.x, map, scale)` for all 60 training points on every call → ~77.7k redundant state constructions per recompute (every map/scale change). _Fix:_ precompute `trainStates` once and add a `kernelScoreS(precomputedStates)` variant. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`kernel-explorer.tsx:135-151` — 36×36 grid calling kernelScore per cell · `kernel.ts:29-33` — kernelScore loops `featureState(p.x, map, scale)` over all train points inside · `kernel-explorer.tsx:125` — `train = makeDataset(..., 60, 1)`) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Accessibility]** CLEARED 2026-06-20 (prior claim refuted — already fixed) — **VqcTrainer result IS announced**. The prior item said the step/loss/accuracy readout is silent to AT; in current code that paragraph carries `role="status" aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="true"`, so it announces. The genuine residual (kernel/barren not announcing) is split out below. (`vqc-trainer.tsx:287-291`.)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** DONE 2026-06-21 (kernel readout in WS-3 #41; barren now has a role=status variance readout — WS-5c) — ✓verified — NEW — **Kernel/barren recomputed readouts are not in a live region**. Unlike VqcTrainer, the kernel-explorer "quantum-kernel accuracy = N%" and "linear baseline = N%" lines are plain paragraphs with no `role`/`aria-live`, so a screen-reader user changing the feature map or scale never hears the accuracy change. _Fix:_ wrap the kernel/barren accuracy readout in `role="status" aria-live="polite"` as VqcTrainer already does. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`kernel-explorer.tsx:227-234` — accuracy + baseline paragraphs, no live region; recompute via the `result` useMemo on map/scale change) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[Consistency & reuse]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-2b) — ✓verified — confirms prior — **Dataset/RNG/type helpers duplicated across the QML modules**. `mulberry32` is defined three times (`barren.ts:3`, `kernel.ts:42`, `vqc.ts:55`); `gauss` twice (`kernel.ts:51`, `vqc.ts:64`); and the training-point type is declared twice under two names with identical shape — `Point` (`kernel.ts:5`) and `Pt` (`vqc.ts:3`), both `{ x:[number,number]; y:-1|1 }`. _Fix:_ extract a shared `rng.ts` (mulberry32 + gauss) and a single `Point` type imported by all three modules. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`barren.ts:3`, `kernel.ts:42`, `vqc.ts:55` — three mulberry32 copies · `kernel.ts:51` + `vqc.ts:64` — gauss · `kernel.ts:5` Point vs `vqc.ts:3` Pt) — added 2026-06-20
- [x] **[UX]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-5c: useDeferredValue(scale) + aria-busy/opacity) — ✓verified — confirms prior (kernel) — **Kernel grid recompute runs synchronously on every scale-slider tick with no busy feedback**. The `result` useMemo (the 1296-cell × 60-point grid above) is keyed on `[train, map, scale]`, so each scale-slider step recomputes the whole decision boundary synchronously before paint, with no debounce/deferral and no pending indicator. (The VqcTrainer "Train" path is a single click-burst, a lesser case.) _Fix:_ feed a deferred scale value (`useDeferredValue`/`startTransition`) into the grid memo and/or show an `aria-busy`/opacity cue while recomputing. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`kernel-explorer.tsx:129-151` — result useMemo keyed on `[train, map, scale]`, driven directly by the scale slider) — added 2026-06-20
- [ ] **[Performance]** SKIPPED 2026-06-21 (loss/acc sit after the parse early-return; a useMemo there breaks rules-of-hooks, and it is clicks-only re-render = Low) — ✓verified — confirms prior — **VqcTrainer recomputes full-dataset loss + accuracy un-memoized in the render body**. `loss = mseLoss(data, theta, bias)` and `acc = accuracyOf(data, theta, bias)` run on every render (each loops all 30 points, simulating a 2-qubit circuit per point). The widget only re-renders on Train/Reset clicks (not a per-frame path), so impact is Low, but both are trivially memoizable on `[data, theta, bias]`. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`vqc-trainer.tsx:224-225` — loss/acc in render body · `vqc.ts:36-40` mseLoss loops vqcOutput per point) — added 2026-06-20

> **2026-07-19 Category B re-audit** — 44 adversarially-verified findings (independent skeptic per claim, default-refute; correctness claims re-derived from first principles, one by porting the shipped code to a measurement harness).

- [x] **[Accessibility]** REGRESSION: the shared slider thumb shrank from 24px back to 16px, re-opening the closed WCAG 2.5.8 finding across all ~45 LabeledSlider inputs — `.slider` is the single class every slider in the engine wears (LabeledSlider hardcodes `className="slider flex-1 focus-ring"`). PR #43 (WS-5a, 2026-06-21) explicitly enlarged its thumb from 16px to 24px to satisfy WCAG 2.2 SC 2.5.8 (Target Size, Minimum) — the ledger records that as `[closed][Accessibility] DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-5a)`. The Instrument redesign (PR #169, 26729d9) rewrote the same CSS block and reverted the thumb to 16x16px while ALSO shrinking the rail from `h-1.5` (6px) to `h-1` (4px). Both regressions are in the current tree. The element's own hit box is now 4px tall, so on touch the draggable area is a 4px-tall strip with a 16px thumb overhanging it — worse than the state the original finding described. The engine's own docstring still asserts the fix is in place, so the contract is now false in code. _Fix:_ In globals.css restore `width: 24px; height: 24px` on both `.slider::-webkit-slider-thumb` and `.slider::-moz-range-thumb`, keeping the Instrument styling (the `2px solid var(--surface-base)` rim, `--bd-2` hairline, `--bar-glow` shadow) so the redesign's look is preserved at the accessible size. Restore the rail to at least `h-1.5` — or keep the 4px visual rail and give the input a transparent vertical hit area (e.g. `py-2.5 bg-clip-content` or a `::-webkit-slider-runnable-track` height) so the pointer target clears 24px without thickening the drawn line. Update the widget-ui.tsx:287 docstring to match whatever ships. A grep-level guard in the existing CSS test suites (like caption-contrast.test.ts pins `.text-caption`) would stop a third regression. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/globals.css:517-518`, `web/src/app/globals.css:525-526`, `web/src/app/globals.css:534-535`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:348`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Performance]** Barren sweep is 3x slower than an equivalent allocation-free RY butterfly — the block lands mid-scroll at mount — Every mount and every depth change runs 14 gradientVariance calls (n=2..8 x 2 costs) synchronously inside a useMemo. The hot path is applyGate1InPlace, which allocates 6 fresh [re,im] tuples per butterfly via cAdd(cMul(...), cMul(...)); at n=8 that is ~24 gates x 128 butterflies x 400 samples x 2 shifts x 2 costs of short-lived garbage. Measured with the shipped code (Node 26, Apple Silicon): 154 ms at depth 2 (the lesson's mount config) and 282 ms at depth 5 — several-fold worse on mobile. Because widget-fence mounts the widget via an IntersectionObserver approach gate 400px before it scrolls into view, the first 154 ms block lands exactly while the reader is scrolling the lesson. useDeferredValue (line 91) correctly de-prioritizes slider recomputes but cannot interrupt the sweep once the memo starts. RY is a real-entried rotation: a specialized zero-allocation butterfly writing components in place is bit-identical (same seeds produced identical variances in my harness) and runs the same sweeps in 53 ms / 83 ms — a 2.9-3.4x cut. _Fix:_ Add a private real-rotation butterfly in barren.ts (it already owns applyCZRingInPlace, so a local `applyRyInPlace(state, cos, sin, q, n)` matches the module's idiom) and use it in buildState; barren.test.ts's determinism test plus a one-off equivalence assertion against applyGate1InPlace pins the math. Optionally add a module-level Map keyed `${n}|${depth}|${cost}|${samples}` — the sweep is fully deterministic (seeded per n), so slider revisits (2 -&gt; 5 -&gt; 2, the widget's natural gesture) become free instead of re-paying the full sweep. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/quantum/math.ts:97-109`, `web/src/components/quantum/barren.ts:29-38`, `web/src/components/quantum/barren-explorer.tsx:96-107`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-fence.tsx:103,120-127`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Architecture]** All four QML widgets place their parse-error early-return after every hook, paying a structural tax: a full wasted sweep in barren and an unreachable null-guard in kernel — Each widget runs `useMemo`-based computation before the `if (!parsed.ok) return <ErrorCard/>` guard, because hooks may not sit after a conditional return. The consequences differ per widget but share one root cause. In barren-explorer the guard is at line 109 while the expensive sweep memo runs at lines 96-107, so a malformed fence executes the entire n=2..8 x {global,local} gradient-variance sweep (7 qubit counts, 300 samples, two statevector rebuilds per sample, up to 2^8 amplitudes) and throws the result away — roughly 20M float ops of blocking main-thread work to render the string "expected JSON like...". In kernel-explorer the same ordering forces both heavy memos to be nullable, which produces a guard branch that can never be taken and a nonsense message: `train` is null only when `!parsed.ok`, and `result` is null only when `!train`, so `parsed.ok && (!train || !result)` is unreachable and the `parsed.ok ? "kernel error" : parsed.error` ternary would render "kernel error: kernel error". In vqc-trainer the same ordering is exactly why the recorded [OPEN][Performance] item on loss/acc was parked as SKIPPED ("a useMemo there breaks rules-of-hooks"). Splitting each widget into a thin parse shell plus an inner component that receives an already-validated config removes all three symptoms with one shape change and unblocks the parked item. _Fix:_ Give each of the four widgets a two-part structure: an exported `X({source})` that only parses and either returns `<ErrorCard>` or renders `<XView config={...} />`, and an inner `XView` holding all hooks with a non-nullable, already-validated config. barren's sweep then never runs on a malformed fence; kernel's `train`/`result` memos become non-nullable, deleting the unreachable guard and the "kernel error" placeholder string; and vqc-trainer's `loss`/`acc` become memoizable, closing the parked [OPEN][Performance] item as a side effect. Keep the widget-fence registry entries and the exported component names unchanged so `qvqc/qkernel/qbarren/qencode` and every existing test import still resolve. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/quantum/barren-explorer.tsx:96`, `web/src/components/quantum/barren-explorer.tsx:109`, `web/__tests__/components/quantum/barren-explorer.test.tsx:26-44`, `web/src/components/quantum/kernel-explorer.tsx:119`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Correctness]** Barren plot's top y-axis tick always renders as literal "100" (meaning 10^0 = 1) — The decade tick labels in the barren-plateau SVG are rendered as flat text `10{d}` with no superscript. With the shipped lesson config ({"depth": 2, "samples": 400} in 04-quantum-ml/GUIDE.md:273-275) I computed the real sweep: max variance is the local cost's ~0.37 (log10 = -0.43), so hiLog = Math.ceil(-0.43) = 0 at EVERY depth 1..5 — the tick loop always emits a d=0 tick whose label renders as the string "100" on the top gridline of a log axis whose true value there is 1. A learner reading the axis sees decades "10-3, 10-2, 10-1, 100" — the top one flatly reads as one hundred, and the negative ones read as hyphenated typos — on the very plot whose whole lesson is 'read the exponential collapse off the log axis'. The variance readout directly below the same plot formats exponents correctly with &lt;sup&gt;, so the widget contradicts its own notation. _Fix:_ Render the tick exponent as a real superscript inside the SVG text: `10<tspan dy="-2.5" fontSize={5}>{d}</tspan>` (matching the readout's &lt;sup&gt; convention). One-line change to the tick &lt;text&gt; at barren-explorer.tsx:183-193; no layout shift since padL already fits three characters. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/barren-explorer.tsx:192`, `web/src/components/quantum/barren-explorer.tsx:119`, `web/src/components/quantum/barren-explorer.tsx:264-266`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[UI]** vqc-trainer was skipped by the Instrument token migration — raw gray utilities where all three siblings use --ink/--mut/text-caption — The VQC trainer's status readout and explainer paragraph still use neutral Tailwind grays while the kernel, barren, and encoding explorers were migrated to the warm-tinted Instrument tokens in PR #169. Adjacent widgets on the same QML lesson page render their equivalent text tiers in two different color systems, and vqc-trainer will not track any future per-theme retune of --ink/--mut. _Fix:_ Finish the #169 sweep in vqc-trainer: line 269 to text-(--ink), line 294 to text-caption, and the LossCurve rect to the field/track surface token used elsewhere, keeping the accent spans as-is. Pure class swaps, no behavior change; the existing vqc-trainer.test.tsx asserts text content only. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/vqc-trainer.tsx:269`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/vqc-trainer.tsx:294`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/vqc-trainer.tsx:178`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/kernel-explorer.tsx:221`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** Barren-plateau curves are distinguished by hue alone, and the amber hue fails 3:1 non-text contrast in light theme — The whole lesson of this chart is 'global collapses, local does not', but the two curves are identical solid polylines with identical r=2 circle markers — only olive (--accent, oklch hue 118) vs amber (hue ~70) separates them, a WCAG 1.4.1 use-of-color failure that turns the chart ambiguous under deuteranopia. Compounding it, text-amber-500 (#f59e0b) on the light glass surface is ~2.1:1, below the 3:1 non-text minimum (1.4.11). The SVG title and the callout also name the curves by color ('the accent curve', 'the amber curve'), which is meaningless to a screen-reader user who never perceives the colors. The repo already solved this exact pattern for the PES explorer's HF curve in WS-B1 (#90); barren was not included in that commit. _Fix:_ Give the local-cost curve strokeDasharray (e.g. "6 3") with a matching dashed legend swatch, shift its light-theme color to amber-600 (~3.2:1 on the light surface) keeping dark:text-amber-400, and reword the &lt;title&gt;/callout to 'the global-cost curve' / 'the local-cost curve'. Mirrors the pes-explorer precedent exactly; barren-explorer.test.tsx asserts legend text only, so nothing breaks. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/barren-explorer.tsx:227-254`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/barren-explorer.tsx:140-149`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/barren-explorer.tsx:163-167,286-290`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/pes-explorer.tsx:206-214`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** Encoding explorer's LiveStatus announces the full Dirac string on every slider tick, violating the component's own concise-line contract — The sr-only live region interpolates the complete Dirac expansion — for IQP, four complex-coefficient terms — and re-fires on every x0/x1 arrow-key press (step pi/60, 120 positions per slider). The sliders already announce via aria-valuetext, so a screen-reader user gets a double announcement per keystroke, one of them a long math string. Every sibling LiveStatus is one short sentence with 1-3 numbers, live-status.tsx's own doc says to keep the announcement to one concise line, and bloch-dial.tsx explicitly warns that per-tick state readouts must stay OUT of live regions. The visible Dirac paragraph just below already serves the state to AT in reading order. _Fix:_ Trim the LiveStatus template to the concise teaching fact: `${ENCODING_LABEL[encoding]} feature map. ||psi|| = ${norm.toFixed(3)}.` — dropping only the dirac term. The existing test (encoding-explorer.test.tsx:20-25) asserts the status matches /feature map/i and the slider valuetext contains 'norm', both preserved. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/encoding-explorer.tsx:123-127`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/encoding-explorer.tsx:174-203`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/live-status.tsx:7-9`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/bloch-dial.tsx:9-10`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[UI]** Barren y-axis decade labels render without superscript — the top tick reads '100' instead of 10^0 — The SVG tick label is the literal string '10' concatenated with the decade integer, so ticks render as '100', '10-2', '10-4'. With the default config the extent ceiling is always 0 (gradient variance &lt; 1, hiLog = Math.ceil of a negative max is 0), so the topmost gridline of a log-scale teaching chart is labeled '100' — reading as the linear value one hundred — and negative decades read as subtraction ('10-4'). The widget's own HTML readout formats the same quantities correctly with &lt;sup&gt;, so the chart contradicts its caption typographically. _Fix:_ Split the label into '10' plus a &lt;tspan dy="-2.5" fontSize={5}&gt;{d}&lt;/tspan&gt; exponent (raise-and-shrink), matching the HTML readout's superscript notation. The labels are aria-hidden decoration, so no AT or test surface changes. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/barren-explorer.tsx:183-193`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/barren-explorer.tsx:117-119,124-126`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/barren-explorer.tsx:264-266`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Performance]** Bar's shimmer overlay is documented as one-shot but declared infinite — every probability bar composites a repeating sweep forever — The .bar-shimmer::after rule (new in PR #169, after the last F12 audit on 2026-07-04; git -S confirms commit 26729d9 introduced it) declares `animation: bar-shimmer 2.8s ease-in-out infinite`, yet both of its own comments call it 'One-shot' ('One-shot shimmer sweep overlay', 'One-shot shimmer never runs under reduced-motion'). With no 0% keyframe the transform snaps back to the base translateX(-100%) each cycle and re-sweeps to 240% every 2.8s, indefinitely, on every Bar fill — widget-ui.tsx applies `bar-shimmer` unconditionally, and ProbBars renders one Bar per basis state, so a 4-qubit CircuitLab keeps 16 composited pseudo-element animations running (and a lesson with a scrubber alongside doubles that). Lesson pages are long-dwell reading surfaces; the compositor never idles while a static widget sits on screen, costing GPU/battery for zero information change. Reduced-motion coverage itself is correct (globals.css:685 kills it under reduce). _Fix:_ Align the declaration with its twice-documented intent: drop `infinite` (animation-iteration-count defaults to 1) and add `animation-fill-mode: forwards` so the sweep exits off-bar and stays gone — a true one-shot entrance flourish, after which the compositor idles. If the periodic sweep is instead the intended smoke-and-glass design, correct both 'One-shot' comments and consciously accept the cost — but the perf-cheap reading is the one the comments already specify. No markup changes; widget-ui.test.tsx's existing motion-reduce assertions are unaffected. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/globals.css:556-565`, `web/src/app/globals.css:118-120`, `web/src/app/globals.css:684-688`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:111`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[UI]** Shared primitives ship off-system cool-gray defaults — two different muted grays render side by side in the same card, and 15/17 consumers hand-override the label default — widget-ui.tsx mixes the Instrument warm token tier (--ink/--mut/--bd/--field, hue 82-88) with raw Tailwind v4 grays (hue ~264, cool blue-lean) inside the same shared components. LabeledSlider's value readout is hardcoded `text-gray-500 dark:text-gray-400` with no valueClassName escape hatch, while Bar's value/label default is `text-(--mut)` — so in CircuitLab (and every widget pairing ProbBars with a slider) the bar percentages are warm --mut and the slider readout directly below is cool gray-500. The label default `text-gray-600 dark:text-gray-300` is so off-system that 15 of 17 consumers override it to text-(--mut), leaving four stragglers (qaoa-explorer x2 sliders, param-compile's SLIDER_LABEL, barren-explorer and pes-explorer on the raw default) rendering off-palette labels. ErrorCard, secondaryActionClass, and fieldClass carry the same legacy gray chrome next to Chip/GateChip's --bd/--field hairlines. _Fix:_ Flip the shared defaults to the token tier: LabeledSlider label → text-(--mut) and value span → text-(--mut) (or add valueClassName defaulting to it), ErrorCard → text-(--mut), and migrate secondaryActionClass/fieldClass gray borders/fills to --bd/--bd-2/--field. Then delete the now-redundant per-caller labelClassName color overrides (15 files) and the four stragglers inherit the correct palette for free. Pure class swap, no behavior change; existing widget-ui tests don't pin these color classes. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. - [x] **[Accessibility]** Barren global-cost curve missed the #172 light-accent re-route the vqc loss curve received — PR #172 (commit e5a77a0) re-routed vqc-trainer's LossCurve stroke from text-accent to text-accent-dark dark:text-accent-light because the light theme's raw --accent computes 2.79:1 on --surface-base — below both the 4.5:1 text and 3:1 non-text (WCAG 1.4.11) minimums, per the repo's own token comments and token-contrast suite. The identical 1.5px data stroke one file over was skipped: barren-explorer's global-cost polyline (className="text-accent", line 232), its r=2 point circles (fill-accent, line 235), and the legend swatch (bg-accent, line 142) still render the lesson's centerpiece curve — the exponential collapse that IS the barren-plateau teaching point — at 2.79:1 in light theme. A repo-wide grep confirms these are the only remaining raw accent data marks in any chart widget. This is the missed second half of the auditors' amber-curve finding: restyling amber alone leaves the accent curve sub-3:1. _Fix:_ Apply the same pairing #172 gave the sibling stroke: polyline className="text-accent-dark dark:text-accent" (or dark:text-accent-light to match vqc exactly), circles "fill-accent-dark dark:fill-accent", legend swatch "bg-accent-dark dark:bg-accent". Dark theme is pixel-identical (dark --accent is the 12.8:1 bright olive); light theme lifts 2.79:1 to the documented 4.93:1. Coordinate with the fix for the auditors' amber finding so both curves land in one restyle of the legend + plot. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/barren-explorer.tsx:227-235`, `web/src/app/globals.css:151-156`, `web/__tests__/token-contrast.test.ts:158-162`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Performance]** Barren sweep simulates every state twice — one shared pass halves the depth-slider block (measured 48-50%) — For each qubit count n, the sweep calls gradientVariance once per cost, each with a fresh mulberry32(n). Identical seeds mean identical theta draws, so the expensive buildState(n, L, tp/tm) state vectors are rebuilt bit-identically for the 'local' run that the 'global' run just built — the cost function is the only thing that differs, and it is a cheap O(2^n) readout on the finished state. At the shipped fence config (04-quantum-ml/GUIDE.md line 274: depth 2, samples 400) I measured the full sweep at 128 ms desktop-node current vs 66 ms shared (48% saved); at depth 5 it is 244 ms vs 122 ms (50%). useDeferredValue keeps the slider responsive but the deferred render still runs this synchronously on the main thread, so every depth change blocks for the full duration — proportionally worse on mobile. _Fix:_ Add a `gradientVariances(n, L, samples, rng): { global: number; local: number }` to barren.ts that builds each (tp, tm) state pair once and evaluates both cost functions on it; have the sweep memo in barren-explorer.tsx call it once per n. The comment at barren-explorer.tsx:94-95 already promises 'the curves are comparable across costs' via the shared seed, and the shared-pass output is float-identical, so the rendered plot does not change by a single pixel. Update barren.test.ts to pin gradientVariances against the two gradientVariance calls (the existing per-cost export can stay for the tests). Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/barren-explorer.tsx:102-104`, `web/src/components/quantum/barren.ts:64-68`, `web/src/components/quantum/barren.ts:40-54`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** Encoding explorer's live region re-announces the full complex Dirac expansion on every slider tick — The LiveStatus in encoding-explorer interpolates the entire diracString into the polite region: `${label} feature map. ‖ψ‖ = 1.000. |ψ⟩ = ${dirac}.` For the IQP map all four amplitudes are complex, so diracString emits four parenthesized terms like '(0.35+0.61i)|00⟩ + (0.20-0.05i)|01⟩ + ...' — and role=status is implicitly aria-atomic, so the whole glyph-heavy string ('‖ψ‖', '⟩', parenthesized complex pairs) re-announces on every x0/x1 slider step (step = π/60, so a drag fires dozens). This breaches the LiveStatus contract documented on the component itself ('Keep the announcement to one concise line... to avoid drag/keystroke spam') and the tuned complementarity pattern hamiltonian-explorer documents (valuetext carries what the slider drives; LiveStatus stays summary-level). Every other LiveStatus consumer announces one compact SR-phrased line (shots-sampler announces only the most-probable basis state; vqe uses hartreeSR). The full Dirac string is already available on-screen as readable text at lines 211-213, so nothing is lost by trimming the announcement. _Fix:_ Keep the LiveStatus but make its announcement a concise SR-phrased summary, e.g. encoding + dominant term: `Angle feature map, norm 1.000. Largest term 0.87 on basis state 00.` (mirroring the shots-sampler phrasing) — or at minimum drop the '|ψ⟩ = ${dirac}' tail while keeping map + norm. The full expansion stays in the visible on-screen readout. Update encoding-explorer.test.tsx:20-24, which asserts the status wording, to the trimmed contract. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/encoding-explorer.tsx:123-127`, `web/src/components/live-status.tsx:8-9`, `web/src/components/quantum/state-readout.ts:13-40`, `web/src/components/quantum/hamiltonian-explorer.tsx:249-253`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Consistency]** widget-ui's raw-gray text defaults are stale post-redesign - 17 of 24 LabeledSlider call sites override them with token classes — The smoke-and-glass redesign moved the muted tier to AA-verified tokens (--mut/--ink, .text-caption, covered by token-contrast.test.ts), and widget-ui half-migrated: Bar, Chip, and GateChip use text-(--mut), but LabeledSlider's label default, its value readout (which has no override knob), ErrorCard, StateReadout, secondaryActionClass, and fieldClass still hardcode Tailwind grays. The result is that nearly every LabeledSlider caller re-specifies labelClassName just to swap the gray for text-(--mut), a handful of stragglers kept the gray so sliders in qaoa/vqe/param-compile render a different muted color than sliders in the rest of the widgets, and the module's own value readout sits beside Bar readouts in the same card in a subtly different gray that the token-contrast suite does not cover. _Fix:_ Flip widget-ui's remaining gray defaults to the token tier (text-(--mut)/text-caption for muted, text-(--ink) for body, bg-(--track) guidance for bar tracks), then delete the now-redundant labelClassName overrides down to width-only strings. The gray stragglers (qaoa, vqe, param-compile sliders, hamiltonian track) converge automatically or via one-line swaps, and the shared primitives come under the token-contrast test's protection. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low.
