- [x] **[Cleaner code]** qjw round-trips a display string back into styling, leaving a dead branch in factorClass — The `factors` memo stores a presentational string as the model: it sets `label = "X/Y"` for the operator's own mode. The render then has to parse that display string back out to pick a style — `factorClass(f.label === "X/Y" ? "X" : f.label)` — so the styling rule depends on the exact spelling of a user-visible label. Change the label to "X or Y" and the cell silently falls through to the identity style. The mapping also leaves `factorClass`'s `label === "Y"` arm permanently dead, because `factors` never emits a bare `"Y"`; the only three values it can ever produce are `"Z"`, `"X/Y"`, and `"I"`. The underlying model is a three-valued kind (parity / operator / identity), and encoding it as prose costs a re-parse and a dead branch. _Fix:_ Model the cell as `{ qubit: number; kind: "parity" | "operator" | "identity" }` and derive both the label text and the class from `kind`. That drops the string re-parse at the call site, removes the dead `"Y"` arm from `factorClass`, and makes the displayed text free to change without touching styling. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/jw-explorer.tsx:141`, `web/src/components/quantum/jw-explorer.tsx:313`, `web/src/components/quantum/jw-explorer.tsx:94`, `web/src/components/quantum/jw-explorer.tsx:136`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Architecture]** `import { H2 as H }` shadows the universal Hamiltonian symbol inside the Hamiltonian widgets, already forcing an underscore workaround — Four widgets alias the H2 dissociation fixture to the single letter `H`. In this feature `H` is the established symbol for a Hamiltonian — chemistry.ts exports a `Hamiltonian` type, `oneQubitHamiltonian`, and `hamiltonianExpectation`, and vqe-explorer binds `const H = oneQubitHamiltonian(...)` to mean exactly that. So inside hamiltonian-explorer, a file whose entire subject is the Hamiltonian, `H.jwTerms` and `H.points` read as "the Hamiltonian's terms" but are actually the fixture's. The collision is not hypothetical: metrics-explorer needs both objects at once and has to name the real Hamiltonian `H_` to get around the alias. The result is that the same identifier means the fixture in four files and the Hamiltonian in a fifth, within one tightly coupled feature. _Fix:_ Drop the alias and import `H2` unaliased in hamiltonian-explorer, pes-explorer, metrics-explorer and checkpoint-explorer, matching vqe-explorer. That frees `H` to mean the Hamiltonian consistently across the feature and lets metrics-explorer's `H_` become a plain `H`. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/hamiltonian-explorer.tsx:6`, `web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:110`, `web/src/components/quantum/vqe-explorer.tsx:14`, `web/src/components/quantum/chemistry.ts:43`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[UI]** The PES plot has no numeric axis ticks, so sighted users get strictly less scale information than the aria-label already gives screen-reader users — `pes-explorer.tsx:273-293` draws only two rotated axis *titles* — "bond length R (Å)" and "energy (Ha)" — and no tick values anywhere on either axis. Meanwhile the plot's own `aria-label` (lines 142-149) spells the full domain and range out for AT: "Bond length from 0.30 angstrom to 2.70 angstrom on the x axis, energy from … to … on the y axis." A sighted learner reading the dissociation curve therefore cannot tell where 1 Å is, how deep the well is relative to the asymptote, or what the y span covers — the numbers exist only at the single scrubber position in the readout `dl` (lines 345-360). This is an inverted affordance: the accessible description is richer than the visual. The campaign already fixed the identical gap on the sibling VQE plot. _Fix:_ Add min/max (and optionally mid) tick labels on both axes using the already-available `sx`/`sy` scales and `rMin`/`rMax`/`yLo`/`yHi`, styled like the VQE ticks (`fontSize={7}`, `aria-hidden="true"`, so the existing aria-label stays the single AT description). This is the same edit shape already applied to qvqe, keeping the two chemistry plots consistent. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/pes-explorer.tsx:273-293`, `web/src/components/quantum/pes-explorer.tsx:142-149`, `web/src/components/quantum/vqe-explorer.tsx:262-274`, `web/src/components/quantum/pes-explorer.tsx:82-83,137`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[UX]** The PES bond-length scrubber steps at 0.01 Å over a 2.4 Å domain — 240 arrow presses end to end — while its sibling snaps to the fixture's own 0.05 Å pitch — `pes-explorer.tsx:337` hardcodes `step={0.01}` on the R scrubber. The committed fixture spans R = 0.3 → 2.7 Å in 49 points at a 0.05 Å pitch, so a keyboard user needs ~240 arrow presses to traverse the curve, and 4 of every 5 stops are pure linear interpolation between committed points (`h2Energies` → `h2Bracket`, `chemistry.ts:329-358`) that reveal no new data. The sibling Hamiltonian explorer solved this deliberately and wrote the rationale down: it derives its step from the fixture pitch so the slider lands on real samples. Two sliders over the identical physical domain, in the same lesson family, differ 5× in keyboard granularity for no teaching reason. _Fix:_ Derive the PES step the same way qham does — from `H.points[1].R - H.points[0].R` — or at minimum coarsen to 0.05 so arrow keys walk committed samples. Either way the two chemistry bond-length sliders share one keyboard cadence and the scrubber stops reporting interpolated values as if they were data points. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/pes-explorer.tsx:337`, `web/src/components/quantum/pes-explorer.tsx:71-76`, `web/src/components/quantum/hamiltonian-explorer.tsx:170-174`, `web/src/components/quantum/chemistry.ts:329-358`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[UI]** JW mixes two neutral systems inside one card: the mode buttons use raw Tailwind grays while the cells directly beneath them use the --bd/--field tokens — `jw-explorer.tsx` renders three columns of small mono cells stacked vertically, and they don't share a neutral palette. The unselected operator-mode buttons (line 244-248) use `border-gray-200 dark:border-gray-700/50 bg-gray-50 dark:bg-gray-900/50` — opaque, cool. The unoccupied occupation-ket cells (line 205) and the identity Pauli cells (line 100) use `border border-(--bd) bg-(--field) text-caption` — translucent, warm, letting the glass card show through. On the Instrument system these are visibly different neutrals appearing within ~120px of each other in the same widget, and at 375px they stack directly on top of one another where the mismatch is most exposed. The selected states are already unified (all three use `chip-selected`), which makes the unselected divergence read as drift rather than intent. _Fix:_ Move the unselected mode-button and dagger-toggle states onto the token recipe already used by GateChip and by the cells below them (`border border-(--bd) bg-(--field) text-(--mut)` plus a token hover), so the whole card resolves to one neutral system. The selected branch already shares `chip-selected` and needs no change. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/jw-explorer.tsx:244-248`, `web/src/components/quantum/jw-explorer.tsx:100`, `web/src/components/quantum/jw-explorer.tsx:203-205`, `web/src/app/globals.css:166,169,221,224`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** JW's creation/annihilation toggle pair has no group name, while the mode picker immediately above it does — `jw-explorer.tsx:230-233` correctly wraps the spin-orbital buttons in `role="group" aria-labelledby={groupId}` pointing at the "Operator mode" heading. The creation/annihilation pair 24 lines later (line 257) is a bare `<div className="flex items-center gap-2">` with two `aria-pressed` buttons and no group, no label, and no visible heading of its own. A screen-reader user tabbing in hears "creation a†, toggle button, pressed" with no statement of what the choice governs — and because the two buttons are mutually exclusive but expressed as independent toggles, there is no announced relationship between them either. Within a single card, two structurally identical control clusters are exposed two different ways. _Fix:_ Wrap the two buttons in `role="group"` labelled by a short name (an sr-only or visible "Operator type" eyebrow matching the "Operator mode" one above), reusing the existing useId pattern. While there, either point something at `headingId` or drop the unused id from line 113. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/jw-explorer.tsx:224-234`, `web/src/components/quantum/jw-explorer.tsx:257-282`, `web/src/components/quantum/jw-explorer.tsx:113-114`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Consistency]** Two hand-rolled bar rows use raw gray track colors instead of the --track token every other bar uses — The shared `Bar` and every other progress track in the app fill their groove with `bg-(--track)`. Two widgets re-implement the bar row and hardcode gray utilities instead — and not even the same gray as each other. `--track` is a low-alpha overlay (oklch(…/0.1) light, oklch(1 0 0 / 0.06) dark) that reads as a subtle recess; `bg-gray-100 dark:bg-gray-800` and `bg-gray-200 dark:bg-gray-800` are opaque and noticeably heavier, so these two widgets' bars sit visually differently from every other bar in the curriculum. _Fix:_ Swap both tracks to `bg-(--track)`. Neither widget needs anything the token cannot express, and this is a two-line change that brings them onto the same recess as the other ~8 bar surfaces. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:106`, `web/src/app/globals.css:168`, `web/src/components/quantum/hamiltonian-explorer.tsx:137`, `web/src/components/quantum/job-explorer.tsx:242`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Correctness]** qmetrics' default stopping_condition is pinned to the equilibrium FCI, so any fence that sets R without a threshold gets an unreachable line silently clamped to the plot floor — metrics-explorer.tsx:55-56 computes `const defaultThreshold = H.equilibrium.fci + 0.02;` — a constant tied to the equilibrium bond length (R = 0.75, fci = -1.137117, per the committed fixture) — while `R` itself is free: parseSource accepts any R and clamps it to the fixture range [0.3, 2.7] (clampR, :47-51). A fence body of `{"R": 1.5}` (legal, and the natural way an author would show dissociation-regime monitoring) therefore keeps the threshold at -1.1171 Ha while the real one-qubit VQE at R = 1.5 bottoms out at -0.9893 Ha. The threshold is 0.13 Ha below anything the curve can reach, so `belowThreshold` is permanently false, and thresholdY (:209) silently clamps the dashed line to `PLOT.h - PLOT.padB` — it renders sitting exactly on the x-axis, visually indistinguishable from a threshold at the bottom of the data band. The learner sees a converged curve, a dashed 'stopping_condition' pinned to the floor, and a chip reading a permanent failure state, with nothing indicating the threshold is off-scale. chemistry.ts:355 already exports `h2Energies(R, points)` returning the interpolated FCI at that exact R — the correct anchor — and metrics-explorer does not import it. _Fix:_ Anchor the default threshold to the R actually being plotted: `const defaultThreshold = h2Energies(R, H.points).fci + 0.02` (computed after R is resolved and clamped), so an R-only fence always gets a reachable, physically meaningful stopping_condition. Independently, when an explicitly-supplied threshold falls outside [yLo, yHi], the clamped dashed line should carry a marker or an aria/text note that it is off-scale rather than masquerading as a line at the plot floor. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:55-56`, `web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:47-51`, `web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:209`, `web/src/components/quantum/chemistry.ts:355`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Correctness]** qmetrics announces "Converged to …" unconditionally at stream end, including the flat-line stationary case its own code comment documents — metrics-explorer.tsx:213-217 builds the screen-reader status as `shown >= total && shown > 0 ? \`Converged to ${hartreeSR(lastEnergy)} at iteration ${lastIndex}; stopping_condition ${belowThreshold ? "met" : "not met"}.\` : ...`. The word "Converged" is asserted purely from the stream reaching the last sample — it is never checked against the optimizer's actual state. Forty steps of parameter-shift descent at LR 0.3 from START_THETA 1.0 does not converge for most of the fixture: replaying the exact kernel over all 49 committed bond lengths, 28 of them finish more than 1 mHa above the exact ground energy `c0 - hypot(cz, cx)`, with gaps up to 0.56 Ha at R = 1.80 and 0.32 Ha at R = 1.75. The extreme case is already documented in the file: the comment at :139-143 explains that at "R near 1.82" START_THETA sits on a local maximum and "collapsing the whole history to ~1e-16 float noise" — I confirmed the energy history there spans 2.9e-10 and never leaves -0.3679 Ha, versus a true ground state of -0.9602 Ha. The widget draws a dead-flat line and tells a screen-reader user the run "Converged to -0.3679 hartree", which is the opposite of the lesson (a stuck optimizer) the flat line is showing. _Fix:_ Gate the verb on the run itself instead of on the stream finishing. Import the already-exported `oneQubitGroundEnergy` and word the end-of-stream announcement from the outcome — e.g. "Settled at X hartree after 40 iterations, Y above the exact ground state; stopping_condition not met" when the gap exceeds a small tolerance, reserving "Converged" for runs that actually reach it. Same wording change applies to the visible phase chip so the flat-line case reads as stalled rather than finished. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:213-217`, `web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:139-143`, `web/src/components/quantum/chemistry.ts:361`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[UX / Correctness]** The curriculum's committed R = 0.74 is an illegal step value for qham's own slider, so the thumb sits on 0.75 while every readout says 0.74 — and the first keypress jumps two nodes — hamiltonian-explorer derives its slider step from the fixture's sampling pitch (0.05) but the only qham instance in the curriculum seeds it with R = 0.74, which is not on that lattice. Per the HTML range spec the UA rounds a step-mismatched value to the nearest allowed one, so the DOM input holds 0.75 while React state holds 0.74. From first paint the thumb, the visible readout, the aria-valuetext and the live region disagree, and because LabeledSlider reads e.target.value off the *sanitized* grid, one arrow press moves the readout 0.74 -&gt; 0.80 on a control whose step is 0.05. The same class of mismatch sits in the sibling qvqe theta slider. _Fix:_ Snap the seeded value onto each control's own lattice before it reaches state. In qham, run `parsed.R` through the nearest-node rule the widget already owns (`nearestPoint`, hamiltonian-explorer.tsx:76-87) when seeding `useState` at :176 — the widget already treats 0.75 as the sampled truth at :283, so this only makes the slider agree with the caption it already prints. In qvqe, seed and reset theta from a value on the `-π + n·π/90` lattice (n = 101 gives 0.3840) rather than the literal 0.4. No behavior is added; the thumb, the readout, the aria-valuetext and the drawn marker simply stop disagreeing, and one arrow press moves one step. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`05-quantum-chemistry/GUIDE.md:111`, `web/src/components/quantum/hamiltonian-explorer.tsx:170-174`, `web/src/components/quantum/hamiltonian-explorer.tsx:242-248`, `web/src/components/quantum/hamiltonian-explorer.tsx:266`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)

### 11. Hybrid-jobs explorables (gap-fill 2026-06-17 — 12 verified · re-confirmed 2026-06-20)

- [x] **[Correctness]** DONE (PR pending) — Metrics-explorer y-axis was seeded from the user `threshold` (not the data), so an out-of-band threshold flattened the VQE curve. FIXED: y-extent seeded from the energy history; threshold line clamped to the plot band. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`metrics-explorer.tsx:121-126,186-192`) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Correctness]** NOT A BUG (audit 2026-06-18) — "Checkpoint counts the failed in-flight iteration as free". Re-verification found the model is internally consistent: `failAt` = completed iterations (cells 0..failAt-1, all redone); cell failAt is the never-completed in-flight one. Count, shading, aria-labels, tests, and GUIDE prose ("480 of 500 = every completed step") all agree. No change. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`hybrid.ts:103-115`, `checkpoint-explorer.tsx:157-171,285`) — added 2026-06-17

> **Re-confirmed 2026-06-20** by direct source review (the F9–F12 verify workflow was throttled): the four items below were re-checked against current code and still hold.

- [x] **[Accessibility]** DONE 2026-06-21 (job delta in WS-3 #41; metrics now has a streamStatus role=status + Stream aria-busy + re-click guard — WS-5c) — ✓verified (re-confirmed 2026-06-20) — **job-explorer delta line + metrics plot lack aria-live; Stream/Reset expose no running state**. The "priority access saves …" delta paragraph is plain (no `role`/`aria-live`), so its recomputed values are silent; the metrics plot has a descriptive `aria-label` (`plotAria`) but it is not a live region, so streamed iterations are not announced, and Stream/Reset carry no `aria-pressed`/`aria-busy` running state. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`job-explorer.tsx:484-493` — delta paragraph, no aria-live · `metrics-explorer.tsx:209-215` — plotAria is a label, not a live region) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Performance]** SKIPPED 2026-06-21 (the linePath/visible geometry genuinely depends on shown, which changes every tick, so a memo keyed on shown still recomputes each tick; the shown-independent parts are a few arithmetic ops = Low) — ✓verified (re-confirmed 2026-06-20) — **Metrics stream loop re-renders the whole widget per tick**. `shown` advances by setState each stream tick, and `sx`/`sy`/`linePath`/plot geometry are recomputed in the render body (not memoized), so the entire SVG regenerates every frame. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`metrics-explorer.tsx:193-200` — geometry in render body driven by `shown`) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** DONE 2026-06-21 (ErrorCard in WS-2a #36; clamp/clampInt/readNumber/numberOr now single-sourced in parse-utils.ts — WS-5d) — ✓verified (re-confirmed 2026-06-20) — **Four near-identical parse/clamp/ErrorCard scaffolds across qjob/qparam/qcheckpoint/qmetrics**. Each hybrid widget defines its own `clamp`/`clampInt` + `ErrorCard` + `readNumber` parse preamble; `lastCheckpoint` is already owned by `hybrid.ts`. _Fix:_ extract a shared hybrid parse/ErrorCard module. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (`param-compile-explorer.tsx:40` clamp · `checkpoint-explorer.tsx:81-85` clampInt + `:92` ErrorCard · `hybrid.ts:111-114` wastedWithCheckpoint owns lastCheckpoint) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[UI]** DONE 2026-06-21 (WS-5c: failX clamped to TIMELINE_W-1; onStream guards re-click while streaming) — ✓verified (re-confirmed 2026-06-20) — **Checkpoint timeline end-edge markers clip; Stream re-click restarts silently**. `failX = failAt*cellW` places the FAILURE/final-tick marker at the right edge where a centered marker clips to half width; clicking Stream while streaming restarts the run with no guard. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`checkpoint-explorer.tsx:125-126` cellW/failX · `metrics-explorer.tsx:370-375` onStream) — added 2026-06-17

> **2026-07-19 Category B re-audit** — 43 adversarially-verified findings (independent skeptic per claim, default-refute; correctness claims re-derived from first principles, one by porting the shipped code to a measurement harness).

- [x] **[UI]** qparam explorer collapses at 375px: slider readouts fully clipped, time bars shrink to 45px — At a 375px viewport the lesson column gives the card 343px (311px inner). Every TimeBar/SliderRow keeps a fixed w-40 (160px) label and shrink-0 w-20 (80px) value column in a single flex row. The range inputs refuse to shrink below their 129px intrinsic width, pushing the three slider value readouts ('50 iter', '8.0 s', '2.0 s') to x=346..426 — entirely past the card's right edge at 359 — where the WidgetCard's overflow-hidden clips them invisibly. The two 'to-scale' comparison SVG bars absorb the squeeze instead and render 45px wide (vs the 220px design width), reducing the widget's central teaching visual to a sliver. The other three F11 widgets measured clean at 375px (qjob stacks via labelAbove; qcheckpoint uses w-28 labels + w-10 values). _Fix:_ Adopt the layout the sibling qjob widget already uses at narrow widths: stack label above the control (LabeledSlider's existing labelAbove prop) below sm:, or replace the fixed w-40/w-20 columns with wrappable ones (e.g. label w-28 + flex-wrap, matching checkpoint-explorer's proven-at-375px geometry). Same for the TimeBar rows so the SVG keeps a usable minimum width. Pure re-layout with existing primitives; no new functionality. Impact: High. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/quantum/param-compile-explorer.tsx:146`, `web/src/components/quantum/param-compile-explorer.tsx:169-171`, `web/src/components/quantum/param-compile-explorer.tsx:104,113`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:241`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** qmetrics' Stream state machine and timer lifecycle — the only timer in F11 — have zero test coverage — metrics-explorer is the only widget in this feature that owns a timer and a multi-state machine (ready -&gt; running -&gt; met/stopped), and two prior audit rounds shipped fixes into exactly that code (the re-click guard and the unmount timer cleanup). No test in the suite ever clicks the Stream button, so none of those fixes are locked: a regression that restarts the run on re-click, leaks the setTimeout chain past unmount, or breaks the reduced-motion instant-reveal branch would ship green. The file's only interaction-adjacent assertion checks that the button is *enabled* at idle. By contrast the sibling checkpoint explorer does fire a slider change and assert the memo/render result, so the least-covered widget here is the one with the most stateful surface. _Fix:_ Add jest fake-timer cases to metrics-explorer.test.tsx following the existing house idiom: (a) click Stream, advance timers, assert the metric-line path (data-testid="metric-line" already exists at :321) grows and the chip goes running -&gt; met; (b) click Stream twice in a row and assert `shown` does not rewind (the guard); (c) unmount mid-stream and assert jest.getTimerCount() is 0; (d) with mockMatchMedia(true), click Stream and assert the full curve is revealed synchronously with no pending timer. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/__tests__/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.test.tsx:52-56`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:171`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:120-127`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:173-176`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** Every F11 slider is a 4px-tall input with a 16px thumb — under the WCAG 2.5.8 24x24 floor, contradicting LabeledSlider's own documented contract — The eight range sliders that are the primary control surface of qjob/qparam/qcheckpoint all render through `LabeledSlider`, whose `.slider` recipe sets the input's box height to 4px and paints a 16px thumb that overflows it. Even taking the most generous hit-testing assumption (the overflowing thumb is grabbable), the drag target is 16px tall; the reliable target — the input's own layout box — is 4px. The rows use `flex items-center` with no vertical padding, so nothing enlarges it. On touch this makes the hybrid-jobs widgets materially harder to operate than the numbers they display suggest, and it is the same control on every explorable in the module. _Fix:_ Keep the hairline look but give the input a real target: set `.slider` to `height: 24px; background: transparent;` and move the track paint to `::-webkit-slider-runnable-track` / `::-moz-range-track` (`height: 4px; background: var(--track); border-radius: 9999px;`), with `margin-top: -6px` on the webkit thumb to re-center it. Visual result is identical; the pointer/touch target becomes 24px. Then either make widget-ui.tsx:287's claim true or amend it. Note this is a cross-cutting change in globals.css — it lifts every explorable, so it should be verified against the F6-F10 widgets too. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. - [x] **[Correctness]** Rigetti is a device choice in the qcost calculator and the platform's superconducting price anchor, but is absent from the qdevices catalog table — The cost calculator builds its Device dropdown from Object.keys(PRICING), so learners can price runs on "Rigetti" ($0.30/task + $0.000425/shot, Cepheus) — and the hybrid-jobs explorer and the live /pricing page both present Rigetti as a first-class backend (the pricing FAQ even teaches the ~180x IonQ-vs-Rigetti per-shot ratio, which is exactly 0.08/0.000425). Yet the qdevices table — the catalog artifact of the same F7 feature — has no Rigetti row, so a learner studying the table concludes superconducting on Braket means only IQM Garnet, then meets Rigetti as an unexplained option one widget below. The lib-side scoping (cost.py: "Rigetti is reference pricing only — not dispatchable via lib.hardware.DEVICES") explains the lambda allowlist, not the teaching catalog: the table already includes non-dispatchable entries (Local, TN1's analog of reference data). The in-repo July-2026 sheet has the facts a row needs (Cepheus-1-108Q, superconducting, 108 qubits). _Fix:_ Add one data row to DEVICES: { technology: "Superconducting", vendor: "Rigetti", model: "Cepheus", qubits: 108, connectivity: "Square lattice", gateModel: true, provider: "Rigetti" } — qubit count and technology sourced from the repo's own pricing.ts sheet (verify connectivity wording against Rigetti's Ankaa-lattice docs before landing). The cost cell then derives automatically via costLabel("Rigetti"), making the FAQ's 180x superconducting-vs-trapped-ion ratio visible in the table learners actually study. If the team instead decides the table must mirror only lib.hardware.DEVICES, the alternative refine is a one-line note in devices.ts documenting that scoping so the omission stops reading as an oversight — but the row is the better teach. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. That turns the currently comment-only knowledge into a checked contract, so the next Braket catalog refresh is a one-file edit that fails loudly when the sibling catalog lags. Impact: Medium. - [x] **[Accessibility]** Metrics explorer Stream button disables itself while focused, ejecting keyboard focus to body — Clicking/activating Stream sets streaming=true, which renders disabled={streaming} on the very button that has focus. The browser drops focus to &lt;body&gt; for the ~2.4s stream and never restores it when the button re-enables, so a keyboard or screen-reader user's next Tab restarts from the top of the document. The disabled attribute is purely redundant as a guard: onStream already ignores re-clicks while a timer is in flight (the WS-3 re-click guard), so disabling adds no protection — only the focus loss. _Fix:_ Drop disabled={streaming} and keep the existing timerRef re-click guard as the sole protection; retain aria-busy={streaming} and the 'Streaming…' label, optionally adding aria-disabled='true' while streaming so AT still perceives the unavailable state. Focus then stays on the button throughout. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. - [x] **[Correctness]** job-explorer's formatDuration renders impossible times like "1h 60m" — the rounded minute never carries into the hour — web/src/components/quantum/job-explorer.tsx:127 computes `const m = Math.round((s % 3600) / 60)` in the hours branch, so any wall-clock whose remainder past the hour lands in [3570s, 3599s] rounds the minutes to 60 without carrying: formatDuration(7199) returns "1h 60m" instead of "2h" (verified by executing the function: 7170, 7185, and 7199 all yield "1h 60m"). This is reachable from the widget's own sliders — e.g. iterations=10, queue wait=600s, compute=119.9s gives standaloneWall = 7199s, and iterations=120, queue=0, compute=59.8s gives 7176s. The impossible reading appears in the widget's central wall-clock readout (SubBar valueText, line 187) and is spoken three times to screen readers via the SubBar aria-label (line 189), the CompareBar sr-only summary (line 169), and headerAria (line 309). _Fix:_ Carry the rounded minute: after `const m = Math.round((s % 3600) / 60)`, if m === 60 return `${h + 1}h`; (or compute m with Math.floor and append the remainder like the sub-hour branch already does at lines 122-124). Add a boundary case to the widget's test file pinning formatDuration-adjacent output (e.g. render with a config that yields 7199s and assert the bar readout says "2h"). Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. - [x] **[Correctness]** Metrics explorer teaches that Braket's stopping_condition is a metric threshold — the real API field is only a max-runtime cap — The widget's entire framing device labels its energy threshold `stopping_condition`: the dashed line's in-SVG label, the header chip that renders 'stopping_condition met', the plot aria, and the caption ('the dashed line is the job's stopping_condition'). The GUIDE doubles down: 'Control spend with max_runtime to cap duration, a stopping_condition to halt on a metric'. But per the current Braket API reference, JobStoppingCondition contains exactly one member — maxRuntimeInSeconds — a wall-clock cap. There is no metric-threshold stopping condition in Braket Hybrid Jobs; halting on a converged metric is user code inside the algorithm script. A learner who absorbs this widget will try to pass an energy threshold to stopping_condition on a real job and fail — and the platform contradicts itself, because the same module's notebook teaches it correctly ('stopping_condition caps wall-clock as a hard backstop to the tol check', stopping_condition={"maxRuntimeInSeconds": 600}). The 'stopped' phase label compounds it slightly: the run completed all 40 iterations, nothing stopped it. _Fix:_ Reword, don't rebuild: rename the threshold label to 'convergence tol' (or 'target') in the SVG text, chip ('tol met'), plotAria, streamStatus, and caption, and have the caption state the correct split the notebook already teaches — 'your script checks this tol each iteration and returns early; stopping_condition (maxRuntimeInSeconds) is the runtime backstop'. Rename phase 'stopped' to 'completed'. Fix the two GUIDE sentences (lines 125 and 179) to match notebook 03's framing. No test asserts the current label (verified by grep), so the change is copy-only. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. - [x] **[Correctness]** qmetrics labels an energy threshold with Braket's `stopping_condition` API name — Braket's stopping_condition only accepts maxRuntimeInSeconds — The metrics explorer draws a dashed horizontal line at an *energy* value (hartree) and labels it with the literal snake_case Braket API identifier `stopping_condition`, in four places: the SVG text label, the phase chip ("stopping_condition met"), the screen-reader stream status, and the caption ("the dashed line is the job's stopping_condition"). Amazon Braket Hybrid Jobs' `stopping_condition` parameter accepts only `{"maxRuntimeInSeconds": N}` — a wall-clock cap. It has no metric-threshold form. A learner who takes the widget at face value will try to configure a metric-valued stopping_condition and fail; the correct mechanism is an in-script convergence `tol` check, which is exactly what the accompanying notebook's entry point actually does. The repo's own notebook teaches the right shape, so the widget contradicts the lesson it illustrates. _Fix:_ Rename the line to what it actually is — a convergence tolerance / target energy (e.g. label it `tol` target or "convergence threshold") — and change the phase chip and stream status to "converged" / "not converged". Then in the caption, keep the Braket tie-in but state it correctly: the in-script `tol` check halts the loop, and `stopping_condition` (`maxRuntimeInSeconds`) is the separate wall-clock backstop. Update GUIDE.md:125 to match so the fence prose and the widget agree. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. - [x] **[Correctness]** qcheckpoint asserts a false quantitative relationship between the 49-point H2 fixture and the iteration count — The checkpoint explorer renders a "49 bond lengths" chip next to the "N iters" chip and states in prose that the sweep "over the H2 curve (49 bond lengths) runs as {iterations} job iterations". The bond-length count is read from the shared H2 fixture (49 points, fixed) while `iterations` is fence-configurable from 2 to 120 and defaults to 40. The two numbers have no mapping — 49 bond lengths cannot "run as" 40 iterations under any natural reading — and they will disagree for essentially every configuration. Worse, `bondLengths` appears nowhere in the checkpointing math (which depends only on failAt and every), so the widget places an inert number in the position a learner reads as a driver, inviting them to look for a relationship that does not exist. The rest of this widget is unusually precise (the timeline geometry, the failure marker at the left edge of cell failAt, and the shaded redo span all agree exactly with wastedWithCheckpoint), which makes the one incoherent claim stand out. _Fix:_ Drop the bond-lengths chip and rewrite the clause so it stops equating the two counts — e.g. "A long VQE sweep over the H2 dissociation curve runs as {iterations} job iterations." That keeps the module-05 tie-in as narrative colour without asserting an arithmetic relationship the model does not implement. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. But the widgets that hand-roll their card shell also hand-roll the eyebrow span, and every one of those copies uses `font-semibold` instead of the shared `font-medium`. This is not a design decision recorded anywhere — the classes are otherwise character-for-character the shared string. _Fix:_ Pick one weight (font-semibold is the 14-site majority and reads better at 10px/0.2em tracking) and one dark color, set it in EyebrowLabel, then replace the 14 hand-rolled spans with `<EyebrowLabel>`. - [x] **[UX]** job-explorer's 60s container startup is invisible in the UI, so the legitimate 'hybrid is slower' state reads as a broken widget — `STARTUP_SEC = 60` is a hard-coded constant folded into the Hybrid Job wall-clock but never named anywhere a reader can see it — not in a chip, not in the card note, not in the honesty paragraph, not in the sr-only summaries. Drag the 'queue wait / task' slider to its minimum (0s, a completely natural learner move: "what if there's no queue?") and hybrid wall-clock is 60 + n·iterSec while standalone is n·iterSec, so the Hybrid Job bar renders visibly LONGER at every iteration count — while the delta sentence directly underneath reads "priority access saves 0s" because the saving is floored at zero. That turns a self-contradicting state into the module's actual lesson (priority access only pays off once the queue you avoid exceeds the startup you pay) without adding any new control or panel. Latent in production (lesson fences never change source at runtime) but it is a genuine dead end in the state machine the moment anything re-renders the widget with a different source (dev hot-reload does today; any future live-editable fence would). _Fix:_ In the same cleanup (or an effect on run identity), also `setStreaming(false)` and `setShown(0)` so a new run always starts from the 'ready' state. Two lines, matches the existing Reset semantics. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. - [x] **[Consistency]** checkpoint-explorer hand-rolls the numeric-field reads its two siblings take from parse-utils readNumber — qjob and qparam read fence numbers via readNumber (typed finite-number error + clamp), but qcheckpoint re-implements three per-key typeof-'number' blocks and clamps afterward. Widget suites exercise sliders indirectly (10 getByRole("slider") calls, 14 aria-valuetext assertions), yet those queries resolve via aria-label, not the &lt;label htmlFor&gt; association — so the useId htmlFor↔id wiring LabeledSlider was extracted to centralize could silently break (visible label no longer focuses/names the input) while every existing test stays green. Pure jsdom/RTL like the file's existing describes — no new dependencies. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. - [x] **[Resilience & tests]** checkpoint-explorer's "memo integrity" test is vacuous — the rect count is 40 with or without the memo it claims to lock — web/__tests__/components/quantum/checkpoint-explorer.test.tsx:59-74 ("preserves base-track rect count after slider interaction (memo integrity)") fires a failAt slider change and asserts each timeline still contains 40 base-cell rects. But baseCells at checkpoint-explorer.tsx:108-114 is `Array.from({ length: iterations }, ...)` memoized on [iterations, cellW] — the count is `iterations` whether or not the useMemo exists, and the slider being changed (failAt) is not even a dependency of that memo. Deleting the useMemo entirely, or breaking its dependency array, leaves the test green. It documents a regression lock it cannot enforce, which is exactly the false-confidence failure mode for the one DOM-structure test this widget has. _Fix:_ Make the test assert something the memo actually controls or rename it to what it really pins. Options within the stack: assert DOM-node identity across the re-render (capture the first rect element before the slider change and expect the same reference after — React reconciliation plus the memo keeps the nodes stable), or drop the misleading "(memo integrity)" claim and re-title it as the structural lock it is ("renders one base cell per iteration after interaction"). Either way the suite stops advertising coverage it does not have. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/__tests__/components/quantum/checkpoint-explorer.test.tsx:59-74`, `web/src/components/quantum/checkpoint-explorer.tsx:108-114`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Consistency]** qjob hardcodes the per-shot provider list that cost.ts already knows how to derive — job-explorer.tsx maintains its own literal list of per-shot QPU providers, duplicating knowledge that lives in cost.ts PRICING (the declared single source of truth mirroring lib/utils/cost.py). cost.ts exports isPerShot() for exactly this discrimination, and the sibling cost widget already derives its list from PRICING keys instead of hardcoding. PRICING demonstrably churns (its own comment records IonQ Aria retiring and being replaced by Forte, plus the Rigetti Cepheus row): the next provider add/retire updates PRICING but silently leaves qjob's select options and its 'provider must be one of IonQ, IQM, QuEra, Rigetti' parse error stale. _Fix:_ Replace the literal with a derivation: `const PROVIDERS = (Object.keys(PRICING) as Provider[]).filter(isPerShot);` (import isPerShot from ./cost). Object-literal key order yields the identical IonQ, IQM, QuEra, Rigetti sequence today, so the select options, chip text, and parse-error string are byte-identical now — but they can never again go stale against the pricing table. No test changes needed (job-explorer.test.tsx only pins 'IonQ' and the error-card prefix). Impact: Low. Effort: Low. A screen-reader user cannot tell the rewind happened; the only feedback is the visual phase chip flipping back to "ready", which is not in a live region. Resetting mid-stream is equally silent even though it cancels an announced in-flight stream. _Fix:_ Give the reset branch a concise announcement (e.g. set streamStatus to "Reset; not started." when the user has just reset), keeping the empty string only for the initial mount so first render stays silent — consistent with the LiveStatus discipline of one polite line per user action. Match the sibling widgets, which announce each figure once. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. - [x] **[Resilience & tests]** Latent dead end: a mid-stream run change strands the widget in a permanent disabled 'Streaming…' state — The cleanup effect keyed on [run] clears the pending timer when the run changes or the component unmounts, but it never resets the `streaming` React state. If `source` (and therefore `run`) changes while a stream is in flight, the timer chain dies but `streaming` stays true, so the Stream button remains disabled with the 'Streaming…' label and the status region stays stuck on 'Streaming N iterations.' forever — a state-machine dead end with no exit except Reset. It is unreachable in production today only because widget-fence passes a static fence body per mount, but any future rerender-with-new-source (or a test rerender) hits it. Adjacent hygiene: the stopStream() call on line 172 is dead code — it sits immediately after the line-171 guard that guarantees timerRef.current is already null. _Fix:_ In the same cleanup, also reset the interaction state (setStreaming(false); setShown(0)) so a run swap lands back at 'ready' — setState in an unmount cleanup is a safe no-op in React 19. Delete the dead stopStream() call on line 172. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:120-127`, `web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:394-401`, `web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:171-172`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)

That is the exact copy-paste WidgetCard was extracted to eliminate, and it forks the two widgets off the shared shell: they cannot pick up header changes (spacing, border token, the `eyebrowAs="h3"` heading-semantics option checkpoint-explorer uses), and they each carry a now-unnecessary `EyebrowLabel` import. Of ~22 widgets in quantum/, only three use `header=` and two of them are these. _Fix:_ Convert both to `eyebrow=` + `chips=`: job-explorer becomes `eyebrow="Standalone vs Hybrid Job" chips={<><Chip>{iterations} iter</Chip><Chip>{provider}</Chip></>}`; metrics-explorer passes its two Chips plus the phase pill as a fragment to `chips`. Drop the now-unused `EyebrowLabel` import from both. The rendered DOM is unchanged (the class strings are identical), so the existing header assertions in job-explorer.test.tsx and metrics-explorer.test.tsx keep passing. cost-calculator.tsx:74-79 is the third instance of the same duplication and can be swept in the same pass. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. - [x] **[Consistency]** qcheckpoint hand-rolls JSON field validation and is the only F11 widget that accepts a non-finite config value — parse-utils.readNumber exists specifically to single-source the fence-config contract: missing -&gt; fallback, present-but-not-finite -&gt; the typed error `"<key>" must be a finite number`, otherwise clamp. checkpoint-explorer ignores it and hand-writes three near-identical `typeof obj[k] !== "number"` blocks whose check is strictly weaker — `typeof` alone passes Infinity, which JSON.parse produces for a literal like `1e999`. The value then falls through to clampInt and is silently coerced to a plausible number. So the same malformed fence body renders an explicit error card in qjob and a silently-wrong-but-plausible timeline in qcheckpoint, in the same lesson. _Fix:_ Replace lines 48-65 with three `readNumber(obj, key, DEFAULTS.key, lo, hi)` calls (wide static bounds: iterations 2..120, failAt 0..120, every 1..120), propagating `.error` the way parseSource already propagates parseJsonObject's. Keep the existing clampInt lines 70-71 for failAt/every since those bounds are *derived* from the resolved iterations and readNumber cannot express that dependency. Net: ~18 lines become 6, the error message wording is single-sourced, and non-finite input now fails loudly in all four F11 widgets. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. Leave 122 (`fill-gray-100 dark:fill-gray-800`) alone or move it to `fill-(--track)` only if the track token reads correctly as an SVG fill — that is the one site where the token is not a drop-in. - [x] **[Cleaner code]** qcheckpoint re-implements clamp with raw Math.min/Math.max although parse-utils is already imported in the file — Two lines derive the render-time clamped values with nested Math.max/Math.min, which is exactly what `clamp(v, lo, hi)` does. The file already imports from ./parse-utils on line 8, and parse-utils re-exports `clamp` (from math.ts) next to the `clampInt` it does import — so this is a reuse miss of a helper that is one identifier away, in a module whose header docstring explicitly says clamp "lived in two files" and was single-sourced to stop exactly this. _Fix:_ Add `clamp` to the line-8 import and write `const clampedFail = clamp(failAt, 0, iterations);` / `const clampedEvery = clamp(every, 1, iterations);`. Behavior is identical (both inputs are already integers from integer-step sliders). Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/checkpoint-explorer.tsx:192-193`, `web/src/components/quantum/checkpoint-explorer.tsx:8`, `web/src/components/quantum/parse-utils.ts:8-9`, `web/src/components/quantum/parse-utils.ts:1-6`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** qmetrics has an unreachable error branch and a user-facing message that can never render — The post-hooks guard tests three conditions and prints a distinct "no run" message when parsing succeeded but a derived memo is null. That state is unreachable: `run` returns null only when `!parsed.ok`, and `geom` returns null only when `!run`. So whenever `parsed.ok` is true both are non-null, and the `"no run"` string is dead — it exists as a TypeScript narrowing artifact, but reads as a real failure mode a maintainer must reason about (and would surface as an unexplained "qmetrics error: no run" card if the invariant ever changed silently). _Fix:_ Collapse to `if (!parsed.ok) return <ErrorCard message={parsed.error} />;` and satisfy the compiler on the two memos by destructuring them after that guard with a single non-null assertion (or by inverting: keep `!run || !geom` in the condition but drop the ternary and always render `parsed.error`, since that branch is only entered when parsing failed). Either way the phantom "no run" string goes away. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:153-155`, `web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:107-108`, `web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:132-133`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Correctness]** qjob's honesty note vouches for the instance rates as "real" and hides the 60s startup constant that sets the break-even — The footer note qualifies exactly one input as illustrative (the queue wait) and then affirms "The cost rates are real Braket per-task / per-shot and SageMaker hourly rates." Two problems. First, hybrid.ts's own docstring calls the ML-instance hourly table "representative ... Illustrative — check current AWS pricing for exact figures", and Braket Hybrid Jobs bills classical instances at Braket's published rates, not raw SageMaker on-demand — so the widget upgrades its own source's hedge into an assertion of fact. Second, STARTUP_SEC is a hard-coded 60s that never appears in the UI, has no slider, and is not mentioned in the note, yet it is one of only two terms in the hybrid wall-clock model and therefore fixes where the standalone-vs-hybrid break-even lands (at the fence defaults, hybrid wins from n≈2). The GUIDE explicitly asks the learner to trust that break-even ("for a handful of quick iterations, the standalone path is cheaper and simpler"), so an invisible, unqualified constant is doing load-bearing pedagogical work while the note implies everything except the queue wait is authoritative. _Fix:_ Split the note's claim: keep "real" for the Braket per-task / per-shot rates (those are genuinely single-sourced from cost.ts and verified), and downgrade the instance hourly rates to "representative" to match hybrid.ts's docstring. Add the startup assumption to the same sentence — "a one-time 60s container startup is assumed" — so the break-even the widget demonstrates is attributable to a stated input rather than an invisible constant. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/hybrid.ts:17-20`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/job-explorer.tsx:473-475`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/job-explorer.tsx:35`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/components/quantum/job-explorer.tsx:283`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)

Retuning a token in globals.css will move three of the four widgets and leave qparam behind. _Fix:_ Sweep param-compile-explorer's six text sites onto `text-(--ink)` / `text-(--mut)` / `text-caption` per tier and its rect onto `--track` (via `fill-[var(--track)]`), and swap job-explorer.tsx:242's track to `bg-(--track)`. Mechanical, no behavior change, and it puts the whole F11 set on one source of truth so future token retunes move all four together. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/param-compile-explorer.tsx:104,135,146,241,246,292`, `web/src/components/quantum/param-compile-explorer.tsx:122`, `web/src/components/quantum/checkpoint-explorer.tsx:231,241,307,310`, `web/src/components/quantum/job-explorer.tsx:242`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[UX]** metrics-explorer's status chip renders the raw literal 'stopped' for the completed-but-threshold-not-met state — The phase chip is the widget's primary status affordance, and three of its four states render the internal enum value verbatim. The "stopped" state is only ever reachable after a stream has run to completion without the energy crossing the threshold — there is no reachable mid-stream pause (Stream is disabled while streaming and Reset zeroes `shown` back to "ready"), so "stopped" never means what the word implies. A learner who set an unreachable threshold sees a chip saying the job stopped, when in fact all 40 iterations completed and the stopping_condition simply was not met. The chip's sibling state spells its meaning out in full ("stopping_condition met"), so the asymmetry is the tell. _Fix:_ Render the terminal state as `stopping_condition not met` to mirror the `met` label, keeping `running`/`ready` as-is (those read correctly as bare words). One expression change at line 243; the sr-only `streamStatus` already says the same thing, so the visible chip and the announcement finally agree. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:211`, `web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:243`, `web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:195-199`, `web/src/components/quantum/metrics-explorer.tsx:213-217`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[UI]** qjob's headline cost delta is formatted to 2 decimals, so "the managed instance adds" reads $0.00 across a wide, teaching-critical part of the slider range — job-explorer.tsx:131-133 defines `function formatUsd(v: number): string { return \`$${v.toFixed(2)}\`; }` and :460-463 renders the delta sentence's second bold number with it: "the managed instance adds **{formatUsd(result.addedCost)}**". `addedCost` is exactly `instanceCost(instance, hybridWall)` (the QPU term cancels), and for the default ml.m5.large at $0.115/hour that requires 156 s of wall-clock before it reaches one displayed cent. Drag iterations to 1 with the GUIDE's own defaults and hybridWall = STARTUP_SEC + 1 x 6 s = 66 s, giving $0.0021 — rendered as "$0.00". Combined with `savedWall = Math.max(0, standaloneWall - hybridWall)` (:286) also flooring to 0 in that regime, the widget's entire teaching sentence becomes "priority access saves 0s of wall-clock; the managed instance adds $0.00" — asserting the two paths are identical precisely where the GUIDE prose (06-hybrid-jobs/GUIDE.md:31) says "for a handful of quick iterations, the standalone path is cheaper and simpler". The sibling cost widget already solved this: cost-calculator.tsx:58-63 itemizes every USD line at `toFixed(4)` (and rates at `toFixed(5)`) precisely because Braket per-unit charges are sub-cent, reserving 2-decimal `$X.XX` for the bold total only. _Fix:_ Give the delta readout sub-cent resolution the way qcost already does — format `addedCost` (and the per-panel `total cost` values, which share formatUsd) with enough significant digits that a nonzero charge is never shown as $0.00, e.g. switch to 4 decimals below $0.01 while keeping 2 decimals for larger totals. Nothing about the model changes; only the rendering stops asserting a zero that the arithmetic never produced. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/job-explorer.tsx:131-133`, `web/src/components/quantum/job-explorer.tsx:460-463`, `web/src/components/quantum/job-explorer.tsx:35`, `web/src/components/quantum/cost-calculator.tsx:58-63`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** qcheckpoint's "memo integrity" test is vacuous — it asserts an invariant that holds identically with or without the memo it claims to guard — checkpoint-explorer.test.tsx:59-74 is titled "preserves base-track rect count after slider interaction (memo integrity)". It fires a change on the "fail at" slider and then asserts each timeline SVG still contains 40 base-track rects. But the memo it purports to lock is `baseCells = useMemo(..., [iterations, cellW])` (checkpoint-explorer.tsx:108-114), whose only inputs are `iterations` and `cellW` — neither of which the "fail at" slider touches. The rendered rect count is `Array.from({ length: iterations })`, so it is 40 whether the useMemo exists, is given the wrong dependency array, or is deleted outright. Deleting `useMemo` from line 108 leaves this test green. The one property that would actually distinguish a memoized from a non-memoized cell array — referential stability of the rect elements across a failAt-only re-render — is never observed. This is the only test in the F11 suite that claims to protect a performance property, so the protection is illusory. _Fix:_ Make the assertion depend on the memo. Capture a reference to a specific base-track `<rect>` node before the slider change and assert the same DOM node instance is still mounted afterwards (React reuses the element only when the memoized child array is referentially stable), or drop the misleading "memo integrity" framing and retitle it as the render-shape smoke test it actually is so no one trusts it as perf protection. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/__tests__/components/quantum/checkpoint-explorer.test.tsx:59-74`, `web/src/components/quantum/checkpoint-explorer.tsx:108-114`, `web/src/components/quantum/checkpoint-explorer.tsx:244-252`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)

### 12. Shared widget engine (gap-fill 2026-06-17 — 8 verified · re-confirmed 2026-06-20)

- [x] **[Correctness]** DONE (PR pending) — Dirac readout showed phantom `0.00|…⟩` terms (filter/format threshold mismatch). FIXED: one shared `DISPLAY_EPS` drives both the visibility filter and the per-component snap, so a shown term can never round to zero. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`state-readout.ts:20`) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Correctness]** DONE (PR pending) — RX/RY/RZ rotation matrices were not pinned to the qcsim fixtures. FIXED: generated `rotations` fixtures from qcsim `_rx/_ry/_rz` at 6 angles each; `math.test.ts` now asserts parity to 1e-10 (18 new cases — all pass, so the matrices were correct, just unverified). Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`math.ts:1-10,42-56`) — added 2026-06-17

> **Re-confirmed 2026-06-20** by direct source review. CORRECTION: the part-1 workflow's F6 pass *incorrectly refuted* the useWebGL per-render allocation; direct review confirms it is REAL — `useSyncExternalStore` calls getSnapshot on every render, and `detectWebGL` allocates a canvas + GL context each call.

- [x] **[Performance]** DONE 2026-06-20 (WS-1) — ✓verified (corrected 2026-06-20) — **useWebGL allocates a canvas + WebGL context on every render of every consumer**. useWebGL's getSnapshot is `detectWebGL`, which does `document.createElement("canvas")` + `getContext("webgl2")||getContext("webgl")` each call; React invokes getSnapshot on every render (not only on store change), and the consumers — bloch-builder-widget, wavefunction-scrubber, scrolly-section — re-render on slider drag / scrub-play / scroll rAF, so a fresh GL context is created and discarded many times per second during interaction (browsers also cap live GL contexts, risking context-loss churn). _Fix:_ memoize WebGL detection at module scope (compute once; return the cached boolean from both subscribe and getSnapshot). The sibling `usePrefersReducedMotion` has the same per-call `matchMedia` allocation (see Feature 6). Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`use-display-caps.ts:20-27` detectWebGL allocates canvas+GL · `:29-36` useWebGL passes detectWebGL as getSnapshot with a no-op subscribe · callers bloch-builder-widget.tsx / wavefunction-scrubber.tsx / scrolly-section.tsx all re-render hot) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Architecture & consistency]** DONE 2026-06-21 (gate set in WS-2 tail #39; ParsedGate.angle renamed to theta so the parsed and runtime ops share one field — WS-5d) — ✓verified (re-confirmed 2026-06-20) — **qsim gate set + op types are double-sourced**. The gate name set lives twice — `math.ts` NAMED_GATES (I,X,Y,Z,H,S,T):60 + the RX/RY/RZ branch in applyOp:131, vs `qsim-dsl.ts` SINGLE:34 / ROT:35 — and the parsed vs runtime op shapes diverge (`ParsedGate.angle` qsim-dsl.ts:23 vs `Op.theta` math.ts:121, bridged by `opsFor`). BlochBuilder hand-rolls its state via `bloch-builder.ts` `stateFromAngles` instead of the math.ts kernel (`zeroState`/`applyGate1`/`probabilities`); `noise.ts` `opMatrix` re-implements the same gate dispatch (see Feature 7). _Fix:_ export one gate table + a shared op/type from math.ts and reuse it in qsim-dsl.ts, noise.ts, and bloch-builder. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low-Medium. (`math.ts:60,131` vs `qsim-dsl.ts:34-35` · `ParsedGate.angle` qsim-dsl.ts:23 vs `Op.theta` math.ts:121 · `bloch-builder.ts:4-17` hand-rolled state) — added 2026-06-17

_New (2026-06-25 deep audit):_
- [x] **[UI · correctness]** DONE 2026-06-25 (WS-6a, PR #60 merged) — **`diracString` prints "+ -0.71" for negative amplitudes** — it joins every term with the literal `"  +  "` with no per-term sign handling, while `formatAmplitude` can return a leading `-`. This is the headline teaching output of every circuit-family explorable (`StateReadout`) and hits very common states: H then Z gives `0.71|0⟩  +  -0.71|1⟩`, and |Φ⁻⟩ shows the same artifact. Make `diracString` sign-aware (emit magnitude, assemble with `" - "`/`" + "`; first term keeps a bare leading minus). Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`state-readout.ts:17-19,27`; consumed by `widget-ui.tsx:87`) — added 2026-06-25
- [x] **[Resilience]** DONE 2026-06-26 (WS-6f, #63 merged) — **The qsim parser accepts a degenerate CNOT with `control == target`, silently producing identity** rather than rejecting it (the #51 index-validation work didn't cover the equal-index case). Reject `control == target` with a parse error. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`qsim-dsl.ts`) — added 2026-06-25
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** DONE 2026-06-26 (WS-6h, #67 merged) — `parse-utils.ts` re-exports `clamp` from `math.ts` (single source); `widget-fence.tsx` collapsed 30 `dynamic()` calls into a `lazyWidget` factory. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`math.ts`, `parse-utils.ts`, `widget-fence.tsx`) — added 2026-06-25
- [x] **[UI]** DONE 2026-06-26 (WS-6g, #64 merged) — **One fixed 192px skeleton can't reserve space for ~30 differently-sized widgets**, contributing to load-time layout shift (cf. the Feature 6 dial→sphere CLS); give the fence per-widget min-heights. Also: the 9px "py" badge is the only sub-10px text in the set. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (`widget-fence.tsx`, `state-readout.ts`) — added 2026-06-25
- [x] **[Accessibility]** DONE 2026-07-03 (WS-B1, PR #90 — GateChip `aria-current="step"` when active, attribute dropped otherwise; py badge `aria-hidden` — note the badge renders in widget-ui.tsx's StateReadout, the `state-readout.ts` citation was stale) — The active gate chip is conveyed by color only with no programmatic "current" state (add `aria-current`); and the decorative "py" badge in `StateReadout` is exposed to AT, duplicating the copy button's label (mark it `aria-hidden`). Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`widget-ui.tsx`, `state-readout.ts`) — added 2026-06-25
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** DONE 2026-07-04 (WS-B3, PR #92 — direct readNumber/parseIndex/parseAngle describes on the @/ alias; dead `numberOr` deleted rather than tested; new rng.test.ts pins the gauss 1e-12 guard; pasted mulberry32 copies in shots/correlation tests replaced by the rng.ts import. clamp/clampInt/parseJsonObject were already covered by WS-6h #67) — `parse-utils.ts`'s validation core has zero direct tests and its reachable non-finite branch is unexercised; `rng.ts`'s `gauss` `log(0)` guard is likewise untested. Add direct unit tests. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`parse-utils.ts`, `rng.ts`) — added 2026-06-25


> **2026-07-19 Category B re-audit** — 35 adversarially-verified findings (independent skeptic per claim, default-refute; correctness claims re-derived from first principles, one by porting the shipped code to a measurement harness).

- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** No error boundary anywhere in the app, so one failed widget chunk destroys the whole lesson page — Every one of the 36 widgets in the registry is mounted through `next/dynamic` (React.lazy under the hood). A rejected import — the realistic production trigger being a stale page held open across an Amplify deploy, since the static export ships content-hashed chunk filenames — rethrows during render. There is no `ErrorBoundary` component, no `componentDidCatch`, no `app/error.tsx` and no `global-error.tsx` anywhere in web/src, so the throw propagates past the whole route and the reader loses the entire lesson (prose included), not just the one widget. The approach gate makes this materially worse rather than better: chunks are fetched lazily on scroll (rootMargin 400px), so the stale-chunk fetch window is the learner's entire reading session rather than the first second of page load. The same unguarded path also catches render-time throws the kernel deliberately raises — chart-utils.ts:14 `extent()` throws on an empty array and math.ts:190 `gateMatrixFor` throws on an unknown gate, both documented as "caller bug" contracts that assume something upstream catches them. Nothing does. _Fix:_ Wrap the `<entry.Widget />` return in a small class-component error boundary (the only React API for this; no new dependency) that renders the existing shared `ErrorCard` from widget-ui.tsx as its fallback — the widget degrades to the same smoke-and-glass error card the explorables already show for bad fence config, and the lesson keeps rendering. Because the boundary lives at the single registry call site it covers all 36 widgets at once. Add a jest case rendering a WidgetFence over a throwing stub to pin that the surrounding content survives. Impact: High. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/quantum/widget-fence.tsx:55`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-fence.tsx:146`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-fence.tsx:58-97`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-fence.tsx:103,110-128`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The Rep/activity widget family never adopted WidgetCard/EyebrowLabel/ErrorCard - nine files hand-roll the card shell with internal drift — challenge, debug-circuit, predict, quiz, review-card, runnable-editor, device-table, scrolly-section, and qpu-submit-panel each hand-roll the widget shell as 'not-prose my-8 ... rounded-card glass shadow-(--shadow-resting)' plus a bespoke eyebrow span, instead of the shared WidgetCard/EyebrowLabel/cardShell in widget-ui.tsx. The copies have already drifted: the eyebrow is font-semibold with dark:text-accent in six files but dark:text-accent-light in three others (EyebrowLabel is font-medium dark:text-accent), so two Rep widgets on the same lesson page render different dark-mode header colors; the appended shadow-(--shadow-resting) utility is redundant because .glass already sets that exact box-shadow; and six parse-error cards reproduce the shared ErrorCard's '{label} error: {message}' contract by hand - predict-widget even defines a private component literally named ErrorCard, shadowing the shared export. _Fix:_ Adopt the shared primitives: route the six parse-error cards through ErrorCard (className="my-8"), and either render the Rep header via WidgetCard's eyebrow/headerRight slots or, if the heavier font-semibold px-4 py-3 header is the intended activity idiom, add that one variant to widget-ui once and delete the nine copies. Pick a single dark-mode eyebrow color and drop the redundant shadow utility everywhere (it is already inside .glass). Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:150-154`, `web/src/components/quantum/debug-circuit-widget.tsx:168-171`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:177-196`, `web/src/app/globals.css:345-349`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The Rep widgets copy-paste their verdict badge and tone constants, and three of the copies have already drifted apart — Six Rep widgets render the same header verdict badge and three of them define the same styling constants locally. The copies are not staying in sync: `VERDICT_STYLES.error` uses a different border token in the two files that otherwise share the constant verbatim, and `TONE.neutral` uses raw gray utilities in one widget where its sibling uses the `--bd`/`--field`/`--mut` design tokens. These are two widgets a learner meets back to back in the same lesson section, so the divergence is user-visible, and neither divergence is explained by a comment. _Fix:_ Add one `VerdictBadge({ tone, children })` to widget-ui covering the accent/warm/solved cases and use it at the six sites, and lift `VERDICT_STYLES` and `OPTION_BASE`/`TONE` to widget-ui as single constants, resolving --bd vs --bd-2 and the raw-gray vs token neutral in the process. That collapses roughly 60 duplicated lines and removes the drift surface entirely. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:29-36`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:209`, `web/src/components/quantum/cost-estimate-widget.tsx:44-49`, `web/src/components/quantum/cost-estimate-widget.tsx:148-156`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** DJ oracle select still uses the pre-#172 alpha focus ring (focus:ring-accent/40) that the site-wide --focus token sweep replaced for failing WCAG 1.4.11 on light surfaces — dj-demo's &lt;select&gt; carries `focus:outline-none focus:ring-2 focus:ring-accent/40` — a 40%-alpha accent ring. globals.css's own .focus-ring comment documents why the site moved to the solid per-theme --focus token: "an alpha ring composites below the 3:1 WCAG 1.4.11 floor on the light surfaces". It also fires on `focus:` rather than `focus-visible:` (ring on mouse click, unlike every other control) and uses `rounded-md` instead of the `rounded-control` token. A repo-wide grep shows this is the single remaining interactive widget control not using .focus-ring/fieldClass — grover, encoding, job, kernel, noise, topology, cost, device selects all do. The select additionally carries a redundant `aria-label="Oracle"` duplicating its &lt;label htmlFor&gt; (no other widget select does this). _Fix:_ Replace the bespoke focus classes with the shared `focus-ring` utility and `rounded-control` (matching grover-visualizer.tsx:167 / the fieldClass idiom), and drop the redundant aria-label so the visible label is the single accessible name. dj-demo.test.tsx locks no class here (it queries by label text), so the change is test-safe. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/dj-demo.tsx:100`, `web/src/app/globals.css:504-510`, `web/src/components/quantum/grover-visualizer.tsx:167`, `web/src/components/quantum/dj-demo.tsx:89-97`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Correctness]** qsim DSL silently ignores extra tokens after a gate's arity, building a wrong-but-silent circuit from learner input — parseProgram consumes only the tokens each gate needs and never looks at the rest of the line: the single-qubit branch reads parts[1] only, CNOT reads parts[1..2], rotations parts[1..2]. A learner who types 'H 0 1' (intending H on both qubits — natural coming from multi-target gate syntaxes) gets H on qubit 0 only, with no error; 'CNOT 0 1 2' (a Toffoli attempt) silently drops the third qubit. This is exactly the 'wrong-but-silent circuit' failure class the same parser family already rejects one level down — parse-utils.ts refuses '0abc' and '1.5xyz' for that stated reason — and it reaches every learner-typed surface: the editable CircuitLab, the qchallenge grader (challenge-grade.ts), and the /playground bench, where a silently-truncated circuit just grades 'wrong' with no explanation. _Fix:_ After consuming a line's expected tokens, throw on any unconsumed remainder (e.g. `unexpected "${parts[k]}" after ${gate}` — surfaces through the existing ErrorCard path all consumers already render). Optionally treat a remainder starting with '#' as an inline comment first to stay forgiving of comment style; the doc header (qsim-dsl.ts:7) currently only promises line-start comments, and no shipped content uses inline ones. Add the 'H 0 1' and 'CNOT 0 1 2' rejection cases to qsim-dsl.test.ts alongside the existing trailing-garbage tests. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/qsim-dsl.ts:89-94`, `web/src/components/quantum/qsim-dsl.ts:65-73`, `web/src/components/quantum/parse-utils.ts:60-63`, `web/src/components/quantum/circuit-lab.tsx:19`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Consistency]** widget-ui.tsx itself never finished the token migration - LabeledSlider and ErrorCard use raw grays while sibling primitives in the same file use the tokens — Within the shared module, Bar defaults its label/value classes to the tokenized text-(--mut) (widget-ui.tsx:68-69), but LabeledSlider's value readout column hard-codes text-gray-500 dark:text-gray-400 and ErrorCard's message does the same - even though .text-caption exists as the documented 'single source for the muted tier' and the newer Rep-family error copies already use it. Likewise fieldClass hard-codes border-gray-200/bg-white raw grays while the Rep textareas use the tokenized border-(--bd) bg-(--field) recipe for the same visual role. A retune of --mut/--bd/--field (the point of the PR #169/#172 token system) would update Bar and the Rep fields but silently skip every slider readout and shared error card platform-wide. _Fix:_ Sweep widget-ui.tsx onto the existing tokens: text-caption (or text-(--mut)) for the LabeledSlider readout and ErrorCard message, and border-(--bd)/bg-(--field) for fieldClass - mirroring exactly the PR #172 sidebar/TOC token sweep. Zero new CSS; the shared primitives then retune as one unit through the token layer. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:355`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:259`, `web/src/app/globals.css:543-548`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:280-281`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[UI / Performance — drift from the #169 restyle]** The #169 bar shimmer is documented as one-shot but runs infinite — every F8 data bar glints forever, in sync — PR #169 (merged yesterday) added a shimmer sweep to every data bar via the shared Bar primitive, and its CSS comments state the intent twice — 'One-shot shimmer sweep across a data bar after it settles' — but the animation is declared `infinite`. The keyframes sweep -100%→240% then hold, so the glint re-crosses every bar every 2.8s forever. All four F8 widgets render their entire teaching surface through Bar (DJ ProbBars 4-8 rows, Grover 4-16, QFT 2×N up to 32, QAOA up to 32), so a single /learn/algorithms page carries dozens of perpetually animating pseudo-elements, all phase-synchronized (identical 1.1s delay), pulling attention to decorative motion on data the learner is trying to read and doing endless compositor work on an otherwise idle page. _Fix:_ Make the code match its documented intent: `animation: bar-shimmer 2.8s ease-in-out 1.1s 1 forwards` (iteration-count 1 + fill forwards parks the pseudo-element off-screen right after the single settle sweep). If the ambient loop was actually intended, the two 'One-shot' comments must be corrected instead — but a synchronized perpetual glint across ~50 bars contradicts 'after it settles' and the repo's own restraint elsewhere (vqe gates its LiveStatus to avoid per-frame spam). No new functionality; one CSS declaration. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/globals.css:117-120`, `web/src/app/globals.css:556-565`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:111`, `web/src/app/globals.css:684-688`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** REGRESSION: Instrument redesign reverted the WS-5a 24px slider thumb to 16px on a 4px rail — WCAG 2.5.8 re-fails across every slider on the platform — The ledger's closed F6 item (docs/feature-optimization.md:439, DONE 2026-06-21 WS-5a) enlarged the shared .slider thumb 16px→24px to clear WCAG 2.2 SC 2.5.8 Target Size (Minimum), and PR #43 (da85e7d) browser-verified it. The Instrument redesign (PR #169, commit 26729d9) rewrote the .slider block and silently shrank both the -webkit and -moz thumbs back to 16px, and the track class is now h-1, so the input element's whole hit box is 4px tall — the grabbable target is a 16px disk on a 4px rail, below the 24px minimum and worse than the pre-WS-5a state (which at least had a 6px rail). LabeledSlider's doc comment still advertises the contract the CSS no longer delivers ('focus ring + 24px touch target'), so every consumer believes it is compliant. This is the primary control of the explorable system: 17 quantum widget files ride LabeledSlider, plus both playground panels and the two pricing sliders — all fiddly to grab at 375px. _Fix:_ Restore a ≥24px effective target in the one .slider block (fixes all ~22 widget inputs + playground + pricing at once). Design-preserving route: keep the 16px visual thumb but grow the input's hit box — give .slider a 24px height with transparent background, paint the 4px rail via ::-webkit-slider-runnable-track / ::-moz-range-track using var(--track), and recenter the thumb (margin-top). Alternatively return the thumb itself to 24px as WS-5a shipped. Either way, correct the stale '24px touch target' claim in widget-ui.tsx:287, and browser-verify at 375px per house rules. Consider a token-contrast-style guard test pinning the thumb size so the next restyle can't silently regress it again. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/globals.css:517-521`, `web/src/app/globals.css:522-541`, `docs/feature-optimization.md:439`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:287`) — added 2026-07-19 — DONE 2026-07-19 (PR pending, Category B)
- [x] **[Correctness]** The hardware GUIDE tells learners to drag the topology explorer's endpoints, but the widget has no drag affordance at all — 02-hardware/GUIDE.md line 96 instructs: 'Drag the endpoints below on a 3x3 grid (IQM-style nearest-neighbor lattice) and watch the SWAP chain the router has to insert.' The qtopo fence at GUIDE.md:104-106 renders TopologyExplorer, whose only controls are two select dropdowns labeled 'Qubit A'/'Qubit B' (topology-explorer.tsx:341-374); the SVG (lines 185-258) carries zero pointer handlers. Every learner reaching the connectivity section's central interactive is told to perform an interaction that does not exist, will try to drag the SVG circles, and will conclude the widget is broken. All three auditors read only the component files, so the prose-affordance mismatch slipped through. _Fix:_ Reword the GUIDE sentence to match the real affordance — e.g. 'Pick the two endpoints from the Qubit A / Qubit B dropdowns below on a 3x3 grid … and watch the SWAP chain'. Pure copy refinement; do NOT add drag support (that would be new functionality). Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. In light theme --accent is oklch(0.66 0.1 118) (globals.css:154); computed WCAG contrast of white on that fill is 3.04:1 — far below AA 4.5:1 for the ~7.5px rendered labels (fontSize 7 in a 300-unit viewBox at max-w-xs). This is precisely the failure contrast-guard.test.ts exists to block ('white text on a solid bg-accent fill computes sub-AA'), but its regex only matches (bg|from|via|to)-accent + text-white (contrast-guard.test.ts:29-30), so the SVG fill-accent/fill-white spelling sails through. PR #172 fixed the sibling contrast issue in this very SVG — highlighted edges went text-accent → text-accent-dark (verified in the e5a77a0 diff) — but left the node fill/label pairing untouched: drift from the #172 sweep. Dark theme (fill-accent-light + fill-gray-900 text) is fine. _Fix:_ Apply the same #172 fix to the nodes: fill-accent -&gt; fill-accent-dark for the light-theme gate-node circle (white label then computes 5.38:1, AA-clean), keeping dark theme as-is. Optionally extend contrast-guard's regex to also catch fill-accent paired with fill-white so the SVG loophole closes. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low.
