## Category C — features 13-33 (analyzed 2026-06-17, big multi-agent run)

> 85 reviewer findings recovered from a rate-limited run (verify stage throttled).
> Items below are tagged ✓verified (re-read in code: 4 inline + `estimate_cost` by
> the workflow) or ~reviewer (file:line evidence, pending a clean verification pass).
> Long tail of Medium/Low items lives in the run transcripts.

### 13-14. In-browser execution runtime
- [ ] **[Resilience]** ✓verified — **No execution timeout**: `runSerialized` awaits `runPythonAsync` with no timeout/abort on a shared module-global queue, so a learner's `while True: pass` hangs the tab AND wedges every subsequent run/grade (the reject-guard only handles errors, not hangs). **DEFERRED**: a JS `Promise.race` cannot interrupt a CPU-bound `while True` (Pyodide blocks the main thread, so `setTimeout` never fires) — the real fix is moving the runtime to a Web Worker (its own PR). Impact: High. Effort: High. (`web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:70,84-95`) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Resilience]** ✓verified — DONE (PR pending) — **A failed Pyodide boot is cached forever**: `getPyodide` assigns `pyodidePromise` before the async boot and returns it unconditionally, so one CDN/wheel failure bricks every runnable cell with no retry. Null the cache on rejection. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:46-64`) — added 2026-06-17
- [ ] **[Correctness]** ~reviewer — stderr is mislabeled as successful stdout in the runnable editor; the stdout/stderr sink is never detached after a run, leaking async output into the next run. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`runnable-editor.tsx`, `pyodide-runtime.ts:84-95`) — added 2026-06-17
- [ ] **[Performance/Consistency]** ~reviewer — every runnable fence eagerly mounts its own Monaco editor on hydration (no viewport gating); qcsim bootstrap is triplicated across 3 authored copies; Pyodide version pinned independently in TS runtime + JupyterLite kernel (driftable). Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`runnable-editor.tsx`, `pyodide-runtime.ts:12`, `jupyterlite-build/`) — added 2026-06-17
- [ ] **[Accessibility]** ~reviewer — run success/error conveyed only by an aria-hidden color dot; disabled "Run in browser" button unreachable by keyboard/SR. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`runnable-editor.tsx`, `notebook-link.tsx`) — added 2026-06-17
- **[Boundary note — not a refinement]** 2026-07-17: the JupyterLite lab remains the one execution surface with no path into the mastery loop — `notebook-link.tsx:70-78` opens `/lab/lab/index.html` in a new tab, and `web/public/lab/` contains zero host-facing `postMessage`/`BroadcastChannel` wiring (the Pyodide kernel runs in a worker, which has no `localStorage`). Wiring lab outcomes to `gradeCard()` would require a custom JupyterLite extension — new plumbing, out of scope for `/optimize-features`. The in-scope route to graded notebook skills is the `tier:"py"` item under `### 15`.


#### Feature 13 — 2026-07-20 Category C re-audit (17 adversarially-verified findings; independent skeptic per claim, default-refute, security and correctness claims re-derived against current tree state)

- [x] **[UI]** The lab opens as an unbranded, always-light JupyterLab tab while the site defaults to dark — "Run in browser" hands the learner off to a new tab that shares nothing with the Instrument design system: the tab is titled "JupyterLite", carries JupyterLite's own favicon, and renders in "JupyterLab Light" — even though the site's own default theme is dark. A learner reading a dark lesson clicks the feature's primary CTA and gets a full-screen white flash from a tab they cannot identify by icon among their other tabs. The lab is the platform's moat surface and it is the one surface with zero brand or theme continuity. The build already post-processes the emitted config, so the wiring exists — it just never sets these. _Fix:_ Add `web/jupyterlite-build/overrides.json` with `{"@jupyterlab/apputils-extension:themes": {"adaptive-theme": true, "preferred-dark-theme": "JupyterLab Dark", "preferred-light-theme": "JupyterLab Light"}}` so the lab follows the viewer's color scheme instead of pinning light (or set `"theme": "JupyterLab Dark"` to match the site's hard default). In the existing step-6b PYLOCK pass, also set `appName` to the site name and repoint `faviconUrl` at the site's brand icon (copy web/src/app/icon.png into the lab output alongside the wheels in step 7), and rewrite the static `<title>` in the emitted lab/index.html in the same loop. Assert the values in the same block that already fails the build when pyodideUrl drifts, so a jupyterlite bump cannot silently revert them. Impact: High. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/app/layout.tsx:71`, `web/jupyterlite-build/.venv/lib/python3.14/site-packages/jupyterlite_core/addons/settings.py:38-66`, `web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:349-383`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Performance]** The lab kernel's 31 MB self-hosted Pyodide tree (/lab/static/pyodide/) has no Cache-Control rule — it revalidates/re-downloads on every lab visit — customHttp.yml is the only response-header surface for the static export. It caches /lab/build/**, /lab/files/wheels/**, /_next/static/**, /pyodide/** and /monaco/** immutably — but PR #58's self-hosted LAB Pyodide distribution lives at /lab/static/pyodide/, which no pattern block matches. That directory is the single largest thing the feature ships: 31 MB, including an 8.6 MB pyodide.asm.wasm, a 2.4 MB python_stdlib.zip and a 6.7 MB matplotlib wheel. Every one of those is fetched on kernel boot, so a returning learner pays a full revalidation (and, per the repo's own live-verified note, a full re-download at max-age=0) on every single 'Run in browser'. The lesson runtime's identical copy under /pyodide/ was fixed on 2026-07-17; the lab's copy was missed, and the header-contract test's list of cached trees does not include it either, so nothing flags the omission. _Fix:_ Add a '/lab/static/**' Cache-Control block to customHttp.yml carrying the same immutable value the /pyodide/** block already uses — the rationale is identical (a version-pinned tree restaged wholesale by build.sh on a Pyodide bump, never mutated in place). Keep the key disjoint (Cache-Control only) so the existing composition invariant holds, and add the new pattern to the it.each list in web/__tests__/infra/custom-http.test.ts:70-80 so the guard covers it. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`customHttp.yml:53-84`, `web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:146`, `customHttp.yml:62-66`, `web/__tests__/infra/custom-http.test.ts:70-80`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Architecture]** ~122 lines of build-critical Python are embedded as 5 shell heredocs in build.sh, invisible to ruff and unreachable by pytest — build.sh is a 407-line bash script that carries five separate inline Python programs totalling roughly 122 lines (~30% of the file). Because they live inside single-quoted shell heredocs, `make lint` (`ruff check .`, which otherwise walks the whole repo) cannot see them and `pytest` (testpaths = ["tests"]) cannot import or exercise them. The most consequential of the five is the 40-line closure-coverage guard, whose own comment states it is "the real guard" against a failure mode nothing else can catch — the e2e third-party-request assertion is blind to it because a missing wheel 404s SAME-ORIGIN. That guard's correctness is therefore asserted by nobody. The repo already establishes the opposite idiom in this exact directory: prepare_notebooks.py is a standalone, ruff-linted module that build.sh invokes on one line (`python prepare_notebooks.py`). _Fix:_ Extract the heredocs into standalone modules beside prepare_notebooks.py — e.g. pyodide_closure.py (the lock walk, parameterised by roots and an `augment_by_imports` flag) and check_notebook_coverage.py — and have build.sh call them as one-liners the way it already calls prepare_notebooks.py. They become ruff-linted automatically, and the coverage check in particular becomes unit-testable in tests/ against a small synthetic lock fixture (the repo already has fixture generators in scripts/gen_*_fixture.py). No behavior change; build.sh drops to roughly 280 lines of actual orchestration. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:105-119`, `web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:167-186`, `web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:204-243`, `web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:200-202`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Performance]** The closure augmentation ships and runtime-loads a 1.6 MB numpy test-suite wheel, on a misreading of loadPackagesFromImports — and `import numpy` never actually loads numpy — build.sh augments the lab wheel closure with "any lock package that shares an import name with a closure package", justified by the comment that "Pyodide's loadPackagesFromImports loads ALL packages providing an imported name". That is not what Pyodide does. The staged pyodide.asm.js builds _import_name_to_package_name as a Map and does a single .get(name) per import — one package per import name, last writer wins. In this lock 'numpy-tests' is keyed after 'numpy' and also declares imports ['numpy'], so `import numpy` resolves to numpy-tests, whose depends is []. Two consequences: (1) the augmentation adds exactly one package, numpy-tests, a 1,647,599-byte wheel containing only numpy's test suite (500 entries of numpy/**/tests and _*_tests.so, no numpy/__init__.py), which is shipped in the payload AND fetched + installed at runtime by 30 of the 32 runnable notebooks; (2) loadPackagesFromImports never loads the real numpy wheel at all — the notebooks only work because the injected bootstrap's piplite.install("qcsim") pulls numpy by package name through micropip. The wheel is pure waste, and the load path is masked rather than correct. _Fix:_ In build.sh, drop the import-collision augmentation (lines 181-184) and instead strip test-only sibling packages (numpy-tests and any future *-tests entry) from the STAGED static/pyodide/pyodide-lock.json, using the same post-build JSON-patch idiom the PYLOCK block already uses at build.sh:357-383. With numpy the sole provider of the 'numpy' import name, `import numpy` resolves to the real wheel and 1.6 MB leaves both the deployed payload and every session's runtime fetch. The lock strip is the load-bearing half: removing the wheel without removing the lock entry would make loadPackage('numpy-tests') 404 same-origin, and per kernel.py:110's except-branch the kernel would show a traceback and silently skip executing the cell in 30 of 32 notebooks. Correct the misleading comment at build.sh:160-166 while there. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:181-184`, `web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:160-166`, `web/jupyterlite-build/prepare_notebooks.py:19-32`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** Lab section chips fail Label in Name — every one of the seven accessible names omits the visible text — The Lab's section radiogroup builds a visible chip label from the index plus a trimmed title, then overrides the accessible name with the untrimmed title. The two never match: the visible string is not contained in the accessible name for any of the seven sections, because the visible label gains a numeric prefix the name lacks and loses a "Quantum " the name keeps. A speech-input user saying "click 01 Computing Foundations" — reading the chip aloud exactly as rendered — activates nothing, which is the failure mode WCAG 2.5.3 exists to prevent. This is the primary navigation control for the browser-runnable notebook list. _Fix:_ Derive the accessible name from the visible label rather than beside it: `aria-label={`${short} — ${s.title}`}` makes the visible text a literal prefix of the name, satisfying 2.5.3 while keeping the full title available to screen-reader users. Drop the now-redundant `title` attribute (it never appears on touch and duplicates the name), or keep it in sync from the same expression so the two can't drift again. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/workspace/lab.tsx:71-72`, `web/src/components/workspace/lab.tsx:84-85`, `web/src/components/workspace/lab.tsx:95`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[UI]** The notebook card's meta row collapses to an ellipsis at 375px, and its compact copy-button override is silently inert — The second line of every notebook card packs a truncating monospace filename, a copy button, and the "Pyodide" chip into one non-wrapping flex row. At 375px there is roughly 114px of shared width and the two fixed children consume about 100px of it, so the filename — the exact string the copy button copies — renders as one character plus an ellipsis. Compounding it, the caller's attempt to shrink the copy button to 24px never takes effect: `h-6 w-6` loses to CopyButton's own `h-8 w-8` on CSS source order, so the button occupies 32px while its icon is correctly shrunk to 12px by a higher-specificity descendant rule. The result is a button that is 8px wider than designed, visually misaligned against an 11px text row, and eating the width the filename needed. _Fix:_ Let the meta row wrap (`flex-wrap` with the filename keeping `min-w-0`), or move the "Pyodide" chip up onto the title row where it belongs semantically — it describes the notebook, not its path — freeing the second line for the filename plus copy button. Separately, remove the dead `h-6 w-6` from the CopyButton call and give CopyButton an explicit size prop (or move `h-8 w-8` behind a default that the className can actually override), so the compact variant either works or is not claimed; the 32px box is the accessible size, so keeping it and dropping the override is also a valid resolution. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/notebook-link.tsx:51-64`, `web/src/components/notebook-link.tsx:55-59`, `web/src/components/copy-button.tsx:96`, `web/src/app/learn/[section]/page.tsx:88`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Architecture]** build.sh step 7 re-copies lib/ into a directory `jupyter lite build` already populated, nesting a second full copy of the curriculum library at /lab/files/lib/lib — and it ships — `jupyter lite build` (step 6) already copies every staged content file into `../public/lab/files/` — that is precisely what step 2b's `ignore_contents` override buys by REMOVING `/lib/` from jupyterlite-core's default ignore list (build.sh:283-289: "so lib is indexed and importable"). Step 7 then runs `cp -R files/lib ../public/lab/files/lib` (build.sh:390) against a destination directory that already exists, so POSIX `cp -R` copies the source *inside* it rather than replacing it. The result is a complete duplicate of the shared library one level deeper, which jupyterlite-core's own `post_build` then indexes into the Contents API as a real browsable folder. Step 7's comment ("Copy items JupyterLite's content service does not auto-serve: lib/") states the opposite of what step 2b established — the cp is not only redundant, it is the thing creating the duplicate. It also never self-heals: `rm -rf files/lib` (step 4) clears the STAGING copy, but nothing ever clears `../public/lab/files/lib/lib`, so a deleted or renamed lib module lingers in the nested copy across rebuilds. _Fix:_ Delete step 7 (build.sh:385-390) — `jupyter lite build` already serves files/lib because step 2b un-ignored it, and the e2e already proves `from lib.circuits import ghz_state` resolves in the kernel (lab-pyodide.e2e.ts asserts the late "all qubits agree" output specifically to guard lib/ being served). If the cp is kept as belt-and-braces, make it idempotent and destination-clearing the way every other staging step in this file already is (`rm -rf ../public/lab/files/lib` before the copy, or `cp -R files/lib/. ../public/lab/files/lib/`), and correct the step-7 comment, which currently contradicts step 2b. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:390`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:283-289`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/jupyterlite-build/.venv/lib/python3.13/site-packages/jupyterlite_core/addons/contents.py:32-47`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** files/startup.py is dead boot code — nothing executes it, the config actively ignores the file that was supposed to wire it, and it ships to learners as the first entry in the lab's file browser — `files/startup.py` was designed as a kernel-startup hook, but the mechanism that would run it (a `files/overrides.json`) was never built: no overrides.json exists anywhere in the output, `.gitignore` ignores `files/overrides.json`, and the generated `ignore_contents` list explicitly contains `/overrides\.json` — so even if someone created one under files/, the contents addon would skip it. The installed `jupyterlite_pyodide_kernel` package contains zero references to "startup", and the shipped lab JS contains zero references to "startup.py". Meanwhile build.sh:387 asserts the opposite in a load-bearing comment: "wheels/ and startup.py are picked up automatically". The file is therefore (a) never run, (b) shipped and indexed as browsable lab content — it is the FIRST name in the lab root's Contents API listing, so learners opening the lab see a mysterious `startup.py` above the curriculum folders, (c) a booby trap: it is the obvious place a maintainer would edit to change boot behaviour, and it silently does nothing, and (d) a divergent duplicate of the real boot path — it inserts only `/files` on sys.path while the live injected bootstrap inserts `/drive`, `/home/pyodide`, `/files`, and it carries the try/except diagnostic ("[startup] qcsim install/import failed") that the live path lacks entirely. It even costs ongoing lint maintenance: `make lint` runs `ruff check .` and pyproject.toml excludes only `*.ipynb`, which is why the file carries a `# noqa: F704` for its module-level await. _Fix:_ Delete `web/jupyterlite-build/files/startup.py` and correct build.sh's step-7 comment, which currently names it as an auto-picked-up artifact. If the try/except diagnostic is worth keeping, fold it into `prepare_notebooks.py`'s BOOTSTRAP_SOURCE — the one boot path that actually executes — so there is a single source for kernel boot behaviour instead of one live copy and one silently-dead copy with divergent sys.path roots. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:387`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/jupyterlite-build/files/startup.py:1-22`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/jupyterlite-build/prepare_notebooks.py:19-32`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/docs/superpowers/plans/2026-05-25-runnable-notebooks-jupyterlite.md:107,112`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[UI]** Every lab notebook opens on the injected Pyodide bootstrap cell, above its own title — and the cell already carries a tag that nothing consumes — `prepare_notebooks.py` inserts the bootstrap at `cells.insert(0, cell)`, so in all 45 staged notebooks the first thing rendered in JupyterLite is a raw code cell reading `# Auto-injected Pyodide bootstrap (no-op under real Python).` / `import piplite` / `await piplite.install("qcsim")` / a `sys.path.insert` loop. The notebook's own H1 and objectives are pushed below it. That is the first impression of the headline feature: a learner clicks "Run in browser" and lands on build-tooling boilerplate about a package they have never heard of, above the lesson they asked for. The cell is already stamped `metadata: {"tags": ["pyodide-bootstrap"]}` — a tag that nothing in the build or the lab reads — so the intent to mark it as machinery exists but is inert. JupyterLab's own input-collapse mechanism (`metadata.jupyter.source_hidden`) is present in the shipped lab bundle and would hide the source while leaving execution, ordering, and Run All Cells semantics completely unchanged. _Fix:_ In `prepare_notebooks.py`'s injected cell metadata, add `"jupyter": {"source_hidden": true}` alongside the existing `tags`. The cell still runs first and Run All Cells is unchanged; the notebook now opens on its own title, and the collapsed input stays one click away for anyone curious about the bootstrap. Keep the BOOTSTRAP_MARKER idempotency check working by leaving the source text untouched. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/jupyterlite-build/prepare_notebooks.py:49-57`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** NotebookLink inlines the notebook label and lab-URL logic that workspace.ts already single-sources — and workspace.ts's own comments claim NotebookLink is the source — web/src/lib/workspace.ts exports humanizeNotebook() and defines notebookHref(), both documented as mirroring NotebookLink — humanizeNotebook's docstring literally reads "the way NotebookLink does — one source for the label". But NotebookLink does not import either helper; it re-derives both inline. The result is two independent copies of the lab route in production code, so changing the JupyterLite URL shape (or the label rules) requires finding both, and the doc comment actively misleads a maintainer into believing the helper is authoritative. The sibling surface workspace/lab.tsx does the right thing, consuming the precomputed nb.href/nb.label. There is also a latent divergence between the two label implementations: NotebookLink uses an unanchored string replace while workspace.ts uses an anchored regex. _Fix:_ Move humanizeNotebook() and notebookHref() into src/lib/manifest.ts — which already owns the manifest-derived link helpers getRepoUrl() and isNotebookRunnable() and is already imported by notebook-link.tsx — then have both NotebookLink and workspace.ts consume them. That leaves exactly one definition of the lab route and one label rule, resolves the anchored/unanchored divergence in favour of the regex, and makes workspace.ts's 'one source' comment true instead of aspirational. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/workspace.ts:132-138`, `web/src/lib/workspace.ts:140-142`, `web/src/components/notebook-link.tsx:21-23`, `web/src/components/notebook-link.tsx:24-27`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Architecture]** build.sh clears every staging target before writing except the notebooks themselves, so renamed or deleted notebooks persist as ghosts in local lab builds — build.sh clears-before-staging in six places, and one of those carries an explicit comment warning about exactly this hazard for the qcsim wheel. The notebook staging step is the sole exception: it only does `mkdir -p` then `cp`, never removing the section directory first. Because files/&lt;section&gt;/ is gitignored, it is a persistent local artifact — so if a notebook is renamed or deleted from the curriculum, its stale copy survives in files/, gets a Pyodide bootstrap injected by prepare_notebooks.py (which rglobs every .ipynb it finds), and is built into public/lab/files/. Amplify clones fresh so deploys are unaffected, but this repo's verification culture leans hard on local browser checks of the lab, and the locally-built lab silently diverges from what ships. Separately, .gitignore hardcodes the seven section directories that content-manifest.json already owns — build.sh:327 reads that list from the manifest precisely so the loop 'can never drift', but the ignore list can. _Fix:_ Add `rm -rf "files/$section/notebooks"` immediately before the mkdir in the staging loop (matching the `rm -rf files/lib` idiom directly below it), and collapse the seven hardcoded .gitignore entries to a single `files/*/` pattern with a `!files/startup.py` negation so the ignore list stops duplicating the manifest's section inventory. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:329-330`, `web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:335`, `web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:255-256`, `web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:99`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** The Pyodide lock-closure walk and the wheel-fetch loop are each written twice in build.sh, in a file that already extracts a shared helper for the same purpose — The transitive dependency walk over pyodide-lock.json is implemented twice, line-for-line identical apart from renamed locals (by_norm vs by) and different roots; the lab copy adds one extra imports-augmentation pass at the end. The wheel-fetch loop that follows each is likewise duplicated, identical apart from the CDN base, destination, and log wording. This is not a case where duplication was unavoidable: build.sh already extracted fetch_pyodide_core() to share the tarball download between the same two stagings, so the helper idiom is established in this very file and simply was not applied to the two steps that follow it. A fix to the closure algorithm (or the fetch's retry/validation behavior) currently has to be made in two places, and the two copies can silently drift. _Fix:_ Fold the two lock walks into one closure function parameterised by roots plus an augment_by_imports flag (naturally done as part of extracting the heredocs to a module), and add a `fetch_closure_wheels <cdn> <dest> <label> <wheels>` bash helper beside fetch_pyodide_core so both stagings call it. Confirmed safe: the lesson runtime legitimately omits the imports-augmentation because pyodide-runtime.ts never calls loadPackagesFromImports (it only loadPackages micropip and micropip.installs qcsim), so the flag preserves current behavior exactly rather than papering over a divergence. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:105-119`, `web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:167-186`, `web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:122-126`, `web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:189-193`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Architecture]** runnable-manifest.json is a fully redundant second manifest with zero web consumers, parked in the Next app's src/lib and drift-guarded twice — validate_runnable.py generates two manifests. The second, runnable-manifest.json, is labelled in its own source comment as a 'Legacy flat runnable list', and its contents are exactly derivable from content-manifest.json — I verified the 32 entries are identical to the list derived from the per-notebook `runnable` booleans. Nothing in web/ imports it: grepping src/, __tests__/, and e2e/ for 'runnable-manifest' returns no hits, so it is not bundled and not read by any TypeScript. Its only consumer is a Python test. The result is a build artifact that lives inside the Next.js application's source tree purely to serve a pytest assertion, carries its own separate staleness gate in two independent places, and imposes a regeneration step documented in the README on anyone who marks a notebook runnable. _Fix:_ Delete runnable-manifest.json and MANIFEST_PATH/build_manifest(), and have tests/test_notebook_contract.py derive RUNNABLE from content-manifest.json (which validate_runnable.py --check already drift-guards). That removes a redundant generated file from the web source tree, one of two staleness gates, and a contributor step from the README, with no loss of coverage — the flat list is recomputable in one line. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`scripts/validate_runnable.py:57-58`, `scripts/validate_runnable.py:209-222`, `scripts/validate_runnable.py:303-307`, `tests/test_notebook_contract.py:54-61`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The disabled Run chip hardcodes one blocking reason that is factually wrong for 2 of the 13 non-runnable notebooks, against the repo's explicit honest-copy idiom — NotebookLink's non-runnable branch states a single fixed reason — 'Requires AWS Braket hardware access' — in both the hover title and the sr-only qualifier. I classified all 13 non-runnable notebooks by their actual imports: 11 genuinely touch braket.aws/boto3, but 2 are blocked solely because PennyLane cannot install under Pyodide and have no AWS surface at all. For those two, the only explanation a learner or screen-reader user gets is untrue. This breaks a pattern the codebase states explicitly elsewhere: section-gate-modal.tsx computes a 'Truthful per-section runnable note' with a comment noting coverage varies, and workspace/lab.tsx renders 'an honest empty row, never a dead panel'. The manifest already distinguishes runnable from non-runnable but stores no reason, so the component has nothing to key on and falls back to a guess. _Fix:_ Soften the chip's copy to a reason-agnostic statement that is true for all 13 (e.g. 'Runs locally only — not supported in the browser runtime'), keeping the existing sr-only + title structure. If a per-notebook reason is wanted later, validate_runnable.py already computes the distinction and could emit a `reason` field alongside `runnable` in content-manifest.json — but the copy fix alone removes the false claim without touching the manifest schema. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/notebook-link.tsx:85-90`, `scripts/validate_runnable.py:69-78`, `web/src/components/section-gate-modal.tsx:44-54`, `web/src/components/workspace/lab.tsx:13-14`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[UX]** The disabled "Run in browser" chip gives a reason that is false for a third of the notebooks it appears on — Every non-runnable notebook row renders the same hardcoded explanation — "Requires AWS Braket hardware access" — in both the hover title and the sr-only text. That is wrong for the notebooks excluded because they import PennyLane or OpenFermion: those run fine with no AWS account at all, just a local Python environment. The learner's takeaway from a chemistry or QML lesson is "I need to pay for quantum hardware to do this", which is exactly the misconception the free browser lab exists to remove, and it is the only sentence offered at that dead end. _Fix:_ Replace the single hardcoded reason with copy that is true for every case — e.g. "Not available in the browser runtime — run this one locally with the full Python environment" — in both the `title` and the sr-only span, and lean on the "View on GitHub" link already sitting beside it as the next step. If per-notebook precision is wanted later, validate_runnable.py already computes the exact violation and writes the manifest, so a `reason` string can ride along the existing `runnable: false` entry with no new plumbing; the copy fix alone removes the falsehood today. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/notebook-link.tsx:84-90`, `scripts/validate_runnable.py:69-70`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** Notebook staging is the only build step that neither clears its destination nor fails on error, so a dev's incremental lab silently diverges from what ships — Step 3 stages curriculum notebooks with `mkdir -p` + `cp ... 2>/dev/null || true`, without clearing the destination first and with copy failures suppressed. Every neighbouring stage step does the opposite — lib/ is `rm -rf`'d before copying and the qcsim wheel is `rm -f`'d before staging, with comments explaining exactly why. The consequence on a local incremental build: a renamed or deleted notebook keeps its stale staged copy, prepare_notebooks.py skips it as already-bootstrapped, and `jupyter lite build` ships it into the lab a developer is verifying against. And because `|| true` swallows cp failures, a section whose notebooks fail to stage produces a quieter, emptier lab rather than a failed build. The step 1d closure-coverage check cannot see either problem: it reads the REPO copies under ../../&lt;section&gt;/notebooks/, not the staged ones. CI checks out fresh so production is unaffected — this is a local-verification-fidelity gap, not a live defect. _Fix:_ Add `rm -rf "files/$section/notebooks"` before the mkdir in build.sh:328-331 so staging is clear-then-copy like its neighbours, and drop the `2>/dev/null || true` so a failed copy aborts under `set -euo pipefail` (if a genuinely notebook-less section is possible, replace the blanket suppression with an explicit test for that case so real errors still fail loudly). Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:328-331`, `web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:335-336`, `web/jupyterlite-build/prepare_notebooks.py:46-47`, `web/jupyterlite-build/build.sh:222`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** README's "runnable-notebook contract" section describes a gating pipeline that no longer exists — it names content.ts as the marker reader and runnable-manifest.json as the UI gate — The README's headline JupyterLite section documents the wrong code path in two places. It states that the portal "(web/src/lib/content.ts) reads this exact marker to decide whether to show the Run in browser button" — but content.ts does not parse notebooks at all for runnability; it imports `isNotebookRunnable` from `./manifest`, which reads `content-manifest.json`. The mermaid diagram in the same section draws `web/src/lib/runnable-manifest.json` with an edge labelled "gates UI button" pointing at the Run action, which is likewise not what happens — `runnable-manifest.json` has no web consumer. This is the repo's front door for the feature and the first thing a contributor reads before touching the Run gate; both statements send them to files that would not change the behaviour they are trying to change. _Fix:_ Update the README section to describe the pipeline that actually runs: the marker is read by `scripts/validate_runnable.py` (`is_marked_runnable`, which explicitly documents itself as mirroring the portal), the scan result is baked into `content-manifest.json`, and the web gate is `manifest.ts`'s `isNotebookRunnable` consumed by `content.ts` and `workspace.ts`. Retarget the mermaid "gates UI button" edge at content-manifest.json. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/lib/content.ts:4`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/web/src/lib/manifest.ts:46-53`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)

### 15. Code challenge auto-grader
- [x] **[Correctness]** ✓verified — DONE (PR pending) — **Slider-bound `theta` in a target program grades against the wrong state**: both graders build the reference via `opsFor(target, 0)`, so a `theta` rotation in the author's `target.program` collapses to RY(0)=identity. Reject reference programs with `hasTheta === true`. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`challenge-grade.ts:38`, `pyodide-grader.ts:53`, `qsim-dsl.ts:71-73,102`) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Security/Resilience]** DONE (PR pending) — author-supplied `spec.qubits` unclamped into `1<<n` allocation; parse errors swallowed. FIXED: `MAX_QUBITS` clamp at both grader entries (`challenge-grade.ts`, `pyodide-grader.ts`); parse-error guard added in #21. RECLASSIFIED by the security audit to **author-only / Low** — the learner path is already hard-clamped at `MAX_QUBITS=4` (`qsim-dsl.ts:91`), so this is defense-in-depth against an author typo, not a learner-triggerable DoS. Impact: High→Low. Effort: Low. — added 2026-06-17
- [ ] **[Accessibility]** ~reviewer — the verdict `role="status"` region is conditionally mounted, so SR misses the announcement; add `aria-live` always-present. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`challenge.tsx:168-175`) — added 2026-06-17
- [ ] **[Security/Tests]** ~reviewer (RECLASSIFIED + DEFERRED) — Tier-B `gradePy` raw-Python concat. Security audit verdict: **not a security vuln** — Pyodide is the learner's own same-user WASM sandbox, so "injection" self-harms only. Residual is grading-INTEGRITY (a learner can false-pass by stubbing `circuit`) — Low, and only matters once `tier:"py"` content ships (none today; cross-run leakage already prevented by the fresh per-run namespace). DEFERRED: namespace the grading epilogue away from learner symbols. The "no SRI on the Pyodide/Monaco CDN" half is promoted to its own audit finding below. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (`pyodide-grader.ts`) — added 2026-06-17
- [ ] **[Security]** PARTIAL 2026-06-25 — ✓verified (audit 2026-06-18, re-verified 2026-06-25). **DONE (PR #56/#58):** the Pyodide half is closed — the lesson runtime and lab kernel are now self-hosted SAME-ORIGIN (`/pyodide/`, `/lab/`) as the primary path, with the jsdelivr fallback carrying SRI + `crossorigin` on the bootstrap script (`web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:18-32,53-66`). **STILL OPEN:** (1) **Monaco** still loads its editor core from a CDN with no SRI — `@monaco-editor/react` fetches it on first mount (`web/src/components/code-editor.tsx:13-25`), so a tampered Monaco CDN still executes with full page privileges; (2) there is **no site-wide CSP** to backstop either loader (`customHttp.yml` carries only cache headers — see the Feature 31 item in the 27-33 tail). Fix: self-host or pin+SRI Monaco, and ship a CSP via a staged report-only rollout. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/code-editor.tsx:13-25`, `customHttp.yml`) — added 2026-06-18, narrowed 2026-06-25
- [x] **[Architecture & UX]** CLOSED 2026-07-17 (PR #167, c637988) — **The Tier-B Python grader is fully built and wired — and zero shipped content uses it** — `gradePy` runs learner Braket-style Python in the same-origin Pyodide worker and compares state vectors up to global phase, and its solve verdict flows through the same `apply()` → `gradeCardIfDue` path as Tier A (`challenge.tsx:155-156,138`), but no `qchallenge` fence anywhere sets `tier:"py"` (the only mount is the unlinked `/e2e-fixtures/py-challenge`) and `rep-schema.ts:19-22` rejects the tier outright because CI cannot exercise the Pyodide path. This is the highest-leverage rail for grading notebook-exercise responses: author `tier:"py"` Reps in the lesson pages that mirror the notebook exercises' target skills, so real Python answers get graded and land in the existing per-user mastery loop (FSRS card → `qc:*` sync → bench/records) with no new machinery. Prerequisites: extend the Playwright e2e suite (which already boots real Pyodide) with a grading assertion per shipped py Rep so the rep-schema gate can be relaxed honestly, and close the deferred grading-integrity item first (namespace the grading epilogue away from learner symbols in `pyodide-grader.ts`). Impact: High. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:136-148,155-168`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-grader.ts:29-88`, `web/src/lib/rep-schema.ts:19-22`) — added 2026-07-17


#### Feature 14 — 2026-07-20 Category C re-audit (16 adversarially-verified findings; independent skeptic per claim, default-refute, security and correctness claims re-derived against current tree state)

- [x] **[Resilience]** `/monaco/**` and `/pyodide/**` are cached `immutable` for a year, but their entry files are unhashed — a version bump permanently breaks the editor for returning learners — customHttp.yml grants both self-hosted runtime trees `public, max-age=31536000, immutable`, justified in-file as "version-pinned trees restaged wholesale on upgrade — the same immutable contract as the lab assets above." That premise holds for `/lab/build/**` (JupyterLite emits content-hashed filenames) but is false for these two trees: their entry points are stable, unhashed names whose CONTENT changes on a version bump, while the chunks they reference are content-hashed and therefore get new URLs. A returning learner holding a year-long immutable cache entry for the old entry file will request hashed chunk URLs that the new deploy no longer contains. For Monaco this is an unrecoverable break — `loader.config` pins `/monaco/vs` with no fallback origin (unlike Pyodide, which has one), so the AMD load fails, `onMount` never fires, the 15s watchdog trips, and every runnable cell renders "Couldn't load the editor." The remedy the component itself offers — a `window.location.reload()` button — cannot fix it, because `immutable` instructs the browser to skip revalidation on a normal reload; only a hard reload or cache clear recovers. For Pyodide the failure is quieter but more interesting: `pyodide.js`, `pyodide.asm.js` and the 8.6 MB `pyodide.asm.wasm` are all unhashed and independently evictable, so a partially-evicted cache after a version bump yields an Emscripten JS/wasm mismatch, a boot-error, and a silent fall through to the jsdelivr CDN — reintroducing exactly the third-party request PRs #56/#58 spent two rounds eliminating. No e2e catches that, because Playwright runs a fresh browser every time. _Fix:_ Make the immutable grant honest rather than removing it. Cleanest fix matching repo idiom: stage into version-stamped paths — `stage-monaco.mjs` already reads `version` from `monaco-editor/package.json` (line 48) purely to log it, so have it write `/monaco/<version>/vs` and emit that constant for `loader.config` (the same generated-constant pattern `gen:tutor-core` already uses); do the equivalent for `/pyodide/<version>/` in `jupyterlite-build/build.sh`, which already knows the kernel's pin. Then every URL in both trees changes on a bump and `immutable` is correct by construction. Cheaper interim: drop both patterns to the bounded `public, max-age=86400` already used for the unhashed `/welcome/**` tree (customHttp.yml:81-84). Either way, add a case to `web/__tests__/infra/custom-http.test.ts` asserting `immutable` is granted only to trees whose entry filenames carry a hash or version — the existing suite checks that the rules are present but never that they are appropriate. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`customHttp.yml:71-78`, `customHttp.yml:61-66`, `web/src/components/code-editor.tsx:20`, `web/src/components/code-editor.tsx:25,52-57,79-86`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** Monaco is never exercised in any browser test — the self-hosting migration is proven only by a fully-mocked unit test and an `existsSync` check — The Monaco self-hosting migration is the newest and least-proven part of this feature, and nothing runs it. The Jest suite mocks `@monaco-editor/react` wholesale and asserts only that `loader.config` was CALLED with the right path — not that anything resolves there. The RunnableEditor suite substitutes a plain textarea. The build-time guard checks a single file, `vs/loader.js`, which is the one file that does NOT reference the hashed chunks, so an otherwise-truncated copy passes it. And although four e2e specs assert zero third-party requests, none of them mounts Monaco: the `tier:"py"` fixture uses a plain textarea (`getByLabel("Your circuit")`), and the lab boots the JupyterLite kernel, not the lesson editor. The consequence is that an incomplete staging, a `cpSync` path change across a monaco-editor major, or a regression back to `@monaco-editor/loader`'s jsdelivr default all ship fully green — and surface to learners as a permanent "Couldn't load the editor" on 01-foundations, which today holds the only `runnable` fence in the entire curriculum. _Fix:_ Add `web/e2e/runnable-editor.e2e.ts` mirroring the structure of the four existing specs: navigate to the foundations lesson, scroll the runnable fence into the widget-fence approach gate, wait for Monaco's textarea (`aria-label="Editable Python code"`, set at code-editor.tsx:99) to become editable, click Run, assert real qcsim output, and apply the identical exact-origin request filter those specs already use so the Monaco boot is covered by the zero-third-party guarantee too. Independently, widen the `stage-monaco.mjs` assertion to also require `vs/editor/editor.main.js` and `vs/basic-languages/monaco.contribution.js` — the two unhashed entry points that actually pull the chunk graph — so a partial copy fails the build instead of the browser. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`web/__tests__/components/code-editor.test.tsx:17-27,49`, `web/__tests__/components/runnable-editor.test.tsx:10-24`, `scripts/stage-monaco.mjs:43-46`, `web/e2e/challenge-py-grader.e2e.ts:73,117-120`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** The one `runnable` fence that ships to learners is the only executable content surface in the repo with no CI gate — Feature #14's entire user-facing content is a single ```runnable fence at 01-foundations/GUIDE.md:251, whose body executes real Python against the qcsim wheel in the learner's browser. Nothing in CI ever runs it. Every sibling executable surface has a gate: notebooks are executed under qcsim by tests/test_notebook_contract.py:80 (`test_runnable_notebook_executes_under_qcsim`), and tier:"py" Rep fences are gated 1:1 against `PY_REP_E2E_IDS` and graded for real by web/e2e/py-reps.e2e.ts. The GUIDE-fence collector that exists precisely for this class of risk — web/__tests__/content/guide-reps.test.ts:72 — matches six tokens (`qchallenge|qpredict|qblochtarget|qcostestimate|qdebug|qexpect`) and omits `runnable`, even though `runnable` is a registered fence token (widget-langs.ts:16, widget-fence.tsx:76). That test's own header (lines 7-12) states the rationale it was never extended to cover: fences rendered to learners were "validated only at runtime, in the learner's browser… would ship silently and render as an error card… in production." _Fix:_ Extend the existing machinery rather than adding any: widen the guide-reps.test.ts collector regex (or add a sibling collector) to harvest ```runnable bodies, and run each body through the qcsim-compatibility AST scan already implemented in scripts/validate_runnable.py (`scan_notebook` / `_import_module_violation`, lines 150-208) — or execute it under qcsim in tests/test_notebook_contract.py the same way notebook cells are. Also assert the corpus is non-empty, mirroring `test_found_runnable_notebooks` (tests/test_notebook_contract.py:39), so a regex regression fails loudly instead of passing vacuously on zero fences. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`01-foundations/GUIDE.md:251`, `web/__tests__/content/guide-reps.test.ts:72`, `web/__tests__/content/guide-reps.test.ts:7-12`, `tests/test_notebook_contract.py:80`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The `runnable` fence is bucketed into the `tall` skeleton, but the mounted editor is roughly 110px shorter — widget-fence maps `runnable` to the `tall` skeleton (`min-h-[460px]`), but RunnableEditor's mounted idle height is fully determined by fixed values and comes to roughly 346px: a 2px ribbon, a ~52px header, the CodeEditor's fixed `height = 260` container, and the ~30px keyboard-exit hint. The output panel does not exist until after a run, so nothing fills the gap. The skeleton also carries `my-6` while every activity card (including this one) carries `my-8`, adding another 16px. So the approach gate and the chunk-loading state both over-reserve, and the page collapses ~110px the moment the widget mounts — the layout shift the gate exists to prevent. The unit test only asserts the gate skeleton matches the *loading* skeleton, never that either matches the widget. _Fix:_ Re-bucket `runnable` to `medium` (`min-h-[360px]`), which matches the widget's fixed composition, and align WidgetSkeleton's margin with the `my-8` the activity cards use (or give lazyWidget a margin field) so gate → loading → mounted is a no-shift sequence. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/widget-fence.tsx:76`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-fence.tsx:11`, `web/src/components/code-editor.tsx:46`, `web/src/components/code-editor.tsx:114`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[UX]** One static "Booting Python (first run takes a few seconds)…" string is the only feedback for a window that ranges from ~200ms to 150 seconds — The runnable editor has exactly one busy state. `runnable-editor.tsx:120-123` renders the literal "Booting Python (first run takes a few seconds)…" whenever `busy && !result`, with no notion of whether Python is actually booting. Three separate cases collapse onto that one string, and it is wrong or actively misleading in all but the first: 1. **Every run after the first.** The interpreter is cached for the whole session, so runs 2..N never boot anything — they execute in milliseconds while the panel claims a boot is underway. 2. **A runaway loop.** The run watchdog is 30s. A learner who writes `while True:` reads "Booting Python (first run takes a few seconds)" for 30 straight seconds, then gets a timeout message telling them to check for an infinite loop. The one moment the learner most needs to understand what the machine is doing, the UI misdirects them toward a boot problem. 3. **The both-origins-down path.** `getPyodide()` awaits same-origin `bootFrom` and then CDN `bootFrom` sequentially, each bounded by its own 75s watchdog. Worst case the learner stares at copy promising "a few seconds" for **150 seconds** with the Run button disabled, no cancel, and no escalation, before any error appears. Nothing else fills the gap: `pyodide-run.ts:28-31` buffers the worker's streamed `output` chunks into an array and only returns them after the run settles, so the panel is literally just that one sentence for the entire duration. The same literal string is copy-pasted into `challenge.tsx:113`, so the two Python surfaces a learner meets in one lesson share the drift rather than a token. _Fix:_ Give the component a `hasRunRef` (or equivalent) set once the first result lands, and pick the copy from it: the boot sentence only until the first result, plain "Running…" afterward. Single-source both strings from one exported constant shared with `challenge.tsx:113` so the two Python surfaces cannot drift again. For the long-tail cases, let the message escalate rather than sit: an existing `setTimeout` inside the busy branch can swap in a second, honest line (e.g. "Still working — this is taking longer than usual") once the wait passes ~10s, which covers both the runaway-loop and the both-origins-down waits without touching the runtime. No new capability — this is the same status panel, told accurately. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:120-123`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:216,275-278`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:62`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:71,282-292`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Correctness / UX]** Reset re-enables Run while the abandoned run is still executing, so the next Run silently stalls up to 30s and then blames a timeout the learner thought they cancelled — `reset()` (runnable-editor.tsx:37-42) discards only the UI's claim on the run — it bumps `runToken`, clears the result, and calls `setBusy(false)`. It has no way to stop the worker, which keeps executing the abandoned code until the 30s watchdog. Because `disabled={busy}` (line 79) is the only gate, Run becomes clickable again immediately while the shared interpreter is genuinely occupied. The next run is then enqueued behind the abandoned one on the module-global FIFO (pyodide-runtime.ts:329, `runQueue.then(() => py.run(code, onOutput))`), so it shows the static "Booting Python…" notice for the remainder of the first run's budget. When the watchdog finally fires it kills the worker and marks the handle dead, so the queued run immediately hits pyodide-runtime.ts:163 and rejects with RESET_MESSAGE — text that blames "an earlier run [that] timed out" (lines 113-114). The learner sees an unexplained ~30s hang on trivial code, followed by an error attributing it to a run they explicitly reset. _Fix:_ Make Reset honest about what it can and cannot undo: keep `busy` true until the in-flight `runPython` promise actually settles (drop `setBusy(false)` from `reset` and let the existing `finally` clause at lines 32-34 clear it — the `runToken` guard there already discards the stale result correctly). Reset then still restores the source and clears the output panel, but the Run button stays disabled while the interpreter is genuinely occupied, which is the state that already exists. This is the same busy-state contract the component uses everywhere else; no new capability. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:37-42`, `web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:79`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:329`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:62`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience]** Monaco's 15-second load budget is under half its own critical-path transfer on a slow link, and firing it unmounts a load that would have succeeded — code-editor.tsx:25 sets `LOAD_TIMEOUT_MS = 15_000`, and the timer at lines 52-57 flips `timedOut` if `onMount` has not fired by then. The ternary at line 77 does not overlay the notice — it replaces `<Monaco>` entirely, tearing down the in-flight load, and there is no path that ever clears `timedOut`; the only offered recovery is `window.location.reload()` (line 82). The critical path this budget covers is ~1.07 MB gzipped across four requests (measured on the staged tree), plus parse/eval of 3.67 MB of JavaScript on whatever device the learner has. On a ~1 Mbps link that is roughly 9s of transfer before any execution, so an ordinary slow connection produces a hard, unrecoverable-without-reload failure on an editor that was moments from mounting. The same page's Python runtime gives a comparable payload five times the budget: pyodide-runtime.ts:71 sets `DEFAULT_BOOT_TIMEOUT_MS = 75_000` for a ~3 MB brotli'd wasm and documents it as "Generous for a slow link." Same learner, same network, same session — two budgets differing 5x with no stated reason. _Fix:_ Raise `LOAD_TIMEOUT_MS` to a budget proportionate to the measured payload and consistent with the sibling runtime's stated slow-link reasoning (the Pyodide boot budget is the in-repo precedent), and record the byte figures in the comment the way pyodide-runtime.ts:36-40 records the wasm size behind its version pin. The notice/reload affordance and the `mountedRef` guard are already correct — only the constant and its justification are wrong. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/code-editor.tsx:25`, `web/src/components/code-editor.tsx:52-57`, `web/src/components/code-editor.tsx:77-88`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:71`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The Rep/activity card header is copy-pasted six times, and runnable-editor is the copy that has already drifted — RunnableEditor hand-rolls its header row instead of using the shared `WidgetCard`. Five sibling activity widgets hand-roll the same row with a byte-identical class string; runnable-editor's differs by one token (`py-2.5` instead of `py-3`), so a runnable cell and a challenge card in the same lesson render headers of different heights. `WidgetCard` cannot absorb any of them because it hardcodes `<EyebrowLabel>` without the `strong` prop this family uses, and hardcodes `px-4 py-2` rather than the family's `px-4 py-3 sm:px-5`. The result is a six-way duplication that no shared primitive can currently reach — exactly the drift widget-ui's own doc comments say the module exists to prevent. _Fix:_ Add a `strong` (and header-padding) pass-through to `WidgetCard` so the activity family can render through it, then migrate the six widgets onto it. If a full migration is too broad, minimally align runnable-editor.tsx:59 to the family's `py-3` so the one outlier stops rendering a shorter header than its siblings. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:59`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:136`, `web/src/components/quantum/predict-widget.tsx:149`, `web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:145`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The output panel is a horizontally scrolling region that skips the house `useScrollRegion` idiom — The output `<pre>` sets `overflow-x-auto` but carries no `tabIndex`, `role="region"`, `aria-label`, or `focus-ring`. Python tracebacks and long `print` lines are exactly the content that overflows here, and a keyboard-only reader cannot scroll to see it. The repo already single-sources this as `useScrollRegion` (measure-then-expose, conditional so a fitting container adds no tab stop), and two components consume it; a third, code-block.tsx, hand-rolls the identical logic. The runnable editor's output is the one horizontally scrolling surface in this family that does neither. The wrinkle is that the element already carries `role="status"`, so the region props cannot simply be spread onto it. _Fix:_ Wrap the output `<pre>` in a div that spreads `useScrollRegion<HTMLDivElement>("Scrollable output").regionProps`, moving `overflow-x-auto` off the `<pre>` and leaving `role="status"`/`aria-live` on the `<pre>` itself. That keeps the live region intact and reuses the shared hook rather than adding a third variant of the pattern. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:111`, `web/src/hooks/use-scroll-region.ts:22`, `web/src/hooks/use-scroll-region.ts:6`, `web/src/components/markdown-table.tsx:18`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Performance / build hygiene]** `stage-monaco.mjs` copies the whole `min/vs` tree — 15 MB, most of it language services the Python-only editor can never reach — The staging script does a blanket `cpSync(src, dest, { recursive: true })` of `monaco-editor/min/vs` into `web/public/monaco`, and Next's static export copies `public/` wholesale into `out/`, so every byte lands in the Amplify artifact and is served under the year-long immutable rule. The staged tree measures 15 MB across 121 files. The single call site pins `language="python"` (runnable-editor.tsx:97) and no locale is ever configured, which makes several of the largest members permanently unreachable: `vs/assets/ts.worker-*.js` at 6.8 MB, `css.worker` at 1.0 MB, `html.worker` at 680 KB, `json.worker` at 376 KB (Monaco only spawns those for ts/js/css/html/json models), and twelve `vs/nls.messages.<locale>.js.js` bundles totalling ~1.5 MB. Roughly 10 of the 15 MB is deploy-artifact and hosting weight that no learner request can ever touch. The script's only correctness assertion is that `loader.js` exists (lines 42-46), so there is nothing today that would notice the tree growing further on an upgrade. _Fix:_ Give the existing copy an explicit filter instead of a blanket recurse: skip `assets/{ts,css,html,json}.worker-*` and the non-English `nls.messages.*` bundles (keep `vs/assets/editor.worker-*`, which the core does use). Since the script already fails loudly on a missing `loader.js`, extend that same pattern with an assertion on the resulting tree — the python tokenizer chunk present, and a total-size ceiling — so a future monaco upgrade that reshuffles `min/vs` fails the build rather than silently re-inflating the artifact. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (`scripts/stage-monaco.mjs:38-39`, `web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:97`, `scripts/stage-monaco.mjs:42-46`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** `RunResult.ok` is dead — the component re-derives success from `error` instead — `RunResult` declares `ok: boolean` and `runPython` sets it on every return path, but the sole consumer never reads it. RunnableEditor recomputes the same fact from `result.error`, in two places. That leaves two independent sources of truth for 'did this run fail', which can silently disagree if a future path returns `{ ok: false }` with no `error` string (the panel would render it as a success with '(no output)'). _Fix:_ Either drop `ok` from `RunResult` (making `error === undefined` the single predicate) or make the component read `result.ok` for its status branch. Prefer the drop — the discriminant is already carried by the optional `error`. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/pyodide-run.ts:11`, `web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:45`, `web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:134`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** `__setBootTimeoutMsForTests` is an unused export shipped in the production runtime module — The runtime exports a test-only setter for the boot watchdog that has zero callers anywhere in the repo — not in the Jest suite, not in the Playwright specs, not in the e2e fixture pages. Its sibling `__setRunTimeoutMsForTests` is genuinely used (unit tests and the py-challenge fixture). The docstring asserts it 'mirrors the run-timeout override above', implying a parity that does not exist, which will mislead the next reader into thinking the boot-failover path is exercised the way the run-timeout path is. _Fix:_ Delete the export (and the `bootTimeoutMs` indirection, folding it back to the constant), or give it the caller its docstring implies by scripting `FakeWorker.onBoot` to never reply in pyodide-runtime.test.ts. Do not leave it as an unreferenced production export. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:87`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:79`, `web/__tests__/lib/pyodide-runtime.test.ts:60-155`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** RunnableEditor re-derives one three-state machine four separate times in its JSX — The output panel has exactly three states (idle, booting/running, finished-ok-or-error), but the component expresses them four different ways: a `showOutput` value that is a `boolean | RunResult | null` union used as a truthiness test, a separate `status` ternary chain, and then an inline `busy && !result ? … : result ? … : null` inside the `<pre>`. Any future state (e.g. a queued run) has to be threaded through all four independently, and the union-typed `showOutput` defeats the type checker as a guard. _Fix:_ Compute a single `const phase: "idle" | "running" | "ok" | "error"` once and switch every branch (border class, dot, pre className, pre body) on it. `showOutput` becomes `phase !== "idle"` and returns a real boolean. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:44`, `web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:45`, `web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:102`, `web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:120`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** The learner-facing Python boot notice is duplicated verbatim and rendered on two different tiers — The string 'Booting Python (first run takes a few seconds)…' is hardcoded in both code-running widgets. Worse, the two render it differently: the runnable editor shows it as a muted caption, while the challenge widget smuggles it through as a `GradeResult` with `status: "wrong"`, which routes it through the warm/incorrect verdict styling. A learner who meets both widgets sees the same sentence once as a neutral status and once dressed as a wrong answer, and the challenge's transient state is semantically a false verdict. _Fix:_ Export the boot notice (and the `Couldn't start Python:` prefix) as constants from pyodide-runtime.ts, and give Challenge a dedicated `busy`-derived notice rather than a `status: "wrong"` result, so the same sentence carries the same tier in both widgets. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:121`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:113`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:392`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-run.ts:23`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** `PlayIcon` is declared a third time here, in a repo that centralized `CheckIcon` for exactly this reason — RunnableEditor declares a local `PlayIcon` whose path data is identical to the play glyph already declared in two other components. widget-ui explicitly took the opposite decision for the check glyph, noting it had been 'redeclared byte-identically in six modules, shipping the same markup in six separate dynamic chunks' — the same condition now holds for the play triangle across three code-split chunks. _Fix:_ Move `PlayIcon` into widget-ui alongside `CheckIcon` with the optional `playing` prop (the runnable editor simply omits it) and delete the three local copies. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:161`, `web/src/components/quantum/wavefunction-scrubber.tsx:30`, `web/src/components/playground/state-panel.tsx:23`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:38`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience]** A stalled network gives the learner up to 150 seconds of a disabled Run button under copy promising "a few seconds" — The boot path is a serial two-origin fallback, each bounded by its own 75-second watchdog, so a stalled (as opposed to failed) connection — captive portal, dead proxy, throttled link — costs up to 150 seconds before any error can surface. Throughout that entire window the learner sees one static string, "Booting Python (first run takes a few seconds)…", with the Run button disabled and no indication that anything is wrong or that a fallback is being tried. The asymmetry with the rest of the module is what makes this stand out: the timeout path got carefully written learner-facing copy explaining what happened and what to do, and the both-origins-failed path got an actionable remediation hint naming network blocks and ad-blockers — but the long wait that precedes either of them communicates nothing. Note this is genuinely the stalled case, not the offline case: fully offline, fetch rejects promptly and the actionable message arrives fast. _Fix:_ Escalate the existing status message rather than adding controls (no new affordance needed). Track elapsed time in RunnableEditor and swap the copy once it exceeds a threshold — around 10s, note that the runtime is a several-megabyte download and still in progress; once the same-origin attempt has failed, say a backup source is being tried. The signal already exists at the boundary; `runPython` need only surface a phase change. Also worth reconsidering whether 75s per origin is the right first-attempt budget now that the same-origin assets are immutable-cached and the common warm case completes in seconds — a shorter first-origin watchdog reaches the working fallback sooner on a stalled link. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:71`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:281-292`, `web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:120-123`, `web/src/components/quantum/runnable-editor.tsx:79-80`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)

### 16. Spaced-repetition review
