FIXED: `isValidCardState` (all numeric fields `Number.isFinite`) in review-schedule.ts; `getCardState` discards a failing record so the caller falls back to `newCard`. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`review-store.ts:57-64,86`) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Correctness]** DONE (PR pending) — graduating step shrank the interval on a passing "Hard" review (after "Easy"), violating SM-2 monotonicity. FIXED: a passing review floors the interval at the card's current stability. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`review-schedule.ts`) — added 2026-06-17
- [ ] **[Accessibility]** ~reviewer — "Show answer" disclosure has no `aria-expanded`/`aria-controls` and never moves focus to the revealed answer; nav-badge due-count + dashboard "N due" recompute silently (no aria-live); grade buttons are an unlabeled group and grading throws focus away. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`review-card.tsx:149-170`, `review-nav-badge.tsx`, `review-dashboard.tsx`) — added 2026-06-17
- [ ] **[Performance]** ~reviewer — nav badge runs a full localStorage scan + JSON.parse of every card on every root render; `review-card.tsx` hand-rolls its own store subscription (bypassing shared `subscribe()`). Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`review-nav-badge.tsx`, `review-card.tsx`) — added 2026-06-17


#### Feature 15 — 2026-07-20 Category C re-audit (20 adversarially-verified findings; independent skeptic per claim, default-refute, security and correctness claims re-derived against current tree state)

- [x] **[Performance]** Learner stdout is streamed to the main thread with no cap, so a print-heavy runaway floods postMessage and undoes the worker's responsiveness guarantee — The worker installs a batched stdout/stderr sink that posts one message per flush with no rate or volume limit, and the client dispatches each one for the full watchdog window. For the grader this is pure waste: gradePy calls runSerialized WITHOUT an onOutput sink, so every message is structured-cloned across the worker boundary, dispatched, and dropped on the floor. A submission like `while True: print(x)` therefore emits on the order of 10^5–10^6 messages over the 30s budget, saturating the main thread's event loop — the exact tab-freeze the worker was introduced to eliminate, now reached through the message channel instead of the CPU. The timeout e2e cannot catch it because its runaway case is silent. _Fix:_ Cap the stream at its source: track bytes emitted per run in the worker's `emit`, and once past a ceiling (a few hundred KB) stop posting and send one final truncation notice. That bounds both the postMessage storm and pyodide-run's chunk array with one change, and keeps the grader — which wants no output at all — from paying for output it discards. Extend py-grader-timeout.e2e.ts's runaway to a printing loop so the cap is proven. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`web/public/pyodide.worker.js:102-104`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-grader.ts:94`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:181-182`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-run.ts:28,30,33`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** The Python boot notice is encoded as a "wrong" GradeResult, so a loading message renders in the not-quite verdict panel — `runPy` has a dedicated `busy` boolean but does not use it to render anything. Instead it stuffs the transient boot notice into `result` as a fabricated `{ status: "wrong" }` GradeResult. `GradeStatus` is the grader's verdict vocabulary ("solved" | "wrong" | "error"), not a UI lifecycle state, and the render path keys the panel colour straight off `result.status`. The consequence is visible on every tier:"py" challenge: while Pyodide boots (seconds, by design on first run) the learner sees "Booting Python…" inside the warm amber panel that means "your answer is not quite right". Worse, a learner who has already solved and clicks Check again watches their green "Correct" panel flip to amber before any grading has happened. The `busy` flag meanwhile does nothing but disable the button. _Fix:_ Render the boot notice from the existing `busy` state instead of laundering it through `result`: keep a `notice: string | null` (or derive from `busy`) and render it in the same DOM position with a neutral tone (`VERDICT_STYLES.error` is already the neutral `--field`/`text-caption` recipe, or a plain `text-caption` line). Leave `result` untouched while a run is in flight so a prior verdict is not recoloured mid-boot. This keeps `GradeStatus` meaning only what the grader returns. Both e2e specs assert `getByRole("status").first()` contains "Booting Python", so keep the notice inside the same first status region and they pass unchanged. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:113`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:191`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:395-396`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:56,175`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** challenge.tsx never clears its stale verdict or personal-best caption on edit, a pattern both sibling DSL-editor Reps carry — The challenge textarea's `onChange` does nothing but `setCode`. Every other editor-driven Rep in the family explicitly clears the stale readout when the learner changes their input, and debug-circuit-widget documents this as a deliberate cross-widget convention it inherited from bloch-target. So on a challenge: a learner gets "Correct — your circuit prepares the target state." plus "Solved in 3 gates — your best.", then edits the circuit to try a shorter solution, and both the green verdict and the gate-count caption sit unchanged beneath code they no longer describe. The caption is the more concrete misstatement, since it names a specific gate count for a circuit that is no longer in the editor. _Fix:_ Adopt the debug-circuit onChange body verbatim in challenge.tsx: on change, `if (result !== null) setResult(null)` and `if (solvedGates !== null) setSolvedGates(null)`. No new behaviour — this is the third widget adopting a convention the other two already document. Note the persistent `solved` badge must NOT be cleared (it is solved-once-ever, per use-persistent-solved.ts:29-32); only the per-attempt verdict and caption. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:166`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:188-195,209-216`, `web/src/components/quantum/debug-circuit-widget.tsx:182-190`, `web/src/components/quantum/bloch-target-widget.tsx:42`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Architecture]** Reference-target validation is duplicated verbatim across both grader tiers, and its history shows the double-edit tax is real — `gradeTs` and `gradePy` each carry their own copy of the identical reference-circuit gate: parse the target, bail on parse error, bail on `hasTheta`, compute `n`, bail past `MAX_QUBITS`, then `simulate(opsFor(target, 0), n)`. All three learner-facing message strings are byte-identical across the two files. Only the derivation of `n` genuinely differs (spec.qubits/target.n/learner.n vs log2 of the Python state vector length). This is not hypothetical drift risk: the ledger records both prior fixes to this block as double-edits — the hasTheta [Correctness] item cites `challenge-grade.ts:38, pyodide-grader.ts:53`, and the MAX_QUBITS [Security/Resilience] item cites "both grader entries". A third change to reference-circuit validation that lands in only one tier produces two grader tiers that disagree about whether the same authored fence is valid, and nothing in the suite would catch it: the two guards are asserted independently in two separate test files. _Fix:_ Export one helper from challenge-grade.ts (the kernel module both tiers already depend on — pyodide-grader.ts:38 imports `GradeResult` from it, so the direction is established): `resolveTarget(spec, extraWidth: number): { state: Complex[]; n: number } | { error: string }`, taking the caller-specific width contribution as its only parameter. `gradeTs` passes `Math.max(spec.qubits ?? 0, learner.n)`, `gradePy` passes `Math.round(Math.log2(learnerState.length)) || 1`. Both message sets and the MAX_QUBITS cap then live once. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/challenge-grade.ts:38-59`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-grader.ts:105-123`, `web/__tests__/lib/challenge-grade.test.ts:83`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The FSRS schedule note is copy-pasted across six Rep widgets and has already drifted on spacing — The nested "Reviewed / Added to your review" ternary — surface check, then a `<= 1` singular/plural branch on each arm — appears in six widgets as a structurally identical block. It has already drifted: challenge.tsx and debug-circuit-widget.tsx render it at `mt-2`, while bloch-target, cost-estimate, expectation, and predict render it at `mt-1`. A learner meeting a challenge and a predict Rep in one lesson sees the same sentence at two different offsets. This is precisely the class of duplication widget-ui.tsx exists to absorb, and its own docstrings record the same failure mode twice — VerdictBadge was "previously copy-pasted byte-identically across six widgets", and EyebrowLabel was "copy-pasted across nine files and had already drifted on the dark-mode color". The schedule note is the same shape of duplication at the same multiplicity, simply not yet lifted. The adjacent "Solved in N gates — your best is M. Can you match it?" caption is a second, smaller instance (challenge.tsx and debug-circuit-widget.tsx, differing only in the verb Solved/Fixed). _Fix:_ Add a `ScheduleNote({ surface, days })` component to widget-ui.tsx alongside VerdictBadge and have all six widgets render it, settling the margin on one value (the four-widget `mt-1` majority). Optionally add `BestGatesNote({ verb, gates, best })` for the two-copy caption. Purely a lift of existing markup — no wording or behaviour change — and it retires the spacing drift in the same edit. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:197-207`, `web/src/components/quantum/debug-circuit-widget.tsx:233-243`, `web/src/components/quantum/predict-widget.tsx:246-247`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:356-359`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[UI]** The Pyodide boot notice is painted in the wrong-answer verdict tone — For the four shipped tier:"py" Reps, the first Check kicks off a multi-second Pyodide boot. The busy state is expressed by shoving a fake grade result into the verdict panel with `status: "wrong"`, so the panel renders in the system's warm "not quite" tone — warm left border, warm tint, warm text — for the entire boot. The learner's first experience of the Python tier is their submission appearing in the incorrect-answer color before it has even been graded. The neutral tier already exists in the same record (`error`) and the repo's other Pyodide surface renders the identical copy in `text-caption`. _Fix:_ Stop encoding the busy state as a GradeResult. Hold it in the existing `busy` flag and render the boot line through the neutral treatment runnable-editor already uses (`text-caption`, or `VERDICT_STYLES.error`'s `--bd`/`--field` recipe) so no pre-grade state is ever tinted warm. Both themes are covered because both tiers are token-driven. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/pyodide-grader.ts:1-13`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:113`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:191`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:395-396`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[UX]** A solved/wrong verdict survives edits to the circuit it described — challenge.tsx's textarea onChange only calls setCode. Every other graded Rep in the family clears its stale readout on input because the verdict describes code that no longer exists. In challenge this is worst after a solve: "Correct — your circuit prepares the target state." plus "Solved in 2 gates — your best." stay pinned under the editor while the learner keeps typing, so the card asserts a correctness claim about a circuit it never graded. The `Solved` header badge stays lit too. _Fix:_ Mirror debug-circuit-widget's onChange: on keystroke, clear `result` and `solvedGates` (leaving the persistent solved-once-ever flag and the FSRS card untouched). If the wrong-answer hint is judged worth keeping visible while the learner works, clear only the `solved` verdict and the gate-count caption — those are the two that become false statements. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:166`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:188-216`, `web/src/components/quantum/debug-circuit-widget.tsx:184-189`, `web/src/components/quantum/bloch-target-widget.tsx:128-135`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** A learner circuit with non-finite amplitudes makes the Python grader emit invalid JSON, surfacing as a garbled 'Your code raised: Unexpected token N' — The grading harness serializes the learner's state vector with `json.dumps` at its default `allow_nan=True`, which emits bare `NaN` / `Infinity` tokens. Those are not valid JSON, so the JS `JSON.parse` inside gradePy's try block throws a SyntaxError, and the catch — which cannot tell a grader-side decode failure from a learner exception — reports it under 'Your code raised:'. This is learner-reachable: qcsim's rotation gates accept any float with no finiteness validation, so `circuit.ry(0, np.arcsin(x))` with |x|&gt;1 (a completely ordinary angle-encoding idiom, and `qml-angle-encode-py-1` is a shipped tier:"py" Rep about exactly that) produces NaN amplitudes and this error. The learner is told their code raised a JSON parse error it never raised. _Fix:_ Make the failure legible instead of misattributed: bind `__grader_dumps` to a partial of `json.dumps` with `allow_nan=False` so the harness fails inside Python with a named error, and add an explicit finite-amplitude check on the decoded array in gradePy that returns a teachable error verdict ('your circuit produced a non-finite amplitude — check for an out-of-domain angle like arcsin of a value above 1') rather than routing a decode failure through the 'Your code raised:' path. Add the unparseable-payload case to pyodide-grader.test.ts. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/pyodide-grader.ts:66-69`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-grader.ts:94`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-grader.ts:102`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:295-308`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** Solve banners are never cleared, so a follow-up wrong attempt renders 'Not quite' directly above 'Solved in 2 gates' and 'Added to your review' — apply() sets the `scheduled` and `solvedGates` state on a solve but never resets them on a subsequent 'wrong' or 'error' result, and both blocks render purely on a non-null check. The widget's own copy explicitly invites the follow-up attempt that triggers this — after a solve it prints 'your best is N. Can you match it?' — so the very next Check with a longer or wrong circuit produces a card that simultaneously says the attempt failed and that it succeeded. This is worst on the /review surface, where the whole point of the mount is a fresh re-attempt. _Fix:_ Clear both in apply()'s non-solved branches (`setScheduled(null); setSolvedGates(null);`) so the card shows exactly one coherent verdict per Check, and add a solve-then-wrong case to challenge.test.tsx asserting the schedule note and gate-count line are gone. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:90-109`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:197-207`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:209-216`, `web/__tests__/components/quantum/challenge.test.tsx:141-158`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience / Mobile UX]** The challenge editor is the repo's only free-form Python input without mobile input-assist suppression, so an iOS learner's tier:"py" submission is auto-capitalized into a NameError — `challenge.tsx`'s `<textarea>` carries only `spellCheck={false}`. That attribute does not disable iOS/Android auto-capitalization, autocorrect, or smart-quote substitution. On the DSL tier that is harmless (parseProgram uppercases gate names anyway), but the SAME textarea is the input for the four shipped `tier:"py"` Reps, where it receives free-form Braket Python. iOS capitalizes the first letter after a newline or period, so a learner typing the starter's next line gets `From braket.circuits import Circuit` or `Circuit = Circuit()`, and smart quotes turn a string literal into U+201C. Every one of those reaches `gradePy` and comes back through the misattributing wrapper at pyodide-grader.ts:102 as `Your code raised: invalid syntax` — the learner sees the grader blaming code they typed correctly. The repo's OTHER Python input surface does not have this problem: `code-editor.tsx` mounts Monaco (code-editor.tsx:88-108), and Monaco's own input element sets all four attributes explicitly. _Fix:_ Add `autoCapitalize="off" autoCorrect="off" autoComplete="off"` to the textarea at challenge.tsx:162-169, matching the attribute set Monaco already applies to the sibling Python editor. Three attributes, no behavior change on desktop, no new dependency. The same three are harmless (and arguably correct) on the ts tier, so no tier branch is needed. A one-line assertion in the existing `challenge.test.tsx` pinning the four attributes on the textbox keeps it from regressing. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:162-169`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:125-130`, `web/src/components/code-editor.tsx:88-108`, `web/node_modules/monaco-editor/esm/vs/editor/browser/controller/editContext/textArea/textAreaEditContext.js:127-130`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** The allowedGates whitelist check is implemented twice, inline in one grader and as a named helper in the other — debug-circuit-grade.ts factored the gate-whitelist scan into a small named `gateViolation(parsed, allowedGates)` that returns the offending gate name or null, and calls it three times (learner, broken, target). challenge-grade.ts predates that and still inlines the same loop — uppercase the whitelist into a Set, scan `parsed.gates`, return an error naming the gate and listing the allowed set. The two are semantically identical but their messages have drifted apart in wording ("isn't allowed for this challenge" vs "isn't allowed for this Rep"), and challenge-grade's version returns early from inside the loop while debug's returns a value the caller formats. Since debug-circuit-grade.ts already imports from challenge-grade.ts, the dependency edge for sharing is established and unused. _Fix:_ Move `gateViolation` into challenge-grade.ts as an exported helper and have both graders call it, each formatting its own message from the returned gate name (keeping the challenge/Rep noun difference at the call site where it belongs). Removes one of two implementations of the same rule. Verified safe against content: none of the four shipped tier:"py" fences declares `allowedGates`, so this touches only the tier:"ts" path. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/challenge-grade.ts:24-36`, `web/src/lib/debug-circuit-grade.ts:26-33`, `web/src/lib/debug-circuit-grade.ts:17`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** The editor has no programmatic description — the enforced gate restriction and the tier note never reach the field — The textarea's only accessible name is the sr-only label "Your circuit", and it carries no aria-describedby. Two pieces of information a learner needs before typing are rendered as loose siblings: the `allowedGates` list (which the grader hard-rejects on, returning an error rather than a wrong answer) and, on the py tier, the note that this field expects real Braket Python rather than the qsim DSL. A screen-reader user who tabs straight to the field hears "Your circuit, edit text" and has no statement of either — they can only discover the gate restriction by submitting and failing. _Fix:_ Give the prompt, the allowed-gates line, and the tier note stable ids off the existing `useId` and point the textarea's `aria-describedby` at the ones that are present. Purely additive attributes; no visual change and no new markup. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:159-169`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:150-157`, `web/src/lib/challenge-grade.ts:24-36`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:181-185`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** Check destroys keyboard focus on the py tier and never restores it — `disabled={busy}` is applied to the button the learner just activated. Per the HTML spec a disabled control is not focusable, so the browser blurs it and focus falls to `<body>`. On the py tier that gap is the whole Pyodide boot plus grade — seconds — and when `busy` clears nothing refocuses the button. A keyboard or screen-reader learner who presses Enter on Check is silently returned to the top of the document and has to tab back down through the entire lesson to reach their own answer. The ts tier is unaffected (`busy` never flips). _Fix:_ Keep the control focusable: drop the `disabled` attribute in favour of `aria-busy={busy}` plus an early `if (busy) return;` at the top of `onCheck`, retaining `disabled:opacity-60`'s visual weight via a `busy`-conditional opacity class. Same guard against double-submit, no focus loss. runnable-editor.tsx:79 has the identical pattern and would benefit from the same change. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:175`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:111-122`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:1-220`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Security / Resilience]** Cached challenge `kind` is looked up on a prototype-exposed object, so a corrupt or sync-adopted record crashes the whole /review page — challenge.tsx caches its Rep kind + raw fence source into localStorage so /review can re-mount the live widget. The read path never validates that cached record, and the dashboard then indexes a plain object literal with the untrusted string. Own-property misses fall back correctly, but INHERITED Object.prototype keys ('constructor', 'toString', 'valueOf') resolve to functions, so `Live` is truthy and React renders `<Object source="..."/>` — a component returning a plain object. There is no error boundary anywhere in web/src/app, so the render throw takes down the entire /review route, not just the one card. The value is not merely locally corruptible: mergeSnapshots adopts a remote-only `qc:card-content:*` verbatim, so the cross-device sync blob is an input to this lookup. _Fix:_ Validate the record where it is read, matching the isValidCardState precedent already in the same file: have getCardContent return null unless `prompt`/`answer` are strings and `kind` (when present) is one of the six CardKind literals. Equivalently or additionally, make the dashboard lookup prototype-safe with `Object.hasOwn(LIVE_WIDGETS, content.kind)` before indexing, and extend the existing corrupt-kind test with a `kind: "constructor"` case so the guarantee the comment already claims is actually pinned. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/review-store.ts:61-68`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:75-84`, `web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:223-224`, `web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:225,254`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** Runtime crash and reset messages are wrapped in 'Your code raised:', the same misattribution the timeout path was deliberately fixed to avoid — gradePy passes PythonTimeoutError through verbatim precisely because a watchdog kill is not a Python exception — the header comment spells this out. But the runtime raises two other plain Errors that are equally not learner exceptions: the post-boot worker crash message and the already-dead-runtime reset message. Both fall to the generic branch and are prefixed 'Your code raised:', so a learner whose runtime was killed by a different widget on the page (the runtime is a shared module-level singleton) is told their circuit raised 'The Python environment was reset because an earlier run timed out.' The reachable path is concrete: a runnable editor's timeout kills the shared handle that gradePy is holding, and the pending run rejects through Pyodide.run's dead-check. _Fix:_ Give the two lifecycle messages the same pass-through treatment as the timeout — either promote them to a shared error class alongside PythonTimeoutError (e.g. a PythonRuntimeError base that gradePy checks with one instanceof), or have Pyodide.kill/dead reject with PythonTimeoutError's sibling type. Both messages are already learner-facing and complete; they just need to skip the prefix. Add the two cases to pyodide-grader.test.ts next to the existing timeout assertion. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:156-158`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:163`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-grader.ts:99-102`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-grader.ts:95-98`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** The 'Booting Python' progress message is typed as a wrong-answer verdict and understates the boot budget by two orders of magnitude — runPy publishes its interim boot notice through the same verdict channel as a graded result, with `status: "wrong"`. That renders it in the warm/amber tone the widget reserves for an incorrect answer, so the learner's first impression of a Python challenge is a wrong-answer-styled card — even though a neutral informational tone already exists in the same style map. The copy also promises 'a few seconds' while the runtime's own budget allows 75s same-origin plus 75s CDN fallback before it even errors, so on a blocked or slow network the learner stares at a wrong-toned 'a few seconds' banner for over two minutes with no other signal. _Fix:_ Render the interim notice in the neutral tone rather than the wrong-answer tone — the smallest match to the existing idiom is to publish it as `status: "error"` (already the neutral style) or, cleaner, keep the boot notice out of the verdict state entirely and drive it from the `busy` flag that already exists on line 112. Soften the copy to not promise a fixed duration ('Starting Python in your browser…'), since the code path allows far longer than 'a few seconds'. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:113`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:395-397`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:71-72`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-runtime.ts:281-292`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Correctness / Content integrity]** allowedGates is rendered to the learner as a rule on every tier, but gradePy never enforces it — only the ts grader does — `challenge.tsx` renders the "Allowed gates: …" caption whenever `spec.allowedGates` is non-empty, with no tier check. `gradeTs` enforces the whitelist against the learner's parsed gates. `gradePy` does not reference `spec.allowedGates` at all — it reads only `spec.target.program` and the learner's returned state vector. The only place the whitelist touches a py Rep is `rep-schema.ts:157`, which grades the AUTHOR's target through `gradeTs` — so the constraint is validated against the reference circuit and then silently dropped for the learner. Author a `tier:"py"` Rep with `allowedGates: ["H", "CNOT"]` and the corpus gate accepts it (guide-reps.test.ts's allowed-key list permits `allowedGates` and `tier` on the same fence, and its py branch checks only manifest membership), the widget displays the restriction, and a learner who solves it with `.rx(0, 3.14159)` is told "Correct". No shipped py fence sets allowedGates today, which is why the trap is invisible — but nothing prevents the next one. _Fix:_ Close it at the authoring gate rather than the grader (the grader cannot inspect arbitrary Python without new machinery): in `parseChallenge`, reject a spec that carries both `tier: "py"` and a non-empty `allowedGates` with a message naming the reason — the same shape as the existing explicit-id rejection at challenge-schema.ts:38-40. That makes the combination fail loudly in `guide-reps.test.ts` and `rep-schema.ts` the way every other unshippable spec already does, and the widget's caption then only ever appears when the rule is real. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:150-157`, `web/src/lib/challenge-grade.ts:24-36`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-grader.ts:72-131`, `web/src/lib/rep-schema.ts:157`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** An unrecognized `tier` value is silently coerced to "ts", so a typo turns a Python challenge into a DSL one and CI cannot see it — `parseChallenge` resolves the tier with `data.tier === "py" ? "py" : "ts"` — every value that is not the exact string `"py"` becomes `"ts"` with no error. `tier: "Py"`, `"python"`, or `"pyodide"` therefore ships as a DSL challenge. Both CI gates then pass it: guide-reps.test.ts's key check only asks whether `tier` is a KNOWN KEY (never validates its value); its py-vs-manifest 1:1 test reads the raw JSON and compares `tier === "py"`, so a typo'd fence simply isn't counted as py and the manifest stays balanced; and the ts branch's degenerate-content guard asserts the starter does NOT solve — a Braket-Python starter fails to parse as DSL, yielding status `"error"`, which satisfies `not.toBe("solved")`. The fence ships, and the first learner to click Check gets `Your circuit: unknown gate "from"`. This is exactly the failure class the file already guards for `id` (challenge-schema.ts:38-40 rejects a missing id precisely because a silent fallback orphans learner state); `tier` never got the same treatment. _Fix:_ In `parseChallenge`, return `{ error: 'challenge "tier" must be "ts" or "py"' }` when `data.tier` is present and is neither literal — one conditional beside the existing id/prompt/target guards. Every consumer (widget ErrorCard, rep-schema, guide-reps.test) already renders or fails on a parse error, so the typo becomes a red CI run instead of a broken lesson. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/challenge-schema.ts:48`, `web/src/lib/challenge-schema.ts:38-40`, `web/__tests__/content/guide-reps.test.ts:96-105`, `web/__tests__/content/guide-reps.test.ts:137-146`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code / Stale docs]** The qsim DSL's own module docblock demonstrates inline comments in three of its five example lines, and WS-B7's strict-arity check now rejects all three — The Category B arity fix added `expectArity`, which throws on any token past an instruction's arity. The line filter only drops lines that START with `#` (`!l.startsWith("#")`), so a mid-line `#` is now just an extra token. That means the parser's own documented examples no longer parse: `qubits 2 # optional; inferred…` splits to four tokens and throws `qubits: unexpected extra token "#" — one instruction per line`, and the same for the two `RX`/`RY` example lines. The header line above them still asserts "'#' starts a comment". This is the grader's input path — `gradeTs` calls `parseProgram(learnerSource)` at challenge-grade.ts:19 — and the Challenge widget offers no syntax help of its own, unlike the playground bench, which was updated to state the narrower rule precisely. So a learner who annotates their circuit gets a hard error where the previous parser silently accepted it, with nothing in the widget explaining the restriction. The Category B ledger entry for this fix explicitly floated tolerating a `#` remainder and the option was not taken, but the docblock was never corrected either way. _Fix:_ Correct the docblock at qsim-dsl.ts:7-12 so it states the rule the parser actually enforces — move the three trailing `#` annotations off the example lines and change the header to "a line starting with '#' is a comment", matching compose-panel.tsx:206 verbatim. That removes the self-contradiction in one edit and leaves the (deliberate) strictness intact. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/qsim-dsl.ts:7-12`, `web/src/components/quantum/qsim-dsl.ts:43-55`, `web/src/components/quantum/qsim-dsl.ts:62`, `web/src/lib/challenge-grade.ts:19`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Stale docs]** The feature ledger and the py-challenge fixture still describe tier:"py" as unshipped and CI-rejected, nine months of content after PR #167 shipped four py Reps — Two load-bearing descriptions of this feature still document the pre-#167 world. The feature-optimization table row for #15 states that `gradePy` is wired "but no shipped content uses `tier:"py"` (`rep-schema` rejects it pending a CI story)" — both halves are now false: four GUIDE fences ship the tier, and rep-schema gates on the e2e-coverage manifest rather than rejecting. Its "Where it lives" column also omits the two files that now carry the tier's contract (`py-reps.ts`, `py-challenge-fences.ts`). Separately, the py-challenge fixture's docblock calls itself "the ONLY page that mounts a tier:"py" challenge", which stopped being true when the py-reps fixture page and the four lesson fences landed. This matters because the table row is the scoping input for every future audit of this feature — an auditor reading it would conclude the entire Tier-B path is dead code and skip it. _Fix:_ Rewrite the row 15 "What it does" cell to describe the shipped state (both tiers live; py gated on the `py-reps` e2e-coverage manifest with per-Rep browser proof), add `py-reps.ts` and `py-challenge-fences.ts` to its "Where it lives" column, and bump its Last-reviewed date. Amend the fixture docblock at py-challenge/page.tsx:6 to say it is the fixture for the grader's verdict/watchdog SEMANTICS (the role the sibling page does not cover), rather than claiming exclusivity. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`docs/feature-optimization.md:40`, `docs/feature-optimization.md:882`, `web/src/lib/py-reps.ts:24-29`, `web/src/lib/rep-schema.ts:148-155`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)

#### Feature 16 — 2026-07-20 Category C re-audit (18 adversarially-verified findings; independent skeptic per claim, default-refute, security and correctness claims re-derived against current tree state)

- [x] **[Cleaner code]** The review schedule-note copy is copy-pasted byte-identically across all six graded Rep widgets — and its wrapper has already drifted — Every graded Rep renders the same 6-line nested ternary that produces the feature's core user-facing schedule copy ("Reviewed — next review in N days." / "Added to your review — back in N days."). The string body is byte-identical in all six files, but the surrounding markup has ALREADY diverged on two axes: the top margin (`mt-2` in challenge/debug vs `mt-1` in predict/bloch/cost/expect) and the live-region semantics (challenge puts `role="status"` on the `<p>` itself, debug wraps it in its own `role="status"` div, the other four rely on an ancestor outcome div). This is precisely the failure mode `widget-ui.tsx` was built to stop — and on /review the six widgets are stacked in one roster, so a learner sees the drifted spacing side by side in a single session. _Fix:_ Add a `ScheduleNote({ days, surface })` component to widget-ui.tsx alongside VerdictBadge — it owns the `<p role="status" className="mt-2 text-xs text-caption animate-fade-up">` wrapper and the day-phrasing ternary. Replace the six inline blocks with `{scheduled !== null && <ScheduleNote days={scheduled} surface={surface} />}`, dropping ~42 lines and unifying the drifted margin and live-region semantics. review-card.tsx's variant can take the same component with a third `surface` value (or keep its copy but reuse the day-phrasing helper) so the `=== 1` / `<= 1` boundary stops differing. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:197-207`, `web/src/components/quantum/predict-widget.tsx:239-249`, `web/src/components/quantum/bloch-target-widget.tsx:250-260`, `web/src/components/quantum/cost-estimate-widget.tsx:250-260`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** Three separate implementations of "parse a raw CardState string" — and the one in review-card.tsx skips the validation guard the other two share — review-store.getCardState parses a stored record and discards it unless `isValidCardState` passes — a guard added deliberately (feature-optimization.md item 16, [closed][Resilience]) so a corrupt-but-valid-JSON record cannot feed NaN into the scheduler. progress-merge.ts carries a private `parseCard` that is a line-for-line copy of that logic. review-card.tsx's `useCardState` carries a THIRD copy that omits the guard entirely: it does `JSON.parse(raw) as CardState` and returns it. A stored `{}` therefore yields a non-null state, `reviewedBefore` is true, and the header renders "reviewed undefined×" — the exact class of record the guard was written to reject. _Fix:_ Export a pure `parseCardState(raw: string | null): CardState | null` from review-schedule.ts (it is storage-free and clock-free, matching that module's stated contract). Have review-store.getCardState become `parseCardState(getCardStateRaw(id))`, delete progress-merge's private `parseCard`, and have review-card's useMemo call it — which also closes the unvalidated-record hole for free. Note this is distinct from the recorded [OPEN][Performance] item, which covers review-card's hand-rolled *subscription*, not its parse path. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/review-store.ts:70-81`, `web/src/lib/progress-merge.ts:138-145`, `web/src/components/quantum/review-card.tsx:85-92`, `web/src/components/quantum/review-card.tsx:135,144-148`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** `opacity-80` on reviewed roster items drops caption and verdict text below WCAG AA in both themes — After a card is graded, review-dashboard wraps the entire still-mounted widget subtree in `opacity-80`. Group opacity composites the whole subtree — card background AND text — against the page, which multiplies every foreground contrast ratio inside it. The sticky roster exists specifically so the learner can READ the outcome after grading, so this is not incidental/disabled text and WCAG 1.4.3's exceptions do not apply. The repo's contrast guards (caption-contrast.test.ts, token-contrast.test.ts) scan class strings for token spellings and cannot see a wrapper opacity, so this regression is invisible to CI. _Fix:_ Stop expressing done-ness with subtree opacity. The "Reviewed" chip at review-dashboard.tsx:239-243 already carries that signal unambiguously; drop `opacity-80` entirely, or move the de-emphasis onto non-text chrome only (e.g. a muted border / removed shadow on the card shell) so no text ratio changes. If a visual dim is still wanted, apply it to a background layer rather than a group containing text. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:252`, `web/src/app/globals.css:171,226`, `web/src/components/quantum/error-card.tsx:16`, `web/__tests__/caption-contrast.test.ts:75-84`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Security / Resilience]** Unvalidated `kind` from localStorage indexes the live-widget map through the prototype chain, crashing the whole /review route — `review-dashboard.tsx` looks up `LIVE_WIDGETS[content.kind]` on a plain object literal, using a `kind` string that was never validated on the way out of localStorage. An inherited key resolves to a truthy function instead of `undefined`, so the documented "corrupt kind falls back to the recall card" guarantee silently fails and the page throws during render. With `kind: "constructor"`, `LIVE_WIDGETS["constructor"]` is `Object`; `Live` is truthy; React invokes it as a function component; `Object({source})` returns a plain object; React throws "Objects are not valid as a React child". Because `src/app` contains no `error.tsx` or `global-error.tsx`, one poisoned card takes out the entire /review surface rather than degrading to a text card. The existing regression test gives false confidence: it uses `kind: "not-a-kind"`, an own-property miss that CANNOT reach the prototype chain, so it passes while the hole stays open. `kindLabel = KIND_LABELS[content.kind!]` on the same line has the identical defect and leaks `"function Object() { [native code] }"` into the sr-only announcement. _Fix:_ Gate both lookups on own-property membership instead of raw indexing: `const Live = content.source && content.kind && Object.hasOwn(LIVE_WIDGETS, content.kind) ? LIVE_WIDGETS[content.kind] : undefined;`, and the same `Object.hasOwn` guard before `KIND_LABELS[content.kind]`. Then re-point the existing corrupt-kind test at a prototype key (`kind: "constructor"`) so it actually protects the guarantee its name claims, and add a sibling case for `"__proto__"`. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:223-224`, `web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:225`, `web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:254`, `web/src/lib/review-store.ts:61-68`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Correctness]** ReviewCard's "reviewed N×" header prints the FSRS success streak, so it reads "reviewed 0×" on a card with a long history — `review-card.tsx:144-148` renders the card's history as `reviewed {state.reps === 1 ? "once" : `${state.reps}×`}`, treating `reps` as a lifetime review count. It is not: `review-schedule.ts:21` documents `reps` as "Consecutive successful reviews (reset to 0 on a lapse)", and `review-schedule.ts:102` implements exactly that — `const reps = rating === "again" ? 0 : state.reps + 1;`. The failure is ordinary, not exotic: grade a card "Again" (the whole point of the Again button), and `schedule()` sets `dueEpochDay = today + 1`, so the card is due tomorrow. On that next mount — in the lesson, or as the recall fallback on /review — `reviewedBefore` is true (`review-card.tsx:135`, `state !== null`) and the header tells a learner who has reviewed that card six times that they have "reviewed 0×". The same mislabel produces "reviewed once" for a card reviewed four times, lapsed, then passed. The one piece of history the card surfaces is the one number guaranteed to be wrong for any card the learner has ever struggled with — precisely the cards spaced repetition exists to serve. _Fix:_ Say what the field means instead of mislabeling it. Word the header as the streak the value actually is (e.g. "6 in a row", suppressed entirely at `reps === 0`, where there is no streak to report), so a lapsed card shows nothing rather than a false zero. Do not synthesize a lifetime count from `reps + lapses` — that undercounts any card with passes before its last lapse (good, good, again, good yields 2 for 4 real reviews) — and do not add a new counter field to CardState; the honest refinement is the label, not new stored state. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/review-card.tsx:144-148`, `web/src/components/quantum/review-card.tsx:135`, `web/src/lib/review-schedule.ts:21`, `web/src/lib/review-schedule.ts:102`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Tests]** The content-cache write that puts all 28 authored qcards on /review is asserted by no test — while all six graded Reps have exactly that test — `review-card.tsx:113-115` is the sole writer of `qc:card-content:` for authored qcards: `useEffect(() => { if (spec) setCardContent(spec.id, { prompt: spec.prompt, answer: spec.answer }); }, [spec])`. Without it the dashboard's `getCardContent(id)` returns null and every authored card silently vanishes from the roster (`review-dashboard.tsx:221-222`). That effect is load-bearing for 28 shipped cards — four `qcard` fences in each of the seven GUIDEs (00-prereqs through 06-hybrid-jobs). It is covered by nothing. `__tests__/components/quantum/review-card.test.tsx` is 67 lines and six cases covering reveal, grading, the re-grade no-op and two parse errors; not one of them reads `qc:card-content:`. The dashboard suite cannot catch the regression either, because it seeds content by hand (`__tests__/components/review-dashboard.test.tsx:38` writes the content key directly in `seedDueCard`). The asymmetry is stark: every one of the six graded Reps has this exact test — `__tests__/components/quantum/challenge.test.tsx:130-138`, "caches its kind + raw fence source so /review can re-mount the live widget", asserting both `content.kind` and `content.source`. Delete or break ReviewCard's effect and the entire suite stays green while a quarter of the review corpus stops appearing on the page. _Fix:_ Add the mirror of challenge.test.tsx's caching test to review-card.test.tsx: render a card, then assert `qc:card-content:<id>` parses to the fence's prompt and answer. It closes the one untested link in the authored-qcard path and pins the store contract (`{prompt, answer}`, no kind/source) that review-dashboard's live-vs-recall dispatch at `review-dashboard.tsx:224` branches on. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/review-card.tsx:113-115`, `web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:221-222`, `web/__tests__/components/quantum/review-card.test.tsx:14-67`, `web/__tests__/components/review-dashboard.test.tsx:38`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** review-dashboard's LIVE_WIDGETS map is 50 lines of boilerplate: six wrapper components that exist only to pin surface="review" — The dashboard declares six `dynamic()` calls and then six named wrapper function components whose entire body is `<XLive source={source} surface="review" />`. The wrappers exist only because the local `SourceWidget` type is `ComponentType<{ source: string }>` and so cannot carry `surface`. All six underlying widgets already accept an optional `surface?: "lesson" | "review"` prop, so the wrapper layer buys nothing. The file's own comment says it is "Lazy like widget-fence.tsx", but widget-fence factored this exact shape into a two-line `lazyWidget(factory, minH)` helper and a flat record — the dashboard hand-expands it instead, and re-declares the same `SourceWidget` type alias that widget-fence already declares. _Fix:_ Widen the local type to `ComponentType<{ source: string; surface?: "lesson" | "review" }>` (structurally satisfied by all six widgets, since their extra props are optional), map the six kinds straight to their `dynamic()` components with no wrappers, and pass `surface="review"` at the single call site. Fold the repeated `dynamic(..., { ssr: false, loading: liveSkeleton })` into a local one-line helper mirroring widget-fence's `lazyWidget`. Move the resulting registry into its own module so review-dashboard.tsx keeps only the roster state machine and presentation. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:53-76`, `web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:83-102`, `web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:78`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-fence.tsx:59-64`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** Six one-line *CardId builders duplicate a namespacing scheme the repo already single-sources as solvedFlagKey(kind, id) — challenge-review.ts exports six functions that each return `` `${kind}:${id}` `` for the six Rep kinds, with ~40 lines of near-identical docstring explaining the same collision-avoidance rationale six times. use-persistent-solved.ts already solved this exact problem generically for the sibling key space: one `solvedFlagKey(kind: CardKind, id: string)` typed against the CardKind union. The six *CardId builders take and return bare `string`, so nothing ties the card-id prefix vocabulary to `CardKind` — a seventh Rep kind can be added to the CardKind union (and to KIND_LABELS, and to LIVE_WIDGETS) while its id builder silently uses a prefix that matches nothing, and the compiler will not notice. _Fix:_ Replace the six builders with one `cardIdFor(kind: CardKind, id: string): string` in challenge-review.ts, mirroring solvedFlagKey exactly, and keep one docstring for the shared rationale. Each widget then calls `cardIdFor("challenge", spec.id)` — the same literal it already passes to usePersistentSolved on the adjacent line — so the two key spaces are provably built from the same typed vocabulary and a new kind cannot half-land. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/challenge-review.ts:12-14,47-49,68-70,78-80,89-91,99-101`, `web/src/components/quantum/use-persistent-solved.ts:29-32`, `web/src/lib/review-store.ts:32`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:64`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** The dashboard's stringly-typed snapshot is encoded in one place and decoded by two different parsers — The `"dueIds|total"` snapshot encoding is produced at one site but consumed by two independent decoders in the same file: a `dueIdsOf` helper that splits off the due half, and an inline useMemo that re-splits the same string for both halves. Both hand-implement the empty-string special case (`dueStr ? dueStr.split(",") : []`). The format is load-bearing for the roster generation logic, so a change to the separator or ordering has to be made in three places rather than one. _Fix:_ Keep one `parseSnapshot(key: string): { dueIds: string[]; total: number }` next to `snapshot()`, and have both the useMemo and the roster's previous-due-set lookup call it (`parseSnapshot(roster.key).dueIds`). Encoder and decoder then sit adjacent as an obvious pair. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:121-123`, `web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:116-119`, `web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:136-142`, `web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:151`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** ReviewCard encodes one grading outcome as two disjoint state variables, forcing a dead alias and two non-null assertions — A grade produces exactly one of two mutually exclusive outcomes — it advanced the schedule, or it was a not-due no-op. The component models this as a `CardState | null` plus a separate boolean, which must be kept in sync by hand in the click handler (the success branch has to remember to clear the boolean). Because the two cannot be narrowed together, the render block tests `(feedback || regradeNoop)` and then needs `feedback!` twice to convince TypeScript the state is present. `const feedback = justGraded` at line 136 is a pure rename with no transformation, adding a second name for one value. _Fix:_ Replace the pair with one discriminated union state: `useState<{ kind: "graded"; state: CardState } | { kind: "noop" } | null>(null)`. onGrade sets it in one call (no cross-clearing to forget), the render narrows on `outcome.kind`, and both `feedback!` assertions and the `feedback` alias disappear. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/review-card.tsx:107-108`, `web/src/components/quantum/review-card.tsx:124-133`, `web/src/components/quantum/review-card.tsx:136`, `web/src/components/quantum/review-card.tsx:200-209`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[UI]** Dashboard hand-rolls the check glyph and the Reviewed chip that widget-ui already single-sources — review-dashboard declares a local `CheckMark` that is byte-identical to widget-ui's exported `CheckIcon` apart from a hardcoded size, and its "Reviewed" chip re-implements the `VerdictBadge tone="accent"` recipe with drifted values (gap-1 vs gap-1.5, text-[10px] uppercase vs text-xs). CheckIcon exists precisely because this glyph was being redeclared across modules; the WS-B7 pass that adopted the shared VerdictBadge in review-card.tsx did not reach review-dashboard.tsx, so the /review surface is now the one place that ships its own copy of both. _Fix:_ Delete the local `CheckMark` and use `<CheckIcon size="h-3 w-3" />` — an exact drop-in. Render the Reviewed chip through `VerdictBadge tone="accent"` so /review's completion affordance matches the six Rep widgets it re-mounts on the same page; if the 10px uppercase scale must be preserved for the header row, add it as a size variant on VerdictBadge rather than keeping a seventh copy. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:125-131`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:44-57`, `web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:240-243`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:361-383`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** The "Stuck?" remediation summary is a 16px touch target — the smallest disclosure on the platform — The escape hatch for a learner who cannot solve a live re-attempt is a `<summary>` styled `inline-block ... px-1 text-xs` with no vertical padding. At text-xs (0.75rem font, 1rem line-height) its border box is 16px tall — under WCAG 2.2 SC 2.5.8's 24x24 minimum, and it does not qualify for the inline exception because it is a standalone block-level control, not a target inside a sentence. The repo's two sibling disclosures both carry `py-0.5`; this one dropped it, making the remediation affordance the hardest thing on /review to hit on a phone, which is precisely backwards for a control aimed at a stuck learner. _Fix:_ Match the sibling idiom and clear the SC: move `text-xs` to the `<details>` and give the summary `inline-flex ... px-1 py-1`, which yields 16px + 8px = 24px. Worth raising the two existing 20px siblings the same way while the reasoning is in hand, since all three are the same disclosure control. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:265`, `web/src/components/runbook-dashboard.tsx:405`, `web/src/components/workspace/retention-spectrum.tsx:96`, `web/__tests__/slider-target-size.test.ts:4-25`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** Nav badge count has no accessible name — the link announces as "Review 3" — The due count is rendered as a bare number appended inside the link, so the computed accessible name is the concatenation "Review 3". A screen-reader user hears "Review 3, link" with no indication that 3 is a count of due cards rather than an ordinal, a section number, or part of the label. The badge also appears and disappears silently as cards come due or get graded elsewhere on the site, since nothing announces the change. _Fix:_ Give the link an explicit accessible name that carries the unit — e.g. `aria-label={count > 0 ? `Review, ${count} card${count === 1 ? "" : "s"} due` : "Review"}` — and mark the numeric chip `aria-hidden="true"` so the count is not read twice. Same information, same visual, just an unambiguous name. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/review-nav-badge.tsx:16-27`, `web/src/components/review-nav-badge.tsx:14`, `web/src/components/nav.tsx:69`, `web/src/components/workspace/retention-spectrum.tsx:78-88`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[UI]** Stat row uses an off-token gray separator that is also read aloud, plus mixed spellings of the muted tier — The "/" between the two counts is styled `text-gray-300 dark:text-gray-700` — the only occurrence of that pair anywhere in src/components, so it is genuine drift off the `--bd`/`--mut` token system and will be skipped by any future token retune. It is also purely decorative but not hidden, so screen readers read "3 due now slash 12 cards tracked". Separately, the same file spells the muted tier both ways, against the convention widget-ui documents. _Fix:_ Replace the separator's raw grays with a token (`text-(--bd)` weight or simply `.text-caption` at reduced opacity) and add `aria-hidden="true"` so it stops being read. Normalize the four resting-muted spellings to `.text-caption`, leaving the deliberate `font-semibold text-(--mut)` emphasis numerals at lines 181/187 alone. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:184`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:19-23`, `web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:174,209,245,268`, `web/src/components/workspace/retention-spectrum.tsx:80,86`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience]** `ReviewCard` re-parses the stored card record without `isValidCardState`, bypassing the hardening `getCardState` applies — The closed [Resilience] finding added `isValidCardState` so a corrupt-but-valid-JSON record (`{}`, truncated, old schema) is discarded and callers fall back to a fresh card. `review-card.tsx` never goes through that gate: it reads the raw string via `getCardStateRaw` (correctly, for snapshot stability) but then does its own bare `JSON.parse(raw) as CardState` in a `useMemo`. A record like `{}` therefore parses to a non-null object, `reviewedBefore` becomes true, and the header renders the literal string "reviewed undefined×" — while the /review dashboard and the nav badge, which do route through `getCardState`, correctly treat the same card as never reviewed. The two surfaces disagree about the same record, and the widget prints garbage. This is reachable, not theoretical: `applySnapshot` only applies its plausibility quarantine when `parseCard` SUCCEEDS, so a `qc:card:*` value that fails validation is exactly the case that gets written to localStorage verbatim. _Fix:_ Apply the same validation inside the memo: `const parsed: unknown = JSON.parse(raw); return isValidCardState(parsed) ? parsed : null;` (importing `isValidCardState` alongside the existing `nextIntervalDays`/`CardState` import at review-card.tsx:10). Keeps the raw-string snapshot for `useSyncExternalStore` identity while making every reader agree on what a usable record is. Add a case to `review-card.test.tsx` seeding `qc:card:test-card-1` as `{}` and asserting no "reviewed" chip renders. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/review-card.tsx:85-92`, `web/src/lib/review-store.ts:70-81`, `web/src/components/quantum/review-card.tsx:135,144-148`, `web/src/lib/progress-merge.ts:299-302`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience]** `dueByKind` buckets an unrecognized `kind` under its own key, breaking the documented sum invariant and dropping the card from the workspace breakdown — `dueByKind`'s `?? "unknown"` only catches an ABSENT kind — any unrecognized string is used verbatim as a counter key. Its own docstring promises "the parts always sum to dueCount()", and `workspace.ts` relies on that by iterating a FIXED key list (the six `KIND_LABELS` keys plus `"unknown"`) and filtering, so a card bucketed under a foreign key is counted in the headline `due` number but appears in no breakdown row — the rows silently sum to less than the total. Prototype keys make it worse: with `kind: "constructor"`, `(counts["constructor"] ?? 0) + 1` is not a nullish miss (it inherits `Object`), so it evaluates to the string `"function Object() { [native code] }1"`; with `kind: "__proto__"` the increment is a silent no-op that stores nothing at all (verified in node: the object stays `{}` with no own `__proto__` property). The dashboard test suite already treats `kind: "not-a-kind"` as a reachable stored value, but the `dueByKind` sum-invariant test only exercises the six valid kinds, so nothing protects the invariant it is named after. _Fix:_ Normalize at the read: `const raw = getCardContent(id)?.kind; const kind = raw && Object.hasOwn(KIND_LABELS, raw) ? raw : "unknown";` — this collapses both unrecognized strings and prototype keys into the "unknown" bucket the workspace already renders as "Other", making the docstring's sum invariant structurally true. Extend the existing sum test with a card carrying `kind: "not-a-kind"` and one carrying `kind: "__proto__"` so the invariant is enforced, not just asserted in prose. Shares its root cause with the `LIVE_WIDGETS` finding — both stem from the unchecked `as CardContent` cast at review-store.ts:64. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/review-store.ts:195`, `web/src/lib/review-store.ts:188-191`, `web/src/lib/review-store.ts:196`, `web/src/lib/workspace.ts:218-225`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience]** A due card whose content cache is missing renders as an invisible list item — the roster can go fully blank while the header still counts it as due — `review-store.ts:47-52` documents an invariant — "A card can only become due after being graded — which requires having seen it — so due cards always have their content cached" — that its own code contradicts. `setCardContent` (`review-store.ts:53-59`) swallows a failed write with the comment "the card simply won't appear on /review this session", while `gradeCard` persists the (far smaller, ~120-byte) CardState through a *separate* `localStorage.setItem` at `review-store.ts:106`. So a large content write can fail (quota, eviction) while the schedule write later succeeds, minting a due card with no cached content. The dashboard's only handling is `review-dashboard.tsx:221-222`: `const content = getCardContent(id); if (!content) return null;` — the roster entry stays in `entries` but renders nothing. Three things then lie: the per-item counter `{i + 1} / {entries.length}` (`review-dashboard.tsx:237`) and its screen-reader twin `Review item ${i + 1} of ${entries.length}` (`review-dashboard.tsx:230`) are computed over the *unfiltered* array, so the roster visibly skips a number ("1 / 3", then "3 / 3"); the empty state at `review-dashboard.tsx:207` is gated on `entries.length === 0`, so when every entry is content-less the learner gets a header reading "3 due now" above completely empty space with no message at all; and `review-nav-badge.tsx:14` keeps counting the card via `dueCount()`, which never consults content. The nav badge sends the learner to a page that shows them nothing and explains nothing. _Fix:_ Resolve content once, at roster-derivation time, and let every downstream consumer agree with it: build the renderable list (id, gen, content) before rendering, use *its* length for the `i / N` counter, the sr-only announcement, and the `entries.length === 0` empty-state gate. A due card with no cached content then falls out of the roster cleanly and the learner sees the honest "Nothing due" copy instead of a blank page. Either drop the now-unreachable `if (!content) return null` branch or keep it as an assertion, and correct the review-store comment so it states the invariant as a strong expectation rather than a guarantee the code does not enforce. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/review-store.ts:47-52`, `web/src/lib/review-store.ts:53-59`, `web/src/lib/review-store.ts:106`, `web/src/components/review-dashboard.tsx:221-222`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** readWorkspace's "One scan of the card store" comment sits three lines above two more full scans of the same store — `workspace.ts:202` announces "One scan of the card store: valid states + their content, id-aligned", and `workspace.ts:203-207` does build exactly that — a `cards` array holding every id with its parsed `CardState` and parsed content. Then the function scans the same store twice more: `workspace.ts:216` calls `dueCardIds(nowMs)`, which re-runs `getAllCardIds()` and re-parses every CardState (`review-store.ts:158-163`), and `workspace.ts:218` calls `dueByKind(nowMs)`, which calls `dueCardIds` *again* internally and then re-parses content for each due id (`review-store.ts:192-198`). For N tracked cards that is three passes over `localStorage.length` and 3N `JSON.parse` calls of card state plus 2N of content, where the array built at line 203 already holds every value needed — `due` is `cards.filter(c => isDue(c.state, today))` and the kind breakdown is a group-by over those same entries' `content.kind`. The comment is not a minor inaccuracy; it is the reason a reader trusts this function is already single-pass and does not look further. _Fix:_ Derive both `dueIds` and the kind breakdown from the `cards` array the function already built — filter on `isDue(state, today)` for the due set and group those entries by `content?.kind ?? "unknown"` for the counts — leaving `dueCardIds`/`dueByKind` in place for the callers that genuinely have no scan in hand (`review-nav-badge.tsx`, `review-dashboard.tsx`). The comment then becomes true, and the workspace read does the single pass it claims. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/workspace.ts:202-207`, `web/src/lib/workspace.ts:216`, `web/src/lib/workspace.ts:218`, `web/src/lib/review-store.ts:158-163`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)

### 19-20. AI tutor (Ask the margin) + corpus
- [x] **[Ops]** DONE (PR pending) — **Handler returns HTTP 200 on every Bedrock failure**, so the Lambda Errors metric never fires and no alarm can be built on it; mid-stream failures render as the answer (client never enters its error state). Emit a structured error sentinel + log a metric. Impact: High. Effort: Medium. (`lambda/tutor/index.mjs:53-88`) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Resilience]** DONE (PR pending) — no client timeout/abort: a stalled stream hangs the UI forever and Close cannot cancel it (no AbortController). Impact: High. Effort: Medium. (`ask-tutor.tsx:70-82,121`) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Correctness]** DONE (PR pending) — **corpus build silently truncates 3 of 7 lessons mid-document** at `SECTION_CHAR_CAP=12000` while still advertising all their headings (corpus.json shows 01-foundations/05/06 at exactly 12000 chars). Warn/fail + truncate on a section boundary. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`build_tutor_corpus.mjs:31`, `tutor.ts:40`) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Accessibility]** DONE (PR pending) — streamed answer renders in a plain `<p>` with no aria-live; the modal slide-over has no focus trap and never restores focus to the trigger on close. Impact: High. Effort: Medium. (`ask-tutor.tsx:105-110,131-134`) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Tests/Ops]** DONE 2026-07-02 (PR #89 closed the tail: preflight deploy gate `lambda/tutor/deploy-check.mjs` — corpus-freshness + model-id checks, with `deploy-check.test.mjs` — and the stack-managed `TutorLogGroup` with finite retention in `template.yaml`; handler unit tests had already landed as `index.test.mjs`/`tutor-core.test.mjs`) — ~reviewer (PARTIAL) — Lambda handler/parseBody/corpus builder tests + log-retention + deploy gate. DONE: structured error log + corpus truncation warn (#22); body-size + slug caps + prototype-pollution guard on the `CORPUS[slug]` lookup, reserved-concurrency cap, scoped Bedrock IAM, runtime bumped to `nodejs22.x` (nodejs20.x was EOL), log-retention documented (CLI `put-retention-policy`). (The hourly-invocations CloudWatch alarm → `quantum-tutor-alerts` SNS topic already existed outside the stack — discovered during deploy — so the template doesn't re-declare it.) STILL OPEN: Lambda handler unit tests; declaring the LogGroup in IaC (conflicts with the auto-created group — needs CFN import or the one-off CLI); corpus-freshness/model-id deploy gate. - [x] DONE (PR pending) — the eval-catalogued **tutor faucet**: `ReservedConcurrentExecutions` cap (`MaxConcurrency=5`, the load-bearing control) + Bedrock IAM scoped to the inference-profile + foundation-model ARNs (was `Resource:"*"`). An hourly-invocations CloudWatch alarm → `quantum-tutor-alerts` SNS topic (email-subscribed) already existed outside the stack. `AuthType: NONE` retained (static-site UX) but now bounded by the concurrency cap; CORS recalibrated in the README as a browser-only UX allowlist, **not** an access control. The "answer only from lesson" prompt is UX, not a security boundary.


#### Feature 17 — 2026-07-20 Category C re-audit (13 adversarially-verified findings; independent skeptic per claim, default-refute, security and correctness claims re-derived against current tree state)

- [x] **[Performance]** The quiz skeleton reserves 460px for a card measured at 1036px desktop / 1308px mobile, jumping the on-ramp lesson by up to 848px on mount — The widget registry assigns quiz the fixed `tall` skeleton (min-h-[460px]), the same constant used by WebGL and canvas widgets. Quiz height is linear in question count, and the five shipped quizzes span 4 to 10 questions. I measured the rendered card in a headless browser against the real static export: the 4-question quiz lands at 499px (the constant is well calibrated for it), but the 10-question 00-prereqs quiz renders 1036px at 1280px viewport and 1308px at 390px viewport. When the deferred chunk resolves, the page therefore grows 576px on desktop and 848px on mobile — more than a full mobile viewport — pushing everything below the quiz down. This lands on 00-prereqs, the on-ramp module a learner meets first. Raising the constant is not a fix: it would over-reserve for the four 4-question quizzes and convert their near-perfect fit into a shrink in the other direction. The skeleton is unavoidable here because the approach gate renders it on the server regardless of the dynamic import's ssr flag — `near` initializes to false and only flips inside an effect — so the static HTML never contains the quiz markup even though the fence's JSON source (questions, hints and answers) already ships in the RSC payload. That also means quiz.tsx's docstring claim that "the answer text ships in the HTML and is toggled client-side" is only half true: the text ships as inert payload, never as DOM. _Fix:_ Two contained options, both refinements of existing mechanisms. Minimal: widen `WidgetEntry.minH` to accept `string | ((source: string) => string)` — the fence already holds `source` — and give quiz a sizing function keyed on question count, so the reservation tracks the data instead of one constant that fits 4 questions and understates 10 by more than 2x. More thorough: since quiz is the one registry entry with no browser-only dependency, exempt it from the approach gate and let it render server-side, which removes the skeleton and the shift entirely, makes the quiz readable without JS, and makes the quiz.tsx docstring true — at near-zero payload cost, since the question/hint/answer bytes already ship in the RSC payload. Whichever is chosen, correct the docstring so it describes actual behavior. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/quantum/widget-fence.tsx:75`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-fence.tsx:48`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-fence.tsx:152-170`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-fence.tsx:179-187`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Architecture]** Quiz's 16 KB private chunk bundles the entire math kernel for one 20-line label component — Quiz imports exactly three symbols from `./widget-ui`. Two of them (`cardShell`, `ErrorCard`) actually live in the dependency-free `error-card.tsx` and are only re-exported; the third, `EyebrowLabel`, is 20 lines of pure JSX that happens to sit inside widget-ui.tsx. Because widget-ui.tsx statically imports the state-vector kernel, the Dirac readout, format.ts and CopyButton, that single import pulls all of it into Quiz's code-split chunk. This is not a theoretical tree-shaking concern — I confirmed it in the committed build output. error-card.tsx's own header documents this exact hazard as the reason the split was made, so the mechanism is known; Quiz is simply on the wrong side of it. _Fix:_ Move `EyebrowLabel` into error-card.tsx alongside the other two dependency-free shell primitives and re-export it from widget-ui so the ~30 explorables that import it keep working unchanged. Then switch quiz.tsx's import line to "./error-card" (the widget-fence.tsx precedent), which drops the kernel, state-readout, format and CopyButton from the quiz chunk. review-card.tsx imports the identical three symbols and gets the same benefit for free. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:4-8`, `web/src/components/quantum/error-card.tsx:1-10`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-fence.tsx:5`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:258-284`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The reveal-panel recipe is hand-written in five widgets while widget-ui already owns the token pair — Quiz's answer panel and hint panel each hand-write the accent/warm reveal recipe. Both are exact prefix matches of `VERDICT_STYLES.solved` and `VERDICT_STYLES.wrong` in widget-ui.tsx (identical border/background/dark-mode triples, differing only by the trailing text-color tier, which Quiz overrides on the child `<p>` anyway). Challenge and debug-circuit-widget already consume the constant; quiz, review-card, cost-estimate-widget, expectation-widget and predict-widget hand-write it. That is a five-way copy of a recipe the shared module was written to own, and the widget-ui doc records that this exact family has drifted before ("challenge and debug-circuit had already diverged on the `error` border token"). Note the naming caveat: `VERDICT_STYLES` is keyed solved/wrong/error and Quiz computes no verdict, so consuming it verbatim would be a semantic lie. _Fix:_ Extract the geometry-free tone pair into a semantically neutral constant in widget-ui (e.g. `REVEAL_PANEL: Record<"accent"|"warm", string>` holding just the border/background triples) and define `VERDICT_STYLES.solved`/`.wrong` in terms of it plus their text tier. Quiz's two panels and the four other hand-written copies then reference the shared constant, and a token retune reaches all of them — without mislabelling Quiz's non-graded disclosures as verdicts. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:223`, `web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:207`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:392-398`, `web/src/components/quantum/challenge.tsx:191`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[UX]** The quiz card hardcodes "Placement quiz", contradicting the heading directly above it in 4 of the 5 sections that use it — `Quiz` renders a fixed eyebrow reading "Placement quiz" for every ```quiz fence on the platform. That wording is accurate only in 00-prereqs, where the block genuinely is a placement test. On the other four sections the same block is an end-of-module retention self-check, and the markdown heading immediately above it says so explicitly — so the learner reads a heading that promises a "check" and then sees a card that announces itself as a placement test. On 03-algorithms and 04-quantum-ml the heading ends with the literal phrase "and a check", making the contradiction sit within a few lines of vertical space. This is a flow-clarity defect: it miscasts a low-stakes recall exercise as a gating assessment at the exact moment the learner decides whether to engage with it. _Fix:_ Change the hardcoded string at quiz.tsx:146 to a section-neutral label that matches the prose the authors already wrote around it — "Self-check" reads correctly at all five sites, including 00-prereqs where the `## Placement Quiz` heading already supplies the placement framing. This is a one-string edit inside the existing markup; it adds no schema field, no prop, and no capability. No test pins the string (grep for "Placement quiz" across src/, __tests__/ and e2e/ returns only quiz.tsx:147 itself), so nothing else needs to change. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:146`, `web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:35`, `00-prereqs/GUIDE.md:410`, `01-foundations/GUIDE.md:444`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** Quiz fences are the only JSON-spec fence with a parser and an error-card degrade path that CI never validates — `guide-reps.test.ts` exists specifically because GUIDE fences "validated only at runtime, in the learner's browser" mean a typo "would ship silently and render as an error card ... in production". Its extractor regex covers six tokens; `quiz` is not among them. Quiz is structurally the same shape as the six it does cover — a JSON spec inside a fence, parsed by a dedicated parser, degrading to `ErrorCard` on any failure — so a malformed quiz fence in any GUIDE ships to production and renders as a parse-error card where a 4-to-10 question self-check should be, with nothing failing in CI. Five quiz fences carrying 27 questions are currently unguarded. Two structural details make this cheap to close and slightly worse than it looks: `parseQuiz` is module-local rather than living in `@/lib/*-schema` like every gated kind's parser, and its per-field guards are defeated by a null array entry — `typeof q.q` on `null` throws a raw TypeError that the catch block forwards verbatim into the learner-facing card, bypassing the carefully worded messages the surrounding lines produce for every other malformed shape. _Fix:_ Lift `parseQuiz` out of quiz.tsx into `@/lib/quiz-schema` so it matches the `predict-schema`/`challenge-schema` idiom the gated kinds already follow, and have quiz.tsx import it (the component's behavior and its jsdom test are unchanged). Then add `quiz` to the `collectFences` regex in guide-reps.test.ts with a case asserting `parseQuiz(body).error` is undefined and that every question carries a non-empty `q` and `a`. While the parser is being moved, add a null/non-object entry guard so a bad array element produces the same teachable message as every other malformed shape rather than leaking engine text. Note this does not claim quiz is uniquely neglected — qsim, runnable and qscrolly fences are also ungated — but quiz is the closest structural match to what the gate already covers and by far the cheapest to fold in. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/__tests__/content/guide-reps.test.ts:2-12`, `web/__tests__/content/guide-reps.test.ts:72`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-langs.ts:16`, `00-prereqs/GUIDE.md:416`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** Three of the five quiz fences use zero code chips, so `renderInline` — the component's whole reason to exist — goes dark halfway through the curriculum — `renderInline` (quiz.tsx:64) is the widget's only formatter: backtick spans become branded accent chips, everything else is a plain span. But the authored corpus exercises it in only two of the five sections. 00-prereqs carries 53 code chips and writes math in pure ASCII (`sqrt`, `pi`, `theta`, `cos^2`); 01-foundations carries 21 chips and switches to Unicode (`√ π θ ≈ ² Φ ⟩`); 02-hardware, 03-algorithms and 04-quantum-ml carry **zero** chips, so their math ships as unstyled body prose — 04-quantum-ml Q4 renders the literal run `df/dtheta = (1/2)[f(theta+pi/2) - f(theta-pi/2)]` as ordinary sentence text, while 03-algorithms Q2 renders `~(π/4)√N` as ordinary sentence text one lesson earlier in a different alphabet. Worse, 01-foundations Q4 mixes both ket spellings inside a single answer: "`(|00> + |11>)/√2`, the Bell state `|Φ+⟩`" — ASCII `>` and Unicode `⟩` in the same sentence. A learner walking 00→04 sees the same quantity (pi/2) as an accent-tinted code chip, then as bare ASCII prose, then as a bare Unicode glyph.
