This is distinct from the ledger's deferred KaTeX migration ([OPEN][Correctness], 17-18): even inside today's literal-render regime the corpus contradicts itself. _Fix:_ Normalize the authored corpus, not the component. Pick one spelling of the fallback notation (00-prereqs' ASCII `|+>` / `sqrt` / `pi` / `theta` is the majority and the one the Dirac-to-NumPy table in that same GUIDE teaches) and apply it across all 27 questions; wrap every math/identifier run in backticks so all five quizzes render through the same chip path. Pure content edits in the five GUIDE.md fences — no component change, no new dependency, and it leaves the deferred KaTeX migration strictly easier because every math run will then be delimited. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:64-78`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The Hint eyebrow hand-rolls the EyebrowLabel recipe sixteen lines from a real EyebrowLabel — Quiz renders two micro-labels inside the same question body. The Answer label uses the shared `EyebrowLabel strong` component. The Hint label, sixteen lines above it, hand-writes that component's exact `strong` recipe — `text-[10px] font-semibold uppercase tracking-[0.2em] font-mono` — differing only in hue, because `EyebrowLabel` hardcodes the accent color and exposes no tone knob. So one file contains both spellings of the same primitive, which is the precise failure mode the component's own doc comment describes ("Both spellings were copy-pasted across nine files and had already drifted on the dark-mode color"). Anyone retuning the eyebrow tier will update line 225 and silently miss line 209. _Fix:_ Add a `tone?: "accent" | "warm"` prop to `EyebrowLabel`, defaulting to `accent` so all ~30 existing call sites are untouched, and mapping `warm` to `text-warm-dark dark:text-warm-light`. Replace quiz.tsx:209-211 with `<EyebrowLabel strong tone="warm" className="block mb-1">Hint</EyebrowLabel>`. Rendered output is identical, and the tone-map shape matches the `CHIP_TONE`/`VerdictBadge` idiom already in that file. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:209-211`, `web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:225-227`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:274-280`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:249-257`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** parseQuiz bypasses parse-utils and leaks raw V8 SyntaxError text into the learner-facing error card — Twelve fenced explorables read their JSON config through `parseJsonObject` from parse-utils.ts, which returns a curated "invalid JSON" string. Quiz calls `JSON.parse` directly inside a try/catch and forwards `(e as Error).message` straight into `ErrorCard`, so a malformed fence renders V8 engine text to the reader — e.g. "quiz parse error: Expected property name or '}' in JSON at position 2". The structural validation in parseQuiz is sound (I traced null, array, and scalar inputs; each falls through to the friendly message), so this is narrowly about the malformed-JSON branch and about Quiz sitting outside the shared parse layer. The existing test asserts only the label, so the leak is unpinned. _Fix:_ Route the malformed-JSON branch through `parseJsonObject` so Quiz reports the same "invalid JSON" phrasing as its twelve siblings, keeping the existing per-field checks (which are richer than parse-utils offers) on top of the returned object. Handle the empty-source case that `parseJsonObject` maps to `{ok:true, obj:null}` as the existing "needs at least one question" error. review-card.tsx:44-57 has the identical shape and can follow in the same change. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:37`, `web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:56-57`, `web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:132`, `web/src/components/quantum/parse-utils.ts:23-38`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** `aria-pressed` on the show-all-answers toggle contradicts its own flipping label, announcing a state opposite to the truth — The header control both flips its visible label and exposes `aria-pressed`. When every answer is revealed, `allOpen` is true, so the label becomes "Hide all answers" and `aria-pressed` becomes true simultaneously. A screen reader announces "Hide all answers, toggle button, pressed" — but the label names an action not yet taken while `pressed` asserts that action is already in effect, so the two halves of the announcement describe opposite states. Conversely, in the resting state a user hears "Show all answers, toggle button, not pressed", which reads as though showing has been declined. The per-question buttons in the same component get this right with `aria-expanded`, whose semantics (a region's expanded state) do not collide with a label naming the next action, so the defect is isolated to this one control — the only global affordance the feature has. _Fix:_ Delete `aria-pressed={allOpen}` at quiz.tsx:152. The flipping label already carries the state unambiguously for both screen-reader and sighted users, and the `ChevronIcon open={allOpen}` at line 156 carries it visually. This is the pattern the component's own per-question controls follow. One-line deletion, no behavioral change for pointer or keyboard users. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:152`, `web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:155`, `web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:135`, `web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:182`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[UI]** The "Hint" micro-label hand-rolls EyebrowLabel's exact geometry sixteen lines above a real `<EyebrowLabel strong>` — Inside a single component the same micro-label role is spelled two ways: the revealed hint panel's "Hint" caption is a raw `<span>` carrying `text-[10px] font-semibold uppercase tracking-[0.2em] font-mono` — byte-identical geometry to the shared `EyebrowLabel` primitive in `strong` mode — while the answer panel's "Answer" caption sixteen lines below calls `<EyebrowLabel strong>`. The duplication exists only because the hint needs warm tokens and `EyebrowLabel` hardcodes the accent pair, with its `className` prop documented as layout-only. The consequence is the exact drift widget-ui was created to stop: any retune of the eyebrow geometry (size, tracking, weight, family) reaches "Answer" and silently skips "Hint", so two captions in the same card would render at different scales. A repo-wide grep confirms this is the only remaining hand-roll of that precise recipe — sidebar.tsx uses font-bold and qpu-submit-panel uses text-xs font-medium, so neither is a geometry clone. _Fix:_ Give `EyebrowLabel` an optional `tone?: "accent" | "warm"` (defaulting to `"accent"`) that selects the color pair, mirroring the `CHIP_TONE` record already in the same file, then replace the span at quiz.tsx:209 with `<EyebrowLabel strong tone="warm" className="block mb-1">Hint</EyebrowLabel>`. Geometry ends up single-sourced for both captions while the warm hue is preserved exactly. This refines an existing primitive to absorb a duplicate — it adds no widget behavior and no new rendering path. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:209`, `web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:225`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:275`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:269`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Performance]** The quiz's lazy chunk ships the entire widget-ui primitive module (math kernel, Dirac readout, CopyButton) for three symbols — Quiz needs exactly three things from the shared primitives: `cardShell`, `ErrorCard`, and `EyebrowLabel`. It imports all three from `./widget-ui`, a 542-line module whose own imports pull in the math kernel, the qsim state readout, the number formatter and CopyButton. Because every widget is code-split behind its own `next/dynamic` factory and webpack is not hoisting widget-ui into a shared chunk (41 separate chunks in the build each carry their own copy of `diracString`), the quiz's chunk inlines that whole graph as dead code. This is the exact cost `error-card.tsx` was created to avoid — its docstring says it was split out of widget-ui so consumers could render the failure state "without statically importing the whole primitive module (which pulls in the math kernel, the Dirac readout and CopyButton)". `widget-fence.tsx` honors that lean path; quiz.tsx does not, and the only thing forcing it back onto the heavy path is `EyebrowLabel`, which is itself a zero-dependency presentational component. _Fix:_ Move `EyebrowLabel` out of widget-ui.tsx and into the leaf module alongside `cardShell`/`ErrorCard` (it has no dependencies, so the move is mechanical), then re-export it from widget-ui exactly as line 247 already does for the other two — preserving "widgets keep importing everything from ./widget-ui" for every other consumer. Point quiz.tsx's import at the leaf module. The quiz chunk then drops the math kernel, state-readout, format and CopyButton graph it can never execute, falling from ~5.6KB gzip to roughly the component's own weight. The same move makes the lean path available to any other lightweight widget that only needs shell + label. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:4-8`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:3-9`, `web/src/components/quantum/error-card.tsx:1-9`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-fence.tsx:5`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** Every revealed hint and answer is a named ARIA landmark, so "Show all answers" on 00-prereqs injects ten landmarks at once — Both reveal panels are declared `role="region"` with an `aria-label` (quiz.tsx:205-206 and :221-222). An ARIA `region` **with an accessible name is a landmark**, so it lands in the screen-reader landmark rotor alongside main/nav/banner. The 00-prereqs quiz has 10 questions, so a learner who presses "Show all answers" adds 10 landmarks named "Answer to question 1…10" in one keystroke, and opening every hint too brings the page to 20 quiz landmarks. Every sibling widget in the same family uses exactly one region per widget instance, so quiz is the only component in the repo that multiplies landmarks by content length. The landmark rotor — the primary way a screen-reader user skims a long lesson — becomes unusable on the page that most needs skimming. _Fix:_ Drop `role="region"` from the per-question panels and keep the `id` + `aria-label` for the `aria-controls` relationship (a labelled `<div>` referenced by an expanded disclosure button is already the standard disclosure pattern and needs no landmark). If a role is wanted for grouping, `role="group"` conveys the same containment without entering the landmark rotor. Two attribute edits in quiz.tsx; the existing test at quiz.test.tsx:76-84 queries `getByRole("region")` and would move to `getByLabelText`. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:203-207`, `web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:219-223`, `web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:138-141`, `00-prereqs/GUIDE.md:416`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** The documented-optional `hint` field is exercised by nothing — not one of the 27 authored questions omits it, and no test does either — `hint` is a documented part of the public fence contract (README spells the shape as `{ questions: [{ q, hint?, a }] }`) and the component carries two branches for its absence: the hint button at quiz.tsx:178 and the hint panel at quiz.tsx:202 are both guarded by `item.hint &&`. Neither branch has ever run. All 27 questions across the five GUIDE fences supply a hint, and the unit-test fixture gives both of its questions a hint too. So the layout a hintless question produces — a lone "Show answer" button in the `flex flex-wrap items-center gap-2` row at :177 — has never been rendered in CI or in production. A contributor who takes the README at its word and omits `hint` is the first person to see that state, and the quiz fence (unlike every graded Rep kind) has no corpus gate to catch a mistake first. _Fix:_ Add one case to quiz.test.tsx that renders a question with no `hint` and asserts the Hint button is absent while "Show answer" still renders and reveals — covering the documented-optional branch at the same cost as the existing malformed-data cases at :93-110. No production change. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`README.md:427`, `web/src/components/quantum/quiz.tsx:178`, `web/__tests__/components/quantum/quiz.test.tsx:8-21`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)


#### Feature 19 — 2026-07-20 Category D re-audit (27 adversarially-verified findings; security claims required a named exploit path, drift claims were checked for current alignment, and the grounding-integrity items were proven by executing the real functions against the seven live GUIDEs)

- [x] **[Security (LLM grounding integrity)]** The corpus builder deletes every underscore and asterisk from the grounding text, so the tutor is fed — and confidently repeats — API and file names that do not exist — `stripGuideForTutor` unwraps inline code spans into bare prose and THEN runs a blanket character-delete over the whole document. Every `_` and `*` in the result is destroyed, including the ones that were inside `` `code` `` a line earlier. The system prompt's single strongest guardrail — "NEVER invent or guess Amazon Braket, PennyLane, or Qiskit APIs, method names, parameters" — is defeated at the source: the model is not inventing anything, it is faithfully repeating corrupted identifiers from grounding text it has been ordered to trust over its own knowledge ("Answer ONLY using the lesson text provided below"). A learner who asks "which script checks my setup?" is told `scripts/checkprereqs.py`; the file on disk is `check_prereqs.py`. This is the exact failure mode the feature exists to prevent, and it is shipping in all seven lessons. _Fix:_ Replace the blanket delete at tutor-core.mjs:50 with paired-delimiter unwraps that only fire on real emphasis — `/(\*\*|__)(.+?)\1/g` and `/(?<![\w*_])([*_])(?=\S)(.+?)(?<=\S)\1(?![\w*_])/g`, both to `$2` — and run them BEFORE the inline-code unwrap at line 46 so code contents are never exposed to them. Then add a fixture with `n_qubits`, `circuit.state_vector()` and `2*n` to tutor-core.test.mjs and web/__tests__/lib/tutor.test.ts asserting the identifiers survive verbatim, so the regression cannot return. Rebuilding the corpus is already a deploy step (build:tutor-corpus), and deploy-check.mjs's freshness gate will force the rebuild. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:46`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:50`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:110`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.test.mjs:30`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Security (LLM grounding integrity)]** SECTION_CHAR_CAP silently amputates six of the seven lessons — 43% of the chemistry guide — and the prompt then teaches the model to refuse the missing half as "outside this lesson" — `SECTION_CHAR_CAP = 12_000` (lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:19) is not a safety margin — it is a hard truncation that every current lesson except one exceeds. I rebuilt the corpus from the working-tree GUIDEs and measured: 05-quantum-chemistry loses 43.3% of its grounding text (20,834 -&gt; 11,818 chars), 01-foundations 23.1%, 06-hybrid-jobs 21.5%, 02-hardware 17.0%, 04-quantum-ml 10.5%, 03-algorithms 3.6%. The dropped tails are not boilerplate: they include "Scaling Up: Basis Sets and Active Space" and "Where This Matters" (chemistry), "Check yourself" and "Hands-On Exercises" (foundations) — exactly the sections a stuck learner asks about. This is worse than a silent gap, because `buildCorpusEntry` filters the advertised headings to only those surviving truncation (tutor-core.mjs:86), and `buildSystemPrompt` then states "The lesson covers these sections: ..." (tutor-core.mjs:102) followed by RULE 1: "Answer ONLY using the lesson text provided below. If the question is outside this lesson, say so plainly" (tutor-core.mjs:109). So the model is affirmatively told the lesson stops where the cap fell. A learner who scrolls to the bottom of the chemistry lesson, reads about active space, and asks the tutor about it is told it is outside the lesson she is literally looking at — the single most damaging failure a grounded tutor can produce. Nothing catches it: the builder's only signal is a `console.warn` at deploy time (scripts/build_tutor_corpus.mjs:34), and `corpusFreshnessProblems` compares the corpus against a rebuild that applies the same cap (lambda/tutor/deploy-check.mjs:87-90), so the preflight gate is structurally blind to truncation. Categories B/C edited these GUIDEs (all seven are dated 2026-07-19/20) and pushed them further past the cap. _Fix:_ Raise SECTION_CHAR_CAP to cover the largest stripped GUIDE with headroom (the current worst case is 20,834 chars ~ 5.2k tokens; 24,000 covers all seven with room for Category B/C growth). Haiku 4.5's context is 200k and `maxTokens` is already capped at 800 (lambda/tutor/index.mjs:91), so the whole corpus fits with no architectural change and a few tenths of a cent per request. Then make truncation loud instead of silent: have `runPreflight` push a problem when `buildCorpusEntry` returns `truncated: true` for any section, so the existing deploy gate fails on an over-cap GUIDE rather than shipping a half-grounded lesson. Both are edits to constants and the existing preflight loop — no new files, no new dependency. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:19`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:86`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:109`, `lambda/tutor/deploy-check.mjs:87-90`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** The streaming live region is self-contradictory: aria-busy stays true for the whole stream while the code claims incremental announcement — The answer container is `aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="false" aria-busy={busy}`, and `busy` is true from the moment Ask is pressed until the stream ends (up to the 65s watchdog). aria-busy="true" instructs assistive tech to defer processing of the region until it flips false. So the two documented intents cannot both hold: on AT that honors aria-busy, a blind learner gets total silence for the entire answer (not even "Thinking…", which also lives inside the busy region) and then one flush; on AT that ignores or partially honors it, every chunk mutates the single text node wholesale, and with aria-atomic="false" the changed node IS the entire accumulated answer, so each of dozens of chunks re-announces the whole answer from the beginning. The comment on the region asserts the incremental behavior as fact, so the failure mode is invisible to future maintainers. _Fix:_ Make the announcement contract deterministic instead of leaving it to per-AT aria-busy interpretation. Keep the answer text out of the live region entirely (plain container, no aria-live), and add one small `sr-only role="status"` node that carries only discrete state transitions — 'Thinking', then 'Answer ready' on stream end (and nothing per chunk). Screen-reader users then read the answer with the virtual cursor, which is the standard pattern for long streamed prose, and get exactly two announcements instead of either silence or dozens of full-text repeats. Update the comment to describe what the markup actually does, and extend the existing test to pin the transition count. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:224`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:222`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:104`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:145`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** The slide-over claims aria-modal="true" while the background stays scrollable, clickable and non-inert — and the repo's inert mechanism already exists unused — The tutor dialog asserts aria-modal="true" but does none of the three things its two sibling modals do to make that claim truthful: it does not lock body scroll, it does not mark the background regions inert, and it renders no scrim or outside-click dismiss. On desktop the 448px panel leaves the lesson fully interactive: a learner can scroll the page behind it, click a sidebar link (which navigates while the 'modal' stays open — see the stale-state finding), and pointer-drag text out of a dialog that told AT the rest of the page does not exist. The repo already exports the exact machinery for this — DRAWER_INERT_REGION_IDS, documented as 'Everything the open drawer must render inert to make aria-modal truthful' — and ask-tutor.tsx already imports from that same module for its trigger id, so the fix is reuse, not invention. Dismissal is also single-point on mobile: with no scrim and no swipe, the only pointer affordance is a 32x32 close button, smaller than the 36x36 the sibling dialog uses for the identical control. _Fix:_ Mirror the sidebar's open-effect in the tutor: on open, set inert on the DRAWER_INERT_REGION_IDS regions (minus the trigger, which the trap already excludes), lock body.style.overflow, and restore both in the cleanup. Add the section-gate's scrim treatment (`animate-backdrop-fade`, aria-hidden, mousedown-to-close) so pointer users have the dismissal affordance the panel currently lacks, and bring the close button to the repo's h-9 w-9 dialog-close size. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:196-200`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:103`, `web/src/components/section-gate-modal.tsx:60-61`, `web/src/lib/layout-regions.ts:12-18`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The panel's "Grounded in" label hand-rolls slug title-casing instead of using the manifest, printing "Quantum Ml" and "Prereqs" — `lessonLabel()` derives the displayed lesson name by stripping the numeric prefix and upper-casing each hyphen-separated word. The repo already has `getSectionBySlug(slug).title`, sourced from `content-manifest.json` — described in manifest.ts as "the curriculum's source of truth" — and the Lambda grounds the model in `section.title` (the GUIDE's H1), which is that same string. So the one line of copy whose entire job is to state what the tutor is grounded in is the only place in the app that does NOT use the canonical title, and it produces text that is wrong or mis-cased on the majority of sections. _Fix:_ Delete `lessonLabel` and import `getSectionBySlug` from `@/lib/sections`, rendering `getSectionBySlug(slug)?.title ?? slug`. That deletes a duplicated transform, fixes "Quantum Ml", and makes the label byte-identical to the `section.title` the Lambda actually puts in the system prompt — so the claim the panel makes matches the grounding the model receives. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:31-37`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:206`, `web/src/lib/sections.ts:38-40`, `web/src/lib/manifest.ts:1-8`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The tutor slide-over claims aria-modal but skips the modal contract both other dialogs in the repo implement — The repo has an explicit, commented modal contract used by its two other dialogs: focus into the container (tabIndex={-1}), lock body scroll, restore focus on close, and — for the drawer — mark the background inert so "the aria-modal claim is truthful". The tutor panel claims `aria-modal="true"` and implements only the focus-restore half. The page behind it keeps scrolling, stays pointer-reachable, and stays in the accessibility tree, with no backdrop and no inert marking. Separately, `useFocusTrap`'s shift-Tab-when-the-container-holds-focus branch is written for dialogs that focus themselves with tabIndex={-1}; the tutor sets neither, so it consumes the shared hook under an assumption it does not satisfy. _Fix:_ Bring the tutor panel onto the same contract as its two siblings: add `tabIndex={-1}`, and in the existing `open` effect lock/restore `document.body.style.overflow` the way section-gate-modal.tsx:58-67 does. The tutor trigger id is already a shared constant in layout-regions.ts, so if the panel should also render the lesson inert, the same `DRAWER_INERT_REGION_IDS` mechanism sidebar.tsx uses is available with no new machinery. Either honour the aria-modal claim or drop it to a non-modal complementary region — the current middle state is the one the repo's own comments argue against. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:193-201`, `web/src/components/section-gate-modal.tsx:58-67`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:91-124`, `web/src/hooks/use-focus-trap.ts:45`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[UX]** The panel survives lesson navigation with stale state, so it asserts "Grounded in: &lt;new lesson&gt;" over the previous lesson's answer — AskTutor is mounted in the ROOT layout, so it never unmounts during client-side navigation between lessons. `slug` is derived live from usePathname and updates immediately, but `answer`, `question`, and `error` are component state that nothing resets on slug change. Ask a question on 03-algorithms, close, navigate to 05-quantum-chemistry, reopen: the header reads 'Grounded in: Quantum Chemistry' while the body still shows the algorithms answer. The panel is making a false provenance claim about text on screen — the exact claim the whole grounding architecture exists to make true. Worse, the unmount-abort effect is documented as covering this case but cannot fire, so a stream started on lesson A keeps writing into `answer` after the learner has navigated to lesson B; the tokens visibly continue filling in under lesson B's header. _Fix:_ Add an effect keyed on `slug` that aborts any in-flight controller and clears `answer`, `error`, and `question` whenever the lesson changes. That single effect both fixes the false provenance and makes the abort-on-navigation behavior the existing comment already promises real. Correct the misleading comment on line 87 to say what actually triggers it. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/layout.tsx:85`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:87`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:41-42`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:206`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** Clicking Ask evicts focus to &lt;body&gt;, silently disarming Escape and the focus trap for the rest of the request — The submit button becomes `disabled` the instant `busy` flips true. Browsers blur an element that becomes disabled while focused, moving focus to document.body. The dialog's Escape handler and focus trap are wired as a React `onKeyDown` prop on the dialog div, so they only fire for events whose target is inside that div — body is an ancestor of the React root, so keydowns there never reach the handler. Net effect on the mouse path (click Ask, not Enter): for the entire streaming window, Escape no longer closes the panel, and Tab is no longer trapped — it walks straight out into the background page, which is not inert. The panel still asserts aria-modal="true" throughout. The sibling drawer does not have this bug because it registers its Escape+trap handler on `document`. _Fix:_ Adopt the sidebar's idiom: move the Escape + trapFocus wiring into a `useEffect` keyed on `open` that listens on `document`, so it cannot be defeated by focus leaving the dialog subtree. Additionally, prefer `aria-disabled` + an early return in `ask()` over the hard `disabled` attribute on the submit, so the button keeps focus (and its place in the trap's FOCUSABLE list) while the request is in flight. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:270-273`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:199`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:162-169`, `web/src/hooks/use-focus-trap.ts:39`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[UX]** Rate-limiting and every edge failure surface to the learner as the raw string "request failed (403)" — The client throws `new Error("request failed (" + status + ")")` on any non-ok response and then renders `(e as Error).message` verbatim in the error slot. The WAF rule blocks with the default action and no CustomResponse, and AWS WAF's default block status is HTTP 403 — so a learner who trips the per-IP rate limit sees the literal text 'request failed (403)'. That is the user-facing outcome of the entire PR #57 edge-protection layer: a bare status code with no explanation, no wait-and-retry guidance, and no way to distinguish rate-limiting from a broken OAC grant (also 403) or a network drop ('Failed to fetch', the raw TypeError message from the same line). Separately, edge.yaml's own prose twice claims the rate limit sheds abuse with '429', which the template does not configure — so the documented contract and the deployed behavior disagree, and any future status-based handling written against the comment would be wrong. _Fix:_ Map status to learner copy at the throw site instead of leaking the code: 403/429 to 'Too many questions in a short window — give it a minute and try again.', 5xx/502 to 'The tutor is unavailable right now — please try again shortly.', and a caught TypeError to 'Could not reach the tutor — check your connection.' Keep the numeric status in a console.warn for debugging. Independently, either add a `CustomResponse` with ResponseCode 429 to the WAF rule so the deployed behavior matches edge.yaml's stated contract, or correct the two comments to say 403. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:123`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:153`, `lambda/tutor/edge.yaml:44-45`, `lambda/tutor/edge.yaml:5`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[UI]** At 375px the slide-over uses inset-y-0 where the sibling drawer uses the dvh idiom, putting the composer under iOS Safari's toolbar — The panel is `fixed inset-y-0`, which sizes a fixed element to the layout viewport — on iOS Safari that equals the LARGE viewport (toolbars retracted). With the toolbar visible, the bottom ~80-110px of the panel is behind it, and that band is precisely where the composer lives: the textarea and the Ask button. On mobile the composer IS the feature, so the learner can open the tutor and find no way to type into it without first scrolling the page to retract the toolbar. The sibling drawer hit this exact problem and solved it with the `supports-[height:100dvh]` progressive-enhancement idiom, with an inline comment explaining why — the tutor never adopted it. _Fix:_ Swap `inset-y-0` for the repo's established pair — `top-0 h-screen supports-[height:100dvh]:h-dvh` — so the panel is bounded by the dynamic viewport where supported and falls back cleanly where not. Same idiom, same progressive-enhancement guard, one class change. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:200`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:244-278`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:174`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:164-166`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** The Lambda's only failure signal is a JS-level catch that a Lambda timeout can never reach, and the client renders any early stream end as a finished answer — Because the response is committed as HTTP 200 before the model call, the in-band sentinel is the sole channel for failure — and it is written only from the handler's `catch`. That catch is unreachable when the invocation itself is killed: the Bedrock client is constructed with no request timeout, so a stalled ConverseStream runs until the 60s Lambda `Timeout` terminates the process, no sentinel is ever written. On the client, the read loop treats `done` as success unconditionally, so a stream that ends early renders the partial text as a complete, authoritative answer with no error and `busy` cleared. Separately, the client watchdog does not do what its own comment claims: it is a total-duration cap armed once before `fetch`, not an idle detector, so a stall two seconds in still pins the UI on "Asking…" for the remaining 63 seconds. _Fix:_ In createHandlerCore, bound the delta loop with a deadline well under the 60s Lambda Timeout (capture `Date.now()` before `client.send` and throw once the loop exceeds e.g. 45s, or race each `read` against a timer) so the existing catch/sentinel path is actually reachable and the learner sees the error state instead of a truncated answer. On the client, make the watchdog idle-based: clear and re-arm the timer inside the read loop on each chunk, so a stall surfaces in seconds and a healthy long stream is never cut. Add an index.test.mjs case where the async generator returns after one delta without throwing, asserting the handler emits the sentinel. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/index.mjs:42`, `lambda/tutor/index.mjs:95-99`, `lambda/tutor/index.mjs:100-109`, `lambda/tutor/template.yaml:86`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** The one failure the grounding design explicitly fears — a missing corpus answering OUT_OF_SCOPE to every learner — is logged but has no metric filter and no alarm — index.mjs guards the corpus read and, on failure, sets `CORPUS = {}` and emits `console.warn(JSON.stringify({ tutorCorpusMissing: true, ... }))` (lambda/tutor/index.mjs:31-40). Its own comment names the stakes: 'otherwise an empty corpus silently answers OUT_OF_SCOPE to every learner.' With `CORPUS = {}`, the `hasOwnProperty` guard at index.mjs:79 misses for every slug, so index.mjs:81-84 streams OUT_OF_SCOPE_MESSAGE — inside a committed HTTP 200, with no Bedrock call and therefore no `tutorError` line. Every observability surface stays green: HTTP status is 200, the Lambda `Errors` metric is untouched, and the TutorErrorMetricFilter matches the literal term `"tutorError"` (template.yaml:182), which does not appear in the `tutorCorpusMissing` payload. HighInvocationsAlarm only fires above 500/hour and would if anything trend down as learners give up. So the feature can be totally dead for days with zero notification — while template.yaml:169-177 and README.md:183-192 both argue at length that the tutorError filter exists specifically because a committed-200 stream hides failures from the status code and the Errors metric. The identical argument applies to this path and was never carried across. deploy-check.mjs guards the corpus at deploy time, but only when someone remembers to run it, and it cannot catch a corpus that fails to read at runtime (bad package, truncated upload, a `files` whitelist change). _Fix:_ Add a second `AWS::Logs::MetricFilter` on TutorLogGroup with `FilterPattern: '"tutorCorpusMissing"'` producing `QuantumTutor/TutorCorpusMissing`, plus a CloudWatch alarm on it (Sum over 5 minutes &gt; 0, TreatMissingData: notBreaching, AlarmActions: [!Ref AlertsTopic]) — an exact structural copy of the TutorErrorMetricFilter/TutorErrorAlarm pair three resources above it. Extend template.test.mjs's existing 'every alarm notifies the alerts topic' test (it already iterates `ofType('AWS::CloudWatch::Alarm')`) and add the same index.mjs term-pinning assertion the tutorError test uses at template.test.mjs:70-71, so the new filter cannot drift from the emission either. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/index.mjs:31-40`, `lambda/tutor/index.mjs:79-84`, `lambda/tutor/template.yaml:178-186`, `lambda/tutor/template.yaml:215-235`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Architecture]** The corpus's section-discovery rule is a third independent enumeration of the curriculum, copy-pasted between builder and preflight and unpinned to the manifest that drives the Ask pill — `buildCorpusEntry` was deliberately lifted into tutor-core so the preflight rebuilds with the exact same logic that wrote the corpus — but the rule for *which sections exist* was not: it is duplicated verbatim in the builder and the preflight, and neither consults `content-manifest.json`, which is what decides where the web actually renders the Ask pill. So there are three independent answers to "what sections are there": validate_runnable.py -&gt; manifest (web), the builder's readdir scan (corpus), and the preflight's readdir scan (gate). The gate validates the corpus against the same scan that produced it, so it can only catch staleness, never a section the web offers the tutor on but the corpus has no key for. That mismatch is the feature's worst silent failure: the pill renders, the learner asks, and the endpoint returns OUT_OF_SCOPE_MESSAGE with no error anywhere. _Fix:_ Have both the builder and the preflight read the committed `web/src/lib/content-manifest.json` (no new dependency — it is a plain JSON file in the repo) and enumerate `sections[].dirName`, so the corpus's key set is derived from the same manifest that decides where the Ask pill renders. The preflight then gains the check it cannot currently make: every section the web will offer the tutor on has a non-empty corpus entry. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (`scripts/build_tutor_corpus.mjs:20-23`, `lambda/tutor/deploy-check.mjs:56-61`, `lambda/tutor/deploy-check.mjs:68-93`, `web/src/lib/manifest.ts:10`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** The offline suite exercises createHandlerCore but never the exported handler, and template.test.mjs pins the log-line contract while leaving the TUTOR_MODEL_ID contract — whose breakage kills the whole feature — unpinned — index.test.mjs genuinely exercises the per-request body: the streaming loop, the sentinel path, the paid-call gate, the oversized-body gate (lambda/tutor/index.test.mjs:48-139). But the production wiring is never constructed by any test. `handler` is built only when `awslambda.streamifyResponse` exists (index.mjs:120-131), which is never true under `node --test`, so three things go untested: the `HttpResponseStream.from(responseStream, { statusCode: 200, headers: { 'Content-Type': 'text/plain; charset=utf-8' } })` contract the client's byte-stream reader depends on, the per-invocation construction, and — most consequentially — `process.env.TUTOR_MODEL_ID` at index.mjs:129. That env var name is a two-sided contract: template.yaml:99 writes `TUTOR_MODEL_ID: !Ref ModelId`, index.mjs:129 reads it. If either side is renamed, `modelId` is `undefined`, every `ConverseStreamCommand` fails, and 100% of learners get the error sentinel — a total feature outage. The repo already invented the right defence for exactly this class of code/template drift: template.test.mjs:70-76 reads index.mjs and asserts the metric filter's literal term still appears in it, so 'an edit to the log line cannot silently disconnect the alarm from the code.' That reasoning was applied to the observability string and not to the env var that makes the function work at all. I confirmed template.test.mjs contains no reference to Environment, Variables, or TUTOR_MODEL_ID. deploy-check.mjs validates a model id you hand it on the command line (deploy-check.mjs:118), never the one the template will actually inject. _Fix:_ Add one assertion to template.test.mjs in the style it already uses: read index.mjs, extract the env key the handler reads (`process.env.<NAME>`), and assert the same key appears under the function's `Environment: Variables:` block in template.yaml — so renaming either side fails CI instead of production. Separately, add a small test file that defines a fake `awslambda` global (`streamifyResponse` returning the passed fn, `HttpResponseStream.from` recording its init) before a dynamic `import('./index.mjs')`, then asserts the handler is a function and that it commits statusCode 200 with `text/plain; charset=utf-8` — the exact contract the client's `res.body.getReader()` loop at ask-tutor.tsx:124-146 depends on. Both stay inside `node --test` with the AWS client stubbed; no new dependency. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (`lambda/tutor/index.mjs:120-131`, `lambda/tutor/index.test.mjs:13`, `lambda/tutor/index.mjs:129`, `lambda/tutor/template.test.mjs:70-76`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Architecture]** The web-side codegen pipeline and its 90-line Jest suite exist to deliver one 22-character constant, re-testing code the lambda suite already covers — `web/src/lib/tutor.ts` re-exports six symbols from the generated copy of tutor-core, and a prebuild/pretest codegen hook exists to produce that copy. But grepping all of `web/src` shows exactly one production consumer: `ask-tutor.tsx` imports `TUTOR_ERROR_SENTINEL` and nothing else. `SECTION_CHAR_CAP`, `OUT_OF_SCOPE_MESSAGE`, `stripGuideForTutor`, `extractSectionHeadings`, and `buildSystemPrompt` have no runtime caller in the web app at all — their only consumer is `web/__tests__/lib/tutor.test.ts`, which tests the *same source file* the lambda suite tests, through a copy. Every assertion in the web suite has a strictly stronger counterpart in `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.test.mjs`, and CI runs both. The three-runtime mirroring story is sound; the web arm of it is carrying five dead re-exports and a duplicate suite. _Fix:_ Narrow `web/src/lib/tutor.ts` to re-export only `TUTOR_ERROR_SENTINEL` (plus `TutorSection` if kept), and replace `web/__tests__/lib/tutor.test.ts` with a short codegen-contract test: the generated module loads under ts-jest and its sentinel is byte-identical to the one in `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs`. That keeps the single-source guarantee the pipeline exists for, keeps the drift alarm on the one constant that crosses the wire, and removes ~90 lines of second-copy tests plus five unused exports. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/tutor.ts:11-18`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:5`, `web/__tests__/lib/tutor.test.ts:27-86`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.test.mjs:26-97`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** Cmd-K re-implements close()'s abort-and-restore-focus inside a setState updater, so the two close paths can silently diverge — `close()` is the single definition of what closing the panel means: abort the in-flight stream, clear `open`, hand focus back to the trigger. The Cmd-K toggle needs the previous state, so it inlines a second copy of the abort-and-focus half inside the `setOpen` updater rather than calling `close()`. That leaves two places that must stay in sync — adding, say, an `setError(null)` to `close()` would leave Cmd-K-to-close silently different — and it puts side effects inside a state updater, which React treats as pure and double-invokes under StrictMode in development. _Fix:_ Track the open state in a ref alongside the state (or read it via a ref set in the existing `open` effect) so the Cmd-K handler can branch outside the updater and call `close()` / `setOpen(true)` directly. One definition of "close", no side effects inside a state updater. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:57-61`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:69-75`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Architecture]** policy.json is a second, account-hardcoded copy of the IAM statement template.yaml already derives from parameters — The shipped deploy path is SAM, and template.yaml builds the least-privilege Bedrock statement from the `ModelId` and `FoundationModelId` parameters. `policy.json` holds the same statement a second time with the account id, the application-inference-profile id, and the foundation-model version all hardcoded, existing solely for a raw-CLI fallback documented in the README. It is already a known maintenance tax: the account-migration runbook has a line item to hand-edit it, and flags it as carrying a stale account reference. _Fix:_ Retire `policy.json`/`trust.json` and the raw-CLI fallback section of the README, leaving `template.yaml` as the single definition of the function's IAM posture. If the fallback must survive the in-flight account migration, at minimum replace the hardcoded ARNs with placeholder tokens so the file cannot read as a live, authoritative policy pinned to the account being migrated away from. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/policy.json:1-24`, `lambda/tutor/template.yaml:100-112`, `docs/account-migration-runbook.md:595`, `lambda/tutor/README.md:16,61-63`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** tutor-core.mjs's header — the map the whole single-sourcing design relies on — still says two consumers after PR #89 made it three — tutor-core.mjs's docstring is the navigational map for the single-source design: it enumerates who imports the module and why the web gets a generated copy instead. PR #89 added `deploy-check.mjs`, which imports `buildCorpusEntry` from it directly, but the header was not updated. Anyone reasoning about a change to the strip/heading/entry logic reads "Two consumers" and misses the deploy gate — the exact file whose correctness depends on rebuilding with identical logic. _Fix:_ Update the header to three consumers and name deploy-check.mjs alongside index.mjs and build_tutor_corpus.mjs, matching what buildCorpusEntry's own docstring already says. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:8-11`, `lambda/tutor/deploy-check.mjs:28`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:72-79`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Consistency]** index.mjs drifts from two lambda-family idioms: per-invocation core construction and a byte cap measured in string length — Two small divergences from the sibling handlers. First, qpu and sync both build their DI-core once at module scope and export a one-line handler; the tutor rebuilds the closure on every invocation, justified by a comment that Lambda env vars are re-read per call — but env-var changes replace the execution environment, so the value cannot change within a container's life and the rebuild buys nothing. Second, `MAX_BODY_BYTES` is compared against `event.body.length`, which is UTF-16 code units for a raw body and base64 characters for an encoded one, neither of which is bytes — the exact class of bug the sync handler calls out and solves. _Fix:_ Hoist the core to module scope (`const core = createHandlerCore({ client, corpus: CORPUS, modelId: process.env.TUTOR_MODEL_ID })`) so all three handlers read the same, and switch the size gate to `Buffer.byteLength(event.body, "utf8")` so the constant's name matches what is measured. Neither changes behaviour for legitimate traffic; both remove a reader's "why is this one different?". Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/index.mjs:126-129`, `lambda/qpu/index.mjs:8-17`, `lambda/sync/index.mjs:136-141`, `lambda/tutor/index.mjs:47,51`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[UI]** The "Grounded in" label is hand-derived from the slug and renders 04-quantum-ml as "Quantum Ml", diverging from the title in the prompt and on the page — `lessonLabel()` reconstructs a display name by stripping the numeric prefix and title-casing hyphen segments. For 04-quantum-ml that yields the visibly broken 'Quantum Ml'. For every section it also diverges from the two authoritative titles the learner and the model actually see: the lesson page renders the GUIDE's H1 ('Quantum Hardware on Amazon Braket'), and the Lambda puts that same corpus title into the system prompt — while the panel header claims grounding in 'Hardware'. So the one line whose job is to state provenance names something that appears nowhere else in the system. The canonical title is already available client-side and byte-identical to the corpus title, so this is a hand-rolled substitute for data the app already has. _Fix:_ Replace `lessonLabel(slug)` with `getSectionBySlug(slug)?.title`, keeping the slug-derived version only as a fallback if the lookup misses. The header then names exactly what the Lambda was grounded in and exactly what the page heading says. Update the assertion in ask-tutor.test.tsx:74 (currently pinning 'Algorithms') to the canonical title. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:31-37`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:206`, `web/src/lib/sections.ts:39-41`, `web/src/app/learn/[section]/page.tsx:76`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[UI]** The trigger pill hardcodes the mac ⌘K glyph and shows it on touch viewports that have no keyboard — The keyboard handler accepts either metaKey or ctrlKey, but the chip on the pill is the literal string '⌘K' at every breakpoint. On Windows and Linux the advertised shortcut is simply wrong — the working chord there is Ctrl-K. And at 375px, where the pill is deliberately repositioned for touch, the chip still occupies width advertising a chord no touch user can press. It is the only piece of the tutor's chrome that states something untrue about the platform it is running on. _Fix:_ Hide the chip below the lg breakpoint (`hidden lg:inline-flex`), matching where the pointer/keyboard affordance is actually usable and where the pill already takes its desktop position. For the glyph, resolve it once on mount from a navigator.platform/userAgentData check into a state value ('⌘K' or 'Ctrl K'), defaulting to the mac glyph so the static export's first paint is unchanged and hydration stays clean. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:67`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:188-190`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:185`, `web/__tests__/components/ask-tutor.test.tsx:58-68`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[UX]** The composer submits on Enter with no indication, so a multi-line textarea silently refuses newlines — The control is a `rows={3}` textarea — an affordance that reads unambiguously as 'multi-line input' — but bare Enter is intercepted and submits. Shift+Enter is the newline escape hatch and is advertised nowhere: not in the placeholder, not near the button, not in the empty-state copy. A learner composing a two-part question presses Enter to break a line and instead fires an incomplete question at a paid model, with no undo and no draft recovery. The affordance and the behavior actively contradict each other. _Fix:_ Add a `.text-caption` hint in the empty left side of the existing button row (line 269's flex container already has `justify-end` — switch to `justify-between`) reading 'Enter to send, Shift+Enter for a new line'. No new markup nesting, no layout change, and it uses the canonical caption token already used elsewhere in the panel. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:265`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:259-264`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:266`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:269-277`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** The deploy preflight gate is a copy-paste line in the README, wired into no script — skipping it is the default failure mode — deploy-check.mjs is a well-built gate that catches exactly the silent catastrophe the handler warns about (a missing or stale corpus makes the tutor answer OUT_OF_SCOPE to every learner, with a 200 status and no alarm). But nothing invokes it. It is line 34 of a hand-typed README block; `sam build` and `sam deploy` on the next two lines run identically whether or not line 34 was executed. There is no `deploy` or `predeploy` script in the Lambda's package.json, no Makefile target, and no root package.json script. CI runs `npm test`, which exercises the gate's pure helpers via deploy-check.test.mjs but never calls `runPreflight` against a real corpus — corpus.json is gitignored, so CI structurally cannot. The gate therefore protects only a deployer who remembers to run it. _Fix:_ Add `"deploy": "node deploy-check.mjs && sam build && sam deploy"` to lambda/tutor/package.json (reading TUTOR_MODEL_ID from the environment, as deploy-check.mjs already does) and collapse the README's deploy block to `npm --prefix web run build:tutor-corpus` followed by `npm run deploy`. The gate then cannot be skipped by skipping a line, which is the only way it is currently skipped. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/deploy-check.mjs:117-131`, `lambda/tutor/package.json:13-15`, `lambda/tutor/README.md:31-38`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml:99-127`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Performance and cost]** The Function URL CORS block sets no MaxAge, so nearly every question pays an extra browser-to-us-east-2 preflight round trip before the first token — Lambda Function URL CORS defaults `Access-Control-Max-Age` to 5 seconds (AWS docs, Creating and managing Lambda function URLs — CORS: "MaxAge | 5 (default), 300"). The template never overrides it. The client always sends `x-amz-content-sha256`, a custom header, which forces a CORS preflight on every POST. Because the tutor's usage pattern is read-think-type — always far more than 5 seconds between questions — the cached preflight has essentially always expired, so each ask is OPTIONS then POST. And the edge cannot absorb it: the distribution's cache policy is Managed-CachingDisabled and CachedMethods is only GET/HEAD, so each OPTIONS travels the full path to the us-east-2 origin. That is a complete extra cross-region round trip added to time-to-first-token on a feature whose entire perceived quality is how fast the stream starts. _Fix:_ Add `MaxAge: 600` to the `FunctionUrlConfig.Cors` block in template.yaml. One line, no code change, and the preflight then amortizes across a whole lesson session instead of being re-paid on nearly every question. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/template.yaml:116-127`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:113-122`, `lambda/tutor/edge.yaml:96-99`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Security (what the Lambda trusts from the client)]** OUT_OF_SCOPE_MESSAGE is returned for three unrelated failures, so an oversized paste is answered with a false claim about scope — The handler funnels three distinct client-input conditions into one learner-facing string: unknown slug, empty question, and a body over MAX_BODY_BYTES. The first is genuinely out of scope; the other two are not. A learner who pastes a long traceback or code block past the 16 KB body cap is told "I can only help with the lessons in this curriculum. Open a lesson and ask me about it there." — while sitting inside the lesson they just asked about. The message is not merely unhelpful, it asserts something false about the request, and the learner has no way to discover the real cause. The 2000-character question cap has the matching problem in the other direction: the Lambda silently truncates, and the textarea sets no maxLength and shows no counter, so an over-long question is answered against a sentence-fragment the learner never sees. index.test.mjs currently pins the oversized-body-to-OUT_OF_SCOPE behavior as correct, which locks the confusion in. _Fix:_ Have parseBody distinguish over-cap from unparseable (return a sentinel rather than `{}`) and emit a separate short message for it — something naming length, not scope. Add `maxLength={2000}` to the textarea so the client enforces the same limit the Lambda does instead of letting a question be silently cut. Update index.test.mjs test D to assert the new message, keeping the no-model-call guarantee it already checks. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/index.mjs:49-58`, `lambda/tutor/index.mjs:81-85`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:31-32`, `lambda/tutor/index.mjs:74`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** Questions over 2000 characters are silently sliced mid-sentence and answered as if complete — a different cap and a different failure from the 16 KB body gate — `createHandlerCore` truncates the question with `String(body.question || '').trim().slice(0, 2000)` (lambda/tutor/index.mjs:74) and then passes the fragment straight to Bedrock as the user turn (index.mjs:90). There is no error, no sentinel, and no signal of any kind — the learner gets a fluent, confident answer to a sentence that was cut off mid-word. This is distinct from the 16 KB body gate at index.mjs:47/51, which at least produces a visible (if mislabelled) out-of-scope response: a 2,500-character question sails past the body check and lands in the silent-truncation path instead. The client offers no protection: the textarea at ask-tutor.tsx:254-268 has no `maxLength`, no character counter, and no client-side length check, so nothing in the UI tells the learner a limit exists. The realistic trigger is ordinary learner behaviour — pasting a traceback, a circuit definition, or a multi-part question about a chemistry Hamiltonian. The 2000 is also a bare magic number that exists only in index.mjs while its stated contract is duplicated in prose in the comment at index.mjs:44, and the repo already has the mechanism for sharing a constant across the two runtimes (`TUTOR_ERROR_SENTINEL` is exported from tutor-core.mjs:28 and reaches the client via web/src/lib/tutor.ts:11-18). _Fix:_ Export the cap from tutor-core.mjs as `MAX_QUESTION_CHARS = 2000` alongside the existing shared constants, consume it in index.mjs:74 in place of the literal, re-export it through web/src/lib/tutor.ts, and set it as `maxLength` on the textarea. The browser then stops the input at the boundary the server enforces, so truncation becomes visible at the keyboard instead of invisible in the answer — and the two caps can no longer drift, which is the same drift-proofing rationale the file header already gives for the sentinel. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/index.mjs:74`, `lambda/tutor/index.mjs:90`, `lambda/tutor/index.mjs:47,51`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:254-268`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Security (deployment posture)]** The README's raw-CLI fallback rebuilds the exact pre-PR-#57 public endpoint the same README spends 30 lines explaining how to close, and its only live smoke test cannot pass against the shipped configuration — The 'Deploy (raw CLI, fallback)' recipe creates the Function URL with `--auth-type NONE` and a CORS AllowHeaders list of `['content-type']` only (lambda/tutor/README.md:71-73). Both values are the pre-hardening posture. template.yaml has moved on: `FunctionUrlAuthType` defaults to `AWS_IAM` (template.yaml:47-55) and AllowHeaders includes `x-amz-content-sha256` (template.yaml:125-127) precisely because OAC requires the body hash on POST. Anyone who takes the documented fallback — and it is documented as a peer option, not as archaeology — stands up an endpoint that is publicly invokable, bypasses the CloudFront distribution entirely, and therefore has no WAF per-IP rate limit in front of paid Bedrock generations. The README itself describes that as the failure the edge stack exists to prevent, sixty lines later at README.md:126-139. The same drift breaks verification: the 'Live end-to-end' curl at README.md:100-105 posts to `<FunctionUrl>` with only `content-type`, no `x-amz-content-sha256` — against the AWS_IAM + OAC deployment that actually ships, that returns 403, so the single documented way to prove the tutor works end to end is unrunnable. This is the same section that consumes the account-hardcoded policy.json, which compounds it. _Fix:_ Delete the raw-CLI create-function-url-config block or rewrite it to `--auth-type AWS_IAM` with both CORS headers and an explicit note that it must be paired with the edge stack and scripts/grant-oac.sh — the SAM path is the supported one and the fallback now only encodes a security regression. Repoint the live smoke test at the CloudFront distribution domain with an `x-amz-content-sha256` value computed from the body (openssl dgst -sha256), so the documented verification exercises the path that actually ships. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/README.md:71-73`, `lambda/tutor/template.yaml:47-55`, `lambda/tutor/template.yaml:125-127`, `lambda/tutor/README.md:126-139`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)

#### Feature 20 — 2026-07-20 Category D re-audit (24 adversarially-verified findings; security claims required a named exploit path, drift claims were checked for current alignment, and the grounding-integrity items were proven by executing the real functions against the seven live GUIDEs)

- [x] **[Accessibility]** The tutor slide-over claims aria-modal="true" but is the only one of the three dialogs that neither locks body scroll nor marks the background inert — `aria-modal="true"` is a promise to assistive tech that everything outside the dialog is unavailable. The tutor makes that claim and then keeps the entire page reachable: the lesson body, site header, footer and the sidebar drawer toggle stay in the accessibility tree, stay pointer-clickable, and the document keeps scrolling behind the panel. Both sibling dialogs do the work — the section gate locks `document.body.style.overflow`, and the mobile drawer locks scroll AND marks a shared list of regions inert precisely to make its aria-modal claim truthful. The concrete failure: a VoiceOver/NVDA user swipes past the panel edge straight into lesson prose the ARIA contract says is not there; a mouse user clicks lesson content, focus leaves the dialog, and from that moment Escape no longer closes the panel and Tab no longer cycles — because both are wired to `onKeyDown` on the dialog div, which only fires when focus is inside it. At 375px the panel is `w-full`, so the page scrolling underneath a full-screen overlay is the default touch experience, not an edge case. _Fix:_ Add an open-gated effect to AskTutor that mirrors sidebar.tsx: lock `document.body.style.overflow` and set `inert` on the header, lesson content, footer and the tutor's own trigger, restoring both in the cleanup. Reuse `lib/layout-regions.ts` rather than hand-rolling ids — export a `TUTOR_INERT_REGION_IDS` const there alongside `DRAWER_INERT_REGION_IDS` (same four ids, and the tutor wants its own pill inert too) so the two modal surfaces share one registry and a rename still breaks loudly. Cover it in web/__tests__/components/ask-tutor.test.tsx the way the drawer is covered: open the panel, assert `document.body.style.overflow === "hidden"` and that a stub `#lesson-content` carries the `inert` attribute, then close and assert both are restored. Impact: High. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:196-200`, `web/src/components/sidebar.tsx:88-120`, `web/src/components/section-gate-modal.tsx:56-67`, `web/src/lib/layout-regions.ts:7-19`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Performance and cost]** SECTION_CHAR_CAP now truncates all seven lessons — 05-quantum-chemistry loses 43% of its grounding text and 7 of its 16 headings — to save ~2k input tokens per request — The 12,000-char cap was set when it clipped a few lessons; the GUIDEs have grown since. Rebuilding every corpus entry from the current GUIDEs with `buildCorpusEntry` shows 7 of 7 sections truncated, and the drops are no longer marginal: 05-quantum-chemistry keeps 11,818 of 20,834 chars (43% discarded) and advertises 9 of 16 headings, 01-foundations discards 3,568 chars and 7 headings, 06-hybrid-jobs 3,246 chars and 4 headings, 02-hardware 2,440 chars and 5 headings. The truncation is boundary-safe and the heading filter is honest (it stops the prompt claiming coverage it lacks), so the failure is silent-by-design: a learner asking about the back half of the chemistry lesson gets a correct-sounding "that's outside this lesson" refusal from OUT_OF_SCOPE-adjacent prompt rules. This is the previously-closed truncation item regressing in magnitude — the mitigations landed, the cap did not move. The cap is nominally a token-cost guard, but the arithmetic no longer supports it: 12,000 chars is roughly 3.2k tokens against Haiku 4.5's 200k context at $1/MTok input, so restoring the full chemistry lesson costs about 2.4k extra input tokens, well under a fifth of a cent per request, against an 800-token output that already costs more. _Fix:_ Raise `SECTION_CHAR_CAP` to a value that clears the largest GUIDE with headroom (24,000 chars covers 05-quantum-chemistry's 20,834 and leaves room for growth) and keep the existing truncate-on-boundary + heading-filter behavior as the backstop for a future runaway GUIDE. One constant in tutor-core.mjs, single-sourced across all three runtimes; re-run build:tutor-corpus and the WARN lines go quiet, which is itself the regression signal for next time. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:19`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:86`, `scripts/build_tutor_corpus.mjs:32-38`, `lambda/tutor/index.mjs:91`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** The emphasis-strip regex deletes every underscore in the grounding text, so the corpus feeds the model corrupted Braket/PennyLane API names — stripGuideForTutor unwraps inline code to bare text first, then strips emphasis with a blunt character-class delete `[*_]{1,3}` over the whole document. Because the code spans have already been flattened into prose by that point, every intra-word underscore in an identifier is deleted along with the Markdown emphasis marks. The corpus therefore tells the model the API is `circuit.statevector()`, `AwsDevice.getdevices()`, `logmetric`, `stoppingcondition`. This directly undermines the two guardrails the prompt is built around — it orders the model to never guess Braket/PennyLane method names and to use the lesson's own notation, while the grounding text hands it names that do not exist. A learner asking "what method gives me the amplitudes?" gets an answer sourced from mangled text. The root cause is that a regex named for emphasis is implemented as an unconditional character delete rather than a span unwrap. _Fix:_ Make the emphasis strip an actual span unwrap instead of a character delete. Handle asterisks with span patterns (`/\*\*\*([^*]+)\*\*\*/g`, `/\*\*([^*]+)\*\*/g`, `/\*([^*\n]+)\*/g` -&gt; "$1") and require non-word boundaries for underscore emphasis (e.g. `/(?<![\w])_{1,3}([^_\n]+)_{1,3}(?![\w])/g` -&gt; "$1") so `state_vector` survives while `_italic_` is unwrapped. Add a case to lambda/tutor/tutor-core.test.mjs pinning that an inline-code identifier with underscores reaches the output intact — the existing tests at :49-50 already lock the emphasis behavior, so both invariants stay covered. Requires rebuilding the corpus before the next deploy; lambda/tutor/deploy-check.mjs will flag it as stale until you do, which is the gate working as designed. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:46`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:50`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:110,112`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.test.mjs:49-50`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[UX]** Cmd/Ctrl-K is preventDefault'd on every page of the site, including the ~100 pages where the tutor renders nothing — The global keydown listener is registered in a `useEffect` that sits ABOVE the component's early return, so it runs on every route the root layout renders. On /glossary, /pricing, /playground, /workspace and the home page the handler fires, calls `e.preventDefault()`, and flips `open` to true — but the component has already returned `null`, so no panel appears. The learner's Ctrl-K (address-bar/search focus in Chrome and Firefox on Windows/Linux) and Cmd-K (Chrome's search-from-address-bar on macOS) are swallowed site-wide with zero visible effect. The same happens on every page when `NEXT_PUBLIC_TUTOR_URL` is unset — i.e. any deploy without the tutor kills Cmd-K everywhere. Separately, the pill's `<kbd>` hardcodes the macOS Command glyph, so Windows and Linux learners are shown a shortcut key their keyboard does not have even though the handler accepts `ctrlKey`. _Fix:_ Move the inert check into the handler: `const onKey = (e) => { if (!url || !slug) return; ... }` and add `[url, slug]` to the dep array (hook order stays intact, so the existing early return is untouched). That restores the browser's native Cmd/Ctrl-K everywhere the tutor is not actually available. For the glyph, derive the label once from a `navigator.platform`/`userAgentData.platform` check at mount and render `⌘K` or `Ctrl K` accordingly, keeping `aria-label="Ask about this lesson"` as the accessible name so WCAG 2.5.3 Label in Name still holds. Extend web/__tests__/components/ask-tutor.test.tsx with a case that sets `mockPathname = "/"`, fires `keyDown(window, {key: "k", metaKey: true})`, and asserts `preventDefault` was not called. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:65-80`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:89-90`, `web/src/app/layout.tsx:85`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:188-190`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[UI]** Every tutor answer renders raw Markdown and LaTeX as literal characters, because the prompt never forbids formatting and the panel renders plain text — `buildSystemPrompt` sets four rules — grounding, no-invention, Socratic, concise/no-emoji — and says nothing about output format. Claude's default register is Markdown, so a typical answer arrives with `**bold**`, `-` bullets, `###` sub-headings and inline `$...$` or backticked identifiers. The panel renders that stream into a single `<p className="whitespace-pre-wrap">`, so the learner literally reads asterisks, hashes and dollar signs. This is worse than generic ugliness here: the rest of the platform renders KaTeX and prose properly, so the tutor is the one surface where a learner sees the markup instead of the math — directly undercutting the rule that tells the model to "use the lesson's own notation". It also compounds with the corpus stripper, which removes `$` delimiters (tutor-core.mjs:44-45) so the grounding text the model reads is already de-mathed while the model's reply is not. _Fix:_ Add one rule line to `buildSystemPrompt` in lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs — the single source of truth, so the build script, the Lambda and the web copy all inherit it with no mirroring risk: `- Reply in plain prose. No Markdown formatting (no asterisks, hashes, backticks or bullet markers) and no LaTeX delimiters — your answer is rendered as plain text.` Then pin it in lambda/tutor/tutor-core.test.mjs next to the existing guardrail assertions (tutor-core.test.mjs:81-92 already asserts each clause by regex), and note in the web test that the panel is a deliberate plain-text sink. This is a prompt refinement, not a new renderer — no new dependency and no change to the static export. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:104-116`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:226-228`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:43-47`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[UX]** Transport failures surface raw exception strings to learners — a WAF rate-limit block reads as "request failed (403)" — Two of the three client failure paths put developer text straight into the learner-facing error line. A non-OK response throws a template string that becomes the visible message, and any other exception has its raw `.message` rendered. The edge stack in front of this endpoint is a WAFv2 rate-based rule that Blocks per-IP abuse, so the single most likely production failure a learner will ever hit — tripping the rate limit — renders as `request failed (403)` (WAFv2's default Block response code; the template's own comment claims 429, which is itself a mismatch worth confirming before writing the copy). A dropped connection or offline device renders as `Failed to fetch` (Chrome), `Load failed` (Safari) or `NetworkError when attempting to fetch resource.` (Firefox). Only the watchdog path has human copy. There is no retry affordance either: the learner has to re-trigger by hand with no indication that the failure is transient. _Fix:_ Map status and exception to learner copy at the two throw sites rather than passing `.message` through. Keep it small and honest — e.g. 403/429 → "You're asking faster than the tutor can keep up. Wait a minute and try again."; 5xx → "The tutor is having trouble right now. Try again in a moment."; anything else (including a thrown fetch TypeError) → the existing generic "The tutor hit an error. Please try again." already used by the in-band sentinel path at ask-tutor.tsx:139-141, so the two failure channels read identically to the learner. Extend web/__tests__/components/ask-tutor.test.tsx with a `{ok: false, status: 403}` case asserting the alert does not contain "403". While there, verify the WAF Block response code against the template's `HTTP 429` comment (edge.yaml:5) so the copy is keyed to reality. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:123`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:147-154`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:237-241`, `lambda/tutor/edge.yaml:42-50`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[UI]** The panel hand-rolls the glass recipe, losing its @supports opaque fallback and swapping the rim-lit elevation token for a raw shadow-2xl — The system owns a `.glass` recipe whose whole point is a guarded translucent fill: the opaque `--surface-1` background is declared unconditionally and the translucent `var(--glass)` fill only inside an `@supports (backdrop-filter: ...)` block, with a comment explaining that the fill must live inside the guard or the fallback can never win. The tutor panel — the one surface where this matters most, since it is an overlay stacked on arbitrary lesson content — bypasses the recipe and applies `bg-(--glass)` raw. Where `backdrop-filter` is unsupported or disabled, the dark theme's `--glass` is white at 4.5% alpha, so the panel is effectively transparent: near-white `--ink` answer text sits directly on top of the lesson prose it is overlapping. The same line also uses `shadow-2xl`, the only raw Tailwind shadow-scale value anywhere in web/src, on a system that replaced exactly that pattern with the rim-lit `--shadow-resting`/`--shadow-raised` tokens; the tutor is visibly a different material from every other elevated surface. The component's own trigger pill, twenty lines earlier, uses `.glass` correctly — so the inconsistency is internal to the file. _Fix:_ Replace `bg-(--glass) shadow-2xl backdrop-blur-xl` with the `glass` class plus a `shadow-(--shadow-raised)` override for the overlay tier, keeping `border-l border-(--bd)` for the single-edge treatment (the recipe's all-round border is overridden by the more specific utility). The panel then inherits the opaque `--surface-1` fallback automatically and reads as the same material as every other elevated surface. If the overlay wants a heavier blur than the recipe's `blur(20px) saturate(1.3)`, keep `backdrop-blur-xl` as an additive utility — the guard still governs the fill. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:200`, `web/src/app/globals.css:343-361`, `web/src/app/globals.css:224`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:185`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** The corpus deploy gate is prose in a README, not an executable step — and the working tree's corpus.json is stale for all 7 sections right now — PR #89 added `lambda/tutor/deploy-check.mjs` as the preflight that fails when corpus.json is missing or stale. But nothing invokes it: it appears only in README/runbook prose and in its own unit test. `lambda/tutor/package.json` has exactly one script (`"test": "node --test"`), so `cd lambda/tutor && sam build && sam deploy` — the path a human actually types — packages whatever corpus.json is sitting on disk with no check at all. I ran `runPreflight()` against the current working tree and it reports every one of the seven sections stale, i.e. a deploy from this tree today would ship grounding text that predates the Category B/C GUIDE edits (qft fence period:2, qtopo drag wording, 02-hardware KaTeX fix). CI cannot cover this — corpus.json is gitignored — so the only place the gate can bite is the deploy command itself, which is exactly where it is absent. _Fix:_ Make the gate the only way to deploy. Add to `lambda/tutor/package.json`: `"build:corpus": "node ../../scripts/build_tutor_corpus.mjs"`, `"predeploy": "npm run build:corpus && node deploy-check.mjs"`, `"deploy": "sam build && sam deploy"` (npm runs `predeploy` automatically, and a non-zero exit from deploy-check aborts before `sam build`). Then rewrite the README's deploy block to the single line `TUTOR_MODEL_ID=<arn> npm run deploy`, keeping the four-command form only as the documented fallback. This uses the repo's existing npm-script idiom, adds no dependency, and turns a step a human can skip into one they cannot. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/deploy-check.mjs:95-115`, `lambda/tutor/package.json:13-15`, `lambda/tutor/README.md:30-47`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml:105,124-125`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Performance and cost]** The handler discards every non-text stream event, so per-request Bedrock token spend and max_tokens truncation are both unobservable — The delta loop reads `item.contentBlockDelta?.delta?.text` and ignores everything else on the stream. Two of the discarded events are the only per-request cost and quality signals the endpoint gets: the `metadata` event carries `usage.{inputTokens,outputTokens,totalTokens}`, and `messageStop` carries `stopReason`. Consequences: (1) the sole automated cost control is the `HighInvocationsAlarm` on raw Lambda Invocations, which counts a zero-token OUT_OF_SCOPE reply (no Bedrock call at all) identically to a full ~3.2k-in/800-out generation — so the 500/hr threshold cannot distinguish a bot spraying empty POSTs from real paid traffic, and nothing tells you the actual cost per learner question. The README's cost-attribution story (application inference profile + cost-allocation tags) is real but lands in Cost Explorer ~24h late and only in aggregate. (2) When the answer hits `maxTokens: 800` it is cut mid-sentence, the client renders the truncated text as a complete answer, and neither the learner nor CloudWatch has any indication — `stopReason: "max_tokens"` was on the wire and thrown away. _Fix:_ In the existing loop, capture `item.metadata?.usage` and `item.messageStop?.stopReason`, and after `stream.end()` emit one structured line in the same shape as the tutorError log: `console.log(JSON.stringify({ tutorUsage: true, slug, inputTokens, outputTokens, stopReason }))`. Then add a `"tutorUsage"` metric filter to template.yaml alongside the existing one, extracting outputTokens as the metric value. That gives a real paid-token metric (distinct from raw invocations) and makes max_tokens truncation countable, using only the pattern the stack already ships. template.test.mjs's existing pin-the-term-to-index.mjs assertion extends to the new filter for free. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/index.mjs:95-99`, `lambda/tutor/index.mjs:91`, `lambda/tutor/template.yaml:215-235`, `lambda/tutor/template.yaml:178-187`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
