- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** edge.yaml — the entire access-control boundary — has zero test coverage, while the alarm wiring next to it is pinned line-by-line — template.test.mjs exists precisely because a silent config drift in this stack is invisible at runtime, and it pins the metric filter, both alarms, the SNS topic name, and the log group's literal name. But it reads only template.yaml (`readFileSync(new URL("./template.yaml", ...))`), so nothing asserts anything about edge.yaml — the file that holds every actual security control — nor about the two security-relevant parameters in template.yaml itself. Four concrete regressions ship green today: (a) `CachePolicyId` changed away from the Managed-CachingDisabled id `4135ea2d-...` would make CloudFront cache one learner's streamed answer and serve it to the next learner asking a different question on a different lesson; (b) `SigningBehavior: always` weakened, or `WebACLId` dropped from the distribution, silently removes OAC signing or the rate limit while everything still returns 200; (c) `FunctionUrlAuthType` left at `NONE` after a cutover (it is an AllowedValue, and the README's step-1 explicitly deploys with `FunctionUrlAuthType=NONE`) re-opens the raw Function URL to unsigned public POSTs, bypassing CloudFront, the WAF rate limit, and every edge control; (d) `AllowHeaders` losing `x-amz-content-sha256` breaks the browser POST preflight through OAC. Every one of these is a single-token YAML edit with no test and no runtime error. _Fix:_ Add `edge.test.mjs` in lambda/tutor reusing template.test.mjs's `section()`/`blocks()` helpers (copy or lift them into a tiny shared module — no YAML dependency, matching the existing rationale) asserting: the distribution references the WebACL, the origin carries the OAC id, `SigningBehavior: always`, and the `CachePolicyId` is exactly the Managed-CachingDisabled uuid. In template.test.mjs add two assertions: `FunctionUrlAuthType` `Default: AWS_IAM`, and the CORS `AllowHeaders` includes `x-amz-content-sha256` (pin it against the header web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:115 actually sends, the same drift-proofing the tutorError test already does against index.mjs). CI already runs this suite via the lambdas matrix, so the coverage is free. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/template.test.mjs:19`, `lambda/tutor/edge.yaml:97-99`, `lambda/tutor/edge.yaml:62-69,80`, `lambda/tutor/template.yaml:47-55,113-127`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Correctness]** The inline-math strip spans two unrelated currency figures on one line, deleting the dollar signs from every price in the grounding text — under a prompt whose headline rule is "NEVER invent … prices, or numbers" — `stripGuideForTutor` unwraps inline math with `t.replace(/\$([^$\n]+)\$/g, "$1")` (tutor-core.mjs:45). The regex cannot tell a math delimiter from a currency symbol, so on any line carrying two prices it matches from the first `$` to the second and deletes both, keeping the text between them. Every cost figure in the curriculum is authored as prose currency, so the grounding corpus the model actually receives contains bare, unlabelled numbers where the lesson said dollars. Where the author escaped the sign as `\$`, the backslash survives the strip and the `$` is eaten, producing literal garbage like `\0.30`. This is a different regex and a different failure from the emphasis/underscore strip on line 50 — fixing the underscore class does nothing here. It matters more than a cosmetic strip artifact because `buildSystemPrompt` (tutor-core.mjs:110) instructs the model to never invent prices and to answer only from this text: the model is being told to trust numbers the pipeline corrupted, on the one topic (Braket cost) the curriculum treats as a discipline. _Fix:_ Make the inline-math replace require a math-ish payload rather than "anything between two dollar signs". Two in-place options in the same line of tutor-core.mjs: (a) handle escaped `\$` first (replace `/\\\$/g` with a placeholder, restore it as `$` after all other passes) and require the inline-math body to contain a LaTeX signal — e.g. `/\$(?=[^$\n]*[\\^_{}])([^$\n]+)\$/g` — so `$\alpha$` and `$|\alpha|^2$` still unwrap while `$0.30) plus … $0.08` is left alone; or (b) exclude a digit immediately after the opening delimiter (`/\$(?![\d.,])([^$\n]+)\$/g`), which is enough for every case in this curriculum. Either way, add the real-GUIDE assertion described in the corpus-fixture finding so a currency figure that loses its `$` fails CI. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:45`, `02-hardware/GUIDE.md:219`, `02-hardware/GUIDE.md:226`, `06-hybrid-jobs/GUIDE.md:179`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Performance and cost]** The one paid gen-AI surface is the only part of the platform the sign-up wall does not cover: &lt;AskTutor /&gt; is mounted as a sibling of &lt;AuthWall&gt;, not inside it — PR #171 (a72b705) put the entire learning platform behind a sign-up wall — `AuthWall` wraps `{children}` inside `<main>`, and `PUBLIC_PATHS` deliberately excludes `/learn/*`. But `<AskTutor />` is rendered three lines further down as a *sibling* of `<main>`, so the wall never sees it, and `AskTutor` itself never consults `useAuth` — its only gate is `NEXT_PUBLIC_TUTOR_URL` plus a regex match on `/learn/<slug>` (ask-tutor.tsx:90). The consequence is not a rendering nit: for a signed-out visitor who deep-links to a lesson, the wall renders `GateScreen` ("Checking your access…") while the floating "Ask ⌘K" pill paints on top of it and Cmd/Ctrl-K opens a fully working tutor panel. The window is not a single frame — `AuthProvider` starts in `"configuring"` and stays there until the lazily-loaded ~30 KB Amplify bridge chunk downloads and resolves the Cognito session; `blocked` is false for that entire time, so no redirect has even been scheduled. Every question asked in that window is a billed Bedrock generation from an anonymous visitor, on the single surface in the product that costs real money per use, while the free lesson text beside it is gated. _Fix:_ Move `<AskTutor />` inside the `<AuthWall>` subtree in layout.tsx, or (if it must stay a fixed-position sibling of `<main>`) have `AskTutor` call the existing `useAuth()` and extend its early return to `if (!url || !slug || (status !== "unconfigured" && status !== "authenticated")) return null;`. Both use mechanisms already in the file, add no dependency, and keep the component fully inert when Cognito env is absent (unit tests, local dev), matching how `AuthWall` itself degrades. State plainly in the comment that this is product gating, not an access-control boundary — the Function URL stays anonymous behind CloudFront/WAF, and closing that would be the infra change described in edge.yaml, not an app change. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/layout.tsx:81-85`, `web/src/components/auth/auth-wall.tsx:14`, `web/src/components/auth/auth-wall.tsx:65,80`, `web/src/components/auth/auth-provider.tsx:40,44`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** Nothing in CI ever runs the strip over a real GUIDE.md — every fixture is synthetic markdown with balanced $ pairs and no underscores, which is precisely why both corruption bugs shipped — The corpus transform has three test files and none of them feeds it curriculum text. `web/__tests__/lib/tutor.test.ts` runs a 17-line hand-written SAMPLE; `lambda/tutor/deploy-check.test.mjs` writes a temp tree containing `"# Intro\n## A\nalpha body\n"`. Both fixtures are constructed — accidentally — to avoid every hazard the real files contain: the SAMPLE's dollar signs come in matched pairs (`$\alpha$` and `$|\alpha|^2$` on one line, so the regex pairs them correctly), it contains no `_`, no escaped `\$`, and no currency at all. The suite therefore asserts the strip is correct on input that cannot expose it, and pins that belief: `expect(out).toContain("|\\alpha|^2")` locks in the very substitution that eats prices in production. The seven real GUIDEs are sitting in the repo, and the repo already has the idiom for reading them from a test — `web/__tests__/content/reps-corpus.test.ts` walks `REPO_ROOT/<section>/GUIDE.md` today. Nothing new is needed to close this; it simply was never wired to the tutor's transform, so the currency bug, the underscore bug, and any future strip regression are all invisible to a green suite. _Fix:_ Add a content-level test beside reps-corpus.test.ts that runs `stripGuideForTutor` over each of the seven real GUIDE.md files and asserts corpus-content invariants rather than shape: every `$<digits>` figure in the source still carries its `$` in the output; no output contains a stray `\` immediately before a digit; no output contains a bare `[` `]` `(` `)` link remnant; and each section's output is non-empty. Keep it in web/__tests__ so it runs in the existing `web` CI job, and keep the synthetic SAMPLE tests as the fast unit layer. Written against today's tree this test fails on 02-hardware, 03-algorithms, 05-quantum-chemistry and 06-hybrid-jobs — which is the point. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/__tests__/lib/tutor.test.ts:9-25`, `web/__tests__/lib/tutor.test.ts:38`, `lambda/tutor/deploy-check.test.mjs:37,39`, `web/__tests__/content/reps-corpus.test.ts:37`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** The one header the deployed transport cannot work without is asserted by no test — deleting it leaves the whole suite green and 403s every production request — Reaching the Lambda through CloudFront OAC requires `x-amz-content-sha256` on every POST; the README calls this out in bold and the Function URL CORS config exists solely to allow the header through preflight. The hash helper is unit-tested well (`web/__tests__/sha256.test.ts` covers empty input, known vectors, determinism, UTF-8). But the *wiring* — that `ask-tutor.tsx` actually attaches the header to the request it sends, hashing the exact body string it posts — is asserted nowhere. The component test inspects `fetchMock.mock.calls[0][1].body` and never touches `.headers`, and the header is behind a `crypto.subtle` guard that does not resolve in the Jest environment (there is no jest.setup and no crypto polyfill in jest.config.ts). So the single line that makes the deployed transport work is covered by neither the unit test above it nor the component test around it: remove it, or hash a different string than the one posted, and CI stays green while 100% of live requests fail at the edge with a 403 that — per the previous finding — is indistinguishable from a rate-limit block. _Fix:_ In ask-tutor.test.tsx, stub `crypto.subtle.digest` on the jsdom global for one test (or point `globalThis.crypto` at `node:crypto`'s `webcrypto` in that file, matching how the same test already patches in `ReadableStream`/`TextEncoder` from node built-ins at lines 10-13) and assert two things about the recorded call: that `headers["x-amz-content-sha256"]` is present, and that it equals `await sha256Hex(recordedBody)` — pinning the body-hash correspondence, not just the header's existence. No new dependency; it reuses the node-globals shim idiom already at the top of the file. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:113-122`, `web/__tests__/components/ask-tutor.test.tsx:95-96`, `web/src/lib/sha256.ts:1-10`, `lambda/tutor/README.md:136-139`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Architecture]** The "third runtime" is test-only: an entire codegen pipeline exists to deliver one 23-character string constant — The feature is documented as mirroring prompt/grounding logic across three runtimes, and the mirroring is solved with a generated copy (scripts/gen-tutor-core.mjs -&gt; web/src/lib/tutor-core.generated.ts -&gt; web/src/lib/tutor.ts). But the web app does not actually run any of that logic. Its only runtime consumer imports a single constant, TUTOR_ERROR_SENTINEL. Everything else the barrel re-exports (stripGuideForTutor, extractSectionHeadings, buildSystemPrompt, SECTION_CHAR_CAP, OUT_OF_SCOPE_MESSAGE) and the TutorSection interface have zero consumers in web/src — they are used only by web/__tests__/lib/tutor.test.ts, which re-tests functions lambda/tutor/tutor-core.test.mjs already covers in CI. I confirmed the unused exports are tree-shaken out of the shipped bundle, so this machinery buys no runtime value at all: it is a build stage wired into BOTH `npm test` and `npm run build`, plus a `// @ts-nocheck` file that erases typing on the one symbol that IS used, plus a duplicated Jest suite. This is the single largest maintenance cost in the feature and it is paid for nothing. _Fix:_ Collapse the web side to what it actually uses: replace web/src/lib/tutor.ts's re-export barrel with a plain `export const TUTOR_ERROR_SENTINEL = "<<TUTOR-STREAM-ERROR>>";` (typed `string`, not `any`), then delete scripts/gen-tutor-core.mjs, the `gen:tutor-core`/`prebuild`/`pretest` hooks in web/package.json, web/src/lib/tutor-core.generated.ts and its .gitignore entry, and web/__tests__/lib/tutor.test.ts. Keep the mirror honest with a source-reading guard test in web/__tests__ that readFileSync's lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs, regexes out the `TUTOR_ERROR_SENTINEL = "..."` literal, and asserts it equals the web constant — the same technique custom-http.test.ts already uses on a repo-root file, no new dependency. Port the one unique assertion ("do not use emojis") into lambda/tutor/tutor-core.test.mjs so no coverage is lost. Net: one build stage, one ts-nocheck hole, one generated file and one duplicate suite removed; the prompt/grounding logic keeps exactly one home (lambda/tutor/) with exactly one test suite, and the one genuinely shared string is pinned by a test that fails loudly if either side drifts. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:5`, `web/src/lib/tutor.ts:11-24`, `scripts/gen-tutor-core.mjs:23-28`, `web/package.json:12,15`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The tutor panel's "Grounded in" line hand-rolls a lesson title from the slug instead of using the canonical section title, and gets 4 of 7 wrong — AskTutor derives the lesson name it displays by title-casing the URL slug, rather than reading the repo's canonical section list. Every other surface in the app — the lesson page heading, its metadata, the glossary chips — goes through getSectionBySlug, whose titles come from the generated content manifest that sections.ts explicitly calls the single source of truth. The result is that the panel's trust line disagrees with the H1 the learner is looking at, and, worse, disagrees with the title the Lambda actually puts in the system prompt: the corpus title is the GUIDE's H1 (tutor-core.mjs:82), which matches the manifest title exactly. So on /learn/05-quantum-chemistry the panel promises "Grounded in: Quantum Chemistry" while the model is told the lesson is titled "Quantum Chemistry & Biochemistry". For a feature whose entire value proposition is verifiable grounding, the one label that advertises the grounding is the one label that is wrong. _Fix:_ Delete lessonLabel and render `getSectionBySlug(slug)?.title ?? slug` instead — the manifest is a compile-time JSON import, so it works unchanged in the static export and adds no client cost. That removes seven lines of bespoke string munging, makes the panel agree with the lesson H1 and with the title the Lambda actually grounds on, and puts AskTutor on the same sections.ts path every other component already uses. The existing ask-tutor test mocks the pathname as /learn/03-algorithms (web/__tests__/components/ask-tutor.test.tsx:18), so an assertion that the panel shows "Quantum Algorithms" pins it. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:31-37`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:206`, `web/src/lib/sections.ts:13-23,39-41`, `web/src/app/learn/[section]/page.tsx:26,50`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The tutor Lambda's only build artifact is produced by an npm script belonging to the web package — corpus.json is the tutor Lambda's deploy artifact: it is gitignored, it is what `sam build` packages via the `files` list, and its absence silently degrades the tutor into answering OUT_OF_SCOPE for every learner. Yet the only named entry point for building it lives in web/package.json, and every instruction in the feature — the script's own header, the README, the preflight's usage block, and the preflight's error strings — tells the operator to reach through the web package to build a Lambda artifact. The script reads repo-root GUIDE.md files and writes into lambda/tutor/; it touches nothing under web/ and needs none of web's dependencies. This is a module-boundary inversion: the deploy artifact of one deployable is owned by an unrelated deployable's package manifest, and it is the one place in lambda/ where that happens. _Fix:_ Add `"build:corpus": "node ../../scripts/build_tutor_corpus.mjs"` to lambda/tutor/package.json and repoint the six documentation/error references at `npm --prefix lambda/tutor run build:corpus`, so the tutor's deploy artifact is produced from the tutor's own package alongside its preflight and its tests. The script file itself can stay in scripts/ (it is a repo-root-scanning build script like validate_runnable.py and stage-monaco.mjs). Drop the web alias once the docs are updated — nothing automated calls it. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/package.json:17`, `scripts/build_tutor_corpus.mjs:12-18,42-43`, `lambda/tutor/package.json:14-16`, `lambda/tutor/deploy-check.mjs:6,79,109`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** Dead no-op statement and an unfinished comment left behind in the truncation test — The truncated-headings test in tutor-core.test.mjs carries leftover scaffolding from an abandoned approach: a `void SECTION_CHAR_CAP;` statement that does nothing (SECTION_CHAR_CAP is genuinely used two lines later, so the void is not even suppressing an unused-binding warning), preceded by a two-line comment whose second clause is an unfinished sentence — "pass a small cap via stripGuideForTutor is internal, so instead assert the invariant on the real cap". A reader trying to understand why this test builds its fixture the way it does is handed a broken explanation and a statement with no effect, in the one test that guards the "never advertise a heading whose text was truncated away" invariant. _Fix:_ Delete line 119 and rewrite the comment to state what the test actually does in one sentence: it forces truncation by making the input exceed the real SECTION_CHAR_CAP, so the invariant is asserted against production's cap rather than an injected one. Move the `md` fixture down next to the sanity check at :127 that uses it, so each fixture sits with its assertion. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/tutor-core.test.mjs:119`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.test.mjs:117-118`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.test.mjs:116,127`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[UX]** The out-of-scope refusal renders as a normal grounded answer and tells the learner to open a lesson they are already reading — When the Lambda has no corpus entry for the slug it streams `OUT_OF_SCOPE_MESSAGE` over a normal 200. The client has no idea this is a refusal — it accumulates the bytes and paints them in the answer `<p>` with `--ink` styling, indistinguishable from a real grounded answer, under a header that confidently reads "Grounded in: Foundations". The copy itself then contradicts the situation: it says "Open a lesson and ask me about it there" to a learner who is provably inside `/learn/<slug>` (the component returns null anywhere else). The realistic trigger is not a bad slug — it is the corpus failing to load in the Lambda, which the handler explicitly acknowledges falls back to `CORPUS = {}` and "silently answers OUT_OF_SCOPE to every learner". In that state every learner on every lesson gets a confident-looking non-answer with no error state and no signal that anything is wrong. The client already imports the constant it needs to detect this, but nothing uses it. _Fix:_ Two small changes. (1) In the client, after the stream closes, compare the accumulated text to the imported `OUT_OF_SCOPE_MESSAGE` and render it in the muted `.text-caption` notice register instead of the `--ink` answer register, so a refusal never wears the costume of a grounded answer. (2) Reword the constant in lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs so it is true wherever it can actually be read — e.g. "I don't have the text for this lesson loaded, so I can't answer from it yet. Try another lesson, or come back shortly." — which is honest both for an unknown slug and for the empty-corpus deploy. Pin the new wording in lambda/tutor/tutor-core.test.mjs:23 (currently asserting `/only help with the lessons/i`) and add a web test that streams the exact constant and asserts it is not rendered with the answer class. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/index.mjs:81-85`, `lambda/tutor/tutor-core.mjs:31-32`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:90`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:145,225-228`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** The live region's stated intent and its own aria-busy attribute prescribe opposite announcement behaviour for the streaming answer — The answer container carries `aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="false" aria-busy={busy}` and a comment declaring the goal is incremental per-token announcement. Those two things cannot both be true: `aria-busy="true"` instructs assistive tech to withhold announcements until the region settles, which is the opposite of announcing tokens as they arrive. The result is behaviour that is undefined by design and splits by AT. NVDA and JAWS honour `aria-busy` on a live region and stay silent through the stream, then announce once at completion — which is the correct experience, but not the one the comment describes. VoiceOver's `aria-busy` support on live regions is unreliable, and because `setAnswer(acc)` rewrites the paragraph's entire text node on every chunk rather than appending new nodes, the diff an AT computes is the whole accumulated answer — so a VoiceOver user can hear the answer re-read from the beginning on every one of dozens of deltas. On a `w-full` panel at 375px, VoiceOver is the dominant AT for this surface, so the unreliable branch is the mobile default. _Fix:_ Commit to the suppress-then-announce-once discipline the code already leans toward, and make it explicit rather than AT-dependent: keep `aria-busy` as the gate, but move the streaming answer out of the live region entirely (plain `<p>`, no aria-live) and put a small `sr-only` `aria-live="polite" aria-atomic="true"` status node beside it that holds only state text — empty while idle, "Thinking" on submit, and the completed answer (or "Answer ready") once `busy` goes false. That removes the per-chunk diff as a source of announcements no matter how the AT treats `aria-busy`, keeps the completion signal that keyboard/AT users currently depend on, and matches the repo's existing sr-only status idiom. Rewrite the comment to describe what actually happens, and add a test asserting the streaming paragraph is not inside `[aria-live]` while the status node is. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:222-224`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:126-146`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:104,157`, `web/__tests__/components/ask-tutor.test.tsx:118-123`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[UI]** The right-anchored slide-over animates in from the left, using a 12px keyframe built for inline content reveals — The panel is pinned to the right edge and reuses the shared `slide-in` keyframe, which translates from `translateX(-12px)` to `0` — motion that travels rightward, i.e. the panel appears to emerge from inside the page content and settle against the right edge, rather than entering from off-screen right. The direction is backwards for the surface, and the magnitude is wrong too: 12px of travel on a 448px-wide (full-width at 375px) full-height panel is not a slide-over at all, it is a fade with an imperceptible nudge. The keyframe's other consumer confirms the mismatch — it is a small inline reveal for a row of pricing buttons, which is what a 12px leftward offset is designed for. The result is that the platform's only slide-over doesn't read as one. _Fix:_ Give the panel its own keyframe rather than bending the shared one — add `@keyframes panel-in { from { opacity: 0; transform: translateX(100%); } to { opacity: 1; transform: translateX(0); } }` and an `.animate-panel-in` utility beside `.animate-slide-in` in globals.css, reusing the same `cubic-bezier(0.22, 1, 0.36, 1)` easing at ~0.3s (0.5s is long for a full-width mobile overlay). Add `.animate-panel-in` to the `prefers-reduced-motion` list at globals.css:695-707 in the same edit — the house rule requires it and the existing block is where reviewers will look. Leave `slide-in` alone so the pricing row is unaffected. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/globals.css:95-102`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:200`, `web/src/app/pricing/page.tsx:140`, `web/src/app/globals.css:695-707`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[UI]** The dialog close button's hover state resolves to the colour it already has, so hovering does nothing — The close button is styled `text-caption hover:text-(--mut)`. `.text-caption` is defined as `color: var(--mut)` — it is the canonical alias for that exact token. So the hover rule sets the colour to the value it already holds and the button gives no visual feedback on hover at all. The only remaining affordance is `.interactive`, whose transition list covers transform, background-color, box-shadow, border-color and opacity but deliberately not `color`, and whose sole visible effect is `active:scale-[0.97]` — a press state, not a hover state. So the primary dismiss control on the panel is inert under the cursor. The sibling dialog's close button does this correctly, hovering from a muted grey up to full ink with a background wash. _Fix:_ Change the hover target to the ink tier so the control actually brightens: `text-caption hover:text-(--ink)`. Add `color` to `.interactive`'s transition list in globals.css:519 so this and the trigger pill's `hover:text-accent` (ask-tutor.tsx:185, currently also an instant snap) both ease rather than jump — one token-level edit that fixes every `.interactive` consumer with a colour hover. The panel's close target is 32px (`p-1.5` + a 20px icon), which clears WCAG 2.2 SC 2.5.8's 24px floor that web/__tests__/slider-target-size.test.ts enforces, so no geometry change is needed. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:213`, `web/src/app/globals.css:566-572`, `web/src/app/globals.css:518-520`, `web/src/components/section-gate-modal.tsx:112`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Correctness]** edge.yaml promises a 429 on rate-limit in four places but emits 403, making a throttled learner indistinguishable from a broken OAC signature in both the client and the CloudFront logs — The WAF rule is declared with a bare `Action: Block: {}` and no `CustomResponse`. WAFv2's default block response is HTTP 403, not 429 — yet the stack description, the `RateLimitPerMinute` parameter description, and the README all state the rate limit yields 429. That documented contract is load-bearing in the wrong direction: 403 is also exactly what the *other* two plausible production failures return — a direct unsigned hit to the AWS_IAM-locked Function URL, and a POST that reaches OAC without the `x-amz-content-sha256` body hash (which the client silently omits whenever `crypto.subtle` is unavailable, ask-tutor.tsx:114-116). So a learner being rate-limited, a learner whose body hash didn't compute, and a genuinely misconfigured OAC grant all surface identically, and anyone implementing the client-side error mapping that the transport-failure finding calls for would naturally map 429 and watch it never fire. _Fix:_ Pick one and make code and prose agree. Preferred: give the rule the response it documents — add `CustomResponse: { ResponseCode: 429 }` under the rule's `Block` action plus a matching `CustomResponseBodies` entry, which restores the distinction (429 = you are going too fast, 403 = signing/auth problem) and lets the client map the two to different learner-facing sentences. If the status must stay 403, correct all four prose sites instead and say so explicitly, so nobody writes a 429 branch that can never execute. Either way, extend lambda/tutor/template.test.mjs — which already pins the alarm wiring line-by-line — with the same text-assertion approach against edge.yaml so the rule's action and its documented status cannot drift again. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/edge.yaml:42-50`, `lambda/tutor/edge.yaml:6-7`, `lambda/tutor/edge.yaml:28`, `lambda/tutor/README.md:132-138`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The client mirrors none of the handler's two input limits, so a long paste is silently truncated mid-sentence or answered with the out-of-scope refusal — The handler enforces two caps that the client knows nothing about: questions are hard-sliced to 2,000 characters, and any body over 16 KiB is discarded wholesale and answered with `OUT_OF_SCOPE_MESSAGE`. The textarea has no `maxLength`, no counter, and no length check in `ask()`. A learner who pastes a long code trace and asks about it gets one of two silently wrong outcomes: at 2,001+ characters the question is cut mid-sentence and the model answers a fragment the learner never sees, with no indication anything was dropped; past 16 KiB the request is thrown away and the learner is told "I can only help with the lessons in this curriculum. Open a lesson and ask me about it there" — while reading that very lesson. This is the same three-runtime drift the feature already guards against for the prompt logic: `SECTION_CHAR_CAP` is single-sourced through tutor-core and re-exported to the web app, but the two request-side limits live only in the handler and are duplicated nowhere. _Fix:_ Export the question cap from tutor-core.mjs beside `SECTION_CHAR_CAP` (e.g. `export const MAX_QUESTION_CHARS = 2000;`), have index.mjs slice by it, and add it to the re-export list in web/src/lib/tutor.ts — the generated-copy pipeline then carries it to the client for free, with no new file and no drift risk. In the panel, set `maxLength={MAX_QUESTION_CHARS}` on the textarea so over-long input is stopped at the keyboard rather than silently amputated server-side. That also removes the 16 KiB path for questions entirely, since 2,000 characters cannot produce a 16 KiB body. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lambda/tutor/index.mjs:74`, `lambda/tutor/index.mjs:47,51`, `lambda/tutor/index.test.mjs:123-139`, `web/src/components/ask-tutor.tsx:254-268`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category D)

### 21, 24, 25. Python library: circuits + hardware + utils
- [x] **[Security/Correctness]** ✓verified — DONE (PR pending) — **`run_circuit` dispatches to real QPUs with no shots cap or cost guard**, violating CLAUDE.md's cost-awareness rule; validate `shots` and surface `estimate_cost` before `device.run`. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`lib/hardware/devices.py:37-54`) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Correctness]** ✓verified — DONE (PR pending) — `estimate_cost` returns negative costs for negative shots/minutes (no input validation), feeding bad numbers into the cost-warning string. Raise on negative inputs. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/cost.py:15-24`) — added 2026-06-17
- [x] **[Architecture]** DONE 2026-06-29 (PR pending — collapsed `DEVICE_ARNS` + `_COST_PROVIDER` into one `DEVICES` table `{arn, provider}`; `DEVICE_ARNS` kept as a derived backwards-compat view; `Rigetti` documented reference-only; added a `provider ∈ PRICING` drift invariant + a derived-view lockstep test. Supersedes the #82 monkeypatch fail-closed test — fail-closed now holds structurally via `estimate_cost`'s "Unknown provider" raise before any network.) ~reviewer — device identity keyed two incompatible ways: `hardware` uses short names (`ionq_aria`), `cost` uses provider names (`IonQ`) — the two halves a learner chains don't compose. Unify to one registry. Impact: High. Effort: Medium. (`lib/hardware/devices.py:8-16`, `lib/utils/cost.py:3-12`) — added 2026-06-17 _(2026-06-29 deep pass confirmed + sharpened: it is actually **three** hand-synced registries — `DEVICE_ARNS`, `_COST_PROVIDER`, and `cost.PRICING` — and they have already drifted (`PRICING` carries an orphan `Rigetti` row absent from both device maps, `lib/utils/cost.py:7`). The drift is load-bearing because the cost gate is fail-open — see new item **24.a** below — so collapsing to one table also closes that safety hole.)_

FIXED: documents the contract and raises if `result.measured_qubits` is exposed and isn't `0..n-1` in order (rather than silently mislabeling); + a column-order pin test and a non-contiguous-rejection test. (The audit cleared the *web* qjw visualizer — its column==qubit is a correct big-endian convention, not a bug.) Remaining sub-items (mock-dtype divergence, GHZ-coverage, half-typed API) are separate, still open. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`lib/utils/results.py`) — added 2026-06-17

### 22-23. Python library: chemistry + QML
- [ ] **[Performance]** PARTIAL 2026-07-03 (the `train_vqc` double-pass half is DONE — #82's batched training, item 23.a, folded the loss/accuracy passes; and `quantum_kernel` now routes through `run_circuit("local")` per #87) — ~reviewer — `quantum_kernel` constructs a fresh `LocalSimulator` on every call across an O(N²) Gram matrix; `train_vqc` evaluates every training circuit twice per epoch (loss + accuracy). Reuse one device; fold the passes. STILL OPEN: `get_device("local")` returns a fresh `LocalSimulator()` on every call (`lib/hardware/devices.py:69`) — only `AwsDevice` is memoized (`_AWS_DEVICE_CACHE`, `devices.py:58`); memoize the local simulator alongside it. DONE: it now validates `len(params) == n_electrons*(n_qubits-n_electrons)` and raises (+ test). PARTIAL 2026-07-03: "the other silent-degrade builders" are DONE — #87/#88 gave every encoder/builder a fail-loud `ValueError` naming the expected shape (`ansatz.py:25-27,78-81`, `classifiers.py:60-62`, `feature_maps.py` guards). STILL OPEN: the three incompatible `params` conventions — `hardware_efficient_ansatz` `(n_layers, n_qubits, 2)` (`ansatz.py:25`), `build_vqc_circuit` `(n_layers, n_qubits)` (`classifiers.py:60`), `uccsd_singles_circuit` flat `n_excitations` (`ansatz.py:78`) — arguably by-design across ansatz families and now at least fail-loud; and the linear (`classifiers.py:32`) vs ring (`feature_maps.py:171`) entangling-topology divergence. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`lib/chemistry/ansatz.py`, `lib/ml/feature_maps.py`, `lib/ml/classifiers.py`) — added 2026-06-17

> **Category E (`lib/`, Features 21–25) — deep refinement pass (2026-06-29).** A dynamic
> workflow ran 23 read-only reviews (5 features × 4 per-feature lenses — Cleaner code,
> Performance, Resilience & tests, Security — plus 3 lib-wide sweeps for Architecture,
> Consistency, and Observability & cost), then adversarially verified every finding
> (file:line real on `main`? a refinement not a new feature? already handled? impact honest?).
> **61 candidates → 58 confirmed, 3 rejected** (a wrong line-cite, the intentional local-path
> shots design, and an over-claimed observability item). The verifier corrected **every**
> claimed-High down to Medium/Low — `lib/` is genuinely strong (tight modules, honest docstrings,
> fail-loud validation, real local-simulator tests), so the result is **4 Medium** items and the
> rest Low, with heavy cross-lens duplication folded here (the cost-gate fail-open surfaced under
> 5 lenses; the H2/LiH builder duplication under 3; the `quantum_kernel` qubit-count bug under 2).
> This pass **sharpens** three still-open 2026-06-17 items rather than duplicating them: the
> device-registry unification (updated in place above), the `quantum_kernel` fresh-`LocalSimulator`
> per call (remains tracked under `### 22-23` above — verified real but its O(N²) Gram amplification
> is hypothetical: no Gram-matrix builder exists in the repo), and the `train_vqc` double-pass
> (superseded by the sharper batching fix, item **23.a**). The new per-feature items below are
> the distinct, file:line-grounded refinements.


#### Feature 18 — 2026-07-20 Category C re-audit (16 adversarially-verified findings; independent skeptic per claim, default-refute, security and correctness claims re-derived against current tree state)

- [x] **[Accessibility]** CodeBlock's scroll-region exposure is dead code — it measures the wrong element, so no wide code fence is ever keyboard-reachable or labelled — `code-block.tsx` measures overflow on the `<pre>`, but the real horizontal scroller is the `<code class="hljs">` inside it: the globally-imported highlight.js theme ships `pre code.hljs { display:block; overflow-x:auto; padding:1em }`. The `<pre>` therefore never overflows, `canScroll` is permanently false, and the `tabIndex={0}` / `role="region"` / `aria-label` / `focus-ring` branch never activates. A learner on a keyboard cannot reach the hidden right-hand text of any wide fence, and a screen-reader user gets no region label — the exact WCAG 2.1.1 / 1.4.10 guarantee the component's own comment promises. Worse, the branch is latently broken even once the measurement is fixed: the ring would be clipped away (see evidence). The unit test only passes because it mocks `HTMLElement.prototype.scrollWidth` globally — a fake that encodes an assumption the real DOM contradicts. _Fix:_ Point the measurement at the element that actually scrolls: keep `preRef` for layout but measure `preRef.current.querySelector("code")` (falling back to the pre), or neutralize the imported `pre code.hljs` overflow rule so the `<pre>` is the sole scroller again — the latter also fixes the double-padding/double-background symptom. Then move the conditional exposure (`tabIndex`/`role`/`aria-label`/`focus-ring`) onto whichever element scrolls, and make the ring survive the wrapper: either drop `overflow-hidden` in favour of `rounded-xl` on the pre itself, or swap `.focus-ring` for an inset treatment (`focus-visible:ring-inset`) on this one surface. Replace the `scrollWidth` prototype spy in the test with a real overflowing child so the assertion can never again pass over a broken path. This is also the natural moment to extract `use-scroll-region.ts` with a deps param, closing the WS-B2 gap. Impact: High. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/code-block.tsx:34,43`, `web/src/components/code-block.tsx:51,81-86`, `web/src/app/globals.css:2`, `web/src/components/code-block.tsx:54`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** CopyButton's className override silently loses to its own base classes — the code-fence copy icon renders at 2.15:1 in the light theme — CopyButton concatenates the caller's `className` after its base classes and assumes the caller wins. Tailwind resolves same-specificity conflicts by stylesheet order, not by string order, and every conflicting base class is emitted later than the caller's. Both override sites in the repo are therefore silently discarded. In `code-block.tsx` the intended `text-gray-300` (10.4:1 on the chip) loses to `.text-caption { color: var(--mut) }`, so in the LIGHT theme the copy icon paints `--mut` = #5a5853 on the `bg-gray-800/80` chip over `bg-gray-900` — 2.15:1, well under the 3:1 WCAG 1.4.11 non-text floor, on a permanently-visible control (the chrome does not hide on touch). In `notebook-link.tsx` the intended compact `h-6 w-6` loses to `h-8 w-8`, so the notebook-path copy button renders 32px with a 12px glyph — a large empty hover/focus pill around a tiny icon, inflating a row built for 11px text and a 10px chip. Notably, `caption-contrast.test.ts` exempts this very line on the belief that it paints `gray-300/400` on a pinned dark surface; the exemption rests on a colour that never paints. _Fix:_ Stop expressing overrides as trailing class strings on this shared component. Give CopyButton explicit props for the two things callers actually vary — a `tone` (default vs the on-dark code-fence chrome) and a `size` (default 32 vs compact 24) — and select the base classes from those, so no conflicting pair is ever emitted. Callers then pass `tone="on-dark"` / `size="sm"` instead of `text-gray-300` / `h-6 w-6`. Extend `caption-contrast.test.ts` (or contrast-guard) with a rule that flags any `className` string handed to a shared component containing a token the component's own base already sets, so the next such override fails loudly instead of silently. Impact: High. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/copy-button.tsx:96-100`, `web/src/components/code-block.tsx:71`, `web/src/components/notebook-link.tsx:58`, `web/src/app/globals.css:564-566`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[UI]** The globally-imported highlight.js theme paints a square-cornered off-palette panel inside the tokenized code-fence wrapper, with duplicated padding — `globals.css` imports `highlight.js/styles/github-dark.css` globally. Besides the token colours the component wants, that file ships box rules — `.hljs { background:#0d1117 }` and `pre code.hljs { display:block; overflow-x:auto; padding:1em }` — which the CodeBlock never accounts for. The result is a second, square-cornered `#0d1117` rectangle inset ~18px inside the 12px-rounded `bg-gray-900/80` wrapper, in both themes: a visible double frame with a hard-edged inner panel that belongs to no Instrument token. Padding is applied twice (pre `14px 16px` + code `14px`), and on touch devices the deliberate `pt-12` headroom stacks on top of the code's own 1em, leaving ~62px of dead space above the first line — the opposite of the compact treatment PR #172 tuned. The KaTeX stylesheet was route-scoped for exactly this class of reason; highlight.js was left global, so every funnel page also pays for a stylesheet it never uses. _Fix:_ Scope the highlight.js theme the way KaTeX already is (import it from markdown-renderer.tsx / code-block.tsx rather than globals.css) and neutralize its box rules at the same time — a small unlayered override next to the existing `.prose :not(pre) > code` block that sets `pre code.hljs { background: transparent; padding: 0; overflow: visible; display: block }`. That removes the inner panel, collapses the doubled padding back to the tuned `px-4 pb-3.5 pt-12/can-hover:pt-3.5` rhythm, restores the `<pre>` as the single scroller (fixing the measurement bug above at its root), and takes the stylesheet off the funnel pages. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/globals.css:2`, `web/src/components/code-block.tsx:54,84`, `web/src/components/code-block.tsx:12`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** The KaTeX + syntax-highlight pipeline has zero end-to-end verification, and a code comment claims CI covers it — Feature #18's entire value — that $math$ becomes rendered KaTeX and fences become highlighted tokens — is verified nowhere in the repo. Every Jest suite that loads the renderer mocks `react-markdown`, `remark-math`, `rehype-katex`, and `rehype-highlight` into inert no-ops, so they assert only that strings are forwarded. The build-smoke CI job builds the static export but asserts only that the `web/out` DIRECTORY exists — it never looks inside the emitted HTML. Worse, markdown-renderer.test.tsx documents the gap as closed, citing a CI assertion that does not exist, so a maintainer reading the file concludes the pipeline is covered. Concretely: deleting `remarkMath` from the plugin array, dropping `\ket` from KATEX_MACROS, removing `rehypeHighlight`, or a breaking option-shape change in a rehype-katex major bump all ship fully green. The failure is silent and site-wide — every ket across 7 lessons renders as a literal `$\alpha\ket{0}$` or a red KaTeX error string with no test, no lint, and no CI step objecting. _Fix:_ Add one assertion step to the build-smoke job immediately after `npm run build`, matching the job's own established idiom of grepping built artifacts (it already greps web/public/pyodide/package.json and web/public/lab/jupyter-lite.json). Assert against a lesson page that exercises all three legs: `grep -q 'class="katex"'` (KaTeX ran), `grep -q 'katex-mathml'` (the screen-reader MathML tree is present, not just visual HTML), a rendered `\ket` glyph such as `⟩` (the shared KATEX_MACROS actually expanded, catching a macro-table regression), and `grep -q 'class="hljs'` (rehype-highlight ran). Then correct the stale comment at markdown-renderer.test.tsx:15 to point at the step that now really exists. No new dependency, no new functionality — this only proves the shipped render path still works. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`web/__tests__/components/markdown-renderer.test.tsx:15`, `web/__tests__/components/markdown-renderer.test.tsx:17-27`, `web/__tests__/components/markdown-renderer.fence-routing.test.tsx:14-18`, `.github/workflows/ci.yml:216-220`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** The dark code-slab recipe is written in three places, and the globals.css copy silently double-applies to every rendered fence — `.prose pre` in globals.css predates CodeBlock and was never removed. It is hand-written (not generated by @tailwindcss/typography), so it carries no `not-prose` exclusion — `.not-prose` only shields the plugin's own `:where(...):not(:where([class~="not-prose"] *))` selectors. CodeBlock's `<pre>` is a descendant of the `.prose` article regardless of the `.not-prose` wrapper between them, so the rule matches and paints a second background, border and radius on the inner element. `border` and `rounded-xl` and `bg-gray-900` are not in the pre's own class list, so nothing overrides them (the utilities layer only wins the `overflow-x-auto` collision). The visible result on every fence of every lesson: the outer 1px gray-800 border and the pre's inherited 1px gray-800 border sit flush, reading as a 2px border, and in dark mode two stacked `bg-gray-900/80` layers composite to ~96% opacity instead of the designed 80%. It is also a maintenance trap — CodeBlock's docstring says it "replicates the dark block styling so the chrome is fully controlled", but a chrome change made in the component leaves a stale second copy in CSS. A third copy of the same recipe lives in the WidgetFence unknown-token fallback. _Fix:_ Delete `.prose pre` and `.prose pre code` from globals.css. Both are fully covered by the components that actually render fences: CodeBlock's wrapper owns bg/border/radius and its pre owns overflow and text colour, and the WidgetFence fallback writes the same utilities inline — verified by reading both class lists, so deletion changes nothing except removing the doubled border and the doubled dark-mode alpha. Then collapse the third copy by rendering the WidgetFence fallback as `<CodeBlock rawText={source} language={language}>{source}</CodeBlock>`, which deletes the duplicated class string and gives the fallback the same copy/scroll affordances as every other fence. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/globals.css:387-389`, `web/src/components/markdown-renderer.tsx:112`, `web/src/components/code-block.tsx:54`, `web/src/app/globals.css:409-411`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[UI]** A KaTeX parse error ships live on /learn/02-hardware — the section's headline cost arithmetic renders as red literal LaTeX — `rehypeKatex` runs with `throwOnError: false`, which is the right call for the static build (a bad expression must not abort the export) but means a malformed expression silently ships as a `.katex-error` span. One is live right now. In `02-hardware/GUIDE.md` the author wrote a bare `$0.30` where the surrounding idiom escapes dollars as `\$`; remark-math opened an inline-math span at that `$` and closed it at the `$` in `\$0.08`, leaving a trailing lone backslash that KaTeX cannot parse. The learner sees `0.30 + 1{,}000 \times \` in red, followed by the orphaned plain text `0.08 = $80.30$`, in the exact sentence that teaches the project's central cost discipline. The error colour KaTeX injects inline is `#cc0000`, which measures 2.70:1 on the dark theme's `--surface-base` — below even the 3:1 non-text floor, so in dark mode the broken text is also barely legible. Nothing in the build or the test suite notices. _Fix:_ Fix the source line to the escaped idiom already used in 06-hybrid-jobs (`\$0.30 + 1{,}000 \times \$0.08 = \$80.30`, which needs no math span at all), then add a guard in the repo's existing build-check idiom: assert that the exported HTML contains zero `katex-error` spans. The repo already ships comparable post-build assertions (the per-notebook closure-coverage check, the e2e zero-third-party assertion), so this fits the house pattern and costs one grep over `out/`. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`02-hardware/GUIDE.md:226`, `web/src/components/markdown-renderer.tsx:118`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** Inline code in prose fails AA in the light theme on two of the seven sections, and the hue-driven pair escapes all three contrast guards — The inline-code chip derives both its text and its background from the section's `--hue`: `color: oklch(0.5 0.16 var(--hue))` on `background-color: oklch(0.72 0.15 var(--hue) / 0.1)`. Because the pair is generated from a hue angle rather than a named token, its contrast varies per section, and at hue 192 it lands at 4.35:1 in the light theme — under the 4.5:1 WCAG 1.4.3 AA floor for normal-size text. Hue 192 is `sectionHue[0]`, and index 6 wraps back to it, so this affects `/learn/00-prereqs` and `/learn/06-hybrid-jobs` — and inline code is the single densest formatting device in this curriculum. The three existing contrast guards all miss it: `contrast-guard` and `caption-contrast` are class-string greps that never evaluate a colour value, and `token-contrast` only parses the `:root` / `.dark` token blocks, so a colour computed from `var(--hue)` outside those blocks is invisible to it. _Fix:_ Darken the light-theme inline-code lightness from `oklch(0.5 ...)` to about `oklch(0.47 ...)`, which clears 4.5:1 at the worst hue (192) while staying within the family's existing visual range — the dark theme already passes comfortably and needs no change. Then close the guard hole: extend `token-contrast.test.ts` with a small case that walks `sectionHue` from sections.ts and asserts the `.prose :not(pre) > code` pair clears 4.5:1 at every hue in both themes, so a future hue addition or retune cannot reintroduce a sub-AA section. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/globals.css:735-736`, `web/src/lib/sections.ts:29,31-33`, `web/src/app/learn/[section]/page.tsx:65,68`, `web/__tests__/token-contrast.test.ts:31-42`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Accessibility]** The code-fence scroll region's focus ring is painted outside the pre and clipped away by the wrapper's overflow-hidden — the keyboard affordance PR #172 shipped has no visible indicator — When a code fence overflows, CodeBlock makes the &lt;pre&gt; a focusable scroll region and adds the house `.focus-ring` recipe to it. But `.focus-ring` is `outline-none` + a `ring-2 ring-offset-2` pair, and Tailwind v4 implements rings as *outset box-shadows* on the element's border box. The pre is the wrapper's only in-flow child, carries no margin (the hand-written `.prose pre` rule sets no margin, and @tailwindcss/typography's margins are suppressed inside `not-prose`), so the pre's border box is flush with the wrapper's padding box. The wrapper is `overflow-hidden`, which clips descendant painting at that padding box — so all 4px of ring-offset + ring falls entirely in clipped territory. `outline-none` has already removed the UA fallback outline. Net result: a keyboard user tabs into a wide code fence and receives zero visual indication that focus moved there. The sibling surface does not have this problem — MarkdownTable spreads the same `focus-ring` onto a plain div in prose flow with no overflow-hidden ancestor, so its ring renders normally. This is drift introduced by the Category A work: the exposure was added to an element that was already sealed inside an overflow-hidden shell. _Fix:_ Move the focus treatment off the clipped child and onto the clipping wrapper: drop `focus-ring` from the pre's class string and instead put `focus-within:ring-2 focus-within:ring-(--focus) focus-within:ring-offset-2 focus-within:ring-offset-(--surface-base)` on the line-54 wrapper (it is already the `group`/`relative` element and is outside its own clip). Alternatively keep the ring on the pre but switch it to an inset treatment (`focus-visible:ring-inset`), which paints inside the pre's box and therefore survives the clip. Either way, add a jsdom assertion to code-block.test.tsx alongside the existing 'becomes a labelled keyboard scroll region' case that pins where the focus recipe lives, so the indicator can't be re-orphaned. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/code-block.tsx:54`, `web/src/components/code-block.tsx:84-86`, `web/src/app/globals.css:508-510`, `web/src/app/globals.css:386-388`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** Both rehype plugin option objects pass options the installed major versions don't accept, and the two comments defending them describe mechanisms that do not exist — markdown-renderer.tsx configures the pipeline with `[rehypeKatex, { macros, throwOnError: false }]` and `[rehypeHighlight, { ignoreMissing: true }]`, each justified by an adjacent comment. Both options are dead against the installed versions. rehype-katex 7.0.1 types its options as `Omit<KatexOptions, 'displayMode' | 'throwOnError'>` and, at call time, spreads the caller's settings first and then hard-overrides `throwOnError: true` — the passed `false` is discarded on every render; the red-error rendering the comment credits to it actually comes from the plugin's own internal catch-and-retry. rehype-highlight 7.0.2 removed `ignoreMissing` entirely — the string appears nowhere in the package, and the transform reads only aliases/detect/languages/plainText/prefix/subset; unknown languages are handled by an internal `Unknown language` catch. Because `PluggableList` types plugin settings loosely, TypeScript never flags either. I ran the real pipeline over all seven GUIDEs: it emits 141 `missing-language` warnings, one per widget fence, each produced by lowlight *throwing* and being caught — work the surviving `plainText` option exists to skip. react-markdown discards the vfile messages, so none of this is visible. A maintainer reading these two comments will believe the build is protected by knobs that are inert. _Fix:_ Drop `throwOnError: false` from both rehype-katex option objects and rewrite the comment to state the actual behaviour (rehype-katex 7 always retries with `strict:'ignore'` and renders a `.katex-error` span, so a bad expression can never abort the export). Replace `{ ignoreMissing: true }` with the option that exists in v7 and does the intended job: `{ plainText: [...WIDGET_LANGS] }` — that skips the 141 throw/catch cycles per build instead of paying for them, and makes the comment true. Both edits are pure deletions/renames of already-inert config; behaviour is unchanged apart from the skipped work. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/markdown-renderer.tsx:116-120`, `web/node_modules/rehype-katex/lib/index.js:9`, `web/node_modules/rehype-highlight/lib/index.js:44-51`, `web/node_modules/rehype-highlight/lib/index.js:108-129`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** CodeBlock still hand-rolls the measure-then-expose scroll region that useScrollRegion now owns — and both the hook and MarkdownTable cite CodeBlock as its source — WS-B2 extracted the measure-then-expose idiom into `useScrollRegion`, but code-block.tsx was left off it, so the repo now has two implementations of the same 10-line effect. Worse, the documentation points the wrong way: the hook's docstring calls itself "the house measure-then-expose scroll-region idiom, single-sourced" and markdown-table.tsx says it "Follows CodeBlock's measure-then-expose idiom (single-sourced in useScrollRegion)" — while CodeBlock, the component both comments name, is the one copy that is NOT single-sourced. The next author who edits the shared hook (say, to switch the overflow test or add a `scrollWidth - clientWidth > 1` tolerance for sub-pixel layouts) will fix tables and miss every code fence on every lesson page. The only real divergence blocking adoption is that CodeBlock must re-measure when `wrap` toggles, which the hook's fixed `[]` dep array cannot express. _Fix:_ Widen `useScrollRegion` minimally to cover the one blocking difference and adopt it in CodeBlock: `useScrollRegion<T>(label, options?: { deps?: unknown[]; className?: string })`, appending `deps` to the effect dep array and appending `className` after the `overflow-x-auto`/`focus-ring` recipe. CodeBlock then becomes `useScrollRegion<HTMLPreElement>(`${language ?? "code"} snippet`, { deps: [wrap], className: `px-4 pb-3.5 pt-12 can-hover:pt-3.5 text-sm leading-relaxed text-gray-200 ${wrap ? "whitespace-pre-wrap break-words" : "whitespace-pre"}` })` and spreads `regionProps` onto the `<pre>`, deleting its local `scrollable` state, effect and `canScroll`. The existing `&& !wrap` guard is subsumed: with `deps: [wrap]` the re-measure runs on toggle, and a `whitespace-pre-wrap break-words` pre cannot overflow horizontally, so `scrollable` alone is already false. MarkdownTable's call site stays valid (options optional), and its docstring should be corrected to name the hook rather than CodeBlock. code-block.test.tsx:79-112 already asserts the exposed/not-exposed contract, so it verifies the swap unchanged. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/hooks/use-scroll-region.ts:22-46`, `web/src/components/code-block.tsx:40-49`, `web/src/components/code-block.tsx:81-86`, `web/src/hooks/use-scroll-region.ts:6-21`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Architecture]** makeComponents is trapped in the module that imports the ESM-only markdown pipeline, forcing an identical 5-line mock preamble in three test files — markdown-renderer.tsx does two unrelated jobs: it configures the react-markdown/remark/rehype pipeline (plus the route-scoped KaTeX stylesheet import), and it builds the pure component-override map (`hastText`, `headingId`, `makeComponents`) which touches none of those imports. Because the two live together, any test that wants the pure map has to neutralize the whole ESM pipeline first — react-markdown v10 and the four plugins are ESM-only and the repo's jest runs ts-jest in CommonJS. That preamble is now copy-pasted verbatim into three files, and it is load-bearing boilerplate: adding or swapping a plugin in the renderer breaks three unrelated test files until each one's mock list is updated by hand. _Fix:_ Move `hastText`, `headingId` and `makeComponents` into a sibling `markdown-components.tsx` (still a Server Component module; it needs only the hast types plus WidgetFence/WIDGET_LANGS/CodeBlock/MarkdownTable imports). markdown-renderer.tsx re-imports `makeComponents` from it and keeps the pipeline config and the KaTeX CSS import — route scoping is unaffected because the renderer remains the module the /learn route pulls in. The three tests then import from `@/components/markdown-components` and drop all fifteen mock lines, and the split gives each module one job: pipeline configuration vs. hast-to-React mapping. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. (`web/src/components/markdown-renderer.tsx:1-16`, `web/src/components/markdown-renderer.tsx:32-43`, `web/__tests__/components/markdown-renderer.headings.test.tsx:10-14`, `web/__tests__/components/markdown-renderer.fence-routing.test.tsx:14-18`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The rehype-katex option object is duplicated across the two math surfaces, half-completing the single-sourcing that katex-macros.ts started — lib/katex-macros.ts exists precisely so the two KaTeX consumers cannot drift — its comment says it is "Used by the lesson MarkdownRenderer and the glossary InlineMarkdown so notation stays uniform." But only the `macros` half is single-sourced; the surrounding option object, including `throwOnError: false`, is retyped at both call sites. That flag is the build-safety switch: if one site is changed and the other is not, a malformed expression renders red on one route and aborts the static export on the other, and nothing in the code or tests connects the two spellings. _Fix:_ Export the whole option object from lib/katex-macros.ts — `export const KATEX_OPTIONS = { macros: KATEX_MACROS, throwOnError: false };` — carrying the build-safety comment once, and have both call sites pass `[rehypeKatex, KATEX_OPTIONS]`. Keep the `katex.min.css` import in each component (that is what makes it route-scoped) but reduce the duplicated rationale to a one-line pointer at the second site. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/lib/katex-macros.ts:1-9`, `web/src/components/markdown-renderer.tsx:116-118`, `web/src/components/glossary/inline-markdown.tsx:26`, `web/src/components/glossary/inline-markdown.tsx:5-7`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** The hover/focus chrome-reveal class string is repeated verbatim inside code-block.tsx instead of being a named recipe constant — The five-utility recipe that hides the fence chrome until hover or focus is spelled out twice in the same file, once on the language chip and once on the button group. It is subtle, conditional behaviour (permanently visible on touch, fade-on-hover on pointer devices, force-visible while any child holds focus) written as an unlabelled 90-character class string with no name explaining the intent — and the two copies must stay byte-identical or the chip and the buttons will fade at different times. The repo's own convention for exactly this situation is a named exported class constant with a rationale comment. _Fix:_ Hoist it to a file-local `const CHROME_REVEAL = "opacity-100 can-hover:opacity-0 can-hover:transition-opacity can-hover:group-hover:opacity-100 group-focus-within:opacity-100";` with a one-line comment ("always visible on touch, fades in on hover, forced visible while a child holds focus") and interpolate it at both sites, matching widget-ui.tsx's named-recipe convention. No new file needed — both consumers are in code-block.tsx. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/code-block.tsx:57`, `web/src/components/code-block.tsx:61`, `web/src/components/quantum/widget-ui.tsx:405-439`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** CopyButton never clears its 1.5s reset timer on unmount, diverging from all three sibling copy buttons — CopyButton schedules a 1500ms `setTimeout` to reset its status and stores the handle in a ref, but the component has no unmount cleanup effect — the only `clearTimeout` is the guard on a subsequent click. When a learner copies a code fence and navigates away inside 1.5s (lesson-to-lesson moves go through transition-link's View Transitions, well under that window), the CodeBlock unmounts with the timer still armed; the callback then fires against a dead fiber, retaining the component's state setter and its closure until it expires. This is the platform's single most-instantiated copy affordance — code-block.tsx mounts one per fence, and the same component also serves the Dirac state, the challenge editor, and notebook paths — yet it is the one copy button in the repo missing the cleanup that its three siblings all have. _Fix:_ Add the house one-liner to copy-button.tsx, copied verbatim from copy-link-button.tsx:9: `useEffect(() => () => { if (timer.current) clearTimeout(timer.current); }, []);`. Import `useEffect` alongside the existing `useRef`/`useState`. This single-sources the cleanup contract across all four copy affordances and closes the leak at its most-instantiated call site. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/copy-button.tsx:85-86`, `web/src/components/glossary/copy-link-button.tsx:9`, `web/src/components/playground/compose-panel.tsx:68-73`, `web/src/components/code-block.tsx:71`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Performance]** highlight.js theme CSS ships globally while its sibling KaTeX stylesheet is route-scoped, so 118 of 122 pages carry unused .hljs rules — globals.css imports the github-dark highlight.js theme at the top of the global stylesheet, so its ~2.1KB of `.hljs-*` token-color rules land in the 122KB render-blocking chunk that every page loads. But `hljs` markup exists on only 4 of the 122 built pages — rehype-highlight runs solely in markdown-renderer.tsx, and the glossary's InlineMarkdown deliberately omits it. This is the exact inconsistency PR #172 fixed for the other half of the feature: the 23.8KB katex.min.css was pulled out of globals.css and route-scoped to the two components that render math, and it verifiably works (the built lesson page links a separate 24KB katex chunk that the welcome and pricing pages never request). The highlight half was left behind, and globals.css:3-6 even documents the scoping rationale directly beneath the un-scoped import. _Fix:_ Move the `@import "highlight.js/styles/github-dark.css";` line out of globals.css and into code-block.tsx (the sole consumer of the tokens), exactly mirroring how markdown-renderer.tsx:16 and glossary/inline-markdown.tsx:7 own katex.min.css. Next.js will then emit it as a route-scoped chunk loaded only where fences render. Update the code-block.tsx:12 comment to say the theme now ships with this component, and extend the globals.css:3-6 note to cover both stylesheets so the scoping rule reads as one policy rather than a KaTeX exception. Jest already maps every `.css` import to a stub (jest.config.ts:9), so no test changes are needed. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/app/globals.css:2`, `web/src/components/code-block.tsx:12`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)
- [x] **[Consistency]** CodeBlock is the only CopyButton call site in the repo that omits `label`, so every code fence on a lesson page exposes an identical unqualified "Copy" button — CopyButton takes a `label` prop that defaults to `"Copy"` and drives the button's `aria-label`. Four of the five call sites in the tree pass a specific one — "Copy notebook path", "Copy OpenQASM", "Copy state notation", "Copy state as runnable Python". CodeBlock, which is by far the highest-volume consumer (one per non-widget fence, and lessons carry many), passes only `getText` and `className` and therefore inherits the bare default. A screen-reader user pulling up the page's button list on a lesson sees a run of indistinguishable "Copy" entries with nothing to tell one fence from another — even though CodeBlock already computes exactly the disambiguating string it needs and uses it two lines below for the scroll region's `aria-label` (`${language ?? "code"} snippet`). The information exists; it just isn't handed to the button. _Fix:_ Pass the language through, mirroring the region label already computed on line 83: `<CopyButton getText={() => rawText} label={`Copy ${language ?? "code"} snippet`} ... />`. Extend the existing 'copies the exact raw source' case in web/__tests__/components/code-block.test.tsx to assert the accessible name carries the language, so the qualifier can't silently regress back to the default. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/code-block.tsx:71`, `web/src/components/copy-button.tsx:59`, `web/src/components/notebook-link.tsx:55-58`, `web/src/components/playground/hardware-panel.tsx:70`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category C)

### 21. Circuit primitives
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** DONE 2026-06-29 (added `test_qft_circuit_matches_analytic_dft`: prepares `|k=1>`, compares the shots=0 statevector to the analytic DFT via a global-phase-/convention-robust fidelity, plus a non-trivial-imaginary check. Teeth-verified — a phase-stripped QFT scores fidelity 0.43 and fails. Kept the uniform-on-`|000>` sanity test.) — QFT correctness test is degenerate — measures `qft_circuit(3)` on `|000>` and asserts a roughly-uniform histogram, but the QFT of *any* basis state is uniform, so a Hadamard-only circuit (all phases/swaps deleted) passes identically; the Fourier structure is effectively untested. Fix: compare the statevector on a non-trivial `|k≠0>` against the analytic DFT amplitudes (the own-adjoint round-trip is itself degenerate — `c·c†=I` for any unitary). Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`tests/test_circuits.py:40-50`, `lib/circuits/common.py:36-39`) — added 2026-06-29
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** DONE 2026-06-29 (both tests now also assert each branch occurs in a 0.35–0.65 band of 1000 shots via `measurement_counts`. Teeth-verified — a dropped Hadamard collapses the state and the new assertions fail.) — Bell/GHZ tests assert correlation only — `row[0]==row[1]` / all-bits-agree pass vacuously on the collapsed product state `|00…0>` (a dropped Hadamard, no superposition/entanglement). Also assert both branches occur (all-zeros and all-ones each in a ~35–65% band; Binomial std ≈1.6% at 1000 shots, safe). Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`tests/test_circuits.py:12-13,26-27`, `lib/circuits/common.py:13,24`) — added 2026-06-29

Add `n_qubits >= 1` guards and a `qubit_0 != qubit_1` guard with teachable messages + `pytest.raises` tests. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/circuits/common.py:14,24-25,38`) — added 2026-06-29

Bring it to parity. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/circuits/common.py:31,38-39`) — added 2026-06-29

### 23. QML toolkit
- [x] **[Performance]** DONE 2026-06-29 (QNode encoding → `features[..., i]` so it broadcasts; `train_vqc` evaluates the whole dataset in one batched call on `default.qubit`/`lightning.qubit`, keeping the per-sample loop as the documented fallback for `braket.local.qubit`. Verified: new `test_vqc_qnode_broadcasts_over_a_batch` (batched == per-sample within 1e-10), the slow convergence test still passes, and an end-to-end run shows both paths converge with the batched path markedly faster. Supersedes the 2026-06-17 "fold the passes" item.) — `train_vqc` evaluates the QNode one sample at a time — pure-Python `for x,y in zip(...)` loops for both the loss/gradient and a *second* full per-sample accuracy sweep, every epoch, because the QNode indexes a 1-D `features[i]`. Change the encoding to `features[..., i]` (unchanged for 1-D, broadcasts for a `(batch, n_qubits)` input) and evaluate the whole dataset in one batched call, keeping the per-sample loop as a documented fallback for `braket.local.qubit`. Supersedes the 2026-06-17 "fold the passes" item. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`lib/ml/training.py:64-69,76`, `lib/ml/classifiers.py:132-142`) — added 2026-06-29

Derive `n_qubits = combined.qubit_count` and add a regression test with `amplitude_encoding`. (Latent — shipped notebooks only pair it with `iqp/angle` encoding.) Impact: Low. Effort: Low. ### 24. Hardware abstraction
- [x] **[Resilience & tests / Security / Observability]** DONE 2026-06-29 (`run_circuit` now fails CLOSED: a non-local device with no `_COST_PROVIDER` entry raises before any `AwsDevice` is constructed, preserving the "Unknown device" message for typos; added `test_every_billable_device_has_a_cost_provider` drift invariant + a monkeypatched `test_run_circuit_fails_closed_when_provider_missing`; 25 tests pass) — **24.a — Cost-awareness gate fails OPEN.** `run_circuit` prints the mandatory cost warning only when `_COST_PROVIDER.get(device_name)` is non-None, so a billable device added to `DEVICE_ARNS` but forgotten in `_COST_PROVIDER` (two hand-synced dicts) dispatches to real hardware with **no** cost notice — contradicting the function's own docstring guarantee and the project's first-class cost rule. (Latent: all 7 keys currently map.) Make it fail closed — raise before constructing `AwsDevice` for a non-local device with no provider — and add a `set(DEVICE_ARNS) == set(_COST_PROVIDER)` (and ⊆ `cost.PRICING`) invariant test. ### 25. Utilities
- [x] **[Cleaner code / Resilience]** DONE 2026-06-29 (PR pending — the "(local execution)" label now fires only when the provider's pricing is the local one (`per_minute == 0.0`); SV1 at 0 minutes is no longer mislabeled — test added) — `format_cost_warning` hardcodes "(local execution)" for every zero-cost result — `estimate_cost("SV1", estimated_minutes=0)` is `0.0`, so a genuinely billable managed simulator prints `[SV1] No cost (local execution)` (a cost-honesty mislabel in the one tool whose job is cost honesty). Gate the parenthetical on `provider == "LocalSimulator"` or use neutral "$0.00" phrasing; add the SV1-zero-minutes test. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/cost.py:36-37`, `tests/test_cost.py:58-60`) — added 2026-06-29
- [x] **[Security]** DONE 2026-06-29 (PR pending — added `math.isfinite` guards for `shots` and `estimated_minutes`; NaN/inf now raise a clear ValueError before any cost is computed — tests added) — `estimate_cost` rejects negatives but lets NaN/inf silently pass the spend gate — `nan < 0` / `inf < 0` are both False, so the guards don't fire and the function returns `nan`/`inf`; a downstream `cost > budget` check against `nan` is always False, silently passing the very gate the validation protects. Extend the guards with `math.isfinite(...)` + parallel tests. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/cost.py:21-24,27-29`) — added 2026-06-29
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** DONE 2026-06-29 (PR pending — guards `total_shots == 0` with a clear ValueError; tests cover both the empty `Counter` and a zero-total `Counter`) — `expectation_from_counts` has no zero-shot guard — an empty `Counter` (exactly what `parse_counts` returns for a zero-shot result) skips the loop and returns a confident `0.0` for an undefined distribution; a zero-total `Counter` raises a bare `ZeroDivisionError`. Guard `total_shots == 0` with a clear `ValueError` + tests for both. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/results.py:34-38`) — added 2026-06-29
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** DONE 2026-06-29 (PR pending — raises a clear ValueError on empty/zero-total counts; tests added for both) — `plot_histogram` silently renders a blank figure on empty counts and `ZeroDivisionError`s on zero-total counts — both uncovered. Guard the empty/zero-total case (clear `ValueError` or explicit empty-state) + add tests. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/visualization.py:13-14`) — added 2026-06-29
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** DONE 2026-06-29 (PR pending — added `test_parse_counts_empty_measurements_is_empty_counter` pinning the guard; it also protects the 25.f rewrite) — `parse_counts`' deliberate empty-measurements guard (`n = len(measurements[0]) if len(measurements) else 0`, the zero-shot path) is never exercised — a refactor dropping it would reintroduce an `IndexError` with no failing test. Add `parse_counts(mock_result_factory([])) == Counter()` (the conftest factory already supports `[]`). One of the parse_counts "remaining sub-items" flagged 2026-06-17. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/results.py:16`) — added 2026-06-29
- [x] **[Performance]** DONE 2026-06-29 (PR pending — one `np.asarray(measurements).tolist()` feeding `Counter("".join(map(str, row)) ...)`; both existing guards preserved. Verified on the browser path: `test_notebook_contract.py` (66 notebooks under qcsim) green) — `parse_counts` builds bitstrings with a nested Python generator calling `str()` on every numpy scalar (boxing each as `np.int64`) — convert once via `np.asarray(measurements).tolist()` (also transparently supports the list-based test mocks) then `"".join(map(str, row))`, preserving the `measured_qubits` guard. Bounded micro-opt (the browser measurement-statistics notebook drives 15×5000 rows in Pyodide). Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/results.py:23`) — added 2026-06-29
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** DONE 2026-06-29 (PR pending — dropped the dead `return 0.0` and the `elif`-after-`return` in favor of a straight `per_minute` return) — `estimate_cost` has an unreachable `return 0.0` and a redundant `elif`-after-`return` — every `PRICING` entry carries `per_shot` or `per_minute`, so the fallthrough is dead code. Use a straight `per_minute` return; make any defensive guard for a malformed entry explicit. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/cost.py:26-30`) — added 2026-06-29
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** DONE 2026-06-29 (PR pending — hoisted `positions = range(len(sorted_counts))` reused for `ax.bar`/`ax.set_xticks`; tuple-unpack labels/probabilities, dropped positional indexing) — `plot_histogram` recomputes `range(len(labels))` on two consecutive lines and uses positional `item[0]`/`item[1]` indexing — hoist `positions = range(len(labels))` and reuse for `ax.bar`/`ax.set_xticks`; optionally unpack `for label, value in sorted_counts`. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/visualization.py:11-18`) — added 2026-06-29

### Cross-cutting (Category E): library-wide conventions
- [x] **[Consistency with existing patterns]** DONE 2026-06-30 (P1, PR pending — added fail-loud `ValueError` guards: non-empty-1D for `angle_encoding`/`iqp_encoding` (shape only — no non-negativity/power-of-2), shape checks for `hardware_efficient_ansatz` and `build_vqc_circuit` (+ `features` length >= `n_qubits`); flipped `test_classifiers.py` IndexError→ValueError and added guard tests in test_feature_maps/test_ansatz. Closes 22.c.) — The lib-wide "fail-loud `ValueError(… got {actual})`" convention is followed by 5 functions (`cost.py:21-24`, `devices.py:74-77`, `results.py:18-22`, `ansatz.py:62-66`, `feature_maps.py:82-93`) but skipped by `angle_encoding`/`iqp_encoding` (no validation) and `hardware_efficient_ansatz`/`build_vqc_circuit` (raw `IndexError`) — one test even codifies the divergence (`test_classifiers.py:26-28` asserts `IndexError`). Add the same up-front guards (non-empty 1-D for the encoders — *not* non-negativity, which is `amplitude_encoding`-specific; shape checks for the builders) and flip that test to `ValueError`. Required decoupling `lib/hardware/devices.py` from `braket.aws` (lazy import via module `__getattr__`) so `lib.ml` stays importable under qcsim — verified by the browser-runnable notebook contract + a braket.aws-blocked import probe) — `ml/classifiers` bypasses both abstraction seams the library defines — it imports `LocalSimulator` directly (instead of `get_device`/`run_circuit`) and reads `result.measurement_counts` directly (instead of `parse_counts`, which has **zero** in-lib callers despite being the validated extractor). Route `quantum_kernel` through `get_device('local')` + `parse_counts` so the documented seams are real. (No latent correctness bug — the all-qubit measurement makes the guard a no-op here — purely DRY/single-source.) Impact: Low. Effort: Low.
