(`lib/ml/classifiers.py:19,74,82,85`, `lib/utils/results.py:6-22`) — added 2026-06-29
- [x] **[Consistency with existing patterns]** DONE 2026-06-30 (P1, PR pending — added `Raises: ImportError` docstrings to `train_vqc`, `build_h2_hamiltonian`, `build_lih_hamiltonian` (train_vqc also documents its `ValueError`)) — The `Raises: ImportError` contract for lazy-`[full]`-import functions is documented on `vqc_qnode` but omitted from `train_vqc`, `build_h2_hamiltonian`, and `build_lih_hamiltonian`, which raise the same way when the extra is absent (the reason the tests `importorskip`). Add a one-line `Raises:` to each. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/classifiers.py:122-126` vs `training.py:27-45`, `hamiltonians.py:15-17,38-40`) — added 2026-06-29


> **2026-07-20 Category E re-audit.** The ledger recorded this category COMPLETE after a seven-PR sweep on 2026-06-29, and I expected a thin result. It was not thin: 66 candidates -> **63 confirmed / 3 refuted** across four verification passes. The seam the June sweep could not see is CROSS-CAMPAIGN DRIFT — `lib/` was swept in isolation, and Categories B/C/D have since corrected the web-side mirrors of the same facts. Verifiers proved claims by execution (mutation experiments, real-SDK introspection, simulator truth tables) and used `git -S` to separate genuine post-sweep residue from restatements of closed work. COVERAGE NOTE: two finder lens-slices (F24 robustness, F25 teaching) were bounced by repeated classifier errors and never ran; their files were still covered by two other lenses plus a critic each, but that gap is real and recorded here rather than glossed.


#### Feature 21 — Category E re-audit (9 adversarially-verified findings)

- [x] **[Consistency]** qft_circuit's phase-sign convention is the teaching payload of the QFT lesson, yet every assertion that touches it is sign-blind — a flipped QFT would pass the whole suite while contradicting the TS mirror — `lib.circuits.qft_circuit` builds positive controlled-phase rotations, which I verified produce the plus convention amp[k] = e^{+2*pi*i*j*k/N}/sqrt(N). That exact convention is what the web mirror `qft.ts` hardcodes and what the QFT notebook teaches at length (Section 5's whole point is that the QFT is `sqrt(N)*np.fft.ifft`, NOT `np.fft.fft` — a pure sign argument). But nothing pins it on the library function. The one phase-sensitive test deliberately maximizes over the conjugate to be convention-agnostic, and the notebook's only assertion tying itself to `qft_circuit` compares statevectors on the |000&gt; input, where the plus and minus conventions are identical (both are the real uniform vector). So the Python helper the curriculum imports and the TS the web renders agree today by coincidence, not by contract. _Fix:_ Make the existing test assert the convention it already computes instead of maximizing it away: drop the `max(..., conj)` and assert `np.allclose(sv, dft, atol=1e-9)` directly against the plus-sign DFT (I confirmed the real circuit matches exactly, so this is a tightening, not a behavior change). Then state the convention in the `qft_circuit` docstring's Returns section alongside the existing bit-reversal note — one line naming `amp[k] = e^{+2*pi*i*j*k/N}/sqrt(N)` and pointing at the `qft.ts` mirror, so the two implementations are linked by a written contract rather than by luck. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lib/circuits/common.py:72-73`, `tests/test_circuits.py:99-104`, `tests/test_circuits.py:84-106`, `web/src/components/quantum/qft.ts:3-4`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Correctness]** The QFT notebook's cross-check against `lib.circuits.qft_circuit` is vacuous, yet it prints "reproduces … exactly" — Cell 4 of `03-algorithms/notebooks/03-qft.ipynb` compares `statevector(build_qft(3))` to `statevector(qft_circuit(3))` on the **default |000&gt; input**, then prints "Verified: build_qft(3) reproduces lib.circuits.qft_circuit(3) exactly." On |000&gt; every controlled phase is a no-op (each control qubit is still |0&gt; when its `cphaseshift` fires, because the controls are the higher-index qubits that have not been Hadamarded yet), and the swap layer is a no-op on the resulting symmetric uniform real vector. So the assert reduces to comparing H⊗H⊗H against H⊗H⊗H — it cannot detect a wrong angle, a dropped phase, or a missing swap. The learner is shown an equivalence-verification pattern that proves nothing and is told, in print, that it proved everything. This is precisely the trap the repo already identified and fixed on the test side (`tests/test_circuits.py:85-87` carries the comment "A uniform-on-|000&gt; check cannot detect a wrong QFT (the QFT of ANY basis state is uniform)"), but the hardening was never carried across to the notebook, which is the surface learners actually read. _Fix:_ Give the notebook's cross-check the same teeth the test already has. Either (a) compare the two circuits' instruction lists directly, which is what "gate for gate" actually means and needs no state preparation — e.g. assert `[str(i) for i in build_qft(3).instructions] == [str(i) for i in qft_circuit(3).instructions]`; or (b) move the comparison after `prep_basis` (cell 6) and loop it over all 8 basis inputs. Then make the printed line state what was actually checked. Option (a) is the smaller edit and makes the print's word "exactly" literally true. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lib/circuits/common.py:68-77`, `03-algorithms/notebooks/03-qft.ipynb:107-110`, `tests/test_circuits.py:84-106`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Correctness]** The notebook cells that demonstrate how to verify `bell_pair` entanglement ship the exact vacuous "both bits agree" check the library's own test was hardened against — and CI executes them as the primitive's only qcsim-side guard — PR #87 hardened `tests/test_circuits.py` against a specific failure mode and wrote the reason into the file: "A dropped Hadamard collapses this to the product state |00&gt; and still passes the 'agree' check vacuously" (test_circuits.py:17-18), then added the both-branches band assertions at :20-21 and :42-43. The three notebooks that teach learners how to verify the same primitive never got that fix. `01-foundations/notebooks/03-multi-qubit-gates.ipynb` prints "Verifying entanglement: every measurement shows both qubits agree" (line 93), asserts only `bitstring[0] == bitstring[1]` (line 96), then declares "All measurements show perfect qubit correlation" (line 97). `05-circuit-composition.ipynb:80-82` does the same for two composed pairs and prints "Verified: ... (two independent Bell pairs)". `01-first-circuit.ipynb:173` is worse — it prints "Only |0000&gt; and |1111&gt; should appear -- all qubits agree" with no assertion at all. I ran the mutant on the LocalSimulator: `Circuit().cnot(0, 1)` (Hadamard dropped) yields `{'00': 2000}` and every one of those assertions passes, and the composed version yields `{'0000': 2000}` and passes too. So the first entanglement verification a learner is ever shown is a check that cannot distinguish a Bell pair from the separable |00&gt;. This is not only a teaching defect: `tests/test_exercise_checks.py` executes each browser-runnable notebook end-to-end under qcsim (its module docstring, lines 24-26), so these cells are the de-facto regression guard for `bell_pair` on the browser engine — and they have no teeth there either. The same notebook already knows better: its own graded exercise check asserts `len(ghz5_counts) == 2` with the message "with 1000+ shots you should see BOTH outcomes" (01-first-circuit.ipynb:243), and `03-multi-qubit-gates.ipynb`'s GHZ cell asserts `set(counts.keys()) == {all_zeros, all_ones}`. Only the Bell-pair demo cells were left behind. _Fix:_ Bring the three demo cells up to the standard the library test and the notebooks' own exercise checks already set: after the "agree" loop, assert that BOTH branches actually occur. In 03-multi-qubit-gates.ipynb cell 4, add `assert set(counts) == {"00", "11"}` (or the 0.35–0.65 frequency band used in test_circuits.py) before the success print. In 05-circuit-composition.ipynb cell 3, assert `set(counts) == {"0000", "0011", "1100", "1111"}` — which is what two genuinely independent Bell pairs must produce and what a dropped Hadamard cannot. In 01-first-circuit.ipynb cell 10, turn the bare print into `assert set(counts) == {"0000", "1111"}`, matching the assertion its own Exercise 1 check already demands. Carry the one-line "why" comment over from test_circuits.py:17-18 so the lesson teaches the reasoning, not just the assertion. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/tests/test_circuits.py:17-21`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/01-foundations/notebooks/03-multi-qubit-gates.ipynb:93,96,97`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/01-foundations/notebooks/05-circuit-composition.ipynb:80-82`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/01-foundations/notebooks/01-first-circuit.ipynb:173`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Consistency]** 04-qpe.ipynb copies `qft_circuit`'s body verbatim into a private `forward_qft`, claims the ordering is "guaranteed to match" the library — and never imports `lib.circuits` at all, so nothing enforces the claim — `03-algorithms/notebooks/04-qpe.ipynb:139` tells the learner: "We build the forward QFT exactly as the shared `lib.circuits.qft_circuit` does — Hadamards interleaved with controlled phase rotations, finished by the bit-reversal swaps — so the inverse-QFT ordering is guaranteed to match step 3." The helper's own docstring repeats it: "Forward QFT on qubits 0..n-1, MSB = qubit 0 (matches lib.circuits.qft_circuit)" (line 150). The body that follows (lines 149-158) is a character-for-character copy of `lib/circuits/common.py:68-78` — same `cphaseshift(j, i, np.pi / (2 ** (j - i)))`, same `swap(i, n - i - 1)` loop. But the notebook's setup cell imports only `from lib.utils.statevector import statevector` (line 37); grep for `from lib` in this notebook returns that line plus three `from lib.grading import check`. `lib.circuits` is never imported, so there is no assert, no comparison, and no import that could break if the library's QFT ever changed. The word "guaranteed" is carrying weight nothing in the file supplies. This is a different defect from the 03-qft notebook's cross-check: that one at least runs a comparison (a vacuous one, on |000&gt;); this one runs none. Unlike 03-qft, the duplication here has no pedagogical justification either — 03-qft explicitly says "we build it inline so every gate is visible", while 04-qpe's stated intent is precisely to reuse the shared implementation's convention. _Fix:_ Replace the private copy with the thing the prose says it is: add `qft_circuit` to the setup cell's imports and call `qft_circuit(t).adjoint()` inside `qpe_t_gate`, deleting `forward_qft` (I verified this is state-vector-identical on both Braket and qcsim). If the inline definition is wanted for readability, keep it but make the claim load-bearing — assert `np.allclose(statevector(prep_basis(3,1) + forward_qft(3)), statevector(prep_basis(3,1) + qft_circuit(3)))` on a NON-trivial input |k≠0&gt; (on |0...0&gt; the comparison is vacuous, since both are the real uniform vector). Either way the "guaranteed to match" sentence becomes true by construction rather than by assertion-free assertion. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/03-algorithms/notebooks/04-qpe.ipynb:139`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/03-algorithms/notebooks/04-qpe.ipynb:150`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/03-algorithms/notebooks/04-qpe.ipynb:37`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/lib/circuits/common.py:68-78`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** test_circuits.py hand-rolls a duplicate statevector reader that mutates its argument — the exact anti-pattern lib/utils/statevector.py exists to prevent and has a dedicated test forbidding — The library already owns a portable, exported, non-mutating state-vector reader whose module docstring explains that the real Braket `state_vector()` registers a result type on the circuit and returns the circuit, so a helper must operate on a copy. There is even a named test asserting the caller's circuit stays clean. Feature 21's own test file bypasses all of that and re-implements the naive form, which rebinds to the same mutated circuit object. It is harmless today only because the call site discards the circuit immediately — but it is a second, divergent copy of the one behavior the library went out of its way to centralize, sitting in the test file for the module that centralization serves. _Fix:_ Delete `_statevector` from tests/test_circuits.py and import `from lib.utils.statevector import statevector`, then call it at the one use site (line 95). That removes four lines, drops the per-call simulator construction, and stops the test file from carrying a divergent copy of the engine-portability rule. If the intent was to keep the circuits test decoupled from lib.utils, that is worth stating in a comment — but the file already couples to LocalSimulator directly, and conftest.py already pulls `ghz_state` into fixtures, so the decoupling is not currently a real invariant. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`tests/test_circuits.py:78-81`, `lib/utils/statevector.py:36-40`, `tests/test_statevector_helper.py:50-57`, `lib/utils/__init__.py:8`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Consistency]** lib/circuits/common.py is the one circuit-builder module that skips the library's Args/Returns/Raises docstring shape and its import ordering — and it is inconsistent with itself — The library has a settled house style for circuit-building functions: numpy imported before braket, and a docstring with all three of Args, Returns, Raises. Both sibling circuit-builder modules follow it without exception. `lib/circuits/common.py` — the most-imported teaching module in the package, and the first library code a learner reads in 01-foundations — follows neither, and is internally split: one of its three functions documents Returns and the other two do not. This is the leftover the 2026-06-30 cross-cutting docstring pass (which added `Raises:` lines across ml/ and chemistry/) did not reach. _Fix:_ Add a one-line Returns section to `bell_pair` and `ghz_state` matching the sibling modules' phrasing (e.g. 'Returns: Circuit preparing the Bell pair on the two given qubits.'), and swap the two import lines so numpy precedes braket. Purely cosmetic, but it puts the module a learner meets first in line with the style the rest of lib/ already holds, and removes the within-file inconsistency where only one of three functions documents its return. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/circuits/common.py:47-65`, `lib/circuits/common.py:7-18`, `lib/ml/feature_maps.py:10-16`, `lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:12-21`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[API honesty]** `qft_circuit`'s docstring describes the gate recipe but never states the transform it computes or which qubit is the MSB — The docstring documents *how* the circuit is wired (Hadamard, controlled phases, swap layer) but never states *what function it computes*: the sign of the exponent, the 1/√N normalization, or the endianness anchor. Its Returns section says the swaps make the register "read in conventional most-significant-first order" — but without naming which physical qubit carries the most-significant bit, that sentence is unfalsifiable, and a learner running `qft_circuit?` in the JupyterLite lab has no way to tell whether qubit 0 or qubit n-1 is the MSB. The repo's own curriculum treats this as the headline hazard: `03-qft.ipynb` cell 9 teaches at length that mixing the sign convention up is "the classic source of off-by-a-sign / off-by-a-factor bugs," and `04-qpe.ipynb` builds its own local copy of the forward QFT specifically so the MSB-first convention is visible. The web-side twin already documents the formula on line 3 of its file, so the Python primitive is the odd one out. _Fix:_ Add one line to the docstring stating the transform and the endianness anchor, e.g. "Computes QFT|j&gt; = (1/sqrt(N)) * sum_k exp(+2*pi*i*j*k/N) |k&gt; with N = 2**n_qubits, where qubit 0 is the most-significant bit of the basis index (Braket's big-endian convention)." This makes the existing "most-significant-first order" sentence checkable and matches the specificity `web/src/components/quantum/qft.ts:3` already sets. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/circuits/common.py:48-65`, `web/src/components/quantum/qft.ts:3`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Correctness]** `ghz_state`'s docstring promises a "maximally entangled n-qubit state" at a documented-legal input that returns a separable state — The summary line calls the result a "GHZ state (maximally entangled n-qubit state)" and the Args section documents `n_qubits` as "Must be &gt;= 1", with the guard rejecting only n &lt; 1. But at n_qubits=1 the CNOT loop body never executes and the function returns a bare Hadamard, i.e. |+&gt; — a single-qubit product state with zero entanglement (entanglement is undefined for one qubit). The parenthetical is false at the documented boundary. Entanglement is the central concept of the 01-foundations section where this helper is first introduced, so a learner probing the boundary (`ghz_state(1)`) is handed a state the docstring labels "maximally entangled" when it is maximally *un*entangled. The state-formula line is fine — `(|0…0>+|1…1>)/√2` does degenerate correctly to `(|0>+|1>)/√2` — it is only the entanglement claim that breaks. _Fix:_ Qualify the claim rather than changing behavior: reword the summary to note the entanglement holds for n_qubits &gt;= 2, e.g. "Create a GHZ state: (|0...0&gt; + |1...1&gt;)/sqrt(2). For n_qubits &gt;= 2 this is a maximally entangled state; n_qubits=1 degenerates to the single-qubit |+&gt;." This keeps the degenerate case callable (composition code can pass n=1 without a special case) while removing the false entanglement promise. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/circuits/common.py:28`, `lib/circuits/common.py:33,38-39`, `lib/circuits/common.py:41-43`, `tests/test_circuits.py:51-53`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** tests/conftest.py ships two dead circuit fixtures, and the dead Bell one hand-rolls the exact primitive `bell_pair` exists to provide — `tests/conftest.py:16-19` defines `sample_bell_circuit`, whose body is `return Circuit().h(0).cnot(0, 1)` — a re-implementation of `bell_pair()` sitting three lines above an import of the real thing. `tests/conftest.py:22-25` defines `sample_ghz_circuit` returning `ghz_state(3)`. A repo-wide grep for both fixture names (`*.py`, `*.ipynb`, `*.md`, `*.toml`, `*.cfg`, excluding node_modules and .venv) returns only their two definition sites — no test in the suite requests either one. They are the sole consumers of two of conftest's imports: `from braket.circuits import Circuit` (line 4) exists only for `sample_bell_circuit`, and `from lib.circuits.common import ghz_state` (line 7) only for `sample_ghz_circuit`. The hand-rolled Bell circuit also contradicts the rule the README states for this library — "notebooks compose building blocks from it instead of re-implementing primitives" (README.md:332) — and it is the kind of duplicate that silently diverges: if `bell_pair` ever changed convention, this copy would not follow, and no test would notice because nothing uses it. _Fix:_ Delete both unused fixtures and the two imports that exist only to serve them (`Circuit` at conftest.py:4 and `ghz_state` at :7), leaving `local_simulator`/`run_local`/`mock_result_factory`, which are actually consumed. If either fixture is meant to be kept as shared scaffolding for future tests, at minimum make `sample_bell_circuit` return `bell_pair()` so the fixture and the primitive cannot drift apart. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/tests/conftest.py:16-19`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/tests/conftest.py:22-25`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/tests/conftest.py:4,7`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/README.md:332`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)

#### Feature 22 — Category E re-audit (11 adversarially-verified findings)

- [x] **[Correctness]** `uccsd_singles_circuit` emits a controlled-Ry ladder, not a Givens single excitation — it creates and destroys electrons — The function's four-gate excitation block is the textbook decomposition of a **controlled-Ry**, not a Givens/particle-conserving single excitation. Because the control is always the occupied qubit — which the Hartree-Fock `x(i)` loop has just pinned to |1&gt; and which nothing ever flips — every block degenerates to an *unconditional* Ry on a virtual qubit. The result is a product state that puts amplitude on 3- and 4-electron determinants while putting **exactly zero** amplitude on any genuine single-excitation determinant. This is the module's headline teaching artifact: it is re-exported by `05-quantum-chemistry/scripts/ansatz.py`, imported by notebook `05-ansatz-design.ipynb`, and driven by the CI exercise harness. _Fix:_ Replace the CRy block with a genuine particle-conserving Givens single excitation G(θ): |occ,virt&gt; = |10&gt; → cos(θ/2)|10&gt; + sin(θ/2)|01&gt;, i.e. `cnot(occ,virt); ry(occ, θ/2); cnot(virt,occ); ry(occ, -θ/2); cnot(virt,occ); cnot(occ,virt)` — the same house pattern the chemistry kit's `h2_double_excitation` already uses for the two-body case. Fix the `# Givens rotation` comment so it describes what is emitted. Add a teeth-bearing test asserting every sampled bitstring has Hamming weight == n_electrons for several random θ (the current test cannot fail). Safe to land: I confirmed the shipped ansatz's energy optimum against the repo's own `H2_TERMS` is -1.116151 Ha, exactly equal to a correct single-excitation ansatz's optimum and to `H2_HF`, so notebook 05's Exercise 3 check (`singles_best_energy > H2_FCI + 0.01`) still passes after the fix. Note while fixing: the docstring's `n_occ × n_virt` count is the standard unrestricted spin-orbital count and is defensible — the spin-forbidden amplitudes simply optimize to zero — so leave the parameter contract alone and avoid rippling into the notebook hint. Impact: High. Effort: Medium. (`lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:88-92`, `lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:69-70`, `lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:86-92`, `lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:52-53,86-87`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Cleaner code (naming / docstring accuracy)]** `uccsd_singles_circuit`'s docstring and inline comment misdescribe the circuit and overclaim its physics — it is not a Givens rotation, not particle-conserving, and recovers exactly zero correlation energy for H2 — The function is documented as a "UCCSD-inspired ansatz for single excitations" that "for H2 (4 qubits, 2 electrons) ... gives the essential physics", and the gate block is labelled "# Givens rotation implementing the excitation". All three claims are false, and I verified each by running the shipped code. The 4-gate block `ry(virt, θ/2); cnot(occ, virt); ry(virt, -θ/2); cnot(occ, virt)` is a **controlled-Ry** with control `occ` and target `virt`, not a Givens rotation: I ran the block in isolation and it maps |00&gt; -&gt; |00&gt; and |10&gt; -&gt; 0.9394|10&gt; + 0.3429|11&gt; (= cos(θ/2), sin(θ/2) at θ=0.7). A Givens rotation would send |10&gt; -&gt; cos|10&gt; + sin|01&gt;, conserving particle number; this one adds occupation to the virtual orbital without ever vacating the occupied one. Consequently the ansatz is not number-conserving and produces no genuine excitation at all. Running the real builder at θ=0.7 on all four parameters, only 34.2% of the probability remains in the physical 2-electron sector (48.6% leaks into 3-electron states |1101&gt;/|1110&gt;, 17.2% into the 4-electron |1111&gt;), and the amplitude of |0011&gt; — the double excitation that actually correlates H2 — is exactly 0. The "essential physics" claim is quantifiably wrong: I built the real STO-3G H2 Hamiltonian from the repo's own fixture (its lowest eigenvalue reproduces the fixture FCI, -1.137117 Ha) and minimized the ansatz energy over its four parameters from 12 random starts; the best reachable energy is -1.116151 Ha, which is *exactly* the Hartree-Fock energy. The ansatz recovers 0.000000 of the 0.020966 Ha correlation energy. This is the same class of teaching overclaim Category B just corrected on the web side, and it is load-bearing: notebook 05-ansatz-design asks the learner to variationally minimize H2's energy with this very function, so a learner following the docstring will conclude their optimizer failed when in fact the ansatz provably cannot do better than mean-field. _Fix:_ Doc-only correction, no behavior change. Rewrite the summary and the line-47 claim to describe what the circuit is: a chain of independent controlled-Ry gates that grow amplitude on the virtual orbitals from the Hartree-Fock reference — a pedagogical stand-in, not a particle-conserving UCCSD singles operator. Replace "this gives the essential physics" with the measured truth: for STO-3G H2 its variational minimum is exactly the Hartree-Fock energy (-1.116151 Ha vs FCI -1.137117 Ha), it recovers no correlation energy, and the correlating double excitation |0011&gt; is out of its reach. Fix the line-88 comment to say "controlled-Ry" rather than "Givens rotation". Add a one-line note that the state leaves the fixed-particle-number sector so expectation values mix electron counts. Optionally (larger blast radius — README.md:375, 05-quantum-chemistry/scripts/ansatz.py:5, the notebook and its solution file) rename to something that does not assert UCCSD-singles semantics. The downstream notebook exercise framing should be re-aligned by the curriculum category, not here. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:47`, `lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:88`, `lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:44,52`, `lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:69-70`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** `uccsd_singles_circuit` is a controlled-Ry that breaks particle-number conservation — it never performs a single excitation, and the only behavioral test is too weak to notice — The per-excitation block in `uccsd_singles_circuit` is labelled "Givens rotation implementing the excitation", but the four gates it emits compose to a **controlled-Ry(theta)** with control = occupied qubit, target = virtual qubit. A Givens rotation is number-conserving by definition (it rotates within {|01&gt;,|10&gt;}); a controlled-Ry is not — it leaves the occupied orbital occupied and *adds* an electron to the virtual one. I verified the block's full truth table on the local simulator: |00&gt;-&gt;|00&gt;, |01&gt;-&gt;|01&gt;, |10&gt;-&gt;cos(t/2)|10&gt;+sin(t/2)|11&gt;, |11&gt;-&gt;-sin(t/2)|10&gt;+cos(t/2)|11&gt;. Consequently the H2 ansatz never produces a single excited determinant at all: starting from HF |1100&gt;, the reachable support is exactly {|1100&gt;,|1101&gt;,|1110&gt;,|1111&gt;} — qubits 0 and 1 are never de-occupied. At theta=0.4 (the value tests/test_ansatz.py:77 uses) 28.0% of the probability sits outside the 2-electron sector (P(3e)=0.2573, P(4e)=0.0230); at theta=1.0, 91.5% does, with |1111&gt; — four electrons in a two-electron molecule — the single most likely outcome at 50.1%. Nothing catches this because the only test that runs the ansatz with nonzero parameters asserts `hf_fraction < 1.0`, a statement true of literally any non-identity circuit, plus a qubit count. No test anywhere asserts Hamming-weight (particle-number) preservation, which is *the* defining invariant of an excitation ansatz. This also hollows out the lesson built on it: notebook 05-ansatz-design Exercise 3 teaches "single excitations alone cannot reach the correlated ground state", but the demonstration works only because the optimizer discovers that every excitation is actively harmful and drives all four amplitudes to zero — I measured the canonical solution's best energy at -1.116151 Ha, exactly H2_HF to six decimals. The learner is shown singles falling short via a circuit that isn't doing singles. _Fix:_ Sandwich each excitation block with `circuit.cnot(virt, occ)` before and after the existing four gates. I verified on the local simulator that this yields the true number-conserving Givens rotation (|00&gt;-&gt;|00&gt;, |11&gt;-&gt;|11&gt;, |01&gt;-&gt;cos(t/2)|01&gt;-sin(t/2)|10&gt;, |10&gt;-&gt;sin(t/2)|01&gt;+cos(t/2)|10&gt;), so the name and the comment become accurate. Then add the invariant test the family is missing: run the ansatz at a nonzero theta and assert every sampled bitstring has Hamming weight == n_electrons (this fails loudly on today's code — teeth-verified — and passes on the corrected version). I confirmed the fix does not disturb the curriculum: re-running the canonical Exercise 3 solution against the corrected ansatz gives -1.114232, which still satisfies both checks (`>= H2_FCI - 1e-6` and `> H2_FCI + 0.01 = -1.127117`), and now for the physically honest reason — Brillouin's theorem, singles cannot improve on HF. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:86-92`, `lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:45-47`, `tests/test_ansatz.py:73-84`, `tests/test_ansatz.py:61-70`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Teaching correctness / drift against the corrected web copy]** The notebook that teaches `hamiltonian_info`'s `n_terms` glosses it as "how many separate Pauli measurements VQE will need … 15" — the repo's own GUIDE says 14 naive / ~5 grouped, and the same markdown cell contradicts itself two lines later — `hamiltonian_info` returns `n_terms` counting every Pauli term *including* the identity, and its docstring is honest about that (`lib/chemistry/hamiltonians.py:97`, and the rendered `summary` at `:115` says only "15 Pauli terms"). But the single teaching surface for that field — cell 5 of `01-molecular-hamiltonians.ipynb`, which walks the learner through exactly the three keys `hamiltonian_info` returns — redefines it as a measurement budget: "**Number of terms** — how many separate Pauli measurements VQE will need. For STO-3G H2 this is **15**." That is wrong twice over. It counts the free identity string, and it ignores commuting-term grouping. The repo already knows this: `05-quantum-chemistry/GUIDE.md:162` (the lesson prose the A–D campaigns corrected) states "the identity string is a classical constant that costs nothing, and the naive protocol measures each of the other 14 as its own hardware task (grouping commuting strings can shrink that to about five settings)", and the priced quiz at `GUIDE.md:167` bills exactly 14 tasks. The notebook is the lone holdout at 15. Worse, the contradiction is *inside one cell*: line 170 of the same markdown source says the `IIII` term "needs no quantum measurement at all" — directly refuting the 15 on line 168. A learner who follows the notebook and sizes a VQE run from `info["n_terms"]` over-budgets by a whole IonQ task per energy evaluation, on a platform whose cost-awareness is a stated teaching goal. _Fix:_ Correct the one notebook line to match the GUIDE the platform already ships: keep `n_terms` defined as the library defines it (all Pauli terms, identity included), and describe the measurement cost separately — "15 Pauli terms; the identity is a free classical constant, so a naive VQE measures the other 14, and commuting-term grouping shrinks that to about five settings." That removes the intra-cell contradiction, aligns the flagship chemistry notebook with `GUIDE.md:162`/`:167`, and leaves `hamiltonian_info`'s honest contract untouched. Optionally add one clause to the `n_terms` docstring at `lib/chemistry/hamiltonians.py:97` noting the identity term needs no measurement, so the next consumer cannot repeat the gloss. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (``lib/chemistry/hamiltonians.py:97` documents the field corre`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** `hardware_efficient_ansatz`'s documented Ry/Rz parameter-axis contract is pinned by no test — I built the axis-swapped mutant and all three of its tests pass, while a single nonzero angle moves |0000⟩ probability from 0.77 to 1.0 — `hardware_efficient_ansatz` promises a specific parameter layout: `params[layer, q, 0]` feeds `Ry` and `params[layer, q, 1]` feeds `Rz` (`lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:15`, implemented at `:34-35`). Nothing tests it. Its three tests are `test_hardware_efficient_ansatz_qubit_count` (counts qubits), `test_hardware_efficient_ansatz_rejects_wrong_param_shape` (rejects a bad shape), and `test_hardware_efficient_ansatz_zero_params_gives_zero_state` (feeds **all zeros**, where `Ry(0)=Rz(0)=I`, so |0000⟩ is invariant under *any* axis assignment or gate ordering). Every nonzero-parameter behavioral assertion in `tests/test_ansatz.py` is on the *other* function, `uccsd_singles_circuit`. So the entire variational behavior of this ansatz — the thing that actually produces energies — is untested. This is not hypothetical: `hardware_efficient_ansatz` is what Exercises 1 and 2 of `05-ansatz-design.ipynb` optimize (`hea_layer_rows`, `energy_per_budget` in `tests/solutions/05-quantum-chemistry/05-ansatz-design.py`), so a silent regression in the axis mapping would change every VQE energy, depth, and CNOT count the notebook teaches while `make test` stays green. It is the same class of gap the auditors flagged for `uccsd_singles_circuit` ("the only behavioral test is too weak to notice"), but on the sibling function nobody examined. _Fix:_ Add one nonzero-parameter test that pins the documented mapping, in the same shots=0 style the repo already uses elsewhere: build `params` with a single nonzero entry at `[0, 0, 0]` (Ry axis) on `n_qubits=1, n_layers=1`, and assert the resulting state matches `Ry(θ)|0⟩` — i.e. P(|0⟩) == cos²(θ/2) — then a second case with the nonzero entry at `[0, 0, 1]` (Rz axis) asserting the populations are unchanged from |0⟩ (Rz is a phase on a computational basis state). Those two asserts kill both the axis swap and the ordering mutant, cost no new dependencies, and bring `hardware_efficient_ansatz` up to the behavioral-coverage bar `uccsd_singles_circuit` already has. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (``lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:15` states the contract: "params: P`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Consistency]** `uccsd_singles_circuit` still validates params with `len()` while its same-file sibling was converted to `np.asarray` + exact `.shape`, so three raw `TypeError`s escape the module's fail-loud convention — The library-wide "fail-loud ValueError(... got {actual})" convention is recorded as closed and complete (ledger items 22.c and cross-cutting X.a, PR #87). Inside lib/chemistry/ansatz.py that fix landed on `hardware_efficient_ansatz` only: it normalizes with `params = np.asarray(params)` and compares the full `.shape` tuple. Its sibling `uccsd_singles_circuit` never normalizes and validates with `len(params)`, which checks only the leading axis. I confirmed three inputs that defeat it and surface raw `TypeError`s from inside numpy/Braket rather than the intended `ValueError`: `np.zeros((4, 3))` has `len() == 4`, so the count guard passes and the build fails at `circuit.ry(virt, theta / 2)` with "only 0-dimensional arrays can be converted to Python scalars"; a scalar (`0.5` or `np.float64(0.5)`) raises "object of type 'float' has no len()" before any guard runs; a nested list of the right length raises "unsupported operand type(s) for /: 'list' and 'int'". The same wrong-shape probe on `hardware_efficient_ansatz` returns the clean message "params must have shape (2, 4, 2) (n_layers, n_qubits, 2), got (4, 2, 2)". The asymmetry also means the two functions disagree on input types: `hardware_efficient_ansatz` accepts a nested Python list (verified), `uccsd_singles_circuit` does not, though both annotate `params: np.ndarray`. _Fix:_ Mirror the sibling exactly: add `params = np.asarray(params)` at the top of `uccsd_singles_circuit`, then validate `params.shape != (n_excitations,)` instead of `len(params) != n_excitations`, keeping the existing message text and appending the observed shape. That closes all three raw-TypeError paths (scalars have shape `()`, nested lists normalize then fail the shape check, `(4, 3)` fails the shape check), makes both builders accept the same input types, and finishes the convention that ledger item 22.c records as complete. Extend tests/test_ansatz.py:9-15 with a wrong-shape/right-length case. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:78-82`, `lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:23-28`, `lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:52-53,76-77`, `lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:62-65`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Correctness]** The docstring claims singles give H2's "essential physics" — they recover exactly 0% of its correlation energy, and the repo's own notebook teaches the opposite — `uccsd_singles_circuit`'s docstring asserts that for H2 the singles-only ansatz captures the essential physics. This is false chemistry. H2's correlation energy lives entirely in the **double** excitation |1100&gt; ↔ |0011&gt;; single excitations are decoupled from the Hartree-Fock reference by Brillouin's theorem, and in minimal-basis H2 they additionally carry ungerade symmetry and so cannot mix with the gerade ground state at all. A learner who reads the library and a learner who does the notebook exercise are told opposite things by the same repository. _Fix:_ Replace line 47 with the physics the rest of the repo already teaches — that for H2 the singles are inert (Brillouin's theorem plus g/u symmetry), so this ansatz reaches at best the Hartree-Fock energy and is included precisely to demonstrate where a singles-only ansatz stops short; the correlation energy requires the double excitation. This turns a false claim into the exact learning objective notebook 05's Exercise 3 is built around, at the cost of one docstring line. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:47`, `lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:45,56`, `05-quantum-chemistry/notebooks/05-ansatz-design.ipynb:94-96`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[API honesty]** `identity_coefficient` — the invariant this feature is named for — ships with no unit and no meaning, and the one comment that explains it names the wrong quantity — The feature is catalogued as having "an identity-energy honesty invariant", but `hamiltonian_info`'s contract for `identity_coefficient` states neither its unit (Hartree) nor its physical meaning, even though it is an energy and is the constant offset every VQE expectation value carries. The only prose anywhere in the repo that explains it is a test comment, and that comment misidentifies it as the nuclear-repulsion shift — a quantity with the opposite sign and 0.8 Ha away. A learner debugging an offset VQE energy is being pointed at the wrong term. _Fix:_ State the unit and the meaning on line 100: `identity_coefficient`: the constant energy offset of the qubit Hamiltonian, in Hartree — the coefficient of the identity Pauli string, equal to the nuclear repulsion plus the normal-ordering constant, not the nuclear repulsion alone. Correct the `# Nuclear-repulsion shift` comment in tests/test_hamiltonians.py:46 to match. This is the honesty invariant the feature is catalogued for; naming it correctly costs two comment edits. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/chemistry/hamiltonians.py:100`, `lib/chemistry/hamiltonians.py:109,114`, `tests/test_hamiltonians.py:46-47`, `tests/test_hamiltonians.py:48`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[API honesty]** Both Hamiltonian builders hide the basis set that determines their return shape, and the H2 default geometry is the fifth unexplained bond length on the platform — `build_h2_hamiltonian` and `build_lih_hamiltonian` never disclose that they hardcode STO-3G. The basis is the single fact that determines the returned `n_qubits` (H2 → 4 only because STO-3G gives two spatial orbitals) and it bounds every energy the learner will subsequently compute, yet a caller reading either public docstring cannot discover it. Compounding this, the H2 default `0.735` carries no stated provenance — while Category B just gave the web side a careful honesty note with different numbers, leaving the platform with five distinct H2 bond lengths and lib holding the only unexplained one. _Fix:_ Add one line to each builder's docstring naming the fixed basis (STO-3G, minimal — hence 4 qubits for H2) and one clause on `bond_length` saying what the default is (the STO-3G equilibrium, distinct from the measured 0.741 Å). Point the H2 note at the same honesty framing the web mirror now carries so the two surfaces stop diverging. Docstring-only; no signature or behavior change. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/chemistry/hamiltonians.py:46-58`, `lib/chemistry/hamiltonians.py:64,86`, `lib/chemistry/hamiltonians.py:45`, `web/src/components/quantum/pes-explorer.tsx:437-441`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** The LiH Hamiltonian test cannot distinguish LiH from H2, and no test pins that `build_h2_hamiltonian` depends on `bond_length` at all — PR #88 merged both diatomic builders onto a single shared helper whose only distinguishing input is the `geometry` list. The test suite cannot detect a mis-wire of that one argument. `test_lih_hamiltonian_returns_correct_shape` asserts `n_qubits >= 4`, `n_electrons >= 2`, and `len(qubit_h.terms) > 0` — H2's own output (4 qubits, 2 electrons, 15 terms) satisfies all three, so `build_lih_hamiltonian` could be silently wired to H2's geometry and the test would stay green. The same blind spot covers the bond length: all three H2 tests call the builder at the identical 0.735 and then assert only geometry-independent bounds — `5 <= n_terms <= 25` (the JW term count for H2/STO-3G is 15 at every internuclear distance, the sparsity pattern does not change with R) and `-10.0 < identity_coefficient < 10.0` (the nuclear-repulsion-shifted identity term stays near -0.1 Ha across the whole binding curve). An implementation that ignored `bond_length` entirely and always built at a fixed distance would pass the full file. The test file's own comment concedes the author did not know the value being bounded: it hedges the identity coefficient as "roughly between -1.5 and 1.5 Hartree depending on convention" and then asserts a range seven times wider. This is the pipeline the whole chemistry track documents as its source of truth, and nothing in the suite ever asserts a physical fact about its output. _Fix:_ Pin the counts exactly instead of bounding them: `n_terms == 15` for H2/STO-3G (with a comment naming the JW canonical form), and for LiH `n_qubits == 12` and `n_electrons == 4` — STO-3G gives Li five basis functions (1s, 2s, 2p x 3) plus H one, so six spatial orbitals -&gt; twelve spin-orbitals, and 3 + 1 = 4 electrons. Add one assertion that makes the geometry load-bearing: build H2 at two distances and assert the identity coefficients differ (or, stronger and worth the one extra import, assert the ground-state eigenvalue of the 0.735 A Hamiltonian is -1.137 Ha to 3 decimals via `openfermion.linalg.get_sparse_operator` — that single number pins the basis, the geometry wiring, the JW convention, and the notebook's hardcoded `H2_FCI` in one stroke). Both are cheap and both fail if the shared helper's `geometry` argument is ever mis-wired. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`tests/test_hamiltonians.py:51-58`, `tests/test_hamiltonians.py:19,30,43`, `tests/test_hamiltonians.py:22-26`, `tests/test_hamiltonians.py:43-48`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** `vqe_chemistry_job.py` hand-rolls the bitstring-column assumption that `parse_counts` was hardened against, has no test at all, and takes `n_layers`/`n_shots` unvalidated from job hyperparameters — This is the chemistry toolkit's only production entry point — a Braket Hybrid Job, where wall-clock is billed — and no test in the repo executes it. Its expectation-value estimator indexes measurement bitstrings by absolute qubit index (`int(bs[q])`), which is only valid when the circuit measures all of qubits 0..n-1. That is precisely the contract `lib/utils/results.parse_counts` was documented and hardened to enforce (the closed ledger item at docs/feature-optimization.md:1070), and this file is the one place that still carries the pre-fix version by hand. It is reachable from configuration, not just theory: `n_layers` is read from `AMZN_BRAKET_HP` with no validation, `hardware_efficient_ansatz` validates only `params.shape` (so `n_layers=0` gives `params.shape == (0, n_qubits, 2)`, a clean match, and returns an empty circuit), and I confirmed on the local simulator that the resulting measurement keys are then only as wide as the qubits the Pauli term itself touches — `measurement_counts` keys come back 2 characters wide while the code evaluates `bs[2]` and `bs[3]`, raising `IndexError: string index out of range`. The same unvalidated-hyperparameter path gives `n_shots=0` -&gt; `ValueError: No result types specified for circuit and shots=0`, a Braket-internal message with no hint that a job hyperparameter caused it. The asymmetry is stark: `bond_length` from this exact env var *is* guarded fail-loud in lib (PR #88 added it specifically because the value originates here), while the two siblings read from the same JSON on the adjacent lines are not. _Fix:_ Route the expectation value through the existing `lib.utils.results.parse_counts`, which already owns this contract and already raises when `measured_qubits` is not `0..n-1` in order — deleting the hand-rolled `int(bs[q])` rather than adding anything new. Extend the fail-loud style the toolkit already uses to the two remaining hyperparameters on the same lines as the guarded `bond_length`: reject `n_layers < 1` and `shots < 1` with a message naming the hyperparameter, so a bad job config fails at submission with an actionable error instead of a Braket-internal one after the instance is already billing. Tightening `hardware_efficient_ansatz` to require `n_layers >= 1` and `n_qubits >= 1` (matching `uccsd_singles_circuit`'s existing range guard) closes the root cause in lib for every caller. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`06-hybrid-jobs/algorithms/vqe_chemistry_job.py:60-64`, `06-hybrid-jobs/algorithms/vqe_chemistry_job.py:13-16`, `lib/chemistry/ansatz.py:23-28`, `Local simulator run replaying vqe_chemistry_job.py:45-65`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)

#### Feature 23 — Category E re-audit (19 adversarially-verified findings)

- [x] **[Consistency]** amplitude_encoding is the only lib/ml encoder without a 1D guard, and silently prepares a wrong state for N-D input — Every other builder/encoder in the QML toolkit rejects a non-1D `features` array with a fail-loud ValueError, but `amplitude_encoding` never checks `ndim`. It derives its size from `features.size`, so an (n_samples, n_features) matrix — the single most natural mistake in an ML module, since `X_train` is exactly 2D — passes validation and reaches `_mottonen_alphas`, where `amplitudes[j*group_size:(j+1)*group_size]` slices along axis 0 only. The group norms are then computed over ROWS instead of over the flattened amplitude vector, the upper-half slice comes back empty, every alpha collapses toward 0, and the function returns a circuit preparing a completely different state with no error at all. The project ledger records this as already handled — docs/feature-optimization.md:1074 claims '#87/#88 gave every encoder/builder a fail-loud ValueError naming the expected shape (... feature_maps.py guards)' — so this is a real gap in work recorded as complete, not a known-open item. _Fix:_ Extract the guard the two siblings already share into one private helper in feature_maps.py (e.g. `_require_1d_features(features)` returning the asarray'd vector and raising the existing 'features must be a non-empty 1D array (got shape ...)' message), then call it from all three encoders. That closes the amplitude_encoding hole and simultaneously removes the byte-identical 3-line block currently duplicated at lines 19-21 and 43-45. Add the matching rejection test alongside the existing tests/test_feature_maps.py:105-117 rejection cases. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/feature_maps.py:92`, `lib/ml/feature_maps.py:20`, `lib/ml/feature_maps.py:44`, `lib/ml/classifiers.py:64`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** The ZZ feature-product — the entire reason iqp_encoding exists — is pinned by nothing; swapping x_i*x_j for x_i+x_j passes every test, both notebooks, and both exercise checks — `iqp_encoding` has exactly three tests (tests/test_feature_maps.py:45-65) and not one of them observes the encoded state. `test_iqp_encoding_qubit_count` asserts `circuit.qubit_count == 3` — true of any 3-qubit circuit. `test_iqp_encoding_rejects_empty` exercises the guard. `test_iqp_encoding_is_unitary` builds `forward.add_circuit(forward.adjoint())` and asserts P(all-zeros) &gt;= 0.95 — a tautology for ANY circuit assembled from unitary gates, since U U-dagger = I by construction regardless of what U contains. So the entire encoding payload of lib/ml/feature_maps.py:54-64 — the single-qubit `rz(i, features[i])` layer and the CNOT-Rz-CNOT sandwich carrying `angle = features[i] * features[j]` — is completely unpinned. This matters right now because a sibling auditor found that the Python and the web's `encoding.ts` iqpState use different angle conventions under the same 'IQP' name; whichever way that is reconciled, nothing in this repo would notice if the reconciliation broke the Python. _Fix:_ Add one exact statevector test alongside the existing three, in the style tests/test_feature_maps.py already uses for `amplitude_encoding` (`lib.utils.statevector.statevector`, no shots). Pin `iqp_encoding(np.array([a, b]), reps=1)` against the analytically-derived 4-amplitude state so the H layer, the two `rz(i, x_i)` phases, and the `x_0*x_1` ZZ phase are each observable: with two qubits the state is uniform in magnitude, so asserting the four relative phases is exactly the assertion that discriminates product from sum. A second assert that `iqp_encoding(x, reps=1)` and `iqp_encoding(x, reps=2)` differ in phase closes the untested `reps` axis at the same time. Both mutations above then fail immediately, and whichever convention the Python/TS reconciliation settles on is locked. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/feature_maps.py:61`, `tests/test_feature_maps.py:55-65`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Correctness]** `diff_method="best"` does not resolve to backprop on 2 of the 3 allowed devices, and both docstrings teach that it does — `vqc_qnode` documents that the default `diff_method="best"` "picks backprop on the local simulator (analytic gradients)", and `training.py`'s module docstring headlines that it replaced an O(n_samples * n_params * 2) parameter-shift loop with "backprop on the local simulator". All three devices in `_ALLOWED_QML_DEVICES` are local simulators, but PennyLane resolves "best" to backprop ONLY on `default.qubit`. On `lightning.qubit` it resolves to adjoint, and on `braket.local.qubit` it resolves to **parameter-shift** — exactly the cost the module docstring claims to have eliminated. A learner who takes the documented `device_name="braket.local.qubit"` route (which notebook 04 teaches as a one-line swap) silently pays 2*n_params+1 circuit executions per sample per epoch while the docstring tells them they are on backprop. The docstring also misattributes the slowdown to "the plugin's per-call serialization overhead" when the dominant factor is the 13x execution count. _Fix:_ Correct both docstrings to state the actual per-device resolution rather than a blanket "backprop on the local simulator": backprop on `default.qubit`, adjoint on `lightning.qubit`, parameter-shift on `braket.local.qubit`. Scope `training.py`'s module-level performance claim to `default.qubit`, and replace the "per-call serialization overhead" explanation in `vqc_qnode` with the real reason — the gradient method degrades to parameter-shift, costing 2*n_params+1 circuit executions per gradient instead of one. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/classifiers.py:145-146`, `lib/ml/classifiers.py:141-143`, `lib/ml/training.py:6-8`, `lib/ml/training.py:68`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Correctness]** `train_vqc`'s loss_history and accuracy_history are one gradient step out of phase, and the final loss is never that of the returned params — `opt.step_and_cost` returns the objective evaluated at the params BEFORE the step, but the loop then rebinds `params` and computes `preds` at the params AFTER the step. Both are appended under the same epoch index, so `loss_history[i]` and `accuracy_history[i]` describe two different parameter vectors one gradient step apart. The consequence a learner actually hits: `loss_history[-1]` is not the loss of the returned `optimal_params`, so the reported final loss and the returned model disagree. The `Returns` docstring presents the two lists as parallel per-epoch histories with no hint of the offset, and the per-epoch print line mixes a pre-step loss with a post-step accuracy on the same line. Notably, notebook 03 hand-rolls the correct pattern (seeds the history with the initial loss, then appends after each step), so the shared library contradicts the pattern the curriculum teaches. _Fix:_ Align the two histories on the same parameters: either record the loss recomputed from the post-step `preds` (loss_fn is a pure function of `predict_all`, so no extra circuit passes are needed beyond the one already run for accuracy), or seed `loss_history` with the pre-training loss so index i means "after i steps" for both lists. Also state in the `Returns` docstring what the loss measures (mean squared error between the predicted P(|1&gt;) on qubit 0 and the 0/1 label) and that accuracy is training-set accuracy at a 0.5 threshold. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/training.py:95`, `lib/ml/training.py:96-99`, `lib/ml/training.py:42-44`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[API honesty]** `vqc_qnode`'s documented return contract is wrong for the batched call its own training loop depends on, and its ValueError is undocumented — The `Returns` block promises "a callable QNode with signature `qnode(features, params) -> float` where `features` is a 1D array of length `n_qubits`". The body deliberately writes `features[..., i]` so the QNode broadcasts over a `(batch, n_qubits)` array, and `train_vqc`'s fast path passes the entire 2D `X_train` and relies on getting back a `(n_samples,)` tensor. So the library's own hot path uses a shape contract the docstring denies exists — a learner reading the docstring would write the slow per-sample loop instead. Separately, the `Raises` block lists only `ImportError`, but the function raises `ValueError` for a device outside the allowlist; every other public function in this module documents its ValueError, making this the lone outlier. _Fix:_ Rewrite the `Returns` block to document both accepted shapes: a 1D `(n_qubits,)` input returns a scalar `<Z_0>`, and a 2D `(batch, n_qubits)` input returns one expectation per row, shape `(batch,)`. Add the `ValueError` for a `device_name` outside `_ALLOWED_QML_DEVICES` to the `Raises` block so it matches the convention every sibling in the module already follows. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/classifiers.py:148-151`, `lib/ml/classifiers.py:170`, `lib/ml/training.py:80-85`, `lib/ml/classifiers.py:153-156`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[API honesty / Teaching correctness]** `train_vqc` documents labels as "0 or 1" but never validates them, so the other standard VQC label convention trains silently against an unreachable target — The docstring constrains `y_train` to "Training labels (0 or 1)", and the loss math depends on it: `predict_all` returns `(1 - <Z>)/2`, which is confined to [0, 1], so the MSE target must live in that range. The function validates `X_train`'s dimensionality and the X/y length match, but places no check on the label values — making this the one documented constraint in the module with no enforcement, against a library-wide fail-loud convention established in the 2026-06-29 sweep. The likeliest learner mistake is passing -1/+1 labels, which is the convention used in the Havlicek paper and the standard PennyLane VQC tutorial. Nothing errors: the -1 target is simply unreachable, the model saturates at p=0, and the reported loss is inflated and not comparable to any 0/1 run. _Fix:_ Enforce the already-documented contract with a fail-loud check that `y_train` contains only 0/1 values, matching the validation convention the rest of lib/ uses, and have the error message name the -1/+1 convention explicitly so a learner arriving from the PennyLane VQC tutorial is told how to convert. If instead the intent is to tolerate any real-valued regression target, the docstring's "(0 or 1)" must be corrected and the accuracy metric's 0.5 threshold documented as assuming 0/1. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/training.py:33`, `lib/ml/training.py:60-65`, `lib/ml/training.py:83-86`, `lib/ml/training.py:97`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** iqp_encoding never validates reps, so reps=0 silently yields an all-ones kernel matrix on the exact path the curriculum uses — iqp_encoding validates `features` but not `reps`. Because the body is `for _ in range(reps)`, any reps &lt;= 0 is a silent no-op returning an empty Circuit with qubit_count 0 — I confirmed both reps=0 and reps=-3 produce 0 qubits and 0 instructions. The consequence differs by call path and neither is acceptable. On the sampled path, quantum_kernel raises `ValueError: Circuit must have at least one non-zero-qubit gate to run on a device` from deep inside Braket, naming neither reps nor iqp_encoding. On the exact statevector path that notebook 02 actually uses for its kernel matrix, there is no error at all: the notebook's `circ.i(n - 1)` padding makes the all-zeros amplitude 1.0 for every pair, and I measured the resulting 4x4 kernel matrix as uniformly 1.000 — every point reported as identical to every other, silently. This is not a hypothetical parameter: the curriculum explicitly asks the learner to sweep reps. _Fix:_ Add `if reps < 1: raise ValueError(f"reps must be >= 1 (got {reps})")` at the top of iqp_encoding, matching the fail-loud style already used for features on the line above and for n_qubits in lib/circuits/common.py. Add a `pytest.raises` case next to test_iqp_encoding_rejects_empty. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/feature_maps.py:44`, `lib/ml/feature_maps.py:49`, `tests/solutions/04-quantum-ml/02-quantum-kernels.py:12`, `tests/test_feature_maps.py:50`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[API honesty]** classifiers.py's module docstring — the first thing a reader sees — says build_vqc_circuit is 'used by quantum_kernel', which is false and misdescribes what the kernel takes — The two-API orientation block at the top of lib/ml/classifiers.py tells the reader that `build_vqc_circuit` is consumed by `quantum_kernel`. It is not. `quantum_kernel` never references `build_vqc_circuit`; it takes a caller-supplied `feature_map_fn` and calls it twice. The false claim is not merely stale bookkeeping — it teaches the wrong mental model of the kernel method, implying the fidelity kernel is built from the trainable VQC ansatz when the whole point (which 02-quantum-kernels.ipynb spends a section on) is that a kernel uses a FIXED feature map with no trainable parameters. A learner following the docstring to find how the kernel builds its circuits lands in the wrong function. _Fix:_ One-line docstring correction at lib/ml/classifiers.py:5-7: drop the false consumer and name the true one — `build_vqc_circuit` is used by 03-variational-classifier.ipynb (the didactic Braket-primitives path), while `quantum_kernel` composes a caller-supplied feature map from `lib.ml.feature_maps` with its adjoint. That also removes the implication that the kernel is parameterized, which is the exact misconception the notebook works to prevent. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/classifiers.py:5-7`, `lib/ml/classifiers.py:102-119`, `lib/ml/classifiers.py:96`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Correctness]** The QML section's own index of the toolkit advertises three capabilities that do not exist — a re-uploading encoder, early stopping, and checkpoint support — The GUIDE's Scripts block is the section's authoritative listing of what the QML toolkit provides, and three of its claims are false against the code. `lib/ml/feature_maps.py` exports exactly three encoders — angle, IQP, amplitude — and no data-reuploading map; the GUIDE lists 're-uploading' as a fourth. `lib/ml/training.py` is 108 lines containing one gradient-descent loop and a `print` every ten epochs; the GUIDE credits it with 'early stopping' and 'checkpoint support', neither of which exists anywhere in the module. Re-uploading is especially misleading because the concept IS taught two bullets earlier at GUIDE.md:36-37 and is exercised in 04-pennylane-braket.ipynb's Exercise 1 — where the canonical solution has to hand-roll `qnode_reupload` from scratch, precisely because the library the GUIDE says provides it does not. Separately, the compat shim the GUIDE points at re-exports only two of the three public names in lib.ml.classifiers. _Fix:_ Correct the three descriptions to the shipped surface (drop 're-uploading' from the feature_maps line; replace 'logging, early stopping, and checkpoint support' with what train_vqc actually returns — PennyLane analytic-gradient descent with per-epoch loss and accuracy history). Do not implement the missing features: re-uploading is deliberately an exercise, and early stopping/checkpointing would be new functionality. Add `vqc_qnode` to the 04-quantum-ml/scripts/classifiers.py re-export list so the shim matches its own stated contract of mirroring lib.ml.classifiers. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`04-quantum-ml/GUIDE.md:382`, `04-quantum-ml/GUIDE.md:384`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Architecture]** The broadcast-capable device list is duplicated in training.py, outside the module that owns the QML device allowlist — `classifiers.py` is the module that owns which PennyLane devices the VQC path accepts — it declares `_ALLOWED_QML_DEVICES` and validates against it, with a comment explicitly warning that the entry must be kept in sync. But the companion fact 'which of those devices can consume a batched (batch, n_qubits) array in one broadcast pass' is declared as an anonymous inline tuple in a different module, `training.py`. The two tuples are hand-synced: the allowlist has three entries, the batch tuple repeats the first two. Adding a device to the allowlist later silently drops `train_vqc` onto the slow per-sample loop with no signal, and adding one to the batch tuple that does not actually broadcast produces wrong training results rather than an error. This is the same drift hazard the module already guards against elsewhere by single-sourcing the entangler topology. _Fix:_ Move the broadcast fact next to the allowlist it subsets — declare `_BROADCASTING_QML_DEVICES` in classifiers.py directly below `_ALLOWED_QML_DEVICES`, and have training.py import it for the `batched` decision instead of repeating the literals. Both device facts then live in the module that owns device dispatch, and a future allowlist entry forces an explicit broadcast decision at the same site. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/classifiers.py:25`, `lib/ml/classifiers.py:24`, `lib/ml/training.py:80`, `lib/ml/classifiers.py:28-35`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Consistency]** Python iqp_encoding and the web's encoding.ts iqpState implement different angle conventions under the same 'IQP' name — Section 04-quantum-ml teaches 'IQP encoding' through two implementations that do not agree. The TypeScript mirror the lesson widgets use implements the canonical Havlicek ZZ feature map and says so in its doc comment — single-qubit phase 2*x_i and interaction phase 2*(pi-x_i)*(pi-x_j). The Python library uses x_i and x_i*x_j (no factor of two, no (pi-x) form) and its docstring pins no convention at all, so a reader has to reverse-engineer it from the code. The two prepare materially different states for the same input, which means the kernel geometry a learner explores in the lesson widget and the one they reproduce in the notebook are not the same object even though both are labelled 'IQP feature map (reps=2), 2 qubits'. _Fix:_ Pin the convention in the Python docstring so the divergence stops being invisible: state the phases it actually applies (phi_i = x_i, phi_ij = x_i*x_j), note that this is a simplified variant of the Havlicek/ZZFeatureMap convention (phi_i = 2*x_i, phi_ij = 2*(pi-x_i)*(pi-x_j)) that the lesson explorer uses, and cite arXiv:1804.11326 as feature_maps.py:73-75 already does for the Mottonen reference. If the two are meant to be the same object, align the Python angles to the Havlicek form instead and update the canonical solutions in tests/solutions/04-quantum-ml/ accordingly. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/feature_maps.py:56`, `lib/ml/feature_maps.py:61`, `lib/ml/feature_maps.py:29-31`, `web/src/components/quantum/encoding.ts:25`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Consistency]** quantum_kernel is the only public function in lib/ml with no argument validation and no Raises: docstring section — Every other public entrypoint in the QML toolkit validates its inputs up front and documents the failure in a `Raises:` block — that fail-loud validation convention is recorded in the ledger as a completed cross-cutting Category E item. `quantum_kernel` has neither. Its `shots` argument flows unchecked into `run_circuit` on the local path (which, by design, skips the billable-device shots gate) and then into a bare division, so a bad value surfaces as an opaque error thrown from inside the Braket SDK rather than as the module's own clear message naming the offending argument. _Fix:_ Give quantum_kernel the same treatment its siblings already have: a `if shots <= 0: raise ValueError(f"shots must be >= 1 (got {shots})")` guard before building the circuits, and a `Raises:` section in the docstring documenting it plus the ValueErrors the supplied `feature_map_fn` may propagate. No behavior change on any valid call. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/classifiers.py:88-119`, `lib/ml/classifiers.py:118`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:171`, `lib/ml/feature_maps.py:16`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** Bare and missing container/callable type hints on the QML module's surface, against precisely-annotated siblings — The QML module annotates most parameters but leaves several return and parameter types either bare or absent, while its own private helpers and the sibling hardware module annotate precisely. `_vqc_entangler_pairs` is the single-sourced entangler topology that both backends iterate and that a parametrized test pins, yet its return type is a bare `list` that says nothing about the (control, target) pair structure — and it is the only bare container annotation in the whole QML feature. `quantum_kernel`'s `feature_map_fn` is the one parameter in the module with no annotation at all, even though its contract (ndarray -&gt; Circuit) is what the whole compute-uncompute path depends on. _Fix:_ Tighten the three annotations to match what the code already returns and what the docstrings already promise: `_vqc_entangler_pairs(...) -> list[tuple[int, int]]`, `feature_map_fn: Callable[[np.ndarray], Circuit]` (Circuit is already imported at classifiers.py:18), and a return annotation on `vqc_qnode`. Documentation-only change with no runtime effect. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/classifiers.py:28`, `lib/ml/classifiers.py:88`, `lib/ml/classifiers.py:122-127`, `lib/ml/feature_maps.py:128`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** amplitude_encoding's Möttönen decomposition is pinned only by shot frequencies, and a broken decomposition passes the 4-dim case — amplitude_encoding is the most intricate routine in the QML toolkit — a recursive uniformly-controlled-Ry ladder with a gray-code CNOT mapping and an O(N^2) sign-matrix angle transform. Its docstring contract is an exact amplitude claim, but every test that checks it does so through sampled measurement frequencies with tolerances of 0.02–0.03, which only constrain |amplitude|^2. I confirmed the routine is currently exactly right (max amplitude error 1.1e-16 for [3,1,4,1] and [1..8]), so this is a regression net, not a live bug — but the net has a hole. Teeth check: I monkeypatched _mottonen_angle_transform to zero out the final theta (a plausible off-by-one in the decomposition) and re-ran the existing assertions verbatim: the 4-dim parametrized case passed 5/5, the uniform case passed, and the amplitude-encoding kernel test passed, while the prepared state was wrong by 0.0294 in amplitude. A full sign flip on that theta was worse still — it flunked only 2 of 5 runs (max dev 0.0323 vs tol 0.03), i.e. the suite's verdict on a definitively broken circuit is a coin toss decided by shot noise. Only the 8-dim case reliably caught it. The repo already owns the fix technique: the 2026-06-29 circuits sweep added exactly this exact-statevector pattern for QFT, and lib/utils/statevector.py exposes an engine-portable helper — but it was never carried across to lib/ml/. _Fix:_ In tests/test_feature_maps.py, import the existing `lib.utils.statevector.statevector` and replace (or augment) the shot-based body of `test_amplitude_encoding_matches_squared_amplitudes` with an exact assertion over the same parametrized feature vectors: `assert np.allclose(statevector(amplitude_encoding(features)).real, features / np.linalg.norm(features), atol=1e-12)` plus an imaginary-part check. This pins the docstring's actual contract (amplitudes, not probabilities) at machine precision, is deterministic instead of shot-noise-bounded, drops 32,000 simulated shots, and matches the precedent test_circuits.py already sets for the QFT. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/feature_maps.py:72`, `tests/test_feature_maps.py:90`, `tests/test_feature_maps.py:73`, `tests/test_classifiers.py:87`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** train_vqc's loss_history and accuracy_history are sampled at different parameter values, and no test can see it — Inside the epoch loop, `opt.step_and_cost(loss_fn, params)` returns the cost evaluated at the parameters BEFORE the step, then `params` is rebound to the post-step values and `predict_all(params)` computes accuracy at the NEW parameters. The two returned histories are therefore one gradient step out of phase with each other: `loss_history[k]` describes params_k while `accuracy_history[k]` describes params_{k+1}. A learner plotting both series against the same epoch axis — the obvious thing to do with a dict of two equal-length histories — is reading misaligned curves. Worse, the loss of the model actually handed back in `optimal_params` appears nowhere in `loss_history`. I verified this on a 12-sample, 6-epoch run: loss_history ended at 0.1688 while the true loss at the returned optimal_params was 0.1636, and accuracy_history[-1] did match the final params exactly. The curriculum consumes the wrong end of this: notebook 04 prints `last loss = lh[-1]`, which is not the returned model's loss. Neither existing test can detect the mismatch — one checks only key presence, list lengths and param shape, the other only that losses[-1] &lt; losses[0] - 0.01. _Fix:_ Move the `preds = predict_all(params)` / accuracy computation to before `opt.step_and_cost`, so both entries at index k describe the same parameters (this costs the same one extra forward pass per epoch that the loop already spends, so it is free). Then add a test that closes the loop: rebuild the QNode at the returned `optimal_params`, recompute the mean-squared-error, and assert it agrees with the reported final-epoch entry — an assertion on real behavior rather than on list lengths. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/training.py:95`, `lib/ml/training.py:96`, `04-quantum-ml/notebooks/04-pennylane-braket.ipynb:309`, `tests/test_training.py:17`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** quantum_kernel is the only public entry point in the QML toolkit with no input validation — zero shots and mismatched point lengths both slip through — Every sibling in lib/ml validates its inputs and fails loud, but quantum_kernel validates nothing. Two edge cases follow. (1) shots: run_circuit's `shots <= 0 or shots > MAX_SHOTS` guard sits inside `if device_name != "local"`, and quantum_kernel hardcodes the local path, so the guard never applies to it. shots=0 surfaces `ValueError: No result types specified for circuit and shots=0. See braket.circuits.result_types` and shots=-5 surfaces a pydantic `ValidationError` on TaskMetadata — both Braket internals that name neither the caller nor the parameter, on a function whose docstring promises a return 'between 0 and 1'. (2) x1/x2 agreement: nothing checks the two points describe the same register. I called quantum_kernel with a 3-feature x1 and a 2-feature x2 under angle_encoding and it returned 0.812 with no error — the unpaired third qubit merely contributes cos^2(x1[2]/2) to the all-zeros frequency, so the number is not a fidelity kernel at all. Nothing in tests/ exercises either case. _Fix:_ Add a validation preamble to quantum_kernel matching the module's established style: coerce x1/x2 with np.asarray, raise ValueError if `x1.shape != x2.shape` (naming both shapes), and raise ValueError if `shots < 1`. Cover both with tests alongside the existing kernel tests, mirroring test_feature_maps.py's `pytest.raises(ValueError, match=...)` convention. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/classifiers.py:88`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:168`, `lib/ml/feature_maps.py:44`, `tests/test_classifiers.py:32`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** The QML toolkit's only convergence assertion is marked slow on a rationale that the broadcast rewrite invalidated — it runs in 30 ms and nothing deselects it — test_train_vqc_loss_decreases is the single test that verifies the training loop actually learns. It carries @pytest.mark.slow and a docstring stating it is 'gated' and must be opted into with `pytest -m slow`, justified by '10 epochs * 20 samples * forward+backward QNode passes'. Both claims are now false. Nothing deselects the marker: pyproject registers it but sets no addopts, there is no pytest.ini/setup.cfg/tox.ini, `make test` is a bare `pytest tests/ -v`, and CI runs `make test` — so the test executes on every run. And the cost rationale predates the closed 2026-06-29 broadcast optimization, which made the QNode evaluate the whole dataset in one pass: I measured the test at 0.03 s in isolation. The result is a marker that misrepresents the suite to anyone reading it, and it invites exactly the wrong maintenance move — a future `-m "not slow"` added for speed would silently drop the toolkit's only end-to-end proof that train_vqc converges, in exchange for saving 30 ms. _Fix:_ Drop the @pytest.mark.slow decorator and rewrite the module and test docstrings to describe what the suite actually does, since the test is now a 30 ms unconditional check. If the marker is kept deliberately, make the docstring true instead — but given this is the toolkit's only convergence assertion, unmarked is the safer state. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`tests/test_training.py:3`, `tests/test_training.py:31`, `lib/ml/training.py:80`, `pyproject.toml:49`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The web's amplitude-encoding explorable happily encodes negative features and the zero vector; lib's amplitude_encoding hard-rejects both, and neither the widget nor the GUIDE says so — The `qencode` explorable mounted in the QML GUIDE lets the learner drag x0/x1 across [-pi, pi] with 'Amplitude' selected, and `amplitudeState` renders a normal-looking signed state — no warning, no error card, norm reads 1.000. The learner then opens 04-quantum-ml/notebooks/01-data-encoding.ipynb, tries the same input, and gets a hard ValueError. The two halves of one lesson disagree about what amplitude encoding accepts. The Python restriction is legitimate and honestly documented inside the function (the Ry-only Möttönen ladder cannot write signs), but that constraint is stated nowhere the learner meets the concept first — the GUIDE's amplitude bullet describes only compactness and gate cost, and its `⌈log₂N⌉` phrasing further implies arbitrary N works, when the Python also rejects any length that is not an exact power of 2. _Fix:_ Copy-only reconciliation on the teaching side — do not add sign support to the Python (that needs the Rz corrections its own docstring rules out). Extend 04-quantum-ml/GUIDE.md:31 to state the two constraints the toolkit actually enforces ('the workspace's Möttönen routine needs non-negative features and a power-of-2 length; for signed data reach for angle encoding'), which is the same sentence notebook 01 cell 8 already teaches. If the widget should mirror the library, the cheapest honest option is a caption line under the amplitude mode noting that the browser view shows the general signed construction while `lib.ml.feature_maps.amplitude_encoding` implements the non-negative Ry-only case. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/feature_maps.py:100-105`, `web/src/components/quantum/encoding.ts:11-23`, `web/src/components/quantum/encoding-explorer.tsx:198-200`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The broadcast rewrite made vqc_qnode the only lib/ml entry point that hard-crashes on a plain Python list, with a TypeError that names nothing about features — Every other public function in the QML toolkit begins with `np.asarray(...)`, so a learner can pass a plain list. `vqc_qnode`'s returned QNode cannot: the batching rewrite changed the encoding line from `features[i]` to `features[..., i]`, and Ellipsis indexing is a numpy-only operation. Passing a list now raises `TypeError: list indices must be integers or slices, not tuple` — an error that mentions neither `features` nor arrays nor the QNode, and points the learner at their own list literal rather than at the missing `np.array`.
