This is a regression introduced by the very PR the ledger closed for this feature (#86, 'QNode encoding -&gt; features[..., i] so it broadcasts'): the pre-#86 code accepted lists. _Fix:_ Restore list tolerance without giving up broadcasting: coerce once inside the QNode body before the encoding loop (`features = qml.math.asarray(features)`, or `pnp.asarray` — either preserves the autograd trace and the `(batch, n_qubits)` broadcast that lib/ml/training.py:85 depends on), so `features[..., i]` always indexes an array. tests/test_classifiers.py:72-84 already pins the batched path, so a one-line list-input case added beside it locks both shapes. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/ml/classifiers.py:170`, `lib/ml/classifiers.py:149-151`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)

#### Feature 24 — Category E re-audit (13 adversarially-verified findings)

- [x] **[Architecture]** The real-money QPU submit path hardcodes the device identity that lib/hardware/devices.py declares itself the single source of — and the module comment claims the opposite — lib/hardware/devices.py opens with "Single source of truth for every billable device: its Braket ARN and the cost.py PRICING provider key," and its allowlist comment promises that allowlisting a cheaper gate QPU "makes it win cheapest_allowlisted_device automatically — no other change." That promise is false. The Lambda that actually submits to hardware carries its own hand-written copy of the device short-name, ARN, and region, and hard-rejects any other device. The cross-language drift guard that exists for this pair covers the PRICES but not the DEVICE IDENTITY, so the asymmetry is invisible in CI: reprice IQM on one side and CI fails; re-ARN or re-allowlist the device and CI stays green while the Lambda keeps submitting to the old machine. _Fix:_ Extend the existing cross-language guard in tests/test_qpu_devices.py — it already regex-parses qpu-core.mjs, so the pattern and the plumbing are in place. Add assertions that the parsed DEVICE equals cheapest_allowlisted_device() (or at minimum is in allowlisted_gate_devices()), that DEVICE_ARN equals DEVICES[DEVICE]["arn"], and that DEVICE_REGION is the region field parsed out of that ARN. Then correct the lib/hardware/devices.py:44 comment: allowlisting a cheaper device requires the Lambda's three constants to move too — say so, or the comment will keep telling the next maintainer a change is free when it is not. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lib/hardware/devices.py:44`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:78`, `lambda/qpu/qpu-core.mjs:28`, `lambda/qpu/qpu-core.mjs:144`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Consistency]** README's device pricing table diverges from cost.py PRICING, and the prose drift guard is structurally blind to that entire table — lib/utils/cost.py declares itself the single source of truth for rates and test_pricing_prose.py exists specifically so learner-facing prose cannot quote a stale figure. README.md is in that test's corpus, yet its Rigetti row quotes $0.00035/shot while PRICING says $0.000425 — a 21% divergence sitting green in CI. The cause is a systematic hole, not a one-off: the extractor's inline-math stripper treats any two dollar signs on one line as a LaTeX span, and every per-shot row in the README table has exactly that shape ("$0.30 / task + $0.08 / shot"), so all four QPU rows are erased before scanning. Only the two single-$ simulator rows are ever checked. The table that documents the device catalog is therefore unguarded precisely where per-shot rates — the expensive ones — live. _Fix:_ Correct README.md:565 to $0.000425 and refresh the header year. Then close the extraction hole so it cannot recur: in _strip_inline_math, skip candidate spans that contain a currency-shaped amount immediately after the opening $ (a real LaTeX span like $2^n$ or $0.74$ does not read as "$0.30 / task + "), or exclude pure-markdown surfaces from math stripping entirely since README/CLAUDE.md carry no inline LaTeX. Extend the canary at line 139 to assert the README table's per-shot rates are among the extracted hits, so the blindness cannot silently return. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`README.md:565`, `tests/test_pricing_prose.py:32`, `tests/test_pricing_prose.py:87`, `README.md:557`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Correctness]** MAX_SHOTS = 100,000 sits above every real Braket per-device shot cap, so the "guardrail" never binds and the cost warning prints authoritative dollar figures for tasks the service will reject — `run_circuit`'s only shot validation is a single global `0 < shots <= MAX_SHOTS`, with MAX_SHOTS = 100,000, commented as a "Guardrail against an accidental huge (expensive) submission on a billable device." Every documented Braket per-task shot limit is tighter than that ceiling, so on the billable path the guardrail is inert — Braket's own validation always fires first — and, worse, the cost warning the project's cost-awareness rule depends on prints a confident dollar amount for a submission that is physically impossible. Per the AWS Braket quotas page ("Additional quotas and limits"): SV1/DM1 max 50,000 shots per task, IQM Garnet max 20,000, QuEra Aquila max 1,000, and IonQ has a *minimum* of 100 shots per on-demand task (2,500 for error-mitigation tasks). The gate enforces none of these and has no minimum beyond `> 0`. This is the module whose entire subject is cost discipline, so a learner is being handed a $500.30 estimate for a QuEra task capped at 1,000 shots, and a $0.38 estimate for a 1-shot IonQ task below IonQ's floor. _Fix:_ The DEVICES table is already the single source for each device's ARN, provider, gate_capable and allowlist — put the real per-task shot bound in the same row and have run_circuit's existing pre-flight block validate against it, keeping MAX_SHOTS as the fallback ceiling for any row that omits one. No new behavior: the gate already validates shots, it just validates against a number no device honors. Minimal alternative if the table change is unwanted: lower MAX_SHOTS to the tightest real cap and rewrite the line-115 comment to say it is a coarse ceiling and Braket enforces the true per-device bound. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lib/hardware/devices.py:115-116`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:171-172`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:189-191`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:83-88`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Correctness]** run_circuit returns None — not a result — when a paid Braket task fails, is cancelled, or out-waits the SDK's 5-day poll, and it blocks the kernel the whole time — `run_circuit` ends with `return device.run(circuit, **run_kwargs).result()`. On the billable path that `.result()` is `AwsQuantumTask.result()`, whose own SDK docstring states it "is a blocking thread call" and "returns `None` if the quantum task did not complete successfully or the future timed out." So the one function the module exists to provide has two undocumented behaviors on exactly the path where real money has already been spent: (1) it silently hands back `None` for a FAILED / CANCELLED task or a poll timeout — the learner's next line (`result.measurement_counts`, `result.measurements`) dies with `AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute ...`, with no task ARN, no state, and no hint that a charge was incurred; and (2) it blocks the caller for as long as the QPU queue takes, because `AwsDevice.run`'s `poll_timeout_seconds` defaults to 432000 (5 days) and `run_kwargs` is only ever `{shots, s3_destination_folder}`, so no caller can shorten it. The docstring devotes six lines to the cost gate and says nothing about either; README's API table says only "Run and return results." This is the residue of the 2026-06-29 sweep: that pass hardened everything *before* `device.run` (shots cap, s3 shape, fail-closed provider, memoization) and never looked at what comes back after it. _Fix:_ Make the existing failure mode honest, without adding capability: after `.result()`, if the returned value is `None`, raise a ValueError/RuntimeError naming the task ARN and its terminal state (both readable off the `AwsQuantumTask` object already in hand) instead of returning `None` — money was spent, so silence is the wrong default. Extend the docstring (and the README row) to state that the billable path blocks until the task leaves the queue, inheriting the SDK's 432000s poll default, and to point at `get_device(name).run(...)` for callers who need the un-awaited `AwsQuantumTask`. Add an offline test alongside `test_run_circuit_fails_closed_on_unpriced_provider` that stubs a device whose `run(...).result()` returns `None` and asserts `run_circuit` raises rather than returning None. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lib/hardware/devices.py:195`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:159-166`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Correctness]** The QPU panel's hand-off to real hardware teaches a run_circuit call that raises ValueError and prints no cost estimate — the exact opposite of the sentence wrapped around it — The graduation copy at the bottom of the sponsored-QPU panel — the platform's documented next step for a learner moving onto their own AWS account — tells them to run `run_circuit(circuit, device_name="iqm_garnet")` "from lib/hardware, which prints a cost estimate before it submits." That call cannot run. `run_circuit` requires `s3_location` for every non-local device, and that check sits *above* the `print(format_cost_warning(...))`, so the invocation as taught raises `ValueError: s3_location required for AWS devices: (bucket, prefix)` and never prints the cost estimate the sentence promises. Both halves of the claim are false for the literal command shown. This is fresh divergence, not old residue: the panel is the Phase-4 QPU surface and its copy was written against an imagined `run_circuit`, while the repo's own API table (README.md:350) correctly documents the s3_location fail-fast — so the platform contradicts itself, and the failure lands on the single highest-intent learner in the funnel (one who just paid attention to a real hardware run and is trying to reproduce it). _Fix:_ Copy-only fix in the panel: show the call that actually works, e.g. `run_circuit(circuit, device_name="iqm_garnet", shots=1000, s3_location=("amazon-braket-<your-bucket>", "quantum"))`, and keep the cost-estimate sentence, which is then true. Since the panel links to the README quickstart anchor (`README_QUICKSTART_ANCHOR`, qpu-submit-panel.tsx:97), make the snippet there and the panel snippet identical so the two hand-off surfaces cannot drift again. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`web/src/components/quantum/qpu-submit-panel.tsx:584-585`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:173-174`, `README.md:350`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Consistency]** list_available_devices() has zero callers while the two shipped device-discovery surfaces hand-roll it — and both hand-rolls are broken (a Python &lt;3.12 SyntaxError, and a Qubits column that can only print N/A) — `list_available_devices()` is one of the feature's three public functions, exported from `lib.hardware`, documented in the README — and nothing in the repo calls it except its own test. Meanwhile the two surfaces that actually list devices for a learner each re-implement it against `AwsDevice.get_devices()`, and both re-implementations carry defects the library function does not. `make devices` — a Key Command in CLAUDE.md — is an inline `python -c` whose header uses nested single quotes inside a single-quoted f-string, a construct PEP 701 legalized only in Python 3.12; `pyproject.toml` declares `requires-python = ">=3.10"`, so on a supported 3.10/3.11 interpreter the command is a hard SyntaxError before it does anything. Separately, both that recipe and `02-hardware/scripts/device_status.py` read `getattr(d.properties, "qubitCount", ...)`, but `qubitCount` lives one level down under `paradigm` on every Braket capabilities schema — so the Qubits column those tools advertise can only ever print "N/A". The repo's own notebook gets this right (`forte.properties.paradigm.qubitCount`), which is what makes the duplication, not the typo, the root cause. _Fix:_ Point both surfaces at the function that already exists: make the `devices` target run `python -c "from lib.hardware import list_available_devices; ..."` (or a two-line script under infra/scripts/ like `make cost` already does) and rewrite `02-hardware/scripts/device_status.py` to render rows from `list_available_devices()`. That deletes the un-runnable f-string outright and gives the function its first real caller. If the Qubits column is to be kept in those printouts, read it off `paradigm` as the notebook does — no change to `list_available_devices()`'s pinned four-key dict contract (tests/test_devices.py:111) is required. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`Makefile:23`, `Makefile:25`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Consistency]** run_circuit reports a missing S3 bucket when the real problem is an unknown or retired device name — exactly the post-Aria-retirement path — run_circuit validates in the order shots -&gt; s3_location -&gt; device existence, so a caller who names a device that no longer exists gets an error about S3 instead of about the device. This is not hypothetical: `ionq_aria` was a valid short-name until the retirement Category B propagated, and any learner running a stale snippet or an older notebook copy hits it. get_device() already produces the correct, helpful message (it lists every known short-name), but run_circuit never reaches it — and duplicates that same raise a few lines later, so the codebase carries the good error message twice and still shows the wrong one first. _Fix:_ Hoist the `spec = DEVICES.get(device_name)` lookup to the top of the non-local branch, ahead of the shots and s3_location checks — the device name is the most fundamental input and the cheapest thing to reject. Since get_device() re-checks it anyway, the raise at line 185 can then be the single one and line 133's duplicate string collapses into a shared lookup helper. Add a test asserting that an unknown device name raises "Unknown device" even when s3_location is omitted. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/hardware/devices.py:171`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:133`, `tests/test_devices.py:24`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** A function-level import of estimate_cost defers nothing, and mimics the load-bearing lazy-import idiom 60 lines above it — cheapest_allowlisted_device imports estimate_cost from lib.utils.cost inside the function body, but the module already imports format_cost_warning from that exact module at the top, so lib.utils.cost is fully loaded before the function can ever run. The deferral saves nothing. It is actively misleading in this file specifically: devices.py is the one module in lib/ where a deferred import IS load-bearing — the PEP-562 __getattr__ that keeps braket.aws out of the browser/qcsim import graph — and it is carefully documented as such. An undocumented function-level import a few lines below reads to the next maintainer as a second browser-safety measure that must not be touched, when it is just noise. _Fix:_ Move estimate_cost into the existing module-level import on line 8 and delete the in-function import. One import statement, and the file's remaining deferred import is unambiguously the one that matters. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/hardware/devices.py:8`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:107`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:11-24`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** The hardware package's declared public surface omits the two QPU-phase functions it exports from its own module — lib/hardware/__init__.py uses the explicit `X as X` re-export form, which is the deliberate way to declare a package's public API for type checkers and readers. It lists the five names that existed before the QPU-submit phase and silently omits the two public functions added with it, plus MAX_SHOTS. The result is an arbitrary split in the import surface with no rationale anywhere: `from lib.hardware import get_device` works, `from lib.hardware import cheapest_allowlisted_device` raises ImportError. Every consumer that needs the newer functions has to reach past the package into the submodule, which is exactly what the re-export block exists to prevent. _Fix:_ Add allowlisted_gate_devices, cheapest_allowlisted_device, and MAX_SHOTS to the re-export block in the same `X as X` style, and point tests/test_qpu_devices.py at lib.hardware rather than lib.hardware.devices. If any of the three is intentionally internal, mark it with a leading underscore instead — either way the omission stops being ambiguous. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/hardware/__init__.py:3-9`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:95`, `tests/test_qpu_devices.py:12`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[API honesty]** run_circuit will dispatch a gate Circuit to quera_aquila even though the same file records `gate_capable: False` for it — `DEVICES["quera_aquila"]` carries `"gate_capable": False` with the inline comment "analog Hamiltonian device — cannot run gate circuits", and `run_circuit` is typed `circuit: Circuit` — a gate circuit. Yet run_circuit's pre-flight block validates shots, s3_location, and the provider's pricing, prints a cost estimate, then constructs the AwsDevice and submits; it never reads `gate_capable`. Its docstring claims "All gating happens before the AwsDevice is constructed, so a bad request fails fast with no network/credentials" — and a gate circuit aimed at an analog-only device is precisely a bad request that does not fail fast. It fails opaquely at the service instead, after the library has already printed a plausible dollar figure for it. (In fairness, the DEVICES comment scopes the two flags to the lambda/qpu path, so this is a gap in run_circuit's own gate rather than a contradicted promise — but the fact is already in the table, one lookup away from the check that needs it.) _Fix:_ Read the already-present `gate_capable` flag in run_circuit's existing pre-flight block and raise a ValueError naming the analog paradigm before the cost print, alongside the shots/s3/provider checks. It consults data the module already single-sources and adds no device support or new capability. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/hardware/devices.py:83-88`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:167-195`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:159-166`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:95-99`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[API honesty]** list_available_devices() is named and documented as listing "currently available" devices but applies no status filter — OFFLINE and RETIRED devices come back too — The function's docstring says "List all currently available Amazon Braket devices with their status", but the body calls `get_devices()` with no arguments and maps every device it returns, including a `status` field it never acts on. The name and the docstring both assert availability that the code does not establish. The repo's own curriculum states the opposite: notebook 01 documents `forte.status` as "ONLINE / OFFLINE / RETIRED" and Exercise 3 hands the learner a fixture of device objects described as "exactly what `AwsDevice.get_devices()` returns" containing OFFLINE and RETIRED entries that must be filtered out. So the library teaches, via its function name, the exact mistake the notebook teaches learners to avoid — and picking a row from this list can target a retired device, which is the Aria class of error Category B just cleaned out of the web catalog. _Fix:_ Make one of the two true. Either filter to ONLINE so the name is accurate, or — the honest minimum — rewrite the docstring to say it lists the full fleet Braket exposes, each row carrying its ONLINE/OFFLINE/RETIRED status, which the caller must check before dispatch. Either way add a non-ONLINE stub to tests/test_devices.py's fixture so the chosen contract is pinned. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/hardware/devices.py:143-149`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:145`, `tests/test_devices.py:100-111`, `02-hardware/notebooks/01-device-discovery.ipynb:109`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[API honesty]** The hardware GUIDE's documented cost_estimator command fails on first run — `--device ionq` is not an accepted choice — 02-hardware/GUIDE.md tells the learner to run `python 02-hardware/scripts/cost_estimator.py --device ionq --shots 1000`. The script's `--device` argument is restricted to `choices=list(PRICING.keys())`, and those keys are case-sensitive provider names (`IonQ`, `IQM`, `QuEra`, `Rigetti`, `SV1`, `DM1`, `TN1`, `LocalSimulator`). Lowercase `ionq` is rejected by argparse with exit code 2. This is the one command in the hardware module a learner is told to type into a terminal to practice the project's cost-estimate-before-you-run rule, and it errors out. _Fix:_ Correct the GUIDE line to `--device IonQ`. The script's `--help` already enumerates the legal values, so no code change is needed; if the mixed-case keys are considered a usability trap, normalize the incoming value against PRICING case-insensitively rather than loosening `choices`. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`02-hardware/GUIDE.md:382`, `02-hardware/scripts/cost_estimator.py:13-15`, `lib/utils/cost.py:9-20`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Architecture]** The 02-hardware opener redeclares DEVICE_ARNS — the same public name lib.hardware exports — with a different key scheme and a second copy of every ARN literal — `lib/hardware/devices.py` declares DEVICES the single source of truth for device identity and derives `DEVICE_ARNS` from it; `lib.hardware` exports that name, the README documents it, and tests/test_devices.py pins the derivation. The first notebook of section 02-hardware — the section this feature backs — then defines its own module-level `DEVICE_ARNS` dict containing the same six ARNs typed out again, keyed by display labels ("IonQ Forte-1", "IQM Garnet") rather than the library's short names (`ionq_forte`, `iqm_garnet`), and even repeats the library's own `assert arn.startswith("arn:aws:braket:")` invariant inline. Nothing links the two: no test compares the notebook's table to `lib.hardware.DEVICE_ARNS`, and no notebook in the entire curriculum imports `lib.hardware` at all. The failure this invites has already happened once elsewhere — the retired IonQ Aria survived in the web catalog long after cost.py declared it retired — and here it would be worse, because the identifier collides: a learner who meets `DEVICE_ARNS["IonQ Forte-1"]` in the lesson and then does `from lib.hardware import DEVICE_ARNS` gets a dict where that key does not exist. _Fix:_ Have the notebook import the table it is teaching: `from lib.hardware import DEVICE_ARNS` and iterate it directly (the notebook is manifest-marked `runnable: false`, so the import is unconstrained by the browser bundle), keeping any display labels as a separate presentation-only mapping if the pretty names are wanted. The live-discovery block then indexes `DEVICE_ARNS["ionq_forte"]`, teaching the same key the learner will type against `run_circuit(device_name=...)`. If the literals must stay for pedagogical reasons, add one assertion in the notebook comparing `set(DEVICE_ARNS.values())` to `set(lib.hardware.DEVICE_ARNS.values())` so a fleet change fails the notebook-contract run instead of silently teaching a retired ARN. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/hardware/devices.py:92`, `tests/test_devices.py:44`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)

#### Feature 25 — Category E re-audit (11 adversarially-verified findings)

- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** plot_bloch_angles renders any state with a nonzero phase as a shrunken or invisible arrow, and nothing in the repo would catch it — the tests assert no geometry and the sole notebook consumer throws the helper's output away — `plot_bloch_angles(theta, phi)` computes `x = sin(theta)*cos(phi)`, `z = cos(theta)` and draws an arrow of length `sqrt(x^2 + z^2)` inside a unit circle. For any phase off the X-Z plane the arrow is silently shortened, and at `phi = pi/2` it collapses entirely: the `|+i>` state — a perfectly ordinary pure state, and exactly what the same notebook's section 4 teaches the S gate produces — renders as a ~0.02-unit speck at the origin inside an otherwise empty unit circle. Arrow length inside a Bloch circle reads as state purity, so a pure state at `phi = pi/3` drawn at half length teaches the learner it is half-mixed, and `|+i>` teaches that the state vanished. The reason this survived a deep sweep is that the helper is verified by nothing on either side. `tests/test_visualization.py:54-72` asserts only `isinstance(fig, plt.Figure)` and `ax.get_title()` — no assertion touches the arrow at all, so the helper could draw the arrow backwards, at the wrong length, or not at all and every test would still pass — and all four cases pass `phi=0`, leaving the entire phase axis unexercised. On the curriculum side, the one notebook that imports it calls it in a loop and immediately `plt.close()`s all four figures, then hand-reimplements the helper's whole body onto its own subplot axes. That copy has already drifted from the original in four constants, and it means the tested helper's rendering is displayed to a learner nowhere in the curriculum — the only Bloch drawing anyone actually sees is the untested inline duplicate. _Fix:_ Decide the projection semantics and then test them. Either make the helper honest about being an X-Z slice — state in the docstring that arrow length is the in-plane component and not state purity, and give the degenerate case a visible treatment (a marked dot plus an annotation naming the out-of-plane phase) instead of an invisible speck — or plot the in-plane angle at unit length so every pure state reaches the circle and the phase is carried by the label. Either way, add the geometry assertions the file is missing: for known states assert the arrow's endpoint direction (theta=0 points to +z, theta=pi to -z, theta=pi/2 phi=0 to +x) and add a phi=pi/2 case asserting the chosen non-degenerate behaviour, so the phase axis stops being untested. Separately, delete the discard-then-redraw block in the notebook: the cell should either use the helper's figures or drop the import, so the drawing a learner sees is the drawing the tests cover. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`lib/utils/visualization.py:35-37`, `tests/test_visualization.py:54-72`, `01-foundations/notebooks/02-single-qubit-gates.ipynb:182`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The prose-rate drift guard is blind to every two-rate row in README's headline cost table, and a stale Rigetti rate is sitting there green right now — tests/test_pricing_prose.py exists to catch exactly one class of bug: a learner-facing dollar figure that no longer derives from lib.utils.cost.PRICING. Its `_strip_inline_math` step deletes any `$…$` span that does not look like prose, to protect LaTeX ($2^n$, $0.74$ Angstrom). But README's cost table writes two currency amounts per row — `| IonQ (Forte 36q) | $0.30 / task + $0.08 / shot |` — so the span from the first `$` to the second `$` is `$0.30 / task + $`, which contains no two consecutive words and therefore fails the PROSE_LIKE test and gets stripped as math. The per-task rate is deleted and the per-shot number is left bare with no `$` in front, so the second `collect()` pass cannot see it either. I ran the extractor over that table: it returns only `[(0.075, ...), (0.275, ...)]` — the two single-rate rows. All four two-rate rows (IonQ, IQM, QuEra, Rigetti) are invisible. The consequence is already live: README:564 teaches Rigetti at `$0.00035 / shot` while lib/utils/cost.py:15 has been `0.000425` since the cost sweep — a 21% understatement. I confirmed `0.00035` is not in `valid_amounts()` and is not in the extractor's output for README, and that `pytest tests/test_pricing_prose.py` passes 3/3 anyway. The table header also still reads 'Price (approx., 2025)' while cost.py:5 records the rates as verified 2026-07-06. So the guard passes not because the prose is correct but because it never looked — the 'test asserts its own fixture' failure mode, applied to the repo's most prominent rate table. _Fix:_ Close the blind spot in `_strip_inline_math`: a `$…$` candidate whose opening `$` is immediately followed by a currency amount (the existing AMOUNT pattern already recognizes it) is currency, not math, so it must not be stripped — the same discrimination the function already makes for prose-like spans. Then fix the two figures the reopened guard will surface: README:564's Rigetti per-shot rate to 0.000425 (or drop the row, since cost.py:13-14 already notes no Rigetti device is dispatchable via lib.hardware.DEVICES), and refresh the '(approx., 2025)' header to the 2026-07-06 verification date. Add a case to `test_extractor_separates_currency_from_latex_math` using a real two-rate table row so the hole cannot reopen. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`README.md:564`, `lib/utils/cost.py:15`, `README.md:556`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Architecture]** plot_bloch_angles' only consumer discards its figures and then hand-reimplements the helper's body verbatim — `plot_bloch_angles` has exactly one consumer in the whole curriculum, and that consumer does not use it. Cell 8 of `01-foundations/notebooks/02-single-qubit-gates.ipynb` loops over four states calling `plot_bloch_angles(theta, phi, title=title)` and immediately `plt.close(fig_single)` on every returned figure — four figures built and destroyed, output never rendered, pure dead work. A second loop then redraws all four states inline, line-for-line reproducing the helper's body: the circle patch, `x = np.sin(theta) * np.cos(phi)`, `z = np.cos(theta)`, the `#ff9900` arrow, xlim/ylim, aspect, both dashed axis lines, the title, and the `|0>`/`|1>` text labels — differing only in cosmetic constants (arrow scale 0.85 vs the helper's 0.9, label offset 1.15 vs 1.1, head_width 0.06 vs 0.05). The net effect is that the library function this lesson exists to teach is never actually rendered, and a divergent copy of it ships into the browser lab (the notebook is marked `<!-- browser-runnable -->`). Any future change to `plot_bloch_angles` — colors, scale, adding the discarded y-component — silently fails to reach the one place a learner sees a Bloch circle. The root cause on the library side is that the helper hard-codes `fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5, 5))`, so it owns figure creation and offers a caller no way to draw onto an existing axes; that is precisely why the notebook had to copy it to build a 1x4 grid. _Fix:_ Delete the dead first loop outright — it produces nothing. Then remove the hand-inlined copy by giving `plot_bloch_angles` (and, for symmetry, `plot_histogram`) an optional `ax=None` parameter: when `ax` is None keep today's behavior exactly (create the figure, return it); when an axes is passed, draw onto it and return its figure. The notebook cell then becomes `for ax, (theta, phi, title) in zip(axes, states): plot_bloch_angles(theta, phi, title=title, ax=ax)` plus `plt.tight_layout(); plt.show()`, which is behavior-preserving for the learner and leaves exactly one implementation of the Bloch drawing in the repo. This is a signature refinement to eliminate duplication, not new capability — no new helper and no new rendering the notebook does not already perform by hand. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/visualization.py:29-49`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** statevector() reads result.values[0] blind, so any circuit that already carries a result type gets probabilities (or a scalar expectation) returned as its "exact final state vector" — lib/utils/statevector.py's real-SDK branch does `working = circuit.copy()`, appends a StateVector result type, runs shots=0, and then returns `result.values[0]` — index 0, unconditionally. But `Circuit.copy()` carries the caller's EXISTING result types onto the copy, and `state_vector()` APPENDS, so StateVector lands at the end of the list, not the front. `result.values` is ordered by result-type registration, so index 0 is whatever the caller attached first. The helper then casts it to complex128 and returns it as amplitudes — no shape check, no exception, no warning. I verified all three cases in-process against the real amazon-braket-sdk in the repo's .venv: a circuit with `.probability()` attached returns `[0.5+0j, 0, 0, 0.5+0j]` (the probability vector) where the Bell state `[0.707, 0, 0, 0.707]` is promised; a circuit with `.expectation(Observable.Z(), target=0)` attached returns a 0-d array holding `-2.24e-17` — a scalar expectation value, not a state vector at all. The docstring's headline promise is 'Return the exact final state vector of `circuit` as a complex ndarray.' The same docstring specifically brags that the copy protects the caller's circuit — but the copy is exactly what drags the caller's result types in and breaks the index assumption. This is engine-asymmetric: I confirmed qcsim's Circuit has no `.probability()`/`.expectation()` (checked with qcsim forced ahead of braket), so a browser learner cannot reach it, while every local-SDK notebook and script can. That inverts the module's entire reason for existing — it was written to erase a qcsim/real-SDK divergence and it introduces a silent one. _Fix:_ Select the StateVector result by identity instead of by position: after running, pick the value whose corresponding entry in `result.result_types` is the StateVector result type (or, equivalently, drop the copy's pre-existing result types before attaching StateVector so index 0 is guaranteed). Both are one-line refinements inside the existing branch. Then extend `test_real_sdk_does_not_mutate_the_circuit` (or add a sibling) that attaches `.probability()` and `.expectation(...)` to the caller's circuit and asserts the helper still returns the Bell amplitudes — the case the current test's fresh circuits can never reach. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/statevector.py:55-58`, `lib/utils/statevector.py:30`, `tests/test_statevector_helper.py:53-62`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The only documented invocation of the cost estimator CLI fails with argparse exit code 2 — `02-hardware/GUIDE.md` teaches the cost estimator with `python 02-hardware/scripts/cost_estimator.py --device ionq --shots 1000`. That command does not run. The script derives its `--device` choices from `list(PRICING.keys())`, which are capitalized provider names (`IonQ`, `IQM`, `QuEra`, `Rigetti`, `SV1`, `DM1`, `TN1`, `LocalSimulator`), so `ionq` is rejected. The lowercase form in the prose is not arbitrary: `lib/hardware/devices.py` keys its dispatchable `DEVICES` registry by lowercase short-names (`ionq_forte`, `iqm_garnet`, `sv1`), so the repo really does carry two naming schemes and the GUIDE picked the wrong one for this entry point. This is the one CLI a cost-anxious learner is told to reach for before spending real money, and README.md names it alongside `lib.utils.cost` and `make cost` as the spend-estimation tooling. _Fix:_ Fix the GUIDE line to the value the parser actually accepts (`--device IonQ`). Optionally also make the parser case-insensitive by normalizing the incoming value against a case-folded map of `PRICING` keys, so either spelling works and the two naming schemes stop being a trap — but the prose fix alone closes the defect. The existing `test_pricing_prose.py` drift guard only validates dollar figures in prose, not command invocations, so nothing catches this today. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`02-hardware/GUIDE.md:382`, `02-hardware/scripts/cost_estimator.py:13-15`, `lib/utils/cost.py:9-20`, `lib/hardware/devices.py:56-88`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Architecture]** cost_estimator.py's sys.path bootstrap is dead code that prepends a directory outside the repo — `02-hardware/scripts/cost_estimator.py` starts with `sys.path.insert(0, "../..")`. That relative path is resolved against the *current working directory*, not the script's location. Run from the repo root — which is exactly how `02-hardware/GUIDE.md:382` documents it — it prepends `/Users/cperez/dev`, the repo's grandparent, to the front of `sys.path`. That directory contains no `lib` package, so the hack contributes nothing to the import; `from lib.utils.cost import ...` succeeds only because the workspace is pip-installed editable (`pyproject.toml` `[tool.setuptools.packages.find] include = ["lib*"]`). So the line is simultaneously dead (it never supplies the import it appears to enable) and hazardous (it front-loads an arbitrary user directory ahead of site-packages for every subsequent import in the process). It also diverges from the repo's own established convention. _Fix:_ Delete the `sys.path.insert(0, "../..")` line and the now-unused `import sys` — the editable install already makes `lib` importable, as `device_status.py` in the same directory demonstrates. If a no-install fallback is genuinely wanted, replace it with the repo's own idiom, `sys.path.insert(0, str(Path(__file__).resolve().parents[2]))`, which is correct from any working directory. (The identical `sys.path.insert(0, "../..")` at `05-quantum-chemistry/scripts/vqe_runner.py:33` is the same defect in the chemistry toolkit and should be swept with it.) Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`02-hardware/scripts/cost_estimator.py:7-8`, `scripts/gen_cost_fixture.py:28-29`, `02-hardware/scripts/device_status.py:1-4`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Consistency]** The per-shot/per-minute pricing-shape test is hand-rolled in four places in Python while the TS mirror exports a single named predicate — `PRICING` is a two-shaped table: QPU providers carry `per_shot` + `per_task`, managed simulators carry `per_minute`. Every consumer must discriminate the two shapes, and the Python side re-derives that test inline four separate times — twice inside `cost.py` itself, and once more in the section script. The sibling TypeScript implementation that mirrors this exact table already solved it by exporting a named predicate, `isPerShot(provider)`, which the device table and cost widgets consume. Worse, the four Python sites do not even agree on how to test: two ask `"per_shot" in pricing`, one asks `"per_minute" in PRICING[device]`, and one asks `pricing.get("per_minute") == 0.0`. That last one carries a comment reading `# the local simulator (free)`, but the code it annotates does not test for the local simulator at all — it tests for any zero-rate per-minute provider. The comment and the condition describe different things, which is exactly the kind of drift a named predicate prevents. _Fix:_ Add a module-level `is_per_shot(provider: str) -> bool` to `lib/utils/cost.py` mirroring `cost.ts`'s `isPerShot`, and route all four sites through it. Keep the free-simulator branch a value test but rename the comment to match what it does (`# any zero-rate per-minute provider — today only LocalSimulator`), or make it an explicit `provider == "LocalSimulator"` check; either way comment and condition should agree. The existing `test_zero_minute_simulator_not_labeled_local` in `tests/test_cost.py` already pins the behavior, so the refactor is guarded. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/cost.py:39`, `lib/utils/cost.py:48`, `lib/utils/cost.py:50`, `02-hardware/scripts/cost_estimator.py:29`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** visualization.py is the only utils module with no return annotations, and its stdlib import sits below its third-party imports — Across `lib/utils`, the annotation convention is unambiguous: opaque duck-typed objects that must work under two engines go unannotated (`result`, `circuit`), but every other parameter is typed and every public function annotates its return. `visualization.py` is the sole module that breaks it — neither `plot_histogram` nor `plot_bloch_angles` declares a return type, and `figsize` is untyped. Both provably return `matplotlib.figure.Figure`, and `tests/test_visualization.py` already asserts exactly that with `isinstance(fig, plt.Figure)`, so the type is known, tested, and simply not written down. The same module also inverts the import grouping every sibling uses: it places `from collections import Counter` *below* `matplotlib` and `numpy`. `ruff check` does not catch this because the project's ruff config selects no isort rules, so it survives a green `make lint`. This is residue from the Category-E sweep: `visualization.py` was last touched by PR #84, which hardened its degenerate-input paths but did not apply the package's annotation and import conventions. _Fix:_ Annotate both functions `-> Figure` (importing `from matplotlib.figure import Figure`) and type `figsize: tuple[float, float] = (10, 5)`, then move `from collections import Counter` to the top of the import block, separated by a blank line, matching `results.py`. If the convention is worth enforcing rather than restating, add `"I"` to a `[tool.ruff.lint] select` in pyproject.toml so import grouping is gated by `make lint` instead of left to review. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/visualization.py:8`, `lib/utils/visualization.py:29`, `lib/utils/visualization.py:3-5`, `lib/utils/results.py:3-5`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Consistency]** parse_counts' column-order error message reports an impossible range when the result is empty — `parse_counts` derives the register width as `n = len(measurements[0]) if len(measurements) else 0`, then validates `measured_qubits` against `list(range(n))`. When the result has zero shots but still reports `measured_qubits`, `n` is 0 and the error message interpolates `n - 1` as -1, producing `parse_counts requires measured_qubits == 0..-1 in order` — a range that cannot exist, pointing the reader at a nonexistent register instead of at the real situation (an empty measurement array). The module's other error messages are exemplary — `expectation_from_counts` at line 42 spells out both failure modes and why a silent 0.0 would mislead — so this one is the outlier in an otherwise carefully-worded module. It also contradicts the deliberate empty-result behavior the module already commits to: `tests/test_results.py` pins that an empty measurements array must return an empty `Counter`, but that test omits `measured_qubits`, so the guard is skipped and this branch is never exercised. _Fix:_ Short-circuit the guard when there is nothing to validate — return `Counter()` immediately for an empty measurements array before the `measured_qubits` check, which matches the behavior the existing test already pins and makes the empty case unambiguous. Alternatively keep the ordering and word the message for n == 0 explicitly (e.g. `result reported measured_qubits {list(measured)} but contains no measurements`). Extend `test_parse_counts_empty_measurements_is_empty_counter` to also pass `measured_qubits` so whichever branch is chosen is actually covered. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/results.py:18`, `lib/utils/results.py:20-24`, `tests/test_results.py:58-63`, `lib/utils/results.py:40-44`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** parse_counts dies with a bare TypeError on a real shots=0 result, and the test that claims to cover the zero-shot path asserts a shape the SDK never emits — `parse_counts` has a deliberate empty-measurements guard (`n = len(measurements[0]) if len(measurements) else 0`) that PR #84 added specifically for "the zero-shot path" (ledger line 1156). But the real Braket SDK does not return `[]` for a zero-shot (analytic) task — it returns `None`. `len(None)` raises before the guard is ever consulted, so the exact case the guard was written for produces `TypeError: object of type 'NoneType' has no len()` instead of a clear message. The regression test that locks the guard in place (`tests/test_results.py:60-64`) feeds `[]` from the hand-rolled `MockResult` in `conftest.py`, a shape no real result object ever has — it asserts its own fixture, not real behavior, and its docstring's claim ("a zero-shot / empty result") is false against the installed SDK. This matters because the curriculum routinely runs `shots=0` for state-vector work (02-hardware/notebooks/05-simulator-comparison.ipynb, 03-algorithms/notebooks/03-qft.ipynb), so a learner who reaches for the library's documented counts extractor after an analytic run gets an inscrutable NumPy-flavoured TypeError. A second manifestation of the same root cause: `n` is derived from `measurements` rather than from `measured_qubits`, so a result with empty measurements but populated labels raises `ValueError: parse_counts requires measured_qubits == 0..-1 in order` — a nonsense range. _Fix:_ Normalise the input at the top of `parse_counts`: treat `measurements is None` the same as empty (the analytic/zero-shot case) and raise a message that names the real cause ("this result has no measurements — it came from a shots=0 analytic run; use lib.utils.statevector.statevector(circuit) for amplitudes"), which also points the learner at the sibling helper they actually want. Derive `n` from `measured_qubits` when it is present so the guard message can never print an underflowed range. Then re-point the coverage at reality: replace (or supplement) the `[]` mock case with a test that runs a real `shots=0` task through `LocalSimulator` and asserts the new error — `tests/test_statevector_helper.py` already establishes the in-process real-Braket pattern for this file's sibling, so the harness exists. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/results.py:17-18`, `tests/test_results.py:60-64`, `tests/conftest.py:28-36`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** README's lib.utils API table omits statevector entirely and its carve-out note contradicts __init__.py, which does re-export it — README's `lib.utils` reference block is the documented public surface of this feature. It lists three rows — the `results` helpers, `lib.utils.cost`, and `lib.utils.visualization` — and then states: 'Note: `lib.utils.__init__` re-exports only the `results` helpers; `cost` and `visualization` are reached via their full module paths.' That note is false as written: lib/utils/__init__.py:8 re-exports `statevector` alongside the three results helpers. Worse, `statevector` gets no row in the table at all, even though it is by a wide margin the most-imported symbol in the module — I counted `from lib.utils.statevector import statevector` in 14+ curriculum notebooks across sections 02 through 05, versus a handful of call sites for everything else combined. A reader working from the README's own API table would not learn that the module's most load-bearing helper exists; they only meet it incidentally at README:408, in a parenthetical inside the qcsim section. The module docstring compounds the drift in the other direction — lib/utils/__init__.py:1 advertises 'result parsing, visualization, and cost tracking', naming the two subjects the file deliberately does NOT export while omitting the one it does. _Fix:_ Add a `statevector(circuit)` row to README's lib.utils table describing it as the portable state-vector read (the role README:408 already assigns it), and correct the carve-out note to say __init__ re-exports the `results` helpers plus `statevector`. Update the lib/utils/__init__.py docstring to describe what it actually exports rather than what lives in the package. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`lib/utils/__init__.py:8`, `README.md:387`, `README.md:381-385`, `lib/utils/__init__.py:1`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category E)

### 26. qcsim browser simulator
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** CLOSED 2026-06-25 (PR #55) — the eval-catalogued parity-coverage gap is closed: `tests/test_qcsim_parity.py` now numerically compares qcsim against real Braket across rx/ry/rz, y/z/s/t, swap, cphaseshift, ccnot, cz via a randomized-circuit fuzz (`:203-219`) plus an explicit multi-gate fixture (`:352-359`). PR #55 also corrected three plan-stage errors (Braket-faithful qubit compaction, Gate-like operator, `measured_qubits`). Impact: High. Effort: Medium. — completed 2026-06-25


#### Feature 26 — 2026-07-20 Category F re-audit (21 adversarially-verified findings; every parity claim proven by running the SAME circuit through qcsim and the real Braket LocalSimulator and diffing, with the observed output recorded)

> Verification materially CORRECTED one finding rather than rubber-stamping it. The claim was that the parity suite's global-phase-blind assertion lets a convention regression through. True, but the verifier found (a) `test_gate_fixture.py` already catches the specific Rz example with a phase-sensitive fixture comparison, and (b) the real hole is narrower and worse: the fixture covers 13 of 15 gates, omitting `_cphaseshift` and `_CCNOT` — and mutating `_cphaseshift` by a pure global phase left the ENTIRE 434-test suite green, on the very gate `qft_circuit` is built from. It also proved the proposed fix INSUFFICIENT: with the assertion tightened AND `_rz` mutated, parity still passed 26/26, because no parity CIRCUIT exercises rz/rx/cphaseshift/swap/ccnot/s/t. The gap is circuit coverage, not the assertion. Items below reflect the corrected analysis.

- [x] **[Parity]** print(circuit) lays out one column per GATE, not per moment — contradicting qcsim's own Braket-matching .depth in the same cell — `Circuit.__str__` appends exactly one column for every gate in `self._gates`, so the printed diagram's column count equals the GATE count. Real Braket packs gates into moments, so its diagram's column count equals `.depth`. qcsim already computes the correct moment-packing in `.depth` (verified identical to Braket on 7 circuits) — it just does not use it when rendering. The result is that qcsim's own printed output contradicts its own printed number, in the first-week notebooks whose stated lesson is circuit depth and parallelism. Separately, single-qubit gates render as `name[0]`, so Rx, Ry and Rz all print as an indistinguishable `R`. _Fix:_ Render column-per-moment instead of column-per-gate by reusing the greedy packing `.depth` already implements at circuits.py:389-399: assign each gate the moment `1 + max(last_moment[q] for q in its qubits)` and merge gates sharing a moment into one column. Then the printed column count equals `.depth` by construction, matching Braket and removing the self-contradiction. Widen the single-qubit cell to a 2-char label (`Rx`/`Ry`/`Rz`, `H`, `X`, `S`, `T`) so rotations are distinguishable. No API change — `__str__` only. Impact: High. Effort: Medium. (`qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:440`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:389-399`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:445`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category F)
- [x] **[Resilience & tests]** The real-Braket parity suite numerically exercises only 6 of qcsim's 15 gates — rx, rz, y, z, s, t, swap, cphaseshift and ccnot have zero state-vector/distribution parity coverage — tests/test_qcsim_parity.py is the one place qcsim is checked against ground truth (the installed amazon-braket-sdk). But the CIRCUITS table that drives test_qcsim_matches_braket only ever builds circuits from h, x, i, ry, cnot and cz. _all_gates() DOES construct a circuit using every gate qcsim supports — and its own docstring says so — but it is consumed exclusively by the two .operator.name string tests. Its matrices are never compared to Braket. The result is that nine of fifteen gate matrices can be silently wrong and the parity file stays green. I proved this by mutation: I copied qcsim/src to a scratch dir, shadowed the editable install via PYTHONPATH, and ran 14 single-line mutations. Seven survived tests/test_qcsim_parity.py entirely. Two of them (CCNOT wired to the wrong control pair, CPhaseShift sign-flipped) survive the ENTIRE non-slow suite — 450 passed — and are caught only indirectly, by slow notebook execution, even though test_notebook_contract.py's own docstring advertises -m "not slow" as a supported mode. cphaseshift is the gate 03-qft and 04-qpe are built on; ccnot is the gate 02-grovers-search and 03-multi-qubit-gates are built on. _Fix:_ Add _all_gates to the CIRCUITS table (it already exists at line 386 and is already Braket-compatible), plus one descending-qubit-order builder such as C().h(2).cnot(2,0).ccnot(2,1,0) to pin the multi-qubit axis ordering in _apply_two/_apply_three. Also change test_qubit_count_and_depth to use a circuit where greedy depth differs from gate count (e.g. h(0).h(1).cnot(0,1) -&gt; depth 2, 3 gates). No new API, no new gates — this only routes an existing builder through the existing assertion path. I prototyped exactly this and re-ran the mutants: M2, M3, M5, M6, M7 and M8 all flip from SURVIVED to CAUGHT. Impact: High. Effort: Low. (`tests/test_qcsim_parity.py:181`, `tests/test_qcsim_parity.py:386`, `tests/test_qcsim_parity.py:195`, `tests/test_qcsim_parity.py:285`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category F)
- [x] **[Architecture]** A gate's definition is scattered across four unlinked sites, and an incomplete registration fails silently instead of loudly — Adding or changing one gate requires edits at four separate places in circuits.py with nothing tying them together: (1) the matrix constant or factory in the header block, (2) a Circuit method that repeats the same three-line `_touch` / `_gates.append` / `return self` boilerplate, (3) the `_BRAKET_GATE_NAMES` dict or a `startswith` branch in `_braket_name`, and (4) an `elif` render branch in `__str__`. Sites 3 and 4 both fail OPEN. `_braket_name` ends in `_BRAKET_GATE_NAMES.get(label, label)`, so a gate whose name mapping is forgotten silently reports qcsim's internal label as if it were Braket's — the exact class of error (CNOT vs CNot, SWAP vs Swap) that the name table was built to prevent. `__str__` silently renders an unregistered multi-qubit gate as blank wires. Nothing in the test suite structurally asserts that every public gate method appears in the parity suite's `_all_gates` builder, so a new gate can ship with zero parity coverage and zero warning. _Fix:_ Collapse the four sites into one module-level registry — a single table keyed by qcsim label holding {matrix-or-factory, braket_name, arity, render glyphs} — and drive the gate methods, `_braket_name`, and `__str__` from it. Then make registration fail CLOSED: have `_braket_name` raise a KeyError naming the unregistered label rather than echoing it back, so a half-added gate breaks immediately instead of quietly mis-teaching. Add one structural test asserting the set of public gate methods on Circuit equals the registry keys equals the gates exercised by `_all_gates`, which turns 'forgot a step' from a silent divergence into a red CI run. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:122-148`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:260-337`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:440-461`, `tests/test_qcsim_parity.py:386-405`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category F)
- [x] **[Parity]** qcsim samples from numpy's GLOBAL legacy RNG; real Braket never reads or advances it — so `np.random.seed(0)` freezes the browser histogram and desynchronises every classical draw after a run — `devices.py:73` draws shots with `np.random.choice(...)`, the module-level legacy global RNG. Real Braket's LocalSimulator uses its own generator: it neither honours `np.random.seed()` nor consumes the global stream. Two divergences follow, both silent. (a) Reproducibility inverts: a seeded notebook re-run in the browser lab returns a byte-identical histogram every time, while the SAME notebook under the documented local path (`make setup` + real SDK) returns a different one every run. `04-quantum-ml/notebooks/06-barren-plateaus.ipynb` cell 15 does exactly this — `np.random.seed(1)` then `device.run(circ, shots=2000)` — and prints `sampling error` as an empirical shot-noise measurement whose whole pedagogical point ("a plateau gradient is buried under this noise floor") is that it moves. In the lab it is a constant. (b) A run ADVANCES the global stream, so every subsequent `np.random.*` draw differs browser-vs-local. That half is latent today (no runnable notebook draws globals after a run inside a seeded region) but it is one authored cell away, and nothing detects it. _Fix:_ Give the sampler a module-private `np.random.default_rng()` in devices.py so a run neither reads nor advances the global legacy stream — matching real Braket on both counts. Keep the parity suite deterministic by seeding that private generator directly (the suite already reaches private internals: `circuit._gates`, `circuit._used_qubits()`), not by `np.random.seed`. No public surface changes. Impact: Medium. Effort: Medium. (`/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/qcsim/src/qcsim/devices.py:73`, `/Users/cperez/dev/altivum-dev/quantum/tests/test_qcsim_parity.py:32-35`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category F)
- [x] **[Parity]** A float qubit index is silently accepted and run; real Braket raises TypeError — so `h(n/2)` works in the browser and crashes on the real SDK — `_touch` validates that a qubit index is non-negative and that a multi-qubit gate's targets are distinct, but never validates that the index is an INTEGER. A float sails through, becomes a distinct qubit label, and the circuit builds, runs, and returns results with a float in `measured_qubits`. Real Braket raises `TypeError: Supplied qubit index, 1.5, must be an integer.` at construction. This is the wrong direction of divergence: `n/2` is float division in Python 3, so the single most common learner indexing mistake produces a plausible-looking green run in the browser and a hard TypeError the moment the same notebook is run locally against the real SDK — exactly the failure mode qcsim exists to prevent. _Fix:_ Extend the guard that already exists in `_touch` (circuits.py:230): before the `q < 0` test, reject any index that is not `isinstance(q, (int, np.integer))` with a `TypeError` worded like Braket's (`Supplied qubit index, {q}, must be an integer.`). This tightens an existing validation to match the real SDK's — it accepts everything Braket accepts (Python int, numpy integer, bool) and rejects exactly what Braket rejects. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. (`qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:227-238`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category F)

This script closes that gap.' tests/test_gate_fixture.py repeats the claim — 'No gate is added to or dropped from qcsim without regenerating the fixture.' Neither is true for _CCNOT or _cphaseshift: build_fixture() imports and serializes ten named gates plus three rotations, and those two are simply absent from the import list and the dict. Combined with finding #1 (they are also absent from the parity CIRCUITS), the two gates that carry 03-qft, 04-qpe, 02-grovers-search and 03-multi-qubit-gates are pinned by nothing in the fast suite at all. The precedent for including them already exists: SWAP and CZ are pinned in the fixture even though math.ts implements neither (its NAMED_GATES is only I/X/Y/Z/H/S/T plus applyCNOT), so the fixture already serves as a qcsim-side drift snapshot rather than a strict TS mirror. _Fix:_ Either add _CCNOT and _cphaseshift (at the same fixed angles used for the rotations) to build_fixture() and regenerate, or — if the fixture is meant to stay a TS mirror — correct both docstrings to say it pins the subset math.ts shares and let finding #1's parity coverage be the guard for the rest. Do not leave the current wording, which asserts a completeness the code does not have. Impact: Medium. Effort: Low. Unlike `qubit_count`, `measured_qubits`, instruction `target` and `operator.name` — each of which has an explicit cross-SDK diff against the installed amazon-braket-sdk — `depth` is NEVER compared to real Braket anywhere. Its single assertion, `test_qubit_count_and_depth`, uses `h(0).cnot(0,1).cnot(1,2)`: 3 gates, depth 3. Gate count and depth coincide, so that test cannot tell the two apart. I verified by mutation that the property is entirely unguarded. _Fix:_ Extend the existing cross-SDK object-shape block in test_qcsim_parity.py with a `depth` diff against `BraketCircuit`, on circuits where depth ≠ gate count — e.g. `h(0).h(1).cnot(0,1)` (3 gates, depth 2) and a parallel-gate case — so a gate-count-shaped regression fails CI. - [x] **[Consistency]** All 15 gate methods carry zero docstrings, breaking the lib/ house style and making `Circuit.cnot?` useless in the browser lab — Every public gate method on qcsim's Circuit is completely undocumented — no summary, no Args, no Raises. This diverges from two references simultaneously. Against lib/, which this repo's other Python is held to, the gap is stark: lib/circuits/common.py documents every function with Google-style Args/Returns/Raises sections. Against real Braket, the divergence is directly learner-facing: the real SDK's `Circuit.cnot` ships a full docstring with the matrix in LaTeX plus an Args block. Because the JupyterLite lab bundles only the qcsim wheel and never ships API.md, `Circuit.cnot?` in a browser notebook returns nothing at all, while the identical keystroke against the real SDK during `make lab` returns rich help. The browser is where beginners live, and it is the one environment where introspection is the only documentation available. _Fix:_ Give each gate method a one-to-three-line docstring in lib/'s Google style: what the gate does, an Args block naming the qubit roles (control vs target matters for cnot/cz/cphaseshift/ccnot and is currently guessable only from the parameter names), and a Raises line pointing at `_touch`'s non-negative and distinct-target rules. Keep them short — this is a teaching surface, not an SDK reference — and mirror the real SDK's wording where it is accurate so the browser and local paths read consistently. Add a Raises section to the Circuit class docstring covering the two `_touch` invariants once, rather than repeating it 15 times. Impact: Low. Effort: Medium. - [x] **[Parity]** The parity suite's state-vector assertion is global-phase-insensitive, so a gate-convention regression that shifts every amplitude's phase passes CI while changing what the browser plots and grades — `_assert_state_vectors_equal` compares `abs(np.vdot(sv_q, sv_b))`, which discards global phase. That is the right test for measurement outcomes, but qcsim's raw amplitudes are NOT only used for measurement: browser-runnable notebooks plot `np.angle(amplitude)` directly, and `web/src/lib/pyodide-grader.ts` extracts the real and imaginary part of every amplitude to grade exercises. A future change to a gate's phase convention — e.g. the very common textbook `Rz = diag(1, e^{i*theta})` instead of Braket's `diag(e^{-i*theta/2}, e^{i*theta/2})` — would leave the whole parity suite green while shifting every plotted phase and every graded amplitude. Today's qcsim actually matches Braket EXACTLY (not merely up to phase), so the assertion can be tightened for free. _Fix:_ Change `_assert_state_vectors_equal` to assert exact elementwise agreement (`np.allclose(sv_q, sv_b, atol=1e-12)`) rather than unit overlap magnitude, keeping the overlap value in the failure message as a diagnostic for 'right state, wrong phase'. It passes on the current tree and turns the phase convention — which the browser genuinely renders and grades — into a CI-enforced contract. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`tests/test_qcsim_parity.py:66-67`, `web/src/lib/pyodide-grader.ts:72`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category F)

Real Braket validates the name and raises `ValueError` naming the available devices. Any backend a learner names in the browser — including `braket_dm`, the density-matrix simulator the noise material refers to — is silently answered by the same noiseless state-vector engine, so the browser reports a clean result for a request the real SDK would either reject or answer with different physics. _Fix:_ Validate the argument in `__init__` against the backend names qcsim actually implements (`None`, `"default"`, `"braket_sv"`) and raise `ValueError` naming them for anything else — mirroring Braket's fail-fast behaviour instead of silently substituting a different engine. This adds no capability; it only stops qcsim answering for a device it does not implement. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. - [x] **[Consistency]** adjoint() reports S/T where real Braket reports Si/Ti, and its docstring wrongly calls gate labels "cosmetic" — circuits.py:366-368 daggers each gate MATRIX but reuses the original label verbatim. The adjoint() docstring at circuits.py:359-362 justifies this by asserting labels "are cosmetic, used only by ``__str__``" — that statement is factually false. circuits.py:381 builds every Instruction as `_Operator(name)` from exactly that label, so the label IS the sole input to `operator.name`, the Braket-fidelity surface PR #55 and the whole `_BRAKET_GATE_NAMES` table exist to protect. The consequence: after `.adjoint()`, qcsim reports the daggered S and T gates as "S" and "T" while real Braket reports "Si" and "Ti". A learner in 03-qft / 04-qpe / 06-amplitude-estimation (all of which call `.adjoint()`) who counts or inspects instructions on an inverted circuit gets a different answer in the browser than on hardware — precisely the mis-teaching the operator-name work was meant to eliminate. The false docstring is the bigger hazard: it tells the next maintainer that labels do not matter, so the next gate added to adjoint() will inherit the same bug. _Fix:_ Make adjoint() dagger the LABEL alongside the matrix rather than copying it: map 'S'-&gt;'Si', 'T'-&gt;'Ti' (and back), and negate the angle in the parameterized labels so `Rz(0.400)` becomes `Rz(-0.400)`. Every other gate qcsim supports is self-inverse or angle-parameterized with an unchanged name, so only two new label entries are needed in `_BRAKET_GATE_NAMES` — this makes an existing surface match real Braket, it adds no callable gate. Then delete the false "labels are cosmetic" sentence from the adjoint() docstring and replace it with the real invariant: the label is the sole input to `operator.name`, so it must be daggered in lockstep with the matrix. Extend the existing `test_instruction_operator_name_matches_braket_for_every_gate` to run its `_all_gates` circuit through `.adjoint()` and diff against Braket, so this can never silently regress. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:359-362`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:381`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:366-368`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category F)
- [x] **[Architecture]** add_circuit raises a raw KeyError on a partial target_mapping where real Braket passes unmapped qubits through — circuits.py:345 remaps qubits with `target_mapping[q] if target_mapping else q`. The truthiness test is evaluated per qubit inside the generator and only ever selects between 'map everything' and 'map nothing', so a mapping that covers SOME of the sub-circuit's qubits raises an unhandled KeyError naming only the bare integer. Real Braket treats target_mapping as a partial remap and leaves unlisted qubits at their original index. This is a divergence inside the API subset qcsim claims to cover — API.md:30 advertises `.add_circuit(other)` with an 'optional target_mapping remap' and says nothing about all-or-nothing semantics. The `{}` case happens to produce the right answer today, but only by accident: an empty dict is falsy so it takes the identity branch rather than the lookup branch. _Fix:_ Replace the expression with `(target_mapping or {}).get(q, q)`, which reproduces Braket's pass-through semantics exactly, removes the per-item truthiness test, and makes the empty-dict case correct by construction rather than by luck. Add a parity test in tests/test_qcsim_parity.py that diffs qcsim against real Braket on a partial mapping, alongside the existing cross-SDK object-shape checks. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:345`, `qcsim/API.md:30`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category F)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** Circuit._max_qubit is written in three places and never read anywhere — `_max_qubit` is initialized in `__init__`, maintained on every single `_touch` call (so on every gate added to every circuit), and explicitly propagated by `adjoint()` — but it is never read by any code path. Register width comes from `_used_qubits()` / `_compaction_map()`, `depth` uses its own per-qubit dict, and `__str__` uses `_used_qubits()`. It is a leftover from the pre-compaction design that PR #160's qubit-compaction work superseded. Its survival is actively misleading: `adjoint()` going out of its way to copy the field signals to a reader that inverse circuits depend on it, and a maintainer touching compaction has to prove to themselves it is unused before changing anything. A repo-wide grep confirms the only four occurrences are the three writes and the declaration. _Fix:_ Delete the field and its three write sites. `_touch` then reduces to pure validation (non-negative, distinct targets), which is exactly what its name and its remaining behavior describe, and `adjoint()` loses a line that misleads about what state an inverse circuit needs to carry. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:223`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:232-233`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:365`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:386`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category F)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** __str__ re-implements _compaction_map inline, and devices.py guards a case it has already rejected — Two small correctness-adjacent redundancies. First, `_compaction_map()` exists as a named helper whose entire body is a one-line dict comprehension over `_used_qubits()`, yet `__str__` open-codes that identical comprehension instead of calling it — so the original-label-to-axis mapping, the single most subtle invariant in the file after PR #160's compaction work, has two independent definitions that must be kept in step by hand. Second, `LocalSimulator.run` computes `n = max(circuit.qubit_count, 1)` four lines after raising on `qubit_count == 0`, so the `max()` can never do anything; it reads as defensive coding against a state the method has already made impossible, which invites a reader to wonder whether the guard above is actually reliable. _Fix:_ Have `__str__` call `self._compaction_map()` (it can still keep `used` for the row labels), leaving one definition of the compaction invariant. In devices.py replace `max(circuit.qubit_count, 1)` with `circuit.qubit_count` so the zero-qubit contract is enforced in exactly one place — the explicit guard that raises the Braket-matching error message. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:254-256`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:430,435`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:411`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/devices.py:60-62`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category F)
- [x] **[Consistency]** qcsim uses typing.Optional and typing.Iterable plus quoted forward refs that lib/ has fully retired — All three qcsim modules open with `from __future__ import annotations`, which makes every annotation a string at runtime — yet the code still uses the pre-PEP-604 spellings and quotes its own forward references. devices.py imports `Optional` and annotates `Optional[str]`; circuits.py imports `Iterable` from `typing` rather than `collections.abc`; and Circuit's chainable methods all return `"Circuit"` in quotes, with `Instruction.__init__` taking `"_Operator"` quoted, none of which is necessary under the future import. lib/ has none of this: a grep across the whole package returns zero `typing.Optional` usages, zero `typing.Iterable` imports, and consistent `X | None` annotations. Since qcsim already mixes both styles internally — circuits.py:342 correctly writes `dict[int, int] | None` while devices.py:50 writes `Optional[str]` for the same shape — the file is inconsistent with itself as well as with the rest of the repo. _Fix:_ Switch `Optional[str]` to `str | None`, move `Iterable` to `collections.abc`, drop the now-redundant quotes on `"Circuit"` and `"_Operator"` (keeping the quoted `"Circuit"` in devices.py, which is a genuine TYPE_CHECKING-only reference), and parameterize the counts return as `Counter[str]`. Purely mechanical and ruff-clean, and it makes qcsim read like the rest of the repo's Python for the maintainer who arrives from lib/. Impact: Low. Effort: Low. (`qcsim/src/qcsim/devices.py:6,50`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:11`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:342`, `qcsim/src/qcsim/circuits.py:260-337`) — added 2026-07-20 — DONE 2026-07-20 (PR pending, Category F)
- [x] **[Cleaner code]** str(circuit) renders Rx, Ry and Rz identically as "R", collapsing three distinct gates into one glyph — `__str__` renders every single-qubit gate as the first character of its internal label. For the three rotation gates that character is always 'R', so `Rx(0.1)`, `Ry(0.2)` and `Rz(0.3)` all print as ' R ' and are indistinguishable in the diagram — the axis, which is the entire semantic content of the gate, is exactly what gets dropped. The inline comment at the render site acknowledges the collapse ('for parameterized gates this is R') without treating it as a defect. This surface is learner-facing: `print(circuit)` appears in eight notebooks including 01-foundations/01-first-circuit, the very first lesson. Two of those printed circuits contain rotations (06-hybrid-jobs/02-parametric-compilation prints an rx circuit, 01-foundations/05-circuit-composition prints an ry circuit), so today a learner is shown an unlabeled 'R' where the axis is the point of the exercise; a notebook that ever mixes two rotation axes in one printed circuit would become actively ambiguous. _Fix:_ Render the rotation axis rather than truncating to one character — widen the parameterized single-qubit cell to the two-character 'Rx' / 'Ry' / 'Rz' and pad the fixed-width columns accordingly, so the glyph carries the axis. Driving the render from the gate registry proposed in the gate-registration finding makes this a data change rather than another `elif`, and removes the first-character heuristic that caused the collision.
